actions.thought: A6o from UTG is a marginal hand that's generally too weak to open in early position. It plays poorly multiway and is dominated by better aces. Starting the tournament with solid fundamentals - folding this.

Q6 offsuit is a weak, easily dominated hand and plays poorly out of position. I have several players left to act (Cutoff, Button, SB, BB), so attempting a steal or calling 100 would risk getting 3‑bet or playing a multiway pot where my hand has poor equity. With 100bb stacks and plenty of hands to wait for early in the tournament, preserving chips is the best play — fold.

I am in the Cutoff position and the action has folded to me. Ace-Deuce offsuit is a marginal but standard opening hand in 6-max play from this position. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to attempt to take down the blinds or play the hand in position against the Button or blinds if they call. This establishes an aggressive image early in the tournament.

I am on the Button with KJo facing a standard 2.5x open from the Cutoff. Against a Cutoff opening range, KJo has reasonable equity and blocks some value hands like KK, JJ, AK, and AJ. While 3-betting is an option to isolate, KJo often struggles against a calling range or a 4-bet. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and keep the Cutoff's range wide, including hands I dominate (like KTs, QJs). Therefore, flat calling is the most standard and profitable line.

We’re in the small blind, out of position versus a cutoff open and a button flat, with a weak offsuit hand (9♢5♥) that plays poorly multiway and is often dominated when it makes a pair. Completing 200 more gives us bad realization and tough postflop spots; squeezing is not attractive with this holding at ~100bb effective. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

Early in the tournament with deep stacks (99BB). 4h5s is a suited connector with good implied odds potential for straights and flushes. Facing a CO raise and BTN call, creating a multiway pot. Pot odds are excellent: 150 to call into 650 (pot will be 800), requiring ~19% equity, which 45s achieves against wide ranges. OOP but playable due to playability and deep stacks. Folding is too tight this early; raising is unnecessary with this hand. Standard call to realize equity multiway.

Early in tournament with deep stacks (97.5BB). Flop Ah Ts 5c rainbow hits my 4h5s for bottom pair with poor kicker (4). Facing CO raiser (Flash) and BTN caller (Pro), both with strong-ish ranges likely containing Ax, Tx, sets, etc. OOP first to act in 800-chip multiway pot. Bottom pair has showdown value but vulnerable to domination and draws. No strong draw myself (only backdoor spade flush draw). Betting risks raising out better hands or getting check-raised; checking realizes equity cheaply multiway, controls pot, and allows turn play (fold to bets likely if missed). Standard check to pot control and playability.

I have top pair with an Ace on a dry board (Ah Ts 5c). As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to extract value from middle pairs like Tens or pocket pairs, and to protect my hand. While my kicker (Deuce) is the weakest possible, top pair is still likely the best hand in a three-way pot on this texture. I will bet around 45% of the pot to keep the pot manageable while still applying pressure.

I hold KsJh on an Ah-Ts-5c board. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (requiring a Queen) and position on the aggressor (Flash). Flash c-bets 350 into a pot of 800, which is a standard size on this texture. Getting pot odds of roughly 3.3:1 (requiring ~23% equity), my gutshot (4 clean outs) plus potential backdoor draws and the ability to bluff later in position make a call profitable. Folding is too weak given the price and implied odds if I hit the straight against an Ace. Raising doesn't accomplish much against an Ace-heavy range.

Early in the tournament with deep stacks (~97BB). Holding bottom pair (5s) with a terrible 4 kicker on AhTs5c rainbow board. Pre-flop was a CO raise and BTN flat into BB for good odds with suited connector, but flop action: checked through, then Flash (original raiser) cbets 350 into 800, and Pro (BTN) calls. Now facing a bet and a call multiway OOP. My hand has some showdown value but is crushed by villain ranges (Ax, Tx, sets, better 5x like A5/T5/65). No strong draws (gutshot? No, just pair). Pot odds ~19% (350 into 1850), but equity <15% multiway vs polarized ranges. Calling bloats pot OOP, leads to difficult turns where I'm first to act. Raising is a blatant bluff without fold equity. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in hand 1/150.

I have top pair with the lowest possible kicker (Ace-Deuce). After being called on the flop, checking the turn is a solid pot-control move. My hand is strong enough to beat middle pairs or draws, but I'm likely behind any other Ace or a slow-played set. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply and potentially induce a bluff from a weaker hand or a draw like 89s.

The turn is the 7d, making the board Ah-Ts-5c-7d. Flash checks. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and King-high. Flash's check usually indicates a marginal made hand (like weak Ax or Tx) trying to control the pot, or a give-up with air. Betting here is risky because an Ace rarely folds, and I have very little showdown value currently. Checking back allows me to take a free card to try and hit my straight on the river (4 outs to the nuts). If I hit the Queen, I can value bet the river. If I miss, I can re-evaluate. Taking the free card minimizes variance and maximizes equity realization.

The river paired the Ten, meaning my hand is now two pair, Aces and Tens, with a 7 kicker from the board. Since my kicker (Deuce) is lower than the board's 7, any Ace with a kicker of 7 or lower (A2-A7) results in the same hand. Pro called the flop but checked back the turn, suggesting they likely have a marginal made hand like a middle pair or a weak Ace. If I bet, I am likely only called by hands that beat me (better Aces or a Ten) or hands I chop with. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply and potentially catch a bluff from missed draws or lower pocket pairs.

On the river, the board is Ah-Ts-5c-7d-Th. The pot is 1500. Flash checked to me. I hold KsJh (King-high). Flash's line of bet-flop, check-turn, check-river suggests either a weak Ace, a pocket pair trying to get to showdown, or a busted draw (like KQ, QJ). My King-high has very little showdown value against any pair (Ax, Tx, or pocket pairs), but it does beat Flash's busted straight draws (QJ, KQ, 89). 

Bluffing here is risky. Since I checked back the turn, my range looks capped, and it is hard to credibly represent a Ten (Trips) or a strong Ace that wouldn't bet the turn for value/protection. Consequently, a weak Ace in Flash's range will likely look me up, thinking I am taking a stab at the pot. Additionally, it is Hand #1 of the tournament, and preserving chips rather than running a low-probability bluff is preferable. Checking back allows me to realize my equity against his pure bluffs without risking chips against his made hands.

Q♣4♣ is too weak to open from UTG in a 6-max game. Out of position to five opponents, dominated by most Qx and broadway hands, and with poor postflop playability despite being suited. Early in the tournament with 100bb effective, there’s no reason to invest chips in a marginal hand — preserve stack for better spots. I will fold.

I am in middle position with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand with poor connectivity and low high-card value. In a 6-max tournament structure, opening this hand from middle position is significantly below the standard range. Given we are only on the second hand of the tournament, there is no reason to play suboptimal hands or attempt a high-risk steal here. The most disciplined and standard play is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). The action has folded to me. While the Cutoff is a stealing position, K9o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by calling ranges (KQ, KJ, KT, A9). At this early stage of the tournament (Hand 2/150) with 100BB stacks, there is no need to force action with weak-marginal holdings. Opening here creates reverse implied odds situations. A disciplined fold is the standard play.

Folded to us on the button with QTo (reasonable steal/iso hand in 6-max). We have position and can profitably apply pressure to the blinds’ wide defending ranges. I’ll use a standard small open size to risk little while keeping our range strong and maneuverable postflop; if we face a 3-bet, we can usually fold comfortably given offsuit playability.

AhJh is a premium suited broadway hand, strong enough to 3-bet versus a button open-range which is likely wide including steals. Position is OOP, but isolating the button thins the field and builds the pot for value. BB acts behind and may defend wide, but our hand has good equity and playability. Standard 3-bet size of ~3x the open (750 total bet size) applies pressure while leaving room for 4-bets or post-flop flexibility. Deep stacks support this aggression early in the tournament.
T4o is a very weak hand. Facing a 3-bet to 750 with 650 more to call, this is a clear fold. T4o has poor playability and is dominated by any reasonable 3-betting range.

We opened QTo on the button as a reasonable steal, but versus a small blind 3-bet to 750 this offsuit hand performs poorly: it’s frequently dominated by Elon's value-heavy broadways (AQ/KQ/QJ/AT) and doesn’t realize equity cleanly even with position. With no strong read that Elon is over-3-betting, calling creates too many tough postflop spots and 4-bet bluffing is unnecessary this deep. I’ll take the disciplined peel-off and preserve stack for better defend/4-bet candidates.

I am UTG with 8s 7c. This is a very weak hand to open from the earliest position in a 6-max game. Standard strategy dictates folding such marginal offsuit connectors here to avoid playing a weak hand out of position against potentially stronger ranges from the five players behind me.

I hold Pocket Fives in Middle Position with a deep stack (94 BBs). In a 6-handed game, this is a standard open-raise spot to define ranges and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set-mine if called. Limping invites squeezes and plays poorly post-flop. I will open to 2.5x BB to balance implied odds with pot control.

J8o is too weak versus a middle-position open at 100bb effective. Calling invites dominated-top-pair and reverse implied odds even with position, and it’s not a good candidate to 3-bet bluff (poor blockers, poor playability when called). With no strong read that Pro is opening overly wide, the best EV decision is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

On the button with TJo offsuit facing a small raise to 250 (2.5BB) from middle position after UTG fold. Position is excellent for playing speculative hands. TJo has broadway straight potential, decent playability postflop, and blockers to straight draws. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 into 400 (~1.6:1 direct, better with implied odds deepstacked ~100BB). Flatting realizes equity against MP range, keeps pot small, and leverages position. Folding too tight from BTN vs single raise; 3-betting polarizes too much without strong blockers or suited value. Early tournament, preserve stack while accumulating small edges.

Ac2c is a suited ace giving me nut flush potential. Pot is 650 and I need 200 to call (3.25:1 odds). Out of position but suited aces play well in multi-way pots. Calling is reasonable here - folding is too tight and 3-betting is aggressive with A2s against a raise and a caller.

I’m in the big blind with 8♦5♦ facing a MP raise to 250 and two callers. Pot is 850 and it costs me 150 more (total commit 250) — the pot odds (~15%) are attractive for a suited connector with deep stacks and good implied odds. Multiway pots reduce fold equity so a squeeze/raise is unattractive with this hand; better to see a flop and proceed cautiously. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to significant aggression if I miss, and play aggressively only when I pick up strong pair/flush/straight equity.

I flopped top pair (aces) with the nut flush draw - excellent spot. In a 4-way pot out of position, I have two good options: check to potentially trap/check-raise, or lead for value. With this much equity (top pair + 9 nut flush outs), I'll bet small to build the pot while protecting against free cards. Betting around 1/3 pot keeps weaker hands in while building value.

I have 8♦5♦ on a 4♣ Q♣ A♥ flop and missed completely. Claude’s flop aggression to 350 into a 1,350 pot forces a 350 call. Pot odds = 350 / (1,350 + 350) ≈ 20.6%. I have only 6 clean outs (three 5s, three 8s) → roughly a 24% chance to pair by the river, but many of those pairs will still be behind Ax/Qx or overpairs/sets. With multiple players and a raiser showing strength, my true equity to win is well under the break-even threshold. Raising as a bluff is unattractive (multiway, low fold equity) and calling commits chips with a very weak hand that rarely realizes good implied odds. Preserve the stack early and fold to this flop aggression.

I hold Pocket Fives on a board of Ac Qc 4c. This board texture heavily favors the opponents' ranges, particularly the Small Blind who led out (donk bet) into four players. With an Ace and a Queen on board, my underpair has very low equity and is likely drawing to two outs at best. Furthermore, there is a player left to act behind me. In a multi-way pot where the SB shows aggression on a wet, broadway-heavy board, my hand serves no purpose continuing. I fold to preserve my stack.

Preflop flat from button with TJo offsuit vs single MP raise was standard for position and playability. Flop Ah4cQc is extremely wet: flush draws, open-enders, strong Ax, sets. My TsJh has no pair, no flush draw (wrong suits), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), just jack-high with broadway blockers but zero equity realizations. Claude's SB donk-lead after flatting pre vs raise suggests polarized range: strong hands (Aces, sets, two-pair) or bluffs/draws, but board favors value/draws heavily. Pro (original raiser) folding eliminates their strong Ax range. Pot odds 1350:350 (~23.5% needed) unprofitable without fold equity or implied odds, but villain likely barreling draws/value. Early in tournament (hand 3/150, 98BB deep), no need to speculate with air vs aggressive line on scary board. Clear fold preserves stack for better spots.

I am UTG with 5h Th. This is a very weak hand, being a low suited two-gapper. In a 6-max game, UTG requires a strong opening range (typically pairs, strong broadways, and high suited connectors). Opening with T5s from early position is a losing play long-term due to the high probability of being dominated and playing out of position against 5 opponents. With 91.5 BBs, there is no pressure to make loose moves. The correct play is to fold.

We’re in middle position with J2 offsuit, which has very poor playability and is dominated by the broadway jacks and most reasonable continuing ranges behind us. Limping is -EV (invites isolation and multiway domination), and raising turns a weak hand into a bluff without strong blockers or postflop equity. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

98o is a strong opening hand from the cutoff with two folds ahead, providing good playability, straight potential, and fold equity against the blinds. Deep stacks (nearly 100BB), early in tournament (hand 4/150), so stealing blinds is profitable. Standard open size around 3x BB (300 total) to build pot and define ranges. Limping too passive, folding too tight given position.

64 suited on the button facing a 3x raise from cutoff. While I have position, 64s is too weak to profitably call here. It doesn't play well enough postflop against Elon's opening range to justify the investment. Easy fold.

7♣6♦ offsuit in the small blind facing a 3bb cutoff open to 300. This hand is weak, out of position, has poor blockers and limited backdoor equity. The big blind still acts, so calling invites multiway pots and potential squeezes; 3-betting 76o is too thin a play. Preserve stack and avoid a negative-EV spot early in the tournament — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o. Elon raised to 300 from the Cutoff. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (200 to call for a 450 pot), K3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given Elon has shown some aggression in previous hands and K3o is easily dominated, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

6d7h is an offsuit, low connector and too weak to open from UTG in a 6-max, ~100bb effective game. Opening it will often face 3-bets from late position and we realize equity poorly out of position; limping is also inferior and invites isolation. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better UTG opens.

Js7c is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by better jacks and lacking straight potential. In middle position with multiple players behind, folding preflop is standard to avoid tough out-of-position spots and preserve our deep stack for premium hands early in the tournament.
Pocket 9s in the cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a strong hand to open-raise with position. Standard raise to 2.5x to build the pot or take down the blinds.

I have Qh2s — a weak, marginal hand but I'm on the button so I have positional advantage against the cutoff raiser. Claude opened to 250; 3-betting Q2o as a bluff is too thin (poor blockers, likely to be multi‑way with the SB/BB behind). With ~97bb effective stacks I can call to keep the pot manageable and use position postflop to fold to heavy aggression or apply pressure if I connect. If either blind 3-bets big preflop I'll fold; if we get heads‑up to the flop I'll play aggressively when I hit and control the pot otherwise.

I have Ace-Three offsuit in the Small Blind. Claude raised from the Cutoff and Minni called from the Button. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a 650 pot), A3o is a weak hand to play out of position against two active players. If an Ace flops, I will likely have kicker problems, and if it doesn't, I have very little equity. Folding is the safest play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot of 800, requiring ~18.75% equity), but I am holding 98o, an off-suit connector, out of position against two opponents (Cutoff and Button). While 98s would be a standard defend due to flush potential improving playability and equity realization, 98o suffers significantly in multi-way pots. Being out of position with a marginal off-suit hand often leads to difficult post-flop spots where I will under-realize my raw equity. Against a CO open and a BTN flat, my hand is likely dominated or flipping at best, and the lack of suitedness tips this from a marginal call to a fold.

I have pocket 9s on a Q-T-3 rainbow flop. I was the preflop raiser and Minni called from the button. I have an underpair to both the Q and T, but my hand has decent showdown value. A standard c-bet here makes sense to represent strength and potentially take down the pot. I'll bet around 1/3 pot to stay balanced and get value/protection while not overcommitting with a medium-strength hand.

I have Qh2s — top pair but a very weak kicker on a Q-T-3 board. Claude (cutoff opener) makes a very small flop bet (225 into 875, ~25% pot). That size keeps a wide range (made hands, floats and bluffs) and gives excellent pot odds to continue. Raising is an option, but with a 2‑kicker it’s thin: a raise can get 3‑bet/isolated by better Qx, TT, or sets and puts me in uncomfortable spots out of position on later streets. The correct, lower-variance line is to call and use my positional advantage to control the pot and extract value when appropriate. Plan: call the 225; if Claude barrels big on the turn I’ll fold to heavy aggression, if he checks the turn I’ll cbet for value, and if he fires another small bet I’ll call and re-evaluate on the river.

Turn is a blank 4h. I have an underpair to both Q and T. Minni called my flop c-bet, suggesting they likely have a Q, T, or a draw. Continuing to bet with 99 here is thin value at best - better hands call, worse hands fold. Checking to control pot size and potentially get to a cheap showdown is the better line here.

I have Qh2s — top pair with a very weak kicker. Claude (CO) opened preflop and made a tiny flop c-bet, then checked the turn (Qd Ts 3c 4h). His turn check indicates a wide, often weak or pot‑controlling range. In position I should take a small value stab to extract from worse Qx and some Tens and to charge any straight draws, while keeping the pot manageable because my kicker is poor. I will bet 400 (~35% of the 1100 pot). If Claude check‑raises or comes in large I will fold; if he calls I will re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously versus big bets.

Minni bet 400 into a 1100 pot after I showed weakness by checking. With 99 I have an underpair to Q and T. Their line (call-call-bet when I check) suggests they have at least a pair, possibly Qx or Tx. However, pot odds are 3.75:1 (need ~21% equity). My 99 has some showdown value against bluffs and weaker Tx hands, and I can still improve. The price is reasonable to see the river and potentially catch a 9 or win at showdown if Minni is bluffing.

River Ah is a bad card for me. I now have an underpair to three overcards (A, Q, T). Minni bet the turn when I showed weakness, suggesting they have at least a pair. The Ace could have improved their range (Ax hands). With 99, I have marginal showdown value at best. Betting would be a bluff that gets called by all better hands. Check and hope to win at showdown against busted draws or bluffs.

Check. I have top pair but a very weak 2‑kicker and Claude’s line (CO open, tiny flop c‑bet, turn check then call of my 400, and a river check) is consistent with a lot of hands that beat me — better Qx kickers, Tens, and now any Ace (AK/AQ/AX) that the river completed. The pot is ~1,900 and my hand’s showdown value is marginal; betting risks getting raised or called by many hands that dominate my kicker while extracting little value from worse hands. Pot control is the lowest‑variance line here: check back and take showdown. If Claude fires a meaningful river bet I will fold; I’ll only call very small bets that look like missed draws.

Q7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability and low equity against typical ranges. UTG in a 6-handed game requires a tight opening range to avoid difficult spots OOP. No limpers yet, pot is just blinds, so folding is optimal to preserve stack and wait for better spots.
2h 5d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. From middle position with players still to act behind me, this is an easy fold. No reason to invest chips with such a marginal holding.

Cutoff with Kc6s — a marginal hand but fine to open from late position facing only blinds and the button. Stacks are deep (~107 BB), so I want fold equity and initiative rather than limp and get multi‑way. A 3x BB open (300 total) is a standard size to discourage loose calls from the blinds and the button while still keeping pots manageable with a hand that plays better in position. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet; if called, use position to continuation‑bet favorable flops, realize equity when checked to, and apply pressure on later streets when appropriate. This size maximizes steal equity and retains maneuverability postflop.

I am on the button holding 97o. Minni has opened for 300 from the cutoff. 97o is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, even with positional advantage. It is well below the standard calling or 3-betting range in this spot. Therefore, I will fold and look for a more favorable situation.

I hold Jd4d in the Small Blind facing a 3bb open from Minni in the Cutoff. While my hand is suited, J4 is disconnected and weak. Being out of position against a Cutoff opener and having the Big Blind still to act makes calling unprofitable. The hand lacks the playability or blocker effects to justify a 3-bet bluff. The pot odds of roughly 36% are not sufficient to overcome the positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard play.

72o is the bottom of the deck and plays very poorly out of position versus a cutoff open. Although the immediate pot odds (200 to win 450) look tempting, real equity and realization are both too low against a reasonably strong opening range, and there’s little upside with reverse implied odds. No need to take a marginal defend this early/deep; I’ll wait for better spots.
J6o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, and UTG requires a tight opening range in 6-handed play.

I have 9♦5♥ offsuit in middle position — a weak, unconnected, easily-dominated hand with poor post-flop playability. There's no raise yet; calling 100 into a 150 pot to see a flop is not a good price given how poorly this hand fares multiway and out of position (Button and Cutoff still to act). Raising would be speculative and likely to get me in uncomfortable spots against better ranges. I preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in the cutoff position, there is no strategic justification for playing this hand, especially with three players still to act behind me. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with QJo in an unopened pot. This is a standard opening spot to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. QJo is strong enough to raise for value and fold equity against the blinds. I will open to 2.5x (250) which is a standard sizing to put pressure on the SB and BB.

Facing a 2.5bb BTN open from the small blind, we’ll be out of position for the rest of the hand and also have the BB behind who can squeeze. 8♦4♦ is a very low suited hand with weak connectivity and poor playability—when we make a pair it’s often dominated, and the flush/straight potential is too thin to justify defending from the SB at this stack depth. Without good blocker value for a 3-bet bluff, the best EV line is to let it go and preserve stack for better SB vs BTN defend candidates.

Pocket sixes (66) is a premium hand facing a button isolation raise from Pro after folds around. Button ranges are typically wide (30-50% of hands), against which 66 has excellent equity (~55%+ heads-up). SB folded, so likely heads-up post-flop. Calling OOP is suboptimal as it allows Pro to realize equity with broadway hands and floats. 3-betting takes initiative, builds pot with value, denies equity to Ax, KQ, suited connectors. Sizing to 750 (3x the open) is standard for deep stacks, balancing value and pressure. Pot odds for set-mining are good if flat (150 into 400), but 3-bet preferred for position denial and range advantage.

I opened QJo from the Button for 2.5bb, and the Big Blind (Elon) 3-bet to 7.5bb. I need to call 500 chips to win a pot of 1550, requiring about 32% equity. Although I have position, QJo is a hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, AJ, JJ+). Elon has shown aggression by 3-betting previously (Hand #2), suggesting he is capable, but without a stronger read that he is bluffing excessively wide, QJo is near the bottom of my defense range and suffers from reverse implied odds. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

UTG with Q♦5♠ — a weak, easily-dominated hand in early position. In 6-max action UTG is effectively 'first to act' and this hand has poor playability OOP against the active table (several players have shown willingness to open/3-bet). Calling 100 out of position with Q5o will put me in tough spots on later streets and a raise would be overly spewy. With a healthy stack and many hands left, preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in middle position with 8h 7d. This is a speculative hand that doesn't play well out of position or against many opponents. In a 6-max game, 87 offsuit is below the standard opening range for this position. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

I hold 2d 3c, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em due to its lack of high card strength, low connectedness, and off-suit nature. Although I am in the Cutoff, which is a decent position to steal blinds, opening with this specific hand carries negative expected value, especially with three players yet to act. At this early stage of the tournament (Hand 8/150) with a healthy stack, there is no reason to get out of line. Folding is the only standard play.

Folds to me on the Button with a live 150 in the pot. Even with a marginal offsuit queen, position plus immediate fold equity makes an open profitable, and using the minimum raise size limits the cost if Elon (who has shown a willingness to 3-bet) attacks. Plan: fold to most 3-bets, continue versus flats with positional advantage and play small-pot poker.

Facing a standard button open-raise to 2BB (200) into a 350 pot, needing to call 150 more (total bet 200). My 5h7h is a suited connector with excellent playability, straight and flush potential. SB vs BTN, defend wide (~40-50% hands standard). Pot odds ~2.3:1 direct, but deep stacks (100+BB) provide strong implied odds when we hit. Postflop OOP but hand flops well vs wide BTN range (20-40% hands). BB likely defends wide too, multiway pot favors speculative hands. 3bet not ideal as semi-bluff without great blockers; fold too tight early in tournament. Call to realize equity and play postflop.

I have Q8s in the BB facing a min-raise from the button and a call from the SB. Pot is 500 and I only need 100 to call, giving me 5:1 odds. Q8s is a playable suited hand with flush potential and a decent high card. These pot odds make calling an easy decision.

Preflop call with 5h7h was standard from SB vs BTN open, speculative suited connector with playability and implied odds in deep-stack tournament. Flop 2c9c9d completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw. Paired board favors BTN's wide raising range (pairs, Ax, broadways, some suited connectors) and BB's defending range. First to act OOP with air and zero equity vs two opponents, no fold equity for a bluff (dry paired board, they can have 99 easy). Pot 600, check to induce bluffs or check-raise only with nuts which we don't have. Plan: check, fold to bets unless tiny to realize fold equity post-turn, but standard is check-fold to preserve stack early.

I have Q8s on a 2c9c9d board - completely missed. No pair, no draw (wrong suit for the club flush draw). Board is paired with potential trips out there. With two opponents still in the pot, betting with Q-high has minimal fold equity and I'd be in bad shape if called. Checking to see a free card is the best play here.

Paired flop (9-9-2) is a strong range-advantage texture for the preflop raiser: I have all the overpairs/strong 9x, while blinds have a lot of unpaired junk and small pairs that will often fold to one bet. Multiway reduces fold equity, so I want a small, low-risk c-bet that targets overcards and weak pairs and keeps my range uncapped. With Qc3h I don’t have showdown value, but I do have some backdoor equity (Qc for backdoor clubs) and can comfortably fold if check-raised.

Preflop call with 5h7h suited connector from SB was standard for playability and implied odds deep. Flop 2c9c9d is a complete whiff: no pair, no draws (hearts flush draw blocked by clubs, no straight potential with 5-7 vs 2-9-9). Paired board hits villain's BTN wide raising range hard (99, 9x, A9, etc.), and check-check from BB suggests weakness but doesn't change our zero equity. Facing 200 bet into 800 pot (pot odds ~4:1 or 20% needed), but we have <5% equity even vs bluffs, no fold equity OOP multiway early in tournament. Preserve stack, check-fold as planned; folding is GTO to avoid spew.

I have Q8s on 2c9c9d - complete air with no pair, no draw (wrong suit for clubs). FiveTwo bet 200 into 600, a small sizing but I have zero equity. Even if I spike a Q or 8, I could easily be behind a 9, overpair, or better kicker. Calling with queen-high and no draws is burning chips. Fold.

I am UTG with Ace-Four offsuit. In a 6-max game, this is a very weak hand to open from early position as it is frequently dominated by better Aces and lacks post-flop playability. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, folding is the standard and most profitable play to avoid getting into difficult spots out of position.

I am in Middle Position with a very weak hand (Qd 3c) facing a UTG fold. This hand has no playability, poor equity, and is off-suit. At this stage of the tournament (Hand 9/150) with deep stacks, playing this hand would be a significant mistake. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

As4o is a bit weak but in the cutoff with two players already folded, it’s strong enough to open as a steal and benefits from the ace blocker (reducing the chance of strong Ax/AA behind). Limping is generally lower-EV here; I’d rather apply pressure and take down the blinds/avoid multiway spots. If faced with a 3-bet from Button/SB/BB, this is an easy fold; if called, I’ll play pot-control and avoid building big pots with one-pair weak kicker.

23dd is a very weak suited connector with poor equity against a cutoff raising range, which is likely wide but still favors hands that dominate mine. On the button, position is good, but calling 250 into 400 pot OOP postflop multiway (blinds behind) is too loose early in the tournament with deep stacks. No reason to 3-bet bluff as it's speculative and ranges are wide. Folding preserves stack for better spots.
52s is a weak hand in the small blind facing a cutoff raise. I'm out of position, and even with 2:1 pot odds, this hand doesn't play well postflop. Easy fold.

Facing a cutoff open to 250 from 2.5x with K♠8♥ in the big blind. K8o is not good enough to 3-bet for value or as a reliable bluff (it’s often dominated), but it’s worth defending heads-up. The pot is 400 and I need 150 more to call; calling to a total of 250 gives ~27% break-even equity (150 into a 550 pot after my call), which K8o has against a fairly wide CO opening range. Stacks are deep (~100bb) so I can realize equity postflop. Plan: call, proceed cautiously on the flop — continue with a king or strong draw, pot-control or fold to heavy action when missed.

I defended the 2.5x cutoff open preflop with K♠8♥ and that call was fine. On the A♥ J♥ 5♣ flop I’ve essentially missed — no pair and only a very weak backdoor equity (I’d need both turn and river hearts to make a flush, and a single king on the turn would only give me a marginal pair behind many A/J/paired hands). The cutoff’s opening range is heavy in Ax, broadways and pairs that connect well with this flop, so I don’t want to build the pot with such a weak hand. I’ll check to control the pot and see what FiveTwo does. Plan: fold to a sizable c-bet (pot-sized or large) because my equity is poor; I can consider calling a small c-bet to see a turn (a heart or a king meaningfully improves me), but avoid getting committed without hitting.

Heads-up in position after opening preflop, Minni’s BB defend range contains lots of worse Ax, Jx, 5x, and heart draws that can call a small continuation bet. With A4 (weak kicker, no heart) I want thin value/protection without inflating the pot too much or exposing myself to a costly check-raise line. A small ~1/3-pot c-bet accomplishes that: folds out pure air, charges hearts/overcards, and keeps my range consistent on this texture. If raised large, I can comfortably let it go given kicker/disadvantage vs strong Ax/two-pair/sets.

The flop A♥ J♥ 5♣ completely misses K♠8♥ — I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart (need two more hearts to make a flush). However FiveTwo’s bet is small (200 into a 750 pot ≈27% of the pot). Calling 200 to win a 950 pot requires ~21% equity (200/950 ≈ 21.1%), which is achievable against a wide CO c-bet range. With deep stacks and a wide button/cutoff opening profile, I can realize equity on later streets if I improve. Plan: call the small c-bet and see a turn. If I pick up a king, a heart, or a very favorable card I can continue; if I don’t improve and face substantial aggression (pot-sized+ bet or a raise) I will fold. A raise here is too thin — I lack the blockers and showdown equity to credibly bluff-raise against a wide but reasonable c-betting opponent.

Check. The turn 3♠ is a complete blank for my K♠8♥ — my backdoor heart flush route is dead (I would have needed both turn+river hearts), so I have no pair and essentially only six outs (3 kings + 3 eights) to make a single pair on the river (~6/46 ≈ 13%). Pot control is mandatory: the pot is 950 and I have no value or realistic drawing equity to charge for. I’ll check to see what FiveTwo does — if he fires a large bet I’ll fold; I may call a very small stab on the turn, but raising/leading here would be a thin bluff with little fold equity and bad blocker coverage. Plan: check, go to the river and re-evaluate only if I pair or face a very small bet.

Turn is a relative brick (3s) that doesn’t improve many of Minni’s flop-calling range, while plenty of worse hands can still continue: heart draws, some Jx/5x, and weaker Ax (A2–A9) that defended pre and peeled flop. With A4 (no heart) I don’t want to bloat the pot, but I do want to deny equity and extract thin value. A small second barrel accomplishes that and keeps me able to comfortably fold if check-raised, since raises heavily weight AJ/sets/two-pair/strong Ax.

Board: 5♣ J♥ A♥ 3♠. I hold K♠8♥ — no pair, backdoor heart route is dead after the 3♠, so I only have ~6 clean outs (3 Ks, 3 8s) to hit the river (~13% raw). FiveTwo’s turn bet is tiny (300 into a 1250 pot, ~20–25% pot-size), so the price to see the river is cheap. A raise would be a very thin bluff (I lack relevant blockers and showdown equity), and folding here surrenders too much equity against a wide cutoff double-barrel. I’ll call the small stab to see the river, plan to fold to a pot-sized or large river bet if I miss, and if I pair the river I’ll reassess (call medium bets, be cautious vs heavy action).

Board: 5♣ J♥ A♥ 3♠ 6♦ — I hold K♠8♥. I have no pair and my backdoor heart route is dead. FiveTwo opened from the cutoff and barreled flop (200) and turn (300) — a line that is value-heavy (Ax, Jx, two pair, sets) and not something I block. Pot is 1550 and there is currently no river bet. Betting here as a bluff would be thin: I have poor blockers and very limited fold equity against a player who has shown multi-street aggression. I’ll check to preserve my stack and avoid turning a thin bluff into a losing commitment; if FiveTwo fires the river I will fold to any meaningful bet because my showdown equity is essentially zero.

River brings no obvious draw completion (hearts miss), and after Minni has check/called flop and turn then checked river, their range is a mix of: (1) better Ax/two pair/sets that will happily check-call or occasionally check-raise, and (2) missed hearts/weak pairs that we already beat. With A4 (weak kicker, no heart) a thin value bet risks getting looked up mostly by better aces and trapping hands, while many worse hands may just fold. Checking back secures showdown versus the missed draws and avoids a costly river check-raise spot.

I am in UTG position with Jc8c. In a 6-handed game, UTG is the earliest position and requires a strong range to open. J8s is a marginal hand that is typically outside the opening range for UTG; it is easily dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, or JT and suffers from being out of position post-flop. While it has some playability due to being suited, the gap and low kicker make it a fold in this spot to preserve chips for better opportunities.

We’re in middle position after UTG folded with T3 offsuit. This hand has very poor playability, is dominated by most continuing ranges, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the blinds and late-position players. With no compelling reason to steal (and plenty of better opens available), we preserve stack and avoid marginal spots.

Folded to me in cutoff after UTG and MP folds, this is an ideal stealing spot with dead money in the pot (150). Qh3h is suited, queen-high, offering good playability for flush draws, straight draws, or pair+draw post-flop. Deep stacks (~100BB) and position allow aggressive play. Opponents like Minni and Flash have folded blinds before; button Claude may fold wide. Raising standard 2.5x BB (250 total) balances value and bluffs while maintaining fold equity and pot control.

Q4 suited on the button facing a 2.5x open from the cutoff. This is a marginal hand but I have position. Q4s has some playability with flush potential. Given my positional advantage, I'll call and see a flop. If blinds squeeze, I can fold.

Fold. Holding 9♦2♥ in the small blind versus a CO raise to 250 and a Button call is a very weak, dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability. Calling 200 into a 650 pot gives ~23.5% break‑even equity (200/(650+200)), but against two calling ranges my actual equity is far below that and I’ll be out of position for the rest of the hand with the big blind yet to act. A squeeze raise to 400 is unnecessarily risky with this holding — preserve the 10k stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3c-6h, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Elon raised from the Cutoff and Claude called from the Button. Although I am getting favorable pot odds to call (150 to win a pot of 650), the hand has extremely poor playability and I will be out of position against two opponents for the remainder of the hand. In a multi-way pot with garbage cards, the risk of losing more chips post-flop outweighs the potential benefit of defending the blind. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Top pair (queens) with a weak 3 kicker on a draw-heavy flop (two spades for flush draw, 4-5 for wheel straight draws). Board: 4s Qs 5h. Pre-flop raiser facing a button caller who can have wide range including draws, Ax suited, worse Qx, or floats. Continuation bet standard for value/protection against draws, denies equity, builds pot with likely best hand. Deep stacks (~100BB), no position post-flop but betting initiative crucial. Sizing: ~2/3 pot (433) rounded to 400 for pot control and fold equity vs draws/floats. Backdoor heart draw adds some equity but primary value is pair.

I flopped top two pair (Queens and Fours) on a Q-4-5 board with a spade flush draw present. This is a very strong hand that I need to raise for value and to protect against the flush draw. Elon's c-bet of 400 into 650 is standard, and I should raise to build the pot while charging draws. I'll raise to around 1000.

Facing a flop raise from Claude on the button after calling my preflop raise. Board is 4s Qs 5h, highly draw-heavy with spade flush draws and wheel straight draws (e.g., 23, 36, 67). My Qh3h is top pair with a terrible kicker and only backdoor heart equity. Claude's range for raising includes better Qx (QJ+, QA, QT), sets (unlikely but QQ/44/55), two-pair combos, strong spade draws (e.g., AsKs, JsTs), and straight draws that have equity. I'm out of position post-call or raise, and Q3 is crushed by most value hands while vulnerable to draws hitting. Pot odds to call 600 into 2050 (3.4:1 direct, but post-call pot ~2650 for ~4.4:1) are decent, but implied odds are poor OOP with weak kicker—no room to get value from worse hands, and we'll face aggression on turn/river. Raising risks even more vs monsters; shoving overbet too deep (~100BB). Best play is to fold and preserve stack for superior spots in a long tournament. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression when strong (e.g., Hand 3, Hand 5).

K3o is too weak to enter UTG in 6-max: it’s heavily dominated by better kings and broadways, plays poorly out of position, and has bad reverse implied odds when we make a weak top pair. With several players behind who have shown willingness to raise/3-bet, limping is also unattractive. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Preflop in middle position with 6d2s, a complete trash hand with no high card value, no straight potential, no suitedness. UTG folded, but no reason to enter the pot voluntarily with this hand in a 6-handed game. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.
J8o in cutoff with two folds in front. Marginal hand but decent position with only button/blinds behind. Worth a standard open raise to 2.5x to try to steal the blinds or play in position if called.

Button with Qc6s facing a cutoff open to 250 — hand is marginal offsuit queen but position is very strong. I prefer to call and play the flop in position against what is likely a fairly wide CO opening range in 6-max. I avoid a 3-bet with this hand because it has poor playability and is often dominated; a min-re-raise to 400 is too small to credibly fold out ranges and invites 4-bets from deeper stacks. Calling 250 keeps the pot manageable, preserves stack depth, and lets me use position to realize equity or apply pressure on favorable flops. If I face a 3-bet, I can fold without significant loss.

I am in the Small Blind holding K5o. Claude raised from the Cutoff and Minni called on the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to call into a pot that will be at least 850), K5o is a very weak hand to play out of position in a multi-way pot. The likelihood of being dominated or having to fold to post-flop aggression is high. Strategic discipline in a tournament suggests folding these marginal hands from the blinds to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d Th (T9 offsuit). Claude (Cutoff) raised to 250 and Minni (Button) called. The pot is 650 and I need to call 150. This offers me pot odds of roughly 18.75% (150/800). While my raw equity against two wide ranges might technically exceed this percentage, playing an unsuited connector out of position in a multi-way pot is difficult. My equity realization will be low due to the positional disadvantage and the offsuit nature of my hand, leading to reverse implied odds issues if I hit a pair or draw. Standard strategy often folds T9o in this specific spot (multi-way, OOP) to avoid bleeding chips in marginal situations. I choose to fold.

I completely whiffed this flop with J8o on Qh5d6c. I'm out of position against Minni who has the button. C-betting into a caller with just jack-high on a Q-high board is risky - Minni likely has something if they called my preflop raise. Checking here is prudent; I can fold to a bet or see a free card if checked through.

I hold Qc6s on Qh-5d-6c — that's two pair (Qs and 6s) on a dry, rainbow flop. Claude was the CO opener and checked the flop; his range contains many Qx and single-pair hands I beat, while the handful of hands that beat me (55, 66, QQ, or Q6) are comparatively rare. There are essentially no meaningful straight or flush draws to worry about. With a strong but not invulnerable made hand heads-up, the correct line is a value bet to extract from worse queens/top-pair hands and medium pairs while keeping the pot controlled against the small set of stronger hands. I size to roughly half the pot (325) — large enough to get value from one-pair hands but small enough to induce calls rather than folding out too much of his calling range. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises I’ll proceed carefully (calling or committing depending on sizing and board runout) because sets are in his range.

I have J8o on Qh5d6c - complete air with just a gutshot (need a 7). Minni bet 325 into 650, giving me 3:1 odds. With only 4 outs (~8% equity), this is a clear fold. I'm out of position with no showdown value and the gutshot isn't strong enough to continue.

9s6s is a suited connector with some postflop playability, but from UTG in a 6-handed game, it's outside my opening range. Standard UTG opens are tighter: 77+, ATs+, KJs+, AJo+, KQo+. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots from poor position. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacks (94BB) allow waiting for premium hands. Opponents show aggression, but no need to speculate early position.

J4s is a marginal hand in middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. While suited, it lacks connectivity and the jack is easily dominated. Not profitable to open-raise or limp here. Standard fold.

KcTc suited is a clear open-raise from the cutoff — good high-card strength, suitedness and connectivity with position on the button and blinds. I want to thin the field and build a pot I can play postflop, so I’ll size up from the minimum. Raising to 300 (3x BB) makes callers pay, reduces multiway scenarios, and preserves fold equity vs the button and blinds. With deep stacks (≈104 BB) this hand plays well postflop; I’ll continuation-bet favorable K/T/A-type flops and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards or vs big 3-bets (re-evaluate if faced with a large 3-bet).

I am on the button with 9c 5c. Minni, in the cutoff, has raised to 300. While suited, 9-5 is a weak hand that does not play particularly well against a standard cutoff opening range. Although I have position, calling or 3-betting here with such a weak hand is likely to be -EV in the long run against active players. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 8s, facing a raise to 300 from the Cutoff (Minni). This is a weak off-suit hand (Jack-Eight offsuit) and I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand if I call. The pot odds are roughly 2.8:1, requiring about 36% equity, but my realized equity will be significantly lower due to the positional disadvantage. Against a standard Cutoff opening range, J8o is dominated and does not play well post-flop. With 86 big blinds, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. The correct play is to fold.

Facing a 3bb cutoff open, 9-3 offsuit is near-bottom of range and plays extremely poorly out of position with weak high-card value and little connectivity. Even with decent immediate pot odds, our equity realization is bad and we’ll often be forced to fold postflop after missing. Preserve stack and avoid marginal OOP spots.
4h9s is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is too weak to open from the earliest position at a 6-max table. Easy fold.

I have Jc7c in middle position. UTG folded and there has been no raise; pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop. J7s is a speculative hand with backdoor flush/straight potential but is marginal for an MP open-raise with four players left to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB), some of whom have shown aggression previously (Pro, Elon). Raising to 200 would risk getting 3-bet and creating difficult out-of-position decisions. Calling 100 is cheap relative to my ~106bb stack and lets me realize equity and see a flop. Plan: continue only with a pair, strong draw, or favourable texture; fold to heavy action if I completely miss; consider aggression if I pick up initiative or a strong draw.

Queen-Six offsuit is a very weak starting hand and does not justify entering the pot, even from the cutoff. Minni has already limped from middle position, and there are three players still to act behind me. Given the stack depths and the poor playability of this hand, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

I hold a premium hand (AKo) on the Button facing a limp from Minni. My goal is to isolate the limper and build a pot in position. Limping usually indicates a marginal hand, and calling would let the blinds in cheaply, creating a multi-way pot which is less favorable for AKo. A standard isolation raise size is 3bb plus 1bb per limper. With blinds at 50/100, a raise to 400 (4bb) is appropriate to extract value and take the initiative.

Button is isolating a limper, so his range is likely wide. Td7d has decent playability and benefits from seeing a flop multiway with implied odds. A squeeze/3-bet here would be a fairly low-quality bluff (poor blockers) from out of position, and folding is a bit tight given the price and potential to realize equity when we flop draws. We’ll defend by calling and play postflop cautiously out of position.

AKo is a premium hand from the BB facing a button raise to 400 after a limp and SB defense. Excellent equity, especially against a potentially wide button range. Pot is 1000, calling 300 offers great odds, but 3-betting is better to deny equity to worse hands like Ax, KQ, broadway, thin the field (SB and limp may fold), build the pot for value, and take initiative. Pro has raised/folded previously, suggesting loose opens. Raise sizing to 1200 (3x the raise) is standard with deep stacks, balancing value and fold equity. Position postflop on SB and Minni.

I called the 100 preflop as a cheap speculative play with J♣7♣, but the hand has changed: Pro opened to 400 and Elon 3‑bet to 1,200. I must call another 1,100 into the current pot (which implies I need ≈34% equity to make a call worthwhile). J7♣ has poor equity versus a typical 3‑bet range, will be dominated a large portion of the time, and plays very badly out of position against both the opener and the 3‑better. A 4‑bet or shove as a bluff with ~105bb effective is too risky here — I can’t profitably continue with this marginal hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I hold AKo on the Button, a premium hand. After my isolation raise to 400 and a call from the SB, the BB (Elon) has squeezed to 1200. Elon has shown aggression with 3-bets in previous hands (Hands #2 and #7), suggesting a potentially wider range than just premiums, though it likely includes strong value (JJ+, AK, AQs). Calling is the superior option here for several reasons: 1) It keeps Elon's range wider, including dominated hands like AQ/AJ or bluffs that might fold to a 4-bet. 2) I have position on the post-flop betting rounds, which is a significant advantage. 3) My stack depth (over 80bb) allows for maneuvering post-flop without committing everything pre-flop. 4) The pot odds are favorable; I need to call 800 to win a pot of 2100 (plus potential future bets), requiring only about 27% equity, which AKo easily exceeds against a squeeze range. I call to realize my equity in position.

We’re facing a BB squeeze to 1200 with Td7d from the small blind, now in a 3-bet pot against two opponents and out of position. Although the immediate price looks tempting (800 to win ~2900), the SPR after calling is low (~2–3), which dramatically reduces the implied-odds value of suited connectors. Our hand will realize equity poorly multiway: we’re often dominated on made hands (T-high pairs) and even some flushes/straights can be second-best versus stronger suited/broadway ranges. With no relevant blockers and no credible 4-bet bluff, continuing is likely -EV despite the pot odds. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

Preflop 3-bet with AKo gives me a range advantage over Pro's button calling range, which likely includes Ax, broadway, suited connectors, and pocket pairs. The flop 8h5d3d is low and somewhat drawy with diamond flush draw and gutshots like 46/67/24. My AhKs has ace-high equity, blocks AA/AK, and has backdoor heart draw. As preflop aggressor OOP, continuation betting maintains initiative, denies equity to draws/floats (charging flush draws ~9% equity and straight draws), gets value from weaker Ax/pairs/top pairs like 88/55/33, and protects against overcards. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or inducing bluffs I can call down with. Sizing 1500 (~50% pot) balances value, fold equity, and keeps SPR reasonable (~3.5:1 post-bet with 7400 behind). Deep stacks allow flexibility for turns.

I hold AcKd on a 8h5d3d board, giving me two overcards and a King-high flush draw. This is a very strong drawing hand with significant equity; I am flipping against overpairs (QQ-99) and dominating dominated broadways or bluffs. Elon's bet of 1500 into 2900 is substantial, but given my hand strength, folding is not an option. Raising would likely fold out his bluffs (like AQ/AJ) which I am beating, and isolate me against his value range (Sets, Overpairs) where I am at best a coin flip. Calling allows me to keep his range wide, use my position, and realize my equity. The SPR will be just under 1 on the turn, meaning I am effectively committed, but calling is the superior line to maximize EV against his aggressive profile.

On this turn board of 10s, the texture worsens for my ace-high continuation range: the ten connects with broadway cards like J9, QJ, or KJ that Pro's button calling range could include after calling my preflop 3-bet and flop c-bet. My AhKs remains ace-high with minimal showdown value, no pair, and only a weak heart backdoor that didn't improve. Barreling turn risks getting called by improved hands (sets, two pairs, newly made straights like 67 or overpairs), while value is thin against weaker pairs/draws that fold. Checking OOP pots control, realizes equity against bluffs/floats by offering a cheap card or setting up a river call/fold, maintains balance with my strong preflop range, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal bluff spot given Pro's deep stack and position to bluff us off it. SPR is already low (~2:1 post-check), favoring check-call or check-fold. Deep stacks behind allow patience for better spots over 150 hands.

On the Turn, the board is 8h 5d 3d Ts. I hold Ac Kd, giving me two overcards and the second-nut flush draw. The pot is 5,900 and my remaining stack is 5,900, creating an SPR of exactly 1.0. After aggressively squeezing pre-flop and betting the flop, Elon checks to me. His check signifies either a give-up with air (which I beat with Ace-high), pot control with a marginal made hand (like JJ, QQ, or Tx), or a trap (less likely on a wet board). Betting here would essentially require committing my entire stack given the SPR. If I bet, I likely fold out hands I am already beating (bluffs) and get called by hands that beat me (sets, overpairs), effectively isolating myself against a stronger range. By checking back, I take a free card to realize my high equity (flush draw + overcards) and potentially win at showdown with Ace-high if he is bluffing. I can re-evaluate on the River.

River Qc completes some Qx hands from Pro's calling range (QJ, QT, suited queens) and potentially broadway like KQ/JT, but no flush or straight. My AhKs is still ace-high, which has decent showdown value against bluffs, floats, and missed draws (diamond draws ~9% missed, gutshots missed). Preflop 3-bet, flop c-bet, turn check balances my range. Betting river as a bluff is risky OOP: Pro can hero-call with top pair/Qx or better, or raise improved hands; thin value from Qx unlikely given my range strength. Pot is 5900 with SPR ~1:1 (6750 behind), but checking realizes equity against Pro's checking range (bluffs/weak pairs), induces bluffs if Pro bets, and allows cheap showdown. Pro's turn check-back suggests caution, possibly weak or medium strength unwilling to bloat. Over 150 hands, patient play preserves stack; checking down ace-high postflop aggression is standard and profitable long-term vs button calling ranges.

I hold AcKd on a board of 8h 5d 3d Ts Qc. The pot is 5,900 and my stack is 5,900 (SPR 1.0). Opponent Elon squeezed pre-flop, bet the flop, but has now checked both the turn and the river. His line is consistent with either a missed draw (which I beat), a chop (AK), or a pot-controlled marginal pair (99-JJ) or top pair (AQ/KQ) that is checking for showdown. The River Queen improves his AQ/KQ combos but misses his other broadway floats. As I have significant showdown value beating his missed flush draws and lower Ace-highs, and bluffing is risky given I don't credibly represent a strong made hand after checking back the turn, the best play is to check back. I take the free showdown to realize my equity without risking my tournament life on a bluff that is unlikely to fold out a pair.

T♠3♦ is a very weak, unsuited, easily-dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability. I’m UTG (first to act) in a 6‑handed game — this spot requires a much stronger open‑raise range. Calling (limping) for 100 would leave me out of position against likely stronger ranges and open‑raising to 200+ would be unnecessarily loose with a 105BB stack. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in Middle Position with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) after the UTG player folded. ATo is a strong opening hand in a 6-max game. Raising is the standard play here to take initiative and build the pot. I will open for 3x the big blind (300 chips) to put pressure on the remaining players and the blinds.

I am holding 8h 4s in the Cutoff. This is a very weak unconnected off-suit hand. Facing a 3BB open raise from Middle Position (Flash), I have no equity to call and no blockers or credible story to re-raise as a bluff. The standard and only reasonable play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

Facing a 3x open from Flash with two players still to act in the blinds, 5♠3♠ is a very low suited connector that plays poorly versus a reasonably strong middle-position range and is vulnerable to being squeezed by the blinds. The price isn’t good enough to justify a peel purely for implied odds, and it’s not a great 3-bet bluff candidate either. I’ll pass on the marginal spot and preserve stack for higher-EV button opportunities.

Preflop in the small blind facing a 3x raise to 300 from Flash in middle position after UTG fold, with folds from cutoff and button. My hand is 9hJd offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability out of position postflop. Pot odds are roughly 450:250 (1.8:1, needing ~36% equity), but 9Jo has low equity against a typical MP opening range (dominated by better broadways, pairs, suited connectors). Calling OOP risks difficult spots with a hand that rarely makes strong hands and struggles to realize equity. Raising (3-bet) is not justified without blockers or premium strength. BB still to act but unlikely to squeeze wide. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid marginal situations early in the tournament.
J4o is a weak hand facing a raise from middle position. I'm getting 2.25:1 odds to call 200, but J4o plays poorly postflop and I'll be out of position. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Queen-Deuce of spades. While it is a suited hand, it is extremely weak for an opening range from early position in a 6-max game. I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against most of the table, and the hand is easily dominated. Therefore, the standard professional play is to fold.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit (KQo) in middle position with approximately 88 big blinds. In a 6-handed game, this is a strong starting hand that sits well within a standard opening range from this position. Folding would be too tight, and limping allows opponents to see a cheap flop or isolate me. My goal is to take the initiative, build the pot, and ideally thin the field to play heads-up in position or take down the blinds immediately. A standard open-raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here, consistent with my previous play (e.g., Hand #3, Hand #7).

Facing a 2.5x open from Pro in middle position, 8♦6♠ offsuit is too weak to defend profitably in the cutoff. It plays poorly vs a stronger opening range, is often dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well without position postflop pressure. With no compelling dynamic reason to 3-bet bluff here, folding preserves stack and avoids negative EV spots.

Preflop on the button with Jc6c, a suited connector. Pro raised to 250 from middle position into a 150 pot (blinds + raise), standard size. My position is excellent for speculative hands like this due to implied odds if I hit draws or two-pair/straights. Stacks are deep (mine 9650, pot 400 post-call), so calling realizes equity multiway potentially (SB/BB to act). Folding too tight; 3-betting too strong for this hand without reads. Pro's history shows some aggression but folds to 3bets (Hand 7). Call to play postflop in position.

I have JJ in the small blind - a premium hand. Pro raised to 250 and Elon called. With pocket Jacks, I should 3-bet here to build value and thin the field. A raise to around 1000 (4x the open plus extra for the caller) is appropriate to charge draws and weaker hands while building a pot with my strong holding.

Facing a 3-bet to 1,000 (I’ve posted 100, so I must call 900). Pot is 1,600; calling 900 would make the pot 2,500 — I need about 900/2,500 ≈ 36% equity to break even. My hand (6h3h) is a weak, low suited connector that fares poorly against a typical SB 3‑bet range (pairs, broadway hands, strong Aces) and is especially bad out of position and multiway. Stack is deep enough to speculate, but the combination of a large 3‑bet, likely dominated equity, and the prospect of playing OOP or multiway makes a call or a squeeze raise (+1750 min) unjustified. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am facing a 3-bet squeeze from the Small Blind to 1000 chips (10BB) after the Button flat-called my open. Although King-Queen offsuit is a strong opening hand, it performs poorly against a tight 3-betting range (often dominated by AK, AQ, AA-QQ). The large raise size offers marginal pot odds (~32%) which barely matches my equity against a value range, and the presence of the Button (Elon) behind me creates a risk of being sandwiched or facing a back-raise. In Hand #7, I folded QJo to a smaller 3-bet; this situation warrants a similar disciplined fold to preserve my stack of ~86BB for better spots.

Facing a 3-bet from Claude in the SB after I called Pro's open from the button with Jc6c suited connector. The pot is 1600, and calling 750 commits ~8% of my stack into a pot where I'll be out of position heads-up against a likely strong 3-bet range (AA-JJ, AK, AQs+, etc.). J6s has ~25-30% equity vs a tight 3-bet range but poor playability postflop without hitting hard. Claude has shown aggression (e.g., Hand 3 flop raise, Hand 5 preflop raise), and Pro folded, isolating us. No fold equity for a 4-bet bluff here with this hand. Best to fold and preserve stack for better spots; implied odds aren't there for the risk. Previous call was speculative multiway in position, but dynamics changed.

I am in UTG position with 4d Ts, which is a very weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. In a 6-handed game, the opening range from UTG should be tight (top 15-20% of hands), consisting of pairs, strong broadways, and suited connectors. This hand falls far outside that range. Playing trash hands from early position is a losing strategy due to the likelihood of facing raises and playing out of position post-flop. Therefore, I must fold.

Kd2c offsuit is too weak to enter from middle position in a 6-max lineup. It’s dominated when we make a king, has poor playability postflop, and we risk getting 3-bet by the aggressive players behind (Claude/Elon) without enough equity to continue profitably. Preserve stack for better steal/value spots.

Preflop in cutoff with folds from UTG and MP, unopened pot. Td3d is a suited gapper with straight and flush potential, playable in late position. Deep stacks allow for postflop play. Standard to open-raise wide from cutoff to steal blinds (pot 150) or play in position. Raising to 225 (2.25x BB) balances fold equity vs. calling range, applies pressure on button and blinds. Limping weak, folding too tight for position.

AJs on the button is a premium hand. Facing a min-raise from cutoff (Elon), I have an excellent 3-bet opportunity. I'm in position with a strong hand that plays well postflop. A 3-bet to around 700 should isolate Elon or take down the pot preflop.

I have 2♦7♥ — the worst offsuit hand in hold'em. There's been an open to 225 and a button 3-bet to 700, leaving me to call 650 with no position postflop. This hand is almost never playable in a multi-street pot vs a 3-bettor: it has very poor equity, is easily dominated, and I have little fold equity if I try to bluff. A cold-call commits chips out of position into what looks like a strong range, and a 4-bet bluff here would be unnecessary risk early in the tournament. I’ll conserve chips and fold.

I am holding Pocket Queens in the Big Blind, the third-strongest starting hand. Elon opened from the Cutoff and Claude 3-bet from the Button. Claude has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands (Hands 10 and 15). Flat-calling here would put me in a difficult spot out of position against one or two players. A 4-bet is the standard play to take initiative, define my hand strength, and potentially end the hand pre-flop or isolate Claude. I will raise to 1,850, which is roughly 2.6x Claude's 3-bet, signaling significant strength and forcing him to commit or fold his medium-strength holdings and bluffs.

Preflop action is heavy: I opened to 225 from CO with Td3d, a speculative suited gapper playable in position. Button Claude 3-bet to 700 (strong range: JJ+, AK, maybe some bluffs). BB Flash then 4-bet to 1850, indicating premium strength (QQ+, AK). My Td3d has ~30-35% equity vs typical 4-bet range but drops multiway if called. Pot odds ~2.7:1 to call 1625 into 2825, but implied odds limited by reverse position dynamics and opponents' strength. Raising min to 3000 commits ~33% stack thinly with marginal hand. Folding the 225 is <3BB, preserves stack for 134 hands left. Deep stacks favor waiting for better spots.

Flash 4-bet to 1850 from BB after my button 3-bet. This is a strong line - 4-betting from BB over a button 3-bet typically indicates a premium range (QQ+, AK). AJs is in a tough spot: against this range I'm often dominated by AK or flipping against QQ. Pot odds are ~2.5:1 needing ~29% equity, which is borderline. Being out of position postflop makes this worse. While AJs has decent equity in a vacuum, against a tight 4-bet range it's marginal. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

UTG in a 6-max game with ~85–115bb effective stacks, K6 suited is too weak to open profitably against competent opponents. It’s dominated by the continuing ranges (K9s+/KTo+, better suited kings, broadways) and plays poorly out of position, especially versus frequent 3-bets from players like Flash/Claude. No need to invest chips from UTG here—wait for a stronger open.

Preflop, UTG (FiveTwo) has folded, and I'm in middle position with AdTs (ATo), a strong hand in the top 12-15% range suitable for an opening raise from MP in a 6-handed game. Deep stacks (~90BB effective) and no prior action warrant a standard open-raise to build the pot and take initiative. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots where ATo plays poorly postflop. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a balanced size based on prior action patterns (opens around 225-300), denies equity to limpers/cold-callers, and sets up postflop playability. Pot is 150 (blinds), min-raise 200, but 300 is standard and not oversized.
Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from middle position, I have no business calling or 3-betting with this holding. Easy fold.

Elon opened to 300 from middle. On the button with 9h5h suited I have a marginal but playable hand with good backdoor flush/straight potential and strong implied odds with ~103BB effective stacks. Calling 300 keeps the pot manageable, preserves my positional advantage, and lets me realize equity — a thin 3-bet bluff risks facing a 4-bet or multiway action from the blinds. Plan: call 300, proceed post‑flop aggressively when I hit hearts/9/5 or strong draws; otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

I am holding 4c 2s in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. There has been an open raise to 3bb from Elon and a call from Minni. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with zero equity and poor post-flop playability. Folding is the only sensible option here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 9s (97 offsuit). Facing a raise to 300 from Elon in Middle Position and a call from Minni on the Button. I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 950, offering roughly 4.75:1 odds (approx 21%). However, 97o is a weak hand with poor playability, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against two opponents. The reverse implied odds are significant, and equity realization will be difficult. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve chips.

Flop board As7h7s gives me top pair (aces) with ten kicker and the nut flush draw (Ts with two spades on board, 9 outs). Extremely strong hand heads-up with initiative as preflop raiser against Minni's button call range (wide, including Ax weaker, 76s, 87s sets, draws, floats). Pot 750, first to act. Betting extracts value from worse pairs (77 full house unlikely, weaker Aces, 7x), protects against 7x/turning draws/spades that complete opponent's flush. Check invites free cards or Minni bluff-catch. Standard 1/2 pot bet of 400 builds pot, keeps pressure, allows room for turns/river aggression. Deep stacks support this.

Preflop I called 300 with 9h5h for implied odds and the chance to hit hearts/9/5. Flop: As 7h 7s — Elon bets 400 into a 1150 pot. A call of 400 requires ~25.8% equity (400 / (1150+400)). I have no pair and only a backdoor heart/straight possibility: ~24% to hit a 9 or 5 by the river and ~4.2% for the two-card heart flush (combined improvement chances don’t translate into clean winning equity vs a realistic c-bet range). Elon opened from middle and c-bets an Ace‑high board strongly; many hands (Ax, 7x, overpairs) dominate me. Raising would be a pure bluff with poor fold equity given the Ace on board. Per my preflop plan I should fold to heavy post‑flop aggression when I don’t have a real draw or pair. Preserve my ~10075 stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

A9o UTG in 6-handed play is a standard opening hand. It's ace-high with decent playability, good blocker to AA/KK, and strong equity against calling ranges (~45-55% vs typical opens). Deep stacks (96BB) allow postflop play, and positionally OOP but first in gives fold equity. Previous action with ATo (Hand 17) was raise to 300, consistent sizing here to 3BB standard open. Builds pot with dead money, steals blinds frequently based on history. Limp bad (invites multiway), fold too tight given dynamics. Raise to 300 total.
Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise of 3x, this is a clear fold. No equity or playability to justify calling or 3-betting here.

UTG open to 300 (3x) and I’m in the cutoff with pocket 5s and ~100bb effective. This is a standard set-mining spot — calling keeps the pot size reasonable, preserves implied odds to hit a set, and lets me use position if the button or blinds come along. Raising to 500 is a weak isolation that could induce a 4-bet from a tighter UTG range; folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call 300 to see a flop and proceed cautiously postflop (fold to heavy action if I miss, extract value if I hit a set).

I am holding 8 of Hearts and 3 of Diamonds, which is a very weak hand. Elon raised from UTG and Minni called from the cutoff. Despite being on the button, this hand has zero playability against an early position raise and a caller. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold 7c 5d in the Small Blind. Facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Elon) and a call from the Cutoff (Minni). My hand is very weak (7-high, off-suit gapper) and I would be playing out of position against two opponents, one of whom showed strength from early position. The pot odds (calling 250 into 750, ~25%) are not sufficient to justify playing such a poor hand in a multi-way pot from the worst position. Conserving chips is the prudent play here.

We’re getting a good price to set-mine: calling 200 more to win 750 now (pot will be 950). With ~90–100bb effective stacks vs both Elon and Minni, we have enough implied odds when we flop a set. Squeezing 44 out of position versus an UTG open + caller is too thin and will often get played back at, so the best EV line is to keep the pot small and realize our set value.

Multiway out of position with 44 on A-Q-7r, we have little equity and a big range disadvantage versus the UTG raiser and caller (they have many Ax/Qx). Donk-bluffing is low EV here and bloats a pot where we’ll rarely improve. Best line is to check and proceed only if we pick up a set or favorable runout/spot.

Top pair with ace kicker (Ah9c pairs the As on Qc Ah 7h board, wait no: board Qc As 7h, so pair of aces with 9 kicker. Weak kicker but still strong top pair on dry board. Preflop raiser OOP vs two callers (Minni CO call, FiveTwo BB defend). FiveTwo checked, indicating weakness. C-bet for value against Ax worse (AJ-AT, A-rag), Qx, 87 pairs/draws, and to charge any heart flush draws or gutshots (e.g., JT, 89). Board favors my UTG range (Ax heavy). Pot control less ideal OOP with showdown value. Size 2/3 pot (600 into 950) balances value/protection/fold equity, denies equity to draws. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop. History shows FiveTwo defends wide (e.g., Hand 17 check-fold to cbet), Minni stations calls.

I have pocket 5s on a Qc As 7h flop — an underpair with only two outs to make a set. The pot was 1,550 and Elon bet 600, making the pot 2,150; calling 600 would put 600 into a 2,750 pot (600/2,750 ≈ 21.8%), so I need roughly 22% equity to justify a call. My chance to hit a set by the river is only about 8.5% (2 outs), well below the required equity. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: UTG opened preflop (strong range) and will seldom fold top pairs/overpairs, so I’d be building a big pot out of position with a hand that is behind. With ~98bb effective stacks and plenty of better spots to realize equity, the +EV play here is to fold and preserve chips.

We’re heads-up now, out of position, facing a 63% pot c-bet from the UTG opener on A-Q-7. With 44 we essentially have only two outs to a set (~8% by the river) and no meaningful backdoor straight/flush equity. The 600 call needs ~28% immediate equity and the implied odds aren’t reliable enough (plus we’re sometimes crushed by AA/QQ/77 when we hit). Check-raising as a bluff into an UTG range is also low EV. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

Pocket Aces in UTG - the best starting hand in poker. I need to raise for value. Standard UTG raise of 2.5-3x BB to build the pot while not scaring everyone away. Going with 250 (2.5x) to hopefully get some action.

Claude opened UTG to 250 and I’m in middle position with 8♦2♦ — a very weak suited hand. Versus an UTG opener I’m likely dominated or face tough post‑flop decisions out of position. Calling 250 into a 400 pot gives poor pot odds (~38%) and my equity with 8‑2s vs a UTG range is well below that. A 3‑bet would be a thin bluff with bad blockers and would commit a lot of chips out of position if called. With deep stacks and plenty of hands and spots left in the 150‑hand structure, preserving chips and waiting for a playable hand is correct. Fold.

I am holding Ten of Hearts and Four of Clubs in the Cutoff position. Claude has opened with a raise to 2.5x the big blind from UTG. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well against an early position opening range.
