Given the high probability of being dominated and the fact that there are still three players left to act behind me, the most prudent and standard play is to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold Qc 4d on the Button. This is an unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor playability. Facing a raise from the Under-the-Gun (UTG) player, whose range is typically strong (pairs, big aces, broadways), my hand is dominated. Even with position, calling or 3-betting this hand is -EV. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Claude opens UTG into a 6-handed table, so his range is relatively strong. From the small blind we’ll be out of position the whole hand and often face a squeeze/overcall from the big blind. 9♦7♥ offsuit is too weak and dominated to defend profitably here, and it’s not a good candidate to 3-bet bluff versus UTG. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown aggression but UTG opens suggest a strong range. My 6s8c is offsuit, poorly connected, with minimal playability OOP in the BB. Pot odds are about 400:150 (roughly 3:1, or 25% equity needed), but vs tight UTG range, equity is under 30% and postflop OOP is disastrous without initiative or strong hand. Standard play is to fold speculative trash and preserve stack for better spots.

UTG with pocket threes in a 6-max 50/100 game: this is a standard open-raise. I want to take the initiative, narrow the field and build a pot where my set-mining equity can pay off. A raise to 300 (3x BB) is large enough to discourage loose multiway action and gives me fold equity vs the blinds while keeping the SPR reasonable with ~97 BB effective. Plan: call a single moderate 3-bet to set-mine, fold to very large 4-bets/shoves, and proceed postflop based on texture (c-bet small on dry boards, exercise pot control vs heavy resistance).

I am holding 2c 3d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has opened with a raise to 300 from UTG, indicating a strong range. There are still four players to act behind me. Calling or raising with such a low-equity hand would be a significant mistake. Folding is the only viable strategic option here.

I hold a pair of Eights (8s 8c) in the Cutoff facing a UTG open raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni. In a 6-handed game, pocket eights are a strong enough hand to play against a UTG range, but often function best as a flat call to set-mine or play a medium-strength pot in position. My stack is roughly 84BB, providing sufficient implied odds to call for set value (calling 300 to potentially win stacks of 9000+). 3-betting against a UTG open is risky as it isolates me against a stronger range (99+, AQ+) that dominates 88. Folding is too tight given the hand strength and position. Therefore, calling is the optimal play to realize equity while keeping the pot manageable.

Button with A4o versus UTG open and a cold-call: too weak to flat profitably (dominated Ace, poor playability multiway), but it’s a good squeeze candidate thanks to the Ace blocker and position. A standard squeeze size (3x + 1x for the caller) applies pressure to both ranges, often wins the pot pre, and keeps our plan simple (fold to a 4-bet; proceed with initiative in position when called).

K9o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing an UTG open from Minni (tight range), a cutoff call from Pro, and a button 3-bet to 1200 from FiveTwo. The pot is 1950, and calling 1150 OOP requires ~37% equity, which K9o doesn't have against these ranges (FiveTwo's 3-bet likely premium hands like QQ+, AK). Even multiway with BB behind, it's too weak post-flop with poor playability, dominated often. Preserving stack for better spots in early tournament stages. Standard fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 1200 with Q6 suited. This is a weak hand that doesn't play well OOP against a 3-bet range. I'd need to call 1100 into a pot of 1950. Q6s lacks the strength to continue here - it's not a premium suited connector and has poor playability multiway OOP. Easy fold.

I opened UTG to 300 and the Button (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1,200 — a standard, moderate 3-bet size. I hold pocket 3s, so my plan is set-mining: call a single moderate 3-bet with deep-ish stacks and fold to very large 4-bets/shoves. Calling 900 more (1200 total committed) keeps the SPR playable and preserves the implied odds — pot is 1,950 now, so after my call it will be ~2,850; immediate pot odds are ~31.6% (900/2850) while the chance to flop a set is ~11.8%, but with ~95bb effective stacks the implied odds justify a call. 4-betting to 2,100 would be overleveraging a weak hand and folding loses too much expected value given set-mining equity. Postflop plan: if I miss, fold to meaningful pressure on high/wet boards; if I hit a set, extract maximum value (raise/commit vs one or two opponents). If I face a large 4-bet shove I will re-evaluate (likely fold), but for now the correct play is to call and see a flop.

I hold Pocket Eights (8s 8c) in the Cutoff. After flat-calling the UTG open, the Button (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1200, and the UTG player (Minni) called. I now face a decision to call 900 more into a pot of 2850. While the direct pot odds (approx. 24%) are decent, the stack dynamics are unfavorable. Calling 900 represents more than 10% of my remaining stack (8150), and the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) on the flop would be less than 2. This low SPR denies me the implied odds required to profitably set-mine. Additionally, playing a multi-way 3-bet pot with a middle pair leaves me vulnerable to overcards and higher pocket pairs, often forcing a check-fold on the flop if I don't improve. Given the strength indicated by a Button 3-bet and a UTG call, and the lack of depth to maneuver post-flop, folding is the prudent long-term play.

Check. I flat-called FiveTwo’s 3-bet preflop as a set-mining call and the flop Qd 5s 9d missed my 3s. Heads-up vs the 3-bettor their range contains lots of Qx/9x and diamond combos here, and I have no meaningful backdoor draws (I can only improve to a set). Standard, low-variance play is to control the pot and realize equity: check to see how they act. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (≈≤1/2 pot, ~1,400) to try and hit a set on the turn, fold to large pot-sized/overbets on the flop, and only start building the pot or bluffing if I actually improve or pick up a very clear read that they’re folding.

Heads-up 3-bet pot in position after squeezing. This Qd-9d-5s flop interacts reasonably well with Minni’s UTG call range (Qx, 99/55, TT–JJ, diamond draws), while my specific hand (Ac4h) has essentially no real equity or useful backdoors (no diamonds, very thin straight chances). A small c-bet will get called too often and exposes me to check-raises without the ability to continue profitably. Checking back keeps the SPR manageable, realizes what little equity I have, and lets me stab turns that meaningfully improve my range or my hand (A/K/T/J, or after continued passivity).

I called the Button’s 3‑bet preflop planning to set‑mine but completely missed. The turn 8♦ makes the board Q♦ 9♦ 8♦ 5♠ — three diamonds — which massively favors the 3‑bettor’s range (many high diamonds/flushes, plus strong pairs). I have pocket 3s with only two outs to hit a set on the river (~2/46 ≈ 4.3%), virtually no showdown value and no realistic way to extract value. Betting here would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called or raised by any decent holding (or a made flush). Pot control is the priority: check and fold to meaningful aggression. If villain fires a very small stab (≤ ~1/3 pot) I may consider a call to see the river, but otherwise I will not invest further unless I hit a set on the river.

After checking back flop, Minni checking again on the 8d turn gives me a good delayed-cbet spot. The third diamond is a scare card that I credibly represent as the preflop squeezer (I have plenty of AdX, KdXd, and suited broadways), while Minni’s 3-bet-calling range contains a lot of hands that hate this turn (AK without a diamond, TT–JJ without a diamond, some Qx without a diamond). My actual hand (Ac4h) has essentially no showdown value and poor blockers, so I want a low-risk bluff rather than a big polar stab. I’ll use a small sizing to pick up the pot often, keep my risk contained, and comfortably fold if check-raised.

I called the BTN's 3‑bet preflop to set‑mine with 3♣3♥. The turn 8♦ makes the board Q♦ 9♦ 8♦ 5♠ and massively improves the 3‑bettor's range (many diamond combos, Qx/9x, strong pairs). FiveTwo now bets 950 into 3800 (~25% pot). I only have two outs to hit a set on the river (≈2/46 ≈ 4.35%), so by pure pot‑odds this call is unfavorable (you need ≈20% to break even: 950/(3800+950) ≈ 20%).

That said, the bet is very small relative to the pot and my earlier plan was to call very small turn probes (≤~1/3 pot) to try to realize set‑mining equity while folding to meaningful aggression. Given the small sizing and the chance Villain is making a blocking/probe bet with marginal Qx/9x or small diamonds, I will call one more street to see the river, but I will not commit further without improvement. If I hit a 3 on the river I will look to extract value; if I miss and face a significant river bet or shove I will fold. Total committed after this call will be 1,200 (preflop) + 950 = 2,150 chips.

River Kd completes a diamond-heavy board Qd 5s 9d 8d Kd. I hold 3h3c — I never hit my set and have no diamond, so I have virtually no showdown value. Villain (FiveTwo) 3‑bet preflop and then checked the flop and fired a small turn probe (950) — a line consistent with many diamonds, Qx/9x or medium pairs. Pot is 4750 and effective stacks ~7625. A river bluff would need a healthy fold frequency (for example a 1500 bet needs >24% folds) and against a 3‑bettor showing this line I expect too many calls (flushes, Qx, Kx, some pairs) for a bluff to be +EV; there is also essentially no value to extract. Best play is to shut down: check and fold to any bet.

River brings the 4th diamond, which is an excellent card for my perceived range after I squeezed pre and fired the 3rd-diamond turn. I can credibly have many AdX/KdX and suited diamond combos that checked flop and then bet turn. Minni’s turn check-call range contains plenty of hands that now hate life without a diamond (Qx without a diamond, overpairs without a diamond, some sets), and those should fold very often on this river versus even a modest bet. I don’t block his flushes (bad for bluffing), so I want a low-risk sizing that still forces folds from his non-diamond bluff-catchers while keeping my loss small when he does have a diamond and calls/raises. If check-raised, I’m always folding.

Fold. The pot is 6,150 and FiveTwo bets 1,400; calling requires ~18.5% equity (1,400 / (6,150+1,400) ≈ 18.5%). I hold 3♣3♥ with no diamond and I never improved — I have essentially zero equity to improve. Villain 3‑bet me preflop (a strong range), checked the flop, fired a small turn probe (950) and now makes another small river bet after K♦ completes a very diamond‑heavy board. That line and the board texture strongly favor value hands in their range (made diamonds, Kx, Qx, 9x, sets), not bluffs. Raising as a bluff here would be extremely thin and unnecessarily risky. With negligible showdown equity and a high likelihood villain is value‑betting, the correct play is to fold.

I am in the UTG position with Ten-Eight offsuit. In a 6-max game, this is a very weak hand to open with from early position. My opening range from UTG should be much tighter (typically including pairs, suited broadways, and strong offsuit broadways like AQo). T8o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a limp here, so I will fold.

I am in Middle Position (Highjack) with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) after the UTG player folded. In a 6-max configuration, A7o is generally too weak to open profitably from this position as it is frequently dominated by the calling ranges of the players remaining (Cutoff, Button, Blinds). Standard strategy dictates folding A7o from MP and looking for better spots or better position (CO/BTN) to open this hand. My stack is healthy (81.5 BB), so there is no need to force a marginal play.

CO is first in after two folds, but 6♠3♦ offsuit is at/near the very bottom of an opening range. Opening invites a BTN 3-bet from Elon and blind defenses where we realize equity poorly and get dominated, and limping is also -EV in this spot. Best decision is to pass and preserve stack for higher-leverage hands.

Preflop on the button with 3s2c offsuit, one of the worst possible starting hands. No straight or flush potential worth playing, and low cards unlikely to make a strong hand. Pot is 150 with blinds to act behind (Claude SB and Minni BB, both with deep stacks). While button position allows stealing opportunities with better hands, calling or raising 32o is a spew given the multiway potential and poor equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament.

KTs is a strong hand heads up. Everyone folded to me in the SB, so I'm only facing the BB (Minni). This is a clear raise spot - KTs plays well and has good equity against a random BB defending range. I'll raise to 300 (3x) to put pressure and build a pot with position considerations.

I have pocket nines (9h9s) in the big blind facing Claude’s small-blind open to 300. This is a hand I want to play for value/isolation — Claude has been opening a lot and stacks are deep (~75bb), so a 3-bet makes sense to both get value from worse pairs/Ax/broadways and to deny equity to speculative hands. I’ll raise to 1200 (≈4x the open): big enough to charge callers and get respect, but small enough to leave room for post‑flop play. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet, call or continue aggressively if Claude calls, and use my positional advantage on later streets to control the pot and extract value on favorable textures.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni (4x my open). KTs is a decent hand but not strong enough to 4-bet. Being OOP is a disadvantage, but KTs has good playability with straight and flush potential. Pot odds of ~1.67:1 (need ~38% equity). Against a BB 3-bet range, KTs has reasonable equity. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here - folding is too tight and 4-betting commits too many chips with a hand that plays better as a call.

Flop is 4d Qh 9d. I have KTs with no pair - just a gutshot straight draw (need a J) and two overcards. This board hits Minni's 3-bet range reasonably well (queens, overpairs). I'm OOP after calling a 3-bet, so checking is standard. I'll see what Minni does before committing more chips with just a gutshot and overcards.

I have trip nines (9h9s with 9d on the flop) on 4d Qh 9d. This is almost always the best hand vs Claude’s cold-call/flatting range after my 3‑bet (he opened wide and then called), which will include lots of Qx, medium pairs (TT-JJ), some A/Kx and speculative hands. Only pocket queens (QQ) or a very unusual slow-played hand crush me. I should be extracting value and denying free cards while keeping the pot comfortable with position. Betting about two‑thirds pot builds the pot for later streets and gets calls from Qx, smaller pairs and broadways that I beat. I’ll bet 1,600 into the 2,400 pot (~2/3) to maximize value; if he calls I’ll continue extracting on safe turn runouts, and if he check‑raises a moderate amount I’ll call and re-evaluate (only very large over‑bets/jams would force a tougher decision because QQ is the main worry).

I have KTs on 4d Qh 9d - just a gutshot (need J) and two overcards. Minni 3-bet pre and now bets 1600 into 2400. Pot odds: 1600 to win 4000 (~28% equity needed). My gutshot is only ~16% to hit by river, and my overcards are suspect against a 3-bet range that includes QQ, AQ, KQ, overpairs. No diamond draw. Being OOP makes this worse - even if I call and miss, I face another barrel. The math doesn't work for a gutshot without implied odds, and being OOP limits those. Disciplined fold.

I am holding Jack-Deuce offsuit in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position. This is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Combined with the disadvantage of being the first to act pre-flop and likely being out of position post-flop against any callers, the only strategic move is to fold immediately. There is no justification for entering the pot with this hand.

We’re in middle position with J3 offsuit, a hand that plays very poorly postflop (dominated by all better Jx and has little connectivity). In an unopened pot, limping invites multiway trouble and raising doesn’t have enough equity or playability to justify building a pot from MP. Best EV is to fold and preserve our 125BB stack for stronger opens/spots.

Pocket Queens (QQ) is a premium starting hand, second only to AA preflop. In cutoff position after UTG (Pro) and MP (FiveTwo) have folded, this is an ideal spot to open-raise for value and to isolate against the button (Claude) and blinds (Minni SB, Flash BB). Standard raise size of 3x the big blind (300) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity. Opponents have shown aggression in past hands, but QQ plays well postflop in position. No reason to limp or fold.

8To on the button facing a cutoff raise. This is a marginal hand - while I have position, 8To doesn't play well postflop and there are still two players behind me in the blinds. Not worth calling 300 with this holding. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Jc2c in the small blind — technically suited, but one of the weakest playable hands. Cutoff opened to 300, leaving me OOP against the raiser and with the big blind yet to act. Calling 250 more (to 700 pot) with this hand is a poor postflop proposition: I'm likely dominated when I hit a jack or a club, and playing multiway/OOP postflop will cost chips. A min-raise here with J2s is also unjustified. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with J8 offsuit. Elon (Cutoff) has raised to 300. To call, I need to put in 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me roughly 2.25:1 odds. While the price is okay, J8o is a weak hand to play out of position against a player who has shown consistent aggression. I have a healthy stack and don't need to defend with marginal hands that flop poorly and are easily dominated. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

6-handed UTG, QTo offsuit is too easily dominated by the continuing ranges (AQ/KQ/QJ/AT+), and this table has shown willingness to 3-bet/iso. Opening or limping invites tough spots out of position for marginal EV. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a stronger UTG holding.

T2s is a speculative suited hand with a large gap, not strong enough to play from middle position pre-flop without any action or implied odds justifying a limp or raise. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting where position and hand strength matter over 150 hands.

36 suited is too weak to open from the cutoff. Even though it's suited, this hand has very poor playability and limited high card value. With 3 players still to act behind me, there's significant risk of being raised. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

On the button with A♠5♦ (A5o) — this is a good stealing hand and very playable in position. Both blinds have healthy stacks but I want to maximize fold equity and keep pots manageable if I run into resistance. I open-raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) to take the initiative: small enough to avoid overcommitting vs a 3‑bet, large enough to pressure the SB/BB. Plan: if both fold I take the blinds; if I get one caller I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures and extract value when I pair the Ace or the 5. If I face a substantial 3‑bet (a large sizing that commits a lot of chips) I will fold — A5o is too easily dominated versus a 3‑betting range out of the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o. The Button (Minni) has opened for 300. While Minni has been active from the Button in previous hands, K6o is a weak hand to defend out of position. Calling 250 more into a 450 pot puts me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting here feels overplayed against a standard opening size. With the Big Blind still to act and my hand having poor playability, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni) while in the Big Blind. My hand, Td8d (suited one-gapper), is a standard defense candidate against a wide Button range due to its playability (flush and straight potential). I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 650, roughly 2.25:1). Although I will be out of position, the stack depth (80BB) allows for post-flop maneuvering. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary. I will call to close the action.

On a Flop of 3d-5s-Js, I have missed the board with Td8d, holding only Ten-high and a backdoor flush draw. As the Big Blind defending against a Button raise, standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is not advisable on this texture as it connects better with the aggressor's range (broadways, pairs). I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

I’m heads-up on the button with A♣5♦ (pair of fives with an Ace kicker) after raising preflop. The flop J♠-5♠-3♦ is fairly dry with one overcard (J) and just two spades, so as the preflop aggressor I have good fold equity. A modest continuation bet will fold out many pure overcards and capture value from worse pairs, some Jx calls and spade floats, while keeping the pot manageable against stronger holdings. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (250) to extract value and preserve flexibility; if I face a large raise I’ll fold, if I’m called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

I am holding Td8d on a Flop of 3d-5s-Js. I checked to the aggressor (Minni on Button), who bet 250 into a pot of 650. I have Ten-high with a backdoor flush draw (need running diamonds). My direct equity is very low against a typical value range (Jacks, sets, overpairs), and even against bluffs, I am currently losing or flipping at best. The pot odds are 3.6:1, requiring ~22% equity, which I likely do not realize out of position with such a weak holding. Floating here is a losing play without a specific read that the opponent over-c-bets and gives up on turns. There are better hands in my range to defend (pairs, flush draws, straight draws). Thus, folding is the correct decision.

Qh8h is a suited queen-high hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. In 6-max UTG, this falls within a standard opening range (suited broadways and connectors like QTs+, Q9s-Q8s often opened). Raising takes initiative, builds the pot, and leverages position advantage postflop despite being first in. Limping is suboptimal in NLHE; folding too tight given depths (100+BB). Opponents show aggression but no action yet. Standard 3x raise.

5s8d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise to 300, which typically represents strength, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with this hand out of position.

I have pocket 7s in the cutoff facing an early-position open to 300. With ~91bb effective, this is a good spot to set-mine and use position — calling 300 is a small portion of my stack (~3.3%) and gives strong implied odds if I hit a set. A 3-bet here would bloat the pot out of position against a likely strong UTG range and invite squeezes from players behind, so I prefer pot control and to see a flop. Plan: call, play aggressively if I hit or the flop is favorable; fold or concede pot control if I face heavy action on a bad board.

I am holding Ace-Four of spades on the button, which is a strong speculative hand that plays very well in multi-way pots and in position. Elon raised from UTG and Minni called from the cutoff. Calling allows me to see a flop with excellent pot odds and the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. 3-betting is an option, but against an UTG open, a flat call is safer and maintains the hand's deceptive value while keeping the pot manageable if I don't hit my draw.

I hold 3s 4c in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (low off-suit connector). An UTG raise usually signifies strength, and there are two callers already. Although the pot odds are roughly 5 to 1 (calling 250 to win a pot that will be around 1300), I will be playing out of position in a 4-way or 5-way pot with a hand that has poor playability and low equity. The potential for reverse implied odds is high if I hit a weak pair or a low straight draw. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

Facing a 3x UTG open and two callers, Tc4h is too weak and too dominated to defend profitably out of position. Even with decent pot odds, the hand has poor playability multiway and high reverse implied odds when we make a marginal ten. Not a good squeeze candidate either. Best is to fold and preserve stack.

Preflop, raised Qh8h UTG as a standard suited broadway play for initiative and position. Flop 2d Ts 8s hits me with middle pair (eights) and Queen kicker, a strong but vulnerable hand on a board with a spade flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., J9). Pot is 1050 with two callers (Minni CO, Flash BTN), both capable of having draws, worse pairs, or floats. As preflop raiser first to act, a continuation bet denies equity to draws, gets value from weaker hands like weaker pairs or Ax, and builds pot with showdown value. Betting 2/3 pot (700) sizes up well against multiway pot, charges draws, and leaves room for turns/rivers. Folding or checking off too weak given hand strength and range advantage.

Preflop I called to set-mine; on this flop (2♦ T♠ 8♠) I missed my set and hold an underpair (7♠7♥) with one spade (only a very thin backdoor). Elon (UTG open) c-bets 700 into a 1750 pot — calling 700 gets me ~28.6% pot odds (700/(1750+700)). Versus a reasonable UTG continuation-range (top pairs, overpairs, some A/K and bluffs) my pair of sevens has equity in the high-20s/low-30s, so a call is +EV and preserves implied odds to hit a set. A raise to 1400 would bloat the pot out of position with no redraws and invite stronger action or squeezes from the button; folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call the 700 to see the turn, press when I improve (or if board becomes safe), and fold to heavy continued aggression if I miss further.

I have the nut flush draw (As4s) on a 2d-Ts-8s board. Elon (UTG) bet 700 into a 1050 pot, and Minni called. I am getting excellent pot odds (700 to win 2450, roughly 3.5:1) to call, and I have the nut draw with an overcard. Calling is the best play here to see the turn in position, as raising might face a shove from Elon or force out Minni, whereas calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to extract value if the spade hits.

Turn brings the 5h, a relatively blank card that doesn't complete the primary spade flush draw or major straight draws (board: 2d Ts 8s 5h). Possible new gutshots (e.g., 36, 47) or paired weak kickers, but overall favorable for my middle pair of eights with Queen kicker. Preflop raiser range advantage still holds; multiway callers likely have draws (spades like J9s, 97s, 67s), weaker pairs (77, 66, 55 now improved slightly), or floats Ax. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raises from monsters; betting ~2/3 pot continues aggression from flop, extracts value from worse hands, protects against draws, and builds pot with my showdown value. Deep stacks allow flexibility post-turn. Standard continuation in positionally advantaged spot despite UTG.

I called preflop and the flop to set-mine, but on the turn (2♦ T♠ 8♠ 5♥) I have only two outs (two remaining 7s) — roughly 4.3% to hit a set on the river. Elon’s turn bet is 2,100 into a 5,250 pot; calling would require 2,100 for pot odds of 2,100/(5,250+2,100) ≈ 28.6%, which is far worse than my equity. UTG’s line (open, c‑bet, now a large turn bet) is much more consistent with strong value hands (T, 8, 2, overpairs, sets) than frequent bluffs, and with Flash still in the hand I have very little fold equity if I try to raise as a bluff. Per my earlier plan (call flop to set‑mine but fold to heavy turn aggression when I miss), this is a clear fold — preserve my ~81bb stack and wait for a better spot.

I have the nut flush draw (As4s) and one overcard on a board of 2d-Ts-8s-5h. Elon has double-barreled from UTG into two opponents, showing significant strength, likely holding a strong top pair (like AT), an overpair (JJ-AA), or a set. Minni has folded, so it is now head-up. I need to call 2100 to win a current pot of 5250, giving me pot odds of roughly 2.5:1 (or 28.5% equity needed). My direct odds of hitting a spade on the river are about 19.5%. While the direct pot odds are slightly insufficient, the implied odds are excellent. Elon has $5075 remaining in his stack; if I hit my flush on the river, I am very likely to get paid off or even stack him. Given my position and the strength of the nut draw, calling to realize my equity is the most profitable play.

River 3d is a low brick that doesn't complete the spade flush draw or obvious straights (board: 2d-Ts-8s-5h-3d offers no clear straight completions like 4-6-7-9). Flash called large flop (700) and turn (2100) bets as button, indicating showdown strength: likely top pair (Tx), two pair (T8s, 82s), better 8x, or missed draw turning into bluff catcher. My middle pair Qh8h has showdown value but is vulnerable to domination (e.g., T8, J8, 98). After two aggressive barrels establishing strong range, first to act: checking induces bluffs from missed draws (spades like J9s, 97s), allows cheap showdown, and invites value bets from worse that I can call given pot odds (~7:1 effective if shoved). Betting risks cooler calls from better hands with my ~pot-sized stack (7175 into 7350), thin value at best. Optimal: check, call potential bet with decent kicker.

I have Ace-high (As 4s) on a board of 2d-Ts-8s-5h-3d. I missed both my nut flush draw and my gutshot straight draw. Elon, the UTG aggressor, has checked to me on the river after betting strongly on the flop and turn. While his check could indicate a surrender with a missed draw or a marginal hand, the pot is already quite large (7350) and he only has 5075 chips remaining. If I bet, I would likely have to commit a significant portion of my stack to force a fold from a hand like top pair (Tx) or an overpair, which he might still check-call given the pot odds. Since I have some showdown value with Ace-high against his potential missed non-spade draws (though rare for his UTG range), and considering the risk-to-reward ratio of a large bluff here, I will check behind and take the free showdown to preserve my stack.

K9s from UTG is a marginal hand but playable in 6-max. Suited king with some flush and straight potential. A standard 2.5x open to 250 is reasonable - folding would be too tight and limping is weak from this position.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I have 7♦5♠ — a weak, unsuited, out-of-position hand with poor equity and many dominated combos versus an UTG opening range. Calling 250 to play OOP multiway is a losing, high-variance line and 3-betting as a bluff (min to 400) is unjustified with this holding. With ~81 BB I should conserve chips for better spots, so fold.

I am holding AQo, a premium starting hand, in the Cutoff. Claude has opened from Under the Gun for 2.5bb (250). In a 6-max game, AQo is ahead of a standard UTG opening range. I am choosing to 3-bet to 750 for several reasons: to take the initiative, to build a pot with a strong hand, and to discourage the players behind me (Button, Small Blind, and Big Blind) from entering the pot with speculative hands or attempting a squeeze. My stack size allows me to play aggressively and put pressure on Claude's opening range.

I hold Qd 4h on the Button, which is a very weak hand. The action ahead of me is heavy: Claude opened from UTG to 250, and Flash 3-bet from the Cutoff to 750. Facing an UTG open and a CO 3-bet, my hand has virtually no equity and is easily dominated. The pot odds do not justify entering the pot with this holding. I must fold immediately to preserve my stack.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a cutoff 3-bet. 5♦3♦ is too weak to defend out of position versus a strong, uncapped 3-bet range, and we’ll realize equity poorly (dominated flushes, weak straights, tough postflop spots). The price isn’t good enough to justify a call, and it’s not a great 4-bet bluff candidate here given positions/ranges. Preserve stack and fold.

Facing an UTG open from Claude and a 3-bet from Flash in the cutoff to 750. My T3 suited is a weak suited connector with poor playability post-flop against strong ranges. Pot odds require calling 650 into 1150 (~36% equity needed), but T3c has low equity vs likely QQ+, AK ranges or even lighter 3-bet mixes. Folding preserves stack for better spots in the BB.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with K9s out of position. K9s is too weak to defend against a 3-bet - I'm dominated by many hands in their 3-bet range (AK, KQ, KJ) and crushed by big pairs. Being OOP makes it worse. The pot odds aren't good enough to justify calling with a hand that will be hard to play postflop. Fold is the disciplined play here.

I'm UTG with AcTh and ~81BB. This is a hand worth opening — strong Ace, good playability and flop-equity — and I want the initiative. Limping (call 100) is too passive and invites multiway action and squeezes; a min-raise to 200 is too small. I'll open to a standard ~3x the BB to get fold equity and define ranges while keeping the raise size in line with table tendencies. If I face a large 3-bet from a very deep stack I'll re-evaluate and likely fold; versus single callers I'll play post-flop for value and position.

I am holding J4 offsuit in middle position. Minni has opened from under-the-gun with a raise to 300. My hand is extremely weak, lacks connectivity, and is dominated by almost any range a player would raise with from early position. There is no strategic reason to play this hand, especially against an UTG raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 7h 3h in the Cutoff. Minni opened to 300 from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. My hand is very weak (low ranks, gapped suited connectors) and has poor equity against an UTG range. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me. Calling or raising with this holding is -EV. The standard play is to fold.

Minni is opening from UTG to 3bb, which is typically a stronger range in 6-max. J9o has poor playability versus an UTG range (dominated by AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, and many better broadways) and doesn’t realize equity well even in position due to reverse implied odds. With squeeze risk from the blinds and no strong blockers to turn this into a profitable 3-bet bluff, the highest-EV line is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

Facing a standard UTG raise to 300 from Minni, who often raises with strong ranges based on hand history. My holding 9c8h is offsuit, mediocre connectivity, and low equity against UTG range. Out of position in SB, calling OOP would be -EV due to poor postflop playability and reverse implied odds. BB still to act could squeeze or defend wide. Pot odds are insufficient (calling 250 into ~700 pot for ~2.8:1), but primarily fold due to hand strength and position. Preserve stack for better spots.

I have 9h6h in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). While 96s isn't great against a typically tight UTG range, it has some playability being suited with potential flush and some straight possibilities. I'm closing the action and getting reasonable odds. This is borderline but I'll defend my BB here.

I have 9h6h on Qh8sQc. I completely missed this flop - no pair, just a backdoor flush draw with two hearts needed. The board is paired with queens. I'm out of position against the preflop aggressor. No reason to bet here with no equity. I should check and likely fold to any aggression.

I opened UTG to 300 with AcTh and Claude (BB) just called. Flop is Qh 8s Qc — I have only Ace-high and no meaningful draws, and I’m out of position. As the preflop aggressor I still have initiative and this paired, fairly dry board misses a large part of Claude’s calling range (Kx, broadways, small pairs). I want to press that advantage and fold out hands that can't continue, so I’ll make a continuation bet about half the pot. Bet size = 300 chips (~46% pot) is big enough to apply pressure but small enough to avoid committing too much with a weak hand. Plan if called: proceed cautiously on the turn (check to control pot on blanks, fold to heavy aggression unless I pair an Ace); fold to a sizeable check-raise. If I get the pot now I win 650 without risking a bigger confrontation with a likely better hand.

I have 9h6h on Qh8sQc. I completely missed - no pair, just a backdoor flush draw needing two running hearts (roughly 4% equity). Minni bet 300 into 650, I'm getting 3.2:1 but that's nowhere near enough odds for runner-runner. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Nine offsuit. This is a weak starting hand to open with from early position in a 6-handed game, as it is easily dominated by hands that other players might call or raise with. Given my chip lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold Jack-Eight offsuit in Middle Position. This is a statistically weak hand that does not warrant an open raise, especially with four players left to act behind me. To conserve chips for better spots, I will fold.

Unopened pot in the cutoff, but J4o is a very low-equity, poorly playable offsuit hand that’s frequently dominated when called/3-bet (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, all better Jx, and many 4x). With an aggressive button (Elon) and a big blind (Minni) who has shown willingness to defend/continue, the steal doesn’t realize well and we’ll be forced to fold too often versus pressure. Preserve stack and wait for a better late-position open.

Preflop on the button with AcQc, a premium suited ace hand with excellent playability and equity. Folds to me after UTG, middle, and cutoff folds, so I'm stealing the blinds in position against SB and BB. Standard play is to raise for value and to take down the pot frequently. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a good size: builds the pot when called, applies pressure, and maintains fold equity given the dead money. My stack is deep enough (70BB), and position is ideal for continuation postflop.
7-4 offsuit is a weak hand, and I'm out of position against a button raise. Poor playability and no real equity to justify calling 250. Easy fold.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with K♠9♠. This is a defendable, playable suited K with good blocker value and decent postflop equity against a likely wide button range. Stacks are deep (~83bb effective for me vs Elon’s ~67bb), so I prefer to see a flop and leverage the hand’s playability (top-pair combos, backdoor flush/straight draws) rather than make a small 3-bet that has poor fold equity or overcommit out of position. I’ll call the 200 more to 300 total to keep the pot manageable and play postflop; continue when I hit or have strong fold equity, and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards if I miss.

I’m first to act on Q♠6♠A♥ with K♠9♠. I have a very strong drawing hand: a K‑high spade draw (9 spade outs, ~35% to make the flush by the river) plus a few king outs to pair (though kings aren’t clean outs vs an A). Elon opened from the button and his range is wide, so a lot of hands miss this flop. Betting as a semi‑bluff gives me fold equity against missed hands, builds the pot when I hit, and leverages my K♠ blocker to strong spade combos. I don’t want to inflate the pot too much OOP, so I’ll make a standard ~1/2‑pot c‑bet to pressure his wide range but keep options open if he fights back. If he calls I’ll continue with my draw; if he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (call reasonable-sized raises for the odds, fold to extreme aggression).

Preflop raise with AcQc gets called by BB Minni. Flop Qs6sAh gives me top two pair (Aces and Queens) with a strong kicker, an extremely strong hand on this board. The board has a spade flush draw, so raising protects against draws like spades or straight draws while extracting value from hands like weaker Aces (AJ-AT, A5s), Queens (KQ, QJ), or bluffs. Minni's donk bet of 325 into 650 (half-pot) is often weak or blocking value, but even if she has a set or better (unlikely range given preflop flat), we have position and deep stacks. Raising builds the pot and charges draws. Sizing to 1000 total (~3x her bet, under pot raise to 1300 potential) balances value and fold equity while keeping pot manageable OOP for her.

Flop Q♠ 6♠ A♥, I have K♠9♠. I c‑bet 325 and Elon raised to 1,000 — I’m facing a 675 call. This is a strong drawing hand: K‑high spade draw (9 spade outs, ~35% to make the flush by the river) plus a few king outs. Pot odds on the call are roughly 25–26% (675/(1975+675) ≈ 25.5%), so my drawing equity exceeds the odds. Raising out of position with only a draw is too committal and will often get called or shove‑jammed; I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize my equity. Plan: call the raise to see the turn; if I hit the flush or pair I’ll extract value, if I miss and face heavy action (very large bet or shove) I’ll fold.

Turn 2♠ completed my K♠9♠ into a made king‑high spade flush. That hand beats virtually everything Elon showed on the flop (Ax, Qx, mid pairs, bluffs) — the only realistic hands that now beat me are A♠+spade two‑card combos (Ace‑high spade flushes). Elon raised the flop but then checked the turn; that line makes A♠x♠ less likely (more consistent with A‑x or broadway hands), so I should start extracting value while I’m likely ahead. I’ll bet a sizeable, but not pot‑committing, amount (~65–70% pot) to charge worse Aces, pairs and any marginal hands that called the flop while leaving room to fold to an unlikely huge shove. Bet 1,800 into 2,650 (puts meaningful pressure and builds the pot for rivers where I remain strong); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river, and if he raises very large I’ll respect that as either the rare nut flush line or a strong trap and proceed cautiously.

Facing a turn lead of 1800 into 4450 after Minni called my flop raise with top two pair on a board Qs6sAh2s. The 2s completes the spade flush draw, making the board very scary. My AcQc is top two pair, blocking AA, AQ, Qx, and Ac blocks some Ax. Minni's BB calling range vs BTN raise includes suited broadways, spades, pairs, Ax suited. Her flop donk-call suggests possible Ax (weaker), Qx, sets (QQ,66, AA unlikely), or draws. Turn lead after completing flush could be flush, but sizing is committed (her stack 5375, betting 1800 leaves ~3575). Pot odds 1800 to win 6250 (~3:1, need 25% equity) are good; I have position, deep effective stacks post-call (~39BB behind). Vs flush I'm dead, but vs Ax, Qx, two pair, sets I have equity and often ahead. Raising risks isolation into better or fold equity loss; folding top two too nitty OOP for her. Call keeps pot controlled OOP for her, disguises strength, and allows river play (value bet or check behind if scared). Long-term +EV given range equity >25% and playability.

I made the king‑high spade flush on the turn (Ks9s on Qs6sAh2s3c). Only combos that beat me are A♠+spade hands, which are relatively rare. Elon’s line — raising my flop c‑bet then checking and calling the turn — looks like he often has Ax, Qx or medium pairs rather than the nut ace‑flush. I’m ahead of most of his calling range, so I should extract value instead of checking back. I’ll bet a large value size (~65% pot) to charge worse Aces, queens and pocket pairs and to put maximum pressure on bluffs; if he jams I’ll respect that as a likely A♠X♠. Betting 4,000 maximizes expected value versus his likely holdings while still leaving some stack behind.

Facing a river bet of ~4000 (all-in call 3925 into 10250 pot, needing ~28% equity) with top two pair AcQc on Qs6sAh2s3c. Minni's BB calling range vs BTN raise includes suited aces, suited broadways, spades, pocket pairs. Her line—small flop donk-call of raise on wet AhQs6s, turn lead on 2s flush-completer, river overbet—is extremely strong and polarized toward nutted hands: spade flushes (e.g., KsJs, AsXs), trips QQ (pocket queens + Qs), or set 66 (+6s). Top two blocks some Ax/Qx but not flushes or sets. Bluffs unlikely given aggression on scary board and blank river. Equity vs this range ~15-25% (dead to flushes/trips, beat only weaker two pairs like A6/Q6s <10% of range). Pot odds good but not enough; folding preserves ~40BB stack in tournament for better spots. Opponent tendencies aggressive but this line too nut-heavy.

I am in UTG position with 4s 7c, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand. With 78 big blinds, there is no strategic incentive to enter the pot from early position with such poor holdings. Folding is the only standard play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

5c6d offsuit from MP (UTG+1) is too weak to enter as a limp or open in a 6-max lineup. It has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well multiway versus the blinds and late-position players who can isolate. With ~123bb effective, preserving chips and waiting for better opens is higher EV than attempting to steal/see a flop with this holding.

Preflop in cutoff after two early folds. 9d3d is a weak suited gapped hand, not in standard opening range from cutoff (~20-25% hands). Equity realization poor heads-up against calling ranges, and multiway speculative value minimal given stack depth and tournament stage. Folding preserves stack for premium spots.

Great button spot with A5s. Three players have folded in front of me, leaving just the blinds. This is a textbook steal situation with a suited ace - strong enough to raise for value and fold equity against the blinds.

J♣5♥ is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand. Claude opened to 250 from the button — a range that will often contain many hands that dominate my Jx and that play well postflop. Calling 200 for ~33% immediate pot odds still leaves me out of position against both the button and the big blind with very poor postflop playability and reverse implied odds. A light 3-bet from me is unnecessary with deep stacks and is likely to create tough spots; I should preserve chips for stronger hands and better positions. Fold.

I am in the big blind with 8h7h, a strong suited connector. Claude has raised to 250 from the button. The pot odds are very favorable (150 to call into a pot of 400), and this hand plays well post-flop. I will call to see the flop and see if I can connect.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h7h. The flop is 2s 3s 9s, meaning it is a monotone board of spades. I do not have a spade, a pair, or any meaningful draw. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Claude. My standard play here is to check and see what Claude does. If he bets, I will fold, as I have almost no equity on this board.

Board is all spades (2s 3s 9s) and I have the 5s giving me a flush draw. I also have a gutshot straight draw (need a 4 for the wheel) and an overcard ace. Flash checked, so I should c-bet for value/semi-bluff with my draws and fold equity. Standard ~50% pot bet.

I have 8h7h on a monotone 2s3s9s board. I do not have a spade, any pair, or any straight draw. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has made a continuation bet. Given my lack of equity and the fact that I am out of position, there is no reason to continue in this hand. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

6-handed UTG plays like HJ; KJs suited is comfortably within an opening range with strong playability and decent blockers (K/J reduce combos of KK/KJ/JJ). With no antes and relatively deep stacks, I want to take initiative and build pots where I can realize equity in position postflop against the field. Use a standard, non-committal open size that still pressures blinds and keeps my risk controlled versus occasional 3-bets.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo, who has shown a willingness to open from early position in prior hands but typically folds to 3-bets. My hand 7s2s is a weak suited connector with very low equity against a strong UTG range (top ~15-20% like pairs, broadway, suited aces/connectors). In middle position with 39BB stack, calling would be speculative at best, risking chips multiway without position advantage or strong post-flop playability. Raising is a blatant bluff with this trash hand. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in this 6-handed tournament.
Holding T2o in the cutoff facing a UTG raise. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly even in position. T2 offsuit has poor playability and won't dominate anything. Easy fold.

Call 250. On the Button with Qd9h facing an UTG open to 250, calling is the +EV line: I have position and a hand that can realize equity and play well postflop. A 3-bet with Q9o vs an UTG opener would be thin (a min-raise to 400 won't generate reliable fold equity and just bloats the pot), so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use position to apply pressure or fold to big resistance. Pot odds: calling 250 into a 400 pot gives roughly 2.6:1 (need ≈27% equity), which this hand can achieve against a reasonably wide open range. Plan: see the flop, continue aggressively if I hit (top pair/draws) and fold to large aggression when missed—preserve stack and extract value from position.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ace-Trey suited (Ad 3d). UTG (FiveTwo) raised to 250 and the Button (Minni) called. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to win a pot that will be 850 if I call, roughly 4.25:1) and have a hand that plays well multi-way due to its flush potential and nut-flush draw possibility. While Ace-Trey is not exceptionally strong against an UTG range, the price is too good to fold, and raising might bloat the pot out of position against two opponents. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o facing a raise to 250 from UTG and two callers (Button and Small Blind). The pot is currently 850, and I need to call 150 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of approximately 5.6 to 1 (requiring ~15% equity). Although J9o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position in a 4-way pot, the price is excellent to close the action. My hand has some straight potential and can flop well occasionally. I will call the small amount to realize my equity.

I am first to act on a flop of Qc-4h-2c in a four-way pot. I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) and a backdoor diamond flush draw. Given the multi-way nature of the pot and my position, checking is the most prudent play. I want to see how the UTG raiser and the other players react to this board before committing any more chips. Ace-high has some showdown value but is likely behind a Queen or a pocket pair here.

I am in the Big Blind in a 4-way pot with J9o on a Qc 4h 2c board. I have missed the flop completely, holding only Jack-high with a weak backdoor flush draw (one club in hand). The pre-flop raiser (UTG) is behind me, as is the Button. With no made hand and very little equity, leading out into three opponents is not a viable strategy. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If facing a bet, I will likely fold.

Multiway (4 players) on a very dry Q-high board, my KJs has little immediate equity and doesn’t benefit much from building a pot—most continues (any Qx, 55–JJ, some 4x/2x, club backdoors) will call at least once, and I’m not blocking top pair. With no strong draw, the best line is to keep my range protected by checking, realize equity, and look to improve (K/J) or pick better turn cards to apply pressure later.

Flop: Qc‑4h‑2c. I have Qd9h — top pair with a decent kicker on a fairly dry board. Action checked to me in a multiway pot (UTG opener + SB + BB). This is a good spot to bet for value and protection: opponents' ranges include worse queens, medium pairs and broadways, and there are some potential club-based draws. A ~1/3 pot stab (350 into 1000) extracts value from worse Qx/weaker pairs, charges any draws, and keeps the pot controllable if I face resistance. If I get a big raise from the preflop opener or BB I'll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy aggression); if called I'll proceed cautiously on the turn and look to extract more value or protect as needed.

I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) and a backdoor nut flush draw. Minni's bet of 350 into a 1000-chip pot is quite small (about 1/3 pot), giving me pot odds of approximately 3.8:1. Considering I have an overcard and a draw to the wheel, and the original pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) checked, I have sufficient equity to justify a call to see the turn. Calling is the most appropriate action here as it allows me to realize my equity without overcommitting chips out of position.

I hold J9o with the 9c on a Qc 4h 2c board. I have no made hand (Jack high) and no immediate draws. I only have a weak backdoor flush draw (running clubs) and very distant backdoor straight possibilities. The Button bet 350 into three players, signaling strength (likely a Queen or strong draw), and the Small Blind called, further cluttering the pot. Pot odds are roughly 17%, but my equity is likely far lower (probably <10%) against two active opponents who have already shown interest. Being out of position with a weak hand and almost no equity makes this a straightforward fold.

Minni’s 350 stab into a 4-way checked flop is small and can be a wide protection/probe range (some Qx, medium pairs, occasional club holdings, and some air). Flash’s call tightens things, but I’m getting a very good price (350 to win 1700 now; ~17% required), and KsJs still has live overcard equity plus a few backdoor runouts (K/J turns for pair+showdown, spade turns for BDFD, T/A turns to create credible barrels). Check-raising here is a poor bluff (no Q blocker, multiway, and likely to get called), so the best line is to peel once and proceed cautiously—continue on good turns, otherwise fold to significant further pressure.

I am first to act on the turn after the 8h has been dealt. The board is now Qc-4h-2c-8h. I currently hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). In a three-way pot against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and the flop bettor (Minni), checking is the most disciplined play. Leading here would likely not fold out a Queen or a better pair, and I am out of position. By checking, I can see how my opponents react to the turn and potentially see a free river card if it checks around, or evaluate the price if someone bets.

We have KsJs on Qc-4h-2c-8h after calling a small flop stab multiway. The turn doesn’t give us a real draw (no flush draw possible with one card to come, no straight equity), and we only have thin improvement (K or J) that often still loses versus any Qx/8x/pairs that continue. Leading here would be a pure bluff into two players who have already shown interest (Minni bet flop, Flash called), and we don’t have great blockers to force folds. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and reassess based on Minni’s action and Flash’s response (potentially realizing a free river or folding versus meaningful pressure).

I have Qd9h on Qc‑4h‑2c‑8h — top pair with a decent kicker. I barreled the flop (350) and got two callers (SB and the UTG opener), and both checked to me on the turn. The board is relatively dry and their ranges still include worse Qx, small pairs (44/22) and floats; there are very few realistic draw completions right now. I want to extract value from worse queens/medium pairs and deny cheap river cards to speculative hands, but I also don’t want to bloat the pot out of position against a likely stronger raising range. A bet of 900 (~40–45% of the 2050 pot) balances those goals: it’s large enough to get value from worse hands and charge floats, but small enough to keep weaker hands calling. If I face a significant raise I’ll re‑evaluate and lean to folding (raises will often signify sets or two‑pair here); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the river (pot‑control or a small value bet depending on the river card).

On the turn (8h), the board is Qc-4h-2c-8h. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Minni has bet 900 into a pot of 2050. My pot odds are roughly 3.3:1, requiring about 23% equity to call profitably. With only 4 outs to the straight (approximately 8.7% equity), and with Ace-high being unlikely to win at showdown in a three-way pot, the mathematical requirement isn't met. Furthermore, FiveTwo is still to act behind me, creating a risk of a check-raise. Given the board texture and the action, I do not have sufficient equity to continue.

Minni’s turn barrel (900 into ~2050 before betting) after stabbing the flop and now getting heads-up is a fairly strong line on Qc-4h-2c-8h. With KsJs we have no real draw and only thin improvement (K is a strong out; J is often not). Calling 900 to win 3850 needs ~23% equity, which we don’t have versus a range that contains lots of Qx/8x/sets and some medium pairs. Turning this into a bluff-raise is also unattractive: we don’t block top pair, and Minni has shown willingness to continue with aggression. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.

76o is a speculative hand with poor playability from UTG, the toughest position in a 6-handed game. It has low equity against calling ranges, struggles post-flop without position, and risks getting dominated by better connectors or suited hands. With a 39BB stack, preservation is key early in the tournament; folding avoids difficult spots and waits for premium hands or better positions to build the stack. Opponent stacks are deep, but no history suggests limping is profitable here.

3h9d is a weak hand from middle position. No connectivity, no suited, wide gap. This is a clear fold - not worth investing chips with such marginal holdings when there are still 4 players to act behind me.

4♦7♣ is a very weak, unplayable hand from the cutoff facing three players left to act (button + SB + BB). The pot is unopened (to-call 100) and any raise will likely be met by one of several callers or a 3-bet — I have poor postflop playability and little fold equity. With deep but not enormous stacks and 120 hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct. Fold and avoid getting involved with a marginal hand out of position.

I am on the button with A3 suited, which is a strong starting hand for this position. Since all players before me have folded, I will open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot with position.

I am in the Small Blind holding K2o facing a standard 2.5x open raise from the Button (Flash). K2o is a very weak hand, easily dominated by the opener's range (better Kings, Aces, pocket pairs). Additionally, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds do not justify calling with a hand that has such poor playability and low equity realization. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Button opens to 2.5x and we’re getting a great price in the BB (150 to win a pot that will be 550 after we call). Q9o is comfortably within a standard BB defend range versus a Button open, has decent high-card playability, and doesn’t perform as well as a 3-bet bluff from out of position against a capable opponent. Calling keeps Flash’s range wide and avoids bloating the pot with a dominated-but-playable hand.

We flop top pair (9) with a solid kicker on a paired texture (8-8-9). This board is fairly static and interacts well with the BB defend range (we have lots of 8x/9x), so leading doesn’t accomplish much: worse hands often fold and better hands (8x, 99) continue. Checking keeps Flash’s range wide, induces c-bets from overcards and bluffs, and lets us realize value via check-call lines while controlling pot size out of position. If checked back, we can start value/protection betting many turns.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a paired board of 8h-8c-9s. While this board can occasionally hit a Big Blind's defending range with 8x or 9x, I still have range advantage as the button raiser. FiveTwo has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 45% of the pot to put pressure on his missed hands and likely take the pot down immediately. My Ace-high still has some showdown value if called, but a bet is the standard play here.

Flash’s small c-bet (250 into 800) is very likely range-wide on this paired texture, and Q9 has clear value as top pair with a good kicker. Raising here tends to fold out the hands we want to keep (overcards, backdoors, worse 9x) while getting continued by the portions that dominate us (8x, 99) and occasionally stubborn overpairs. By check-calling we keep his bluffs in, control pot size out of position, and can reevaluate turns (often continuing vs most reasonable barrels, and value-leading if he checks back).

We’ve improved to a strong bluff-catcher/value hand: 9s on a paired 8-8 board gives us two pair (9s and 8s) with a Q kicker. We’re only really in bad shape versus 8x (trips) and 99 (full house), while Flash can still have plenty of overcards, heart draws, straight draws (T6/65), and worse made hands (9x, 7x) that may continue betting if we keep the initiative with him.

Leading the turn often folds out the exact hands we want to keep (A/K/Q-high floats and some backdoors) and risks getting raised primarily by the part of his range that has us crushed (8x), bloating the pot out of position. Checking keeps his range wide, induces barrels from bluffs/draws, and lets us comfortably check-call most reasonable sizes, then value-bet many rivers if he checks back.

The turn 7h is a card that connects well with a Big Blind defending range, potentially completing straights (65, T6) or providing more equity to draws. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against his missed hands or smaller draws, but it is unlikely to fold out a 9 or better by betting again. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot and see a free river.

River brings the Tc, which is a slightly awkward card: it improves a meaningful chunk of Flash’s turn-check range (any Tx now has two pair T+8 and beats our 9+8), while many of the hands we beat (A/K/Q-high floats, small pairs) will often just fold to a bet. If we lead for “thin value,” we risk value-owning ourselves versus Tx/slowplayed 8x/99, and we don’t get enough calls from worse often enough to justify it.

Checking keeps the pot controlled out of position, preserves his bluffing range (missed hearts/overcards), and lets us make a more informed decision based on his sizing. Plan: mostly bluff-catch versus small/medium bets, and be cautious versus large polar bets (where Tx+/8x/straights are heavily represented).

The board is 8h-8c-9s-7h-Tc, which is highly connected and favors the Big Blind's defending range. I checked the turn, which likely capped my range and signaled that I don't have a monster like an 8 or a high straight. Since FiveTwo called my flop continuation bet, they likely have at least a piece of the board (a 9, a 7, or a draw that has now hit like a 10 or a straight). My Ace-high has almost zero showdown value, but a bluff on this river is unlikely to succeed given how many mediocre pairs in FiveTwo's range will feel compelled to call a single bet on this board. I will check behind and concede the pot, preserving my stack for better opportunities.
Pocket 9s from UTG is a solid opening hand. With 84 BBs effective, I should open raise to build the pot and take initiative. Standard sizing of 2.5x works well here.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — that represents a fairly strong, tight range. Q♢4♣ is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability and no real 3‑bet value from MP. There are multiple players left to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB) so this will often go multiway where Q4o performs poorly. Calling 250 out of position is a marginal price (you need a large equity edge vs an UTG range) and 3‑betting is too thin. With a deep but not exploitable stack there’s no reason to gamble — fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the cutoff with 2c 6d, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude raised to 250 from UTG, showing significant strength. There is no reason to play this hand out of position against an early position raiser. I will fold.

I hold Q6 offsuit on the Button. The player in UTG (Claude) has opened with a raise to 250. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, often dominating a hand like Q6o. Despite having position, Q6o plays poorly post-flop and lacks the equity to call or the blockers to 3-bet bluff profitably against an early position opener. The standard play is to fold.

Claude opens UTG to 2.5bb, which is typically a strong/tighter range in 6-max. From the small blind we’d be calling out of position the rest of the hand, and J3o has very poor playability and dominated top-pair issues versus an UTG range. A 3-bet bluff is too loose here given position disadvantage and a short-stacked BB who can complicate the hand. Best EV is to release and preserve stack.

4h6c is a trash hand offsuit with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Facing an UTG raise to 250 from Claude after all other players folded, this isolates me against a strong range. Pot odds are 400:150 (getting ~2.67:1), but equity vs. typical UTG range (top 15-20% hands) is too low (~25% at best, but likely less with position OOP). Post-flop OOP with this hand is a disaster, low playability for implied odds given stacks. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament play. Fold.

6-max UTG with JcTd (JTo) is borderline but perfectly playable for an open — it's a decent broadway/connector that plays well postflop and can steal the blinds. Table has been reasonably passive with many folds to opens, and my stack is deep (~130bb), so I want to take the initiative and avoid limping. I’ll open to a standard 3bb (300) to build fold equity and isolate weaker hands. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet; call small/suspected isolating 3‑bets only vs specific players, otherwise continue vs a single caller with standard c‑bet frequency on dry boards and proceed straightforwardly with top pairs and strong draws.

I am holding King-Seven offsuit in middle position. Minni, who is UTG, has raised to 300. UTG ranges are typically quite strong, and K7o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by better Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) and other high cards (Ace-high hands). Furthermore, there are still four players left to act behind me, increasing the risk of being squeezed or facing further aggression. Calling or raising here is negative EV. Folding is the most prudent play to protect my stack.

I am in the Cutoff facing a raise to 300 from Minni who is Under-The-Gun (UTG). An UTG opening range is typically strong, often consisting of pairs, strong aces, and broadways. My hand, 8c Ts, is an offsuit gapper with very low equity against a strong opening range. It is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, even in position. There are players left to act behind me (Button, SB, BB) who could squeeze or call. The correct and disciplined play with this weak holding is to fold.

Minni opened UTG to 3bb in a 6-max configuration; her range is still relatively strong, and QJo is prone to domination if we build a big pot preflop. On the Button we realize equity well in position, keep in weaker parts of her range, and avoid getting 4-bet off our hand. Calling also reduces variance versus a short-stacked SB squeeze compared to 3-betting with a marginal dominated hand.

Preflop in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni (12725 stack, shows aggression in past hands) and a button call from FiveTwo (11950 stack). My hand is Kd4d, a weak offsuit king with a terrible kicker, unlikely to play well postflop multiway. Pot is 750 but calling 250 commits poorly suited chips early with 37BB stack in a long tournament (32/150 hands). Folding preserves stack for better spots; no fold equity worth pursuing with this trash hand.
32 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even getting 3.75:1 pot odds, this hand has terrible equity against a UTG raise and a call. Folding is clearly correct here.

I opened UTG and was called by the Button; on T♣ 3♥ 8♠ holding J♣ T♦ I have top pair with the best kicker. This is a dry, rainbow flop with very little obvious equity for draws, and the Button’s calling range is wide (medium pairs, weaker tens, broadways). Leading for value is correct: I want to extract from worse tens/small pairs and deny showdown equity. With stacks deep but out of position, a 1/3–1/2 pot c-bet keeps the pot manageable while still getting value — pot is 750 so I’ll bet 350 (~45% pot). If called I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue on safe blanks; if I face a large raise I’ll fold or proceed very cautiously given I’m OOP.

Minni’s small 1/3-pot c-bet on Tc-3h-8s is consistent with a range-bet from an UTG opener. With QJ we have two overcards (6 outs to top pair) plus some backdoor straight potential, and we’re in position getting a good price (350 to win 1450). Raising as a bluff here performs poorly versus her stronger continuing range (Tx, overpairs, strong 8x), and we don’t have a robust draw to back it up. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and sets up turn opportunities to take it away when she slows down or favorable cards arrive (Q/J/K/A/9).

I have top pair (T♦ J♣) with a good kicker on a fairly dry board: T♣ 3♥ 8♠ 7♥. I led the flop and was called, so the Button’s range is wide (weaker tens, medium pocket pairs, some broadways). The 7♥ on the turn is a harmless card for most of that range but it does add a second heart to the board — meaning some one-heart hands now have a heart draw. I want to extract value from worse tens and medium pairs and charge any backdoor heart/straight equity while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A standard half‑pot value bet accomplishes that and keeps options if I face aggression. I will bet ~1/2 pot (725 into 1450). If I get a large raise I will proceed cautiously and likely fold to very large raises; if called I’ll re-evaluate the river and continue to thin-value bet on safe runouts or check to control vs dangerous rivers.

We’re heads-up in position getting 725 to win 2900 (~25% required). With QJ on Tc-3h-8s-7h we have 2 overcards plus a gutshot to the 9 (10-ish outs, though Q/J can be slightly tainted versus AQ/KQ/QT/JT), and our position lets us realize equity and potentially win on rivers when Minni slows down. Raising here has poor fold equity versus an UTG double-barrel range (Tx/overpairs/sets/strong draws) and inflates the pot with a medium-equity hand. Call and reassess river (value on a 9; otherwise decide based on sizing/line).

I have top pair (T♦) with a good but not invulnerable kicker (J♣) on a fairly dry river (T♣ 3♥ 8♠ 7♥ 5♦). I led flop and turn and was called both times, so the Button’s continuing range is wide (weaker tens, medium pocket pairs, some broadways). The 5♦ is a blank that doesn’t complete obvious straights; there’s still value to be taken from worse Tx and medium one‑pair hands that are likely to call. I want a thin value bet that extracts from those hands without bloating the pot OOP, so I’ll bet half‑pot (1,450 into 2,900). If I face a large, polarizing raise I will fold; small raises will be re‑evaluated but I won’t commit lightly on this board.

River is a complete brick for our turn equity: QJ has no pair and no made straight/flush on Tc-3h-8s-7h-5d. Minni’s sizing is a consistent ~1/2-pot triple barrel from an UTG range that is heavily weighted to Tx/overpairs/sets, and our hand blocks some of her natural bluff candidates (QJ/J9-type floats). We’d need her to be bluffing >25% to justify a call, which is unlikely here, and raising isn’t credible (we don’t represent many nutted hands on this runout and get called too often). Fold and preserve stack.

I am UTG with 4s 6s. In a 6-max game, this is a clear fold from early position. While I am the chip leader, playing low suited connectors out of position against aggressive opponents like Minni and Elon is not a winning strategy. I will wait for a better spot.

I am in early/middle position with King-Five offsuit (K5o), which is a very weak hand. With 75 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better opportunities. Opening here is risky as I am likely dominated by any caller and vulnerable to 3-bets from the aggressive big stacks behind me or a shove from the short stack on the button. Folding is the standard and correct play.

We’re in the cutoff with 8♠2♠, a very low-equity hand that performs poorly both vs a limp/call range and vs aggressive blinds that can punish opens (Minni has shown willingness to apply pressure; Elon on the button is short enough to reshove). With only marginal fold equity and bad playability when called, the EV of entering is low and the downside variance is unnecessary with our healthy stack. Best is to conserve chips and wait for better steal candidates.

Folded to me on the button with A5s, a strong suited ace hand with great position against the blinds. A5s has good playability, blocker to AA, and solid equity against typical blind calling ranges. Raising is standard here to steal the dead money (150 in pot) or build pot with position. Blinds have deep stacks, but my 37BB stack supports a standard raise size of 3x BB (300 total). Limping is weak OOP for them; raising applies pressure and defines their range.

Q8o in the small blind facing a button raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Elon has been active, but calling OOP with Q8o invites trouble - we'll often face difficult decisions postflop, and Minni could squeeze from the BB with their big stack. Folding is cleanest here.

Fold. 2♥4♥ is too weak to defend a standard button open to 300. The pot is 450 and calling another 200 (total 300) requires roughly 31% equity — against a BTN opening range my hand is frequently dominated and has very poor post‑flop playability (small pairs and low hearts are often reverse‑dominated by bigger hearts/broadways). Min‑raising to 500 would be spewy and opens me to a stronger 3‑bet or difficult multi‑street decisions. Preserve the healthy stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

I am in UTG position with Ac 9d (A9 offsuit). In a 6-handed game, A9o is generally considered too weak to open from the earliest position (UTG). It is easily dominated by hands like AT, AJ, AQ, and AK, which opponents in later positions or the blinds are likely to hold if they continue. Furthermore, playing A9o out of position post-flop is difficult and often leads to reverse implied odds situations. With a stack of 75 BBs, I want to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold.

ATo is a solid middle-position open in 6-max: it has good top-pair potential, blocks strong Ax, and benefits from taking initiative versus the blinds. With no raise in front and stacks reasonably deep (aside from Elon), I want to raise rather than limp to build fold equity and avoid multiway spots. Standard sizing in this game has been ~2.5x, so I’ll open to 250.

A6s is a strong suited ace hand with good playability from the cutoff position facing a single raise from middle position. FiveTwo has shown aggression in previous hands, often raising wide and continuing post-flop, but my hand has nut flush potential, straight draws, and ace blocker. Pot odds are favorable (250 to call into 650 effective pot), and I have position on the raiser with deep stacks allowing implied odds. Calling keeps the pot multi-way potentially if blinds defend, and I can outplay post-flop. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks too much without a premium. Standard call here to realize equity.

QJo on the button is a solid hand. With a min-raise from FiveTwo and a call from Elon, I have good implied odds and position. Calling to see a flop in position is the standard play here. Could squeeze, but flatting keeps the pot manageable and maintains my positional advantage.

6♠? Actually 6♥2♠ — a very weak, unsuited hand. Facing a min-raise to 250 with two callers already (pot 900) and I’m out of position in the small blind. To call I’d need to put in 200 more to play a multiway pot with almost no equity and poor playability postflop. No reason to defend or attempt a light 3-bet here given table dynamics and deep stacks. Folding preserves chips and avoids a negative-EV spot. I fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 7s. FiveTwo raised to 250, and both Elon and Claude called. I have already committed 100 chips for my big blind, so it costs me 150 more to call. The pot currently stands at 900 chips. With 150 to call, I am getting 6:1 pot odds (or needing about 14% equity to break even). While 76o is a weak hand and I will be out of position, the price is extremely favorable to see a flop in a multi-way pot where I can potentially flop a strong disguised hand like a straight or two pair. Given the deep stacks and the small additional investment, calling is the mathematically sound play here.

I have flopped the current nuts with a straight (4-5-6-7-8). In a four-way pot, while there is a heart draw on the board, checking as the first player to act is a strong strategic move to allow the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) or other active players like Elon to bet. This sets up a potential check-raise to build a significant pot and gain maximum value from sets, two-pair hands, or overpairs.

Four-way on a low, connected flop (8-5-4 with two hearts) heavily favors the callers’ ranges (pairs, two-pair, straights/straight draws). With ATo we currently have just two overcards and only runner-runner backdoors (backdoor nut hearts, very thin wheel possibilities), so a c-bet is unlikely to generate enough folds and risks getting called (or check-raised) while we’re out of position to two players. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets us realize equity, potentially improving on A/T turns and re-evaluating versus any turn action.

Flop board 8h5c4h is coordinated with straight draw possibilities (open-ender on 6-7, gutshots) and two hearts, favoring my As6s which holds a gutshot straight draw to the 7 (4 outs), ace high overcard blocking top pair, and backdoor spade flush draw. Pre-flop pot was 1050 (5-way adjusted for calls), checked by BB Flash and original raiser FiveTwo, suggesting weakness or trapping, now acting first with position on them and only button Claude behind who defended wide. Equity realization is strong with implied odds (deep stacks: mine 3675, others 8k+), fold equity against weak ranges (FiveTwo often agg but checked here), and protection needed vs draws. Semi-bluff bet sizing to ~60% pot (650) builds pot for when I hit, denies equity to floats/draws, and pot controls if called. Checking risks giving free cards to oesd (67) or check-raise from Claude; betting exploits checked range and maintains aggression to maximize stack long-term in tournament.

I have QhJd with a board of 8h5c4h. I completely missed this low, connected board. I only have two overcards and a backdoor heart flush draw (Qh). Elon bet 650 into 1050, and there are still two players to act behind me (Flash and FiveTwo). This board connects well with preflop calling ranges (suited connectors, small pairs). Chasing just overcards for 650 with players behind isn't profitable. Easy fold.
