I have flopped the nuts with a straight (4-5-6-7-8). The board is somewhat coordinated with a heart draw and various two-pair or set possibilities. Elon, who has a relatively short stack remaining (3,025 chips), has led out with a bet of 650. This is an ideal situation to raise for value. By raising now, I can extract chips from Elon's likely overpairs, sets, or high-equity draws while the board is still favorable. A raise to 1,650 puts significant pressure on Elon and forces FiveTwo to either commit or fold their equity. Given Elon's stack size, this raise effectively prices him in to commit the rest of his chips on later streets or even shove now.

We have AhTs on 8h-5c-4h facing a check-raise after Elon’s stab. This is a great spot to continue because we hold the nut flush draw (9 clean heart outs) and occasionally can improve further versus some parts of Flash’s range (e.g., overpairs/8x where A/T can become relevant). Even against very strong value (sets/76), we still have enough equity with two cards to come, and the price is good: calling 1650 to win a pot that will be ~5000+ gives us well under the ~36% we have just from the nut flush draw alone. Raising/shoving would bloat the pot versus a range that’s often strong on this texture and could force us into a big all-in with only 9 outs when dominated by made hands; calling also keeps in Elon (short) so we can potentially realize equity and make good decisions if he jams behind.

Plan: call, then on hearts we can play aggressively; on blanks we proceed carefully versus continued heavy pressure.

Facing a check-raise from Flash (BB) after checking flop and a flat call from FiveTwo (preflop raiser) on a highly coordinated 8h5c4h board (flush draw, OESD with 67, gutshots). My As6s has a gutshot to the 7 (4 outs ~8% equity), ace-high blocker, and backdoor flush draw, but villain ranges are strong: Flash's check-raise suggests value (sets 44/55/88, two-pair 45/84/85, combo draws 67h/76h/37h) or monsters; FiveTwo's call caps medium strength but still ahead (pairs, draws). Pot odds ~5:1 require ~17% equity, but realized equity lower multiway (~10-15% max vs polarized range). Stack 3025 committing 1000 (33%) with poor implied odds post-turn. Raising has minimal fold equity after call, commits too much. Calling floats too deep into tough river spots. Tournament stage (hand 34/150), fold preserves stack for premium hands/edges; history shows FiveTwo/Flash aggressive but this line strong. Standard fold.

The turn brings the 5d, which pairs the board. While I currently hold a straight (4-5-6-7-8), the board pairing is a dangerous development. FiveTwo called my check-raise on the flop, suggesting a strong range that could include sets (88, 55, 44) or a straight (67). With the 5 appearing, hands like 88, 44, 85, and 45 have now improved to full houses, and 55 has become quads. By checking, I am exercising pot control and protecting my stack against these superior hands. This also allows me to see how FiveTwo responds; if he checks behind, I can feel more confident in my straight, and if he bets, I can evaluate based on his sizing whether he is value betting a boat or potentially overvaluing trips or a heart draw.

Flash’s flop check-raise creates a range that’s either strong made hands (8x/sets/two-pair) or a lot of semi-bluffs (straight/flush draws). When he checks this paired turn (8h-5c-4h-5d), it’s a great opportunity to apply pressure in position: many of his semi-bluffs will give up, and a chunk of his medium one-pair hands won’t love facing another barrel on a board where I can credibly have 5x after calling the flop raise.

With AhTs I have the nut flush draw (cleanest draw possible) so betting is strong: I can win immediately with fold equity, and when called I still realize solid equity and build a pot for value on heart rivers. I’ll use a controlled sizing so I can comfortably fold to a big check-jam (my equity versus a shove range with boats/trips is not enough).

Plan: fold to large raises; if called, shove/value-bet most heart rivers and be cautious on blanks depending on river action.

I currently hold a straight (4-5-6-7-8), which was the nuts on the flop. However, the 5d on the turn pairs the board, meaning hands like 88, 44, 85, or 54 have now improved to a full house, and 55 is quads. FiveTwo called my significant check-raise on the flop and is now leading out for 2,000 into a pot of roughly 5,000. This strength indicates he likely has at least a 5, another straight (67), or a full house. While I am vulnerable to a boat, the pot odds (getting 3.5:1) are too high to fold a straight at this stage. Raising is risky because I would likely only be called by hands that beat me or another 67. Calling allows me to see the river and evaluate FiveTwo's final action while keeping the pot size somewhat controlled.

On the river, the board has now paired both the eights and the fives (8-5-4-5-8). While I flopped a straight (4-5-6-7-8), this hand is now extremely vulnerable. Any player holding an 8, a 5, or even 44 has a full house that beats me. FiveTwo's call of my flop check-raise and his lead on the turn suggest extreme strength, likely trips or a set at the time, which have now improved to full houses. Since I am first to act, checking is the only logical move. I want to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible. Betting would not fold out any hand that beats me and is unlikely to be called by any hand I beat.

River comes 8h-5c-4h-5d-8s and we missed the nut flush; we have only A-high. After Flash check-raised the flop and then check-called our turn bet on the paired board, his range is heavily weighted toward hands with showdown value (8x/5x/full houses, some straights like 76, and various one-pair bluffcatchers). Those hands rarely fold to any reasonable river bluff, and the hands that do fold (missed hearts) we already beat at showdown—plus a shove risks running into a trap with a boat. Best play is to take the free showdown and preserve stack.

Ad5o is a marginal UTG (6-max) open and plays poorly multiway/out of position, especially with several aggressive stacks behind who can 3-bet and put us in dominated, low-realization spots. Limping is too weak and invites isolation. Best EV here is to pass and preserve stack for stronger opens.

Th7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and equity. In middle position pre-flop with no action except UTG fold, folding avoids speculative plays with 30BB stack. Preserve chips for better spots in this multi-table dynamic.
J5o is a weak hand. Even in cutoff position with two folds ahead, this hand doesn't have enough value to open-raise. Folding is the disciplined play here.

On the button with Js7s this is a clear spot to open — good position, suited jack has play postflop and decent fold equity. Pot is 150 and a call (100) just gives the blinds a cheap look; I want to isolate and steal the blinds or play last postflop. I'll raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total) — large enough to discourage easy calls but small relative to our deep stack. If Flash (SB) or Pro (BB) 3-bet large, I will fold Js7s; but in position against calls I can play aggressively postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 2c, a very weak suited hand. Minni opened for 300 from the Button. While I have the largest stack at the table and the pot odds are roughly 2.2-to-1 to call, defending such a weak hand out of position against an active and aggressive player like Minni is generally a losing proposition. The Big Blind (Pro) still has to act and could potentially squeeze. Given the low equity and poor playability of 6-2 suited, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni). The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450, requiring roughly 30.7% equity realization. While Q7o has decent raw equity against a wide button opening range, it plays poorly out of position due to reverse implied odds (weak kicker issues) and lack of connectivity. Minni is the chip leader and has been active, but calling a 3x raise out of position with this specific hand is generally -EV compared to folding. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket Kings is one of the strongest starting hands, second only to AA. As UTG with a 30BB stack in a 6-handed game, I need to raise for value to build the pot while thinning the field against speculative hands from later positions. Opponents have been aggressive with frequent pre-flop raises around 250-300, and many deep stacks, but KK plays well post-flop. Standard UTG open-raise size is 3x the BB (300), which is appropriate here to encourage calls from worse hands like AQ+, lower pairs while deterring too many multi-way pots. Min raise is 200, but larger size shows strength and gets value.

Q4o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG open. Elon has a short stack (~27bb) so his opening range is likely reasonable. This hand doesn't play well OOP against an opening range, and I have no compelling reason to call or raise. Easy fold.

Elon opened UTG to 300. In the cutoff with 8♣5♠ this is a clear fold — a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and lots of domination risk versus a UTG opening range. There are three players left to act (button, SB, BB) who can call or squeeze, and I would be out of position postflop. The pot is 450 and calling 300 requires ~40% equity to break even; 8-5o doesn’t have that vs a typical UTG range and 3-betting light isn’t sensible here. Preserve the healthy stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9-5 offsuit. Elon has opened from UTG for 3x. 9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that does not play well against an UTG range, even with the benefit of position. Given the stack sizes and the strength indicated by an early position raise, there is no reason to continue with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 8h (suited connectors). The player Under the Gun (Elon) raised to 300. Elon has a relatively short stack (roughly 27 BBs). While 78s has potential, playing it from the Small Blind against an UTG opening range is generally a losing strategy due to being out of position and the likelihood of the UTG range dominating the board or having higher pairs. The stack depth (SPR ~3.5 post-flop) is not deep enough to justify calling for implied odds to hit a straight or flush, as I won't win enough chips relative to the cost to call and the frequency of missing. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

Elon opens UTG to 3x with a ~27bb stack. With 9♢3♠ offsuit we’re dominated by virtually all of his value range and have poor playability out of position. Although the pot odds (200 to win 650) look tempting, realizing that equity from the big blind versus an UTG range is difficult, and this hand can’t profitably 3-bet bluff (no blockers; risk getting jammed on). Best decision is to release and preserve stack.

8s9s is a suited connector with decent playability. While UTG is a tough position for this hand, it has good implied odds if I can see a flop and connect. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to potentially take it down or see a flop in position against the blinds.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I have K♦3♦ in middle position with a 14,550 (≈145bb) stack. An UTG open represents a rather tight, value-heavy range and K3s is frequently dominated (most Kx, broadways, pocket pairs) and has a weak kicker. Calling 250 into a 400 pot often commits me to a multiway or OOP spot against deeper stacks (Flash/Pro) where this hand plays poorly. 3-betting as a bluff to 400 is suboptimal — K3s doesn’t have the postflop playability to make that profitable against callers or a reraise. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better position/hand, so fold.

Claude has raised to 2.5x the big blind from Under-The-Gun (UTG), which usually indicates a strong range. Holding Queen-Four offsuit in the Cutoff, I am facing a raise from a player in the earliest position. This hand is significantly below the threshold for a call or a 3-bet, especially with three active players behind me who could squeeze. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the Button with JTo facing a 2.5x raise from UTG (Claude). While I have the advantage of position, JTo is easily dominated by a standard UTG opening range (typically consisting of strong Aces, KJs+, QJs, and pairs). Cold calling here leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the blinds and reverse implied odds post-flop if I hit a pair. Against an UTG range, my equity is likely under 40%. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.

AThh is strong enough to continue versus an UTG open in 6-max, but playing a multi-street pot out of position by flatting invites tough spots and lets BB realize equity cheaply. Claude has been opening frequently and should have some folds to a 3-bet. I’ll 3-bet for value/initiative and to deny BB’s equity. If BB (shorter stack) jams, we’re generally priced in with AThh; if Claude 4-bets large, we can comfortably fold.

Tc6s is a weak speculative hand with minimal playability, especially out of position in the big blind facing an UTG open-raise from Claude followed by a strong 3-bet from FiveTwo in the small blind. The pot is 1250 and calling 800 commits over 25% of my 3075 stack into a pot where I'll be OOP multiway if Claude continues. Equity against likely strong ranges is insufficient, and postflop prospects are poor with no domination potential or strong draws likely. Folding preserves stack for better spots in the tournament.

Facing a 3-bet from SB after my UTG open. 8s9s is a speculative hand that doesn't play well against 3-bet ranges out of position. Getting about 2:1 pot odds (650 to win 1250), but suited connectors perform poorly vs tight 3-bet ranges. FiveTwo's SB 3-bet vs UTG raise indicates strength. Better to fold and preserve chips.

J♠3♦ UTG in a 6-handed game is a very weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and lots of dominated combos. I'm out of position against five players to act (including deep stacks and one short stack who could act unpredictably). Standard UTG open ranges are tight in 6-max; this hand is well outside that. Calling 100 to see a multi-way flop OOP or opening to 200 would be unnecessarily speculative and negative EV. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I have King-Four of hearts in middle position. While suited kings have some value, the 4 kicker makes this hand too weak to open from this position in a 6-max game. With aggressive players like FiveTwo and Claude still to act behind me, folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my chip lead.

I hold 8d 2c in the Cutoff, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker (8-high offsuit). Although I am in late position, the hand has virtually no playability or equity. With 73.5 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves or loose steals with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

On the button 6-handed, Ks5s is a standard open: decent high-card value, suited playability, and a K blocker that reduces strong 3-bet combos. With both blinds still to act (one shorter in SB), I prefer raising rather than limping to leverage position and fold equity, while keeping the sizing reasonable so we can comfortably fold to a 3-bet/shove if needed. 2.5bb should pressure the blinds without overcommitting.

AQo is a premium hand with excellent equity against a button opening range, which is typically wide (25-40% of hands). As SB, defending is mandatory; folding is too tight. Calling is passive and leaves me OOP postflop with a hand that benefits from initiative. 3-betting isolates the button, generates fold equity, and sets up postflop advantage. Sizing to 700 (2.8x the open raise) balances fold equity and pot commitment with my ~30BB stack (3025), avoiding min-raise while not overcommitting. BB (Claude) may fold or call light, but overall favorable spot in tournament to build stack.

JTo facing a 3-bet from short-stacked Elon (2375 chips). While I'm getting ~1.75:1 odds, JTo plays poorly against typical 3-bet ranges (pairs, AK, AQ, etc.) and I'm out of position. Elon only has 1675 behind after his 3-bet, so he's likely committed to shoving most flops. Folding is the disciplined play here rather than playing a marginal hand OOP against a short stack who's signaling strength.

Elon’s small-blind 3-bet to 700 off a 23.7bb stack looks like a size that can still contain some 3-bet/folds (rather than a pure 3-bet shove range). With Ks5s we don’t love a flat: SPR would be ~1 and we’ll be forced to overfold postflop, realizing equity poorly and getting dominated when we pair a king.

A 4-bet to a “pressure/commit” size leverages maximum fold equity versus the light part of his range while still being mathematically obliged to continue if he jams (we’ll be getting excellent pot odds). If he calls, he’ll have very little behind and we can effectively put him in on most flops, realizing our suited equity.

So I take the high-EV line: apply pressure now with a sizing that targets ~40%+ fold frequency from his 3-bet range.

Facing a 4-bet from the button after my SB 3-bet with AQo offsuit. Button's 4-bet range is strong: QQ+, AKo, maybe some bluffs but polarized tight given the action. AQo has ~30-35% equity vs that range but plays poorly OOP postflop, especially with my short 24BB stack (2375). Calling 900 into 2400 pot commits much of my stack (leaves ~1475 behind), turning my hand into a bad one postflop. 5-bet shoving adds 1675 (total 2375), but fold equity is low against Button's strong range and deeper stack (5625); they'd likely call with better. Pot odds don't justify the call (need ~27% equity, but dynamic EV negative OOP). Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament; AQo is not strong enough here. FiveTwo (Button) has been aggressive historically, but this sizing indicates strength.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with Eight of Spades and Trey of Spades. This is a very weak hand to open with from early position, even in a 6-max game. While the hand is suited, it has no connectivity and very low rank. Given the tournament context and my current healthy stack, there is no strategic reason to play this hand from this position. I will fold.

I am in Middle Position with Ace-Five of Spades (A5s). The UTG player has folded. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game due to its potential to make nut flushes and straights (wheel), as well as having an Ace blocker. My stack is deep enough (73.5 BB) to play this comfortably. I will raise to 250, which is a standard 2.5x open size observed in this tournament, to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. I need to be aware of the short stack on the Button (Elon) who might shove, but opening is still the correct play.

CO vs MP 2.5bb open, A6o is too weak to flat profitably (dominance issues and plays poorly multiway), but it has a strong blocker (Ad) and benefits from position. I’ll use it as a light 3-bet to attack Pro’s opening range, aiming to win the pot preflop or isolate in position; if Pro 4-bets, I can comfortably fold.

Facing a raise from Pro (MP) to 250 and a 3-bet from FiveTwo (CO) to 800 into a 1200 pot. My 89o is a speculative offsuit hand with mediocre playability. On the button with position, but pot odds are 800 to win 1200 (~1.5:1), and post-flop SPR would be low (~0.8 with my remaining 1575), committing me if I miss. Equity against a typical CO 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, some bluffs) is poor, and history shows FiveTwo and Pro are aggressive but this spot is too strong to speculate profitably. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this long tournament (39/150).
4d2d is a very weak hand, even suited. Facing a raise and 3-bet, I have no business continuing with bottom suited garbage. Easy fold.

I have QQ in the big blind facing Pro opening to 250 (middle) and a 3‑bet to 800 from the Cutoff (FiveTwo). QQ is too strong to fold and I don’t want to limp/call into a multiway pot where my hand loses equity to A/K hands and multiple callers. With ~144 BB I can apply pressure and try to isolate the 3‑bettor. A standard 4‑bet sizing of about 3x the 3‑bet (~2400 total) is big enough to fold out weaker hands (AQ, medium pairs, some bluffs) and extract value from worse hands, while leaving room to call a shove if we face a 5‑bet. Postflop plan: if called heads‑up use position/aggression to c‑bet appropriate textures and be cautious on ace‑high/connected boards; if faced with a 5‑bet shove I will call given QQ’s equity versus a polarized shove range. Therefore I 4‑bet to isolate and build the pot.

I opened to 250 with A5s, faced a 3-bet to 800 from the Cutoff, and then a cold 4-bet to 2400 from the Big Blind. A cold 4-bet indicates extreme strength, likely a premium range like QQ+, AK. Calling would commit over 30% of my stack with a hand often dominated, and shoving as a 5-bet bluff has little fold equity against such a strong line. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve my healthy stack of roughly 70 BBs.

Minni’s cold 4-bet from the BB after Pro opens and I 3-bet is typically very value-heavy (QQ+/AK with maybe a few suited bluffs). With A6o I’m dominated too often and, if I call, the pot becomes ~5100 with only ~4825 behind (SPR < 1), which forces me into high-variance stacks on many flops where I’ll usually have just an ace-high bluff-catcher. Turning it into a 5-bet jam bluff would need an unrealistic fold rate (around 65%) against this 4-bet range. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Hearts (T2s). This is a very weak hand, particularly from early position where I would be out of position against any callers. My stack is healthy at 71 BBs, so there is no need to make loose plays with marginal holdings. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold.

52o is at the very bottom of the range and plays terribly multiway (dominated pairs/5x, no suitedness, poor straight potential). In middle position with aggressive blinds and several opponents left to act, limping is -EV and opening would be spew. Preserve the 72bb stack and wait for better spots.

In cutoff with J9h suited, after two folds, this is an excellent spot to open-raise. J9s is a strong speculative hand with good playability, straight and flush potential, and position on the blinds and button. Pot is 150, blinds posted, standard open to 2.5x BB. Raising builds the pot and takes initiative. My stack of 2375 allows for this without overcommitting. Opponents are deep but history shows frequent folds or calls to raises. Min raise is 200 but 250 is better sizing for value and fold equity.

J3o is a weak hand that doesn't play well even with button position. Elon raised from cutoff, and while he's short-stacked (2125), calling with J3o is -EV. This hand has poor playability post-flop and limited high card value. Easy fold.

Ks2s is a weak, easily dominated hand and I’m out of position facing a CO open to 2.5x. Calling 200 more (250 total) OOP risks difficult post-flop decisions against Elon’s wide but often strong cutoff range and against the big blind who can 3‑bet. A tiny 3‑bet to 400 is thin — Elon (2,125, ~21BB) can easily shove and I’d be in a poor spot with a raggy K. With a healthy stack (15,600) I’ll preserve chips for better spots rather than investing marginal chips out of position. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 7d. Elon, the shortest stack at the table (approx 21 BBs), has raised to 2.5x from the Cutoff. Everyone else has folded. The pot odds are very favorable, as I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 400 (offering 2.6:1). Given my significant chip lead, I can afford to see a flop and potentially eliminate a short-stacked opponent if I connect well, although I must be cautious as I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I have hit middle pair on a very coordinated board (6d-8d-7c) with King-high kicker. As the big blind who defended against a Cutoff raise, I am out of position. This board hits many suited connectors and small pairs in a typical opening range. I will check to see how Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, reacts to this texture. Given he is short-stacked, checking allows me to control the pot size and decide whether to continue based on his bet sizing and the turn card.

Post-flop heads-up vs BB who called preflop raise and checked. Board 6d-7c-8d is coordinated with straight draws (4-5, 9-10) and diamond flush draw. My J9h suited gives gutshot straight draw (4 outs to 10) plus overcard J and backdoor heart flush. ~9% equity to straight by river, plus fold equity and position. As preflop raiser (IP), standard cbet to take initiative, deny equity, build pot with draw or take it down. Flash deep stack but history shows calls then folds to aggression sometimes. Half-pot sizing balances value (if sets/two-pair) and bluffs, SPR low post-bet. Pot 550, bet 300 for good sizing.

I have hit middle pair on a very connected board (6d-8d-7c). Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has a short stack and is betting about 55% of the pot. Folding is not an option given the pot odds (3.8:1) and the fact that I have a piece of the board with a high kicker. Raising is risky because Elon is short enough to be pot-committed with any overpair, draw, or better made hand. Calling allows me to see the turn card and determine if I should continue based on the board development and Elon's remaining stack.

The turn card is the six of hearts, which pairs the bottom card of the flop. I currently hold two pair (sevens and sixes) with a King kicker. The board remains highly coordinated, with several straight possibilities (5-9, 9-T, 4-5) and a diamond flush draw still active. Elon is playing a very short stack (1,825 remaining). By checking, I am looking to control the pot and see how he responds. If he checks behind, I get a free look at the river; if he bets his remaining chips, I will have to evaluate based on the price offered, but leading out myself into a short stack on such a dangerous board is unnecessary when my hand is primarily a bluff-catcher or marginal showdown hand.

Heads-up on turn with position vs Flash in BB. Board 6d-8d-7c-6h is draw-heavy: diamond flush draw, straight draws (e.g., 4-5, 5-9, 9-T, J-T). My J♥9♥ gives gutshot straight draw to 10 (4 outs ~8% by river), live overcards (J pairs topper, 9 may play), backdoor heart flush, and position. No showdown value really (high card loses to most made hands like 6x, 77+, 87, 98 etc.), but range advantage as preflop raiser. Flash checked flop and turn after calling cbet; shows weakness or trapping, but history (e.g., Hand 24: called large bets but checked river) suggests he folds draws often. Pot 1150, my stack 1825 (SPR ~1.6). Half-pot bet ~575 balances thin value (beats bluffs/draws), semi-bluffs equity, denies draws realization, builds pot if called. Protects vs runner-runner draws. Checking risks giving free card to flush/straight outs or check-raise. Betting maintains initiative, exploits perceived weakness.

On the turn, the board has paired with a six, giving me two pair (sevens and sixes) with a King kicker. The board is extremely coordinated, with possible straights (5-9, 9-T) and an active diamond flush draw. However, Elon is a short stack with only 1,250 chips remaining after this bet. My pot odds are exactly 3:1 (575 to win 1,725), and I only need to be right about 25% of the time to justify a call. Given Elon's aggression and short-stack status, he could be betting with overcards, a flush draw, or a smaller pair. Folding is too tight given the price and my relative hand strength, while raising doesn't accomplish much against a player who is already pot-committed. I will call to see the river.

The river 9c is a dangerous card that completes several possible straights (any 5 or 10). My hand, which provides two pair (sevens and sixes), is now a marginal showdown hand or a bluff-catcher. Since I am first to act on the river and the board texture is highly connected, I will check to see Elon's reaction. Betting here does not provide much value as I am likely only called by hands that beat me. Given Elon's short stack, I will decide whether to call a potential shove based on the final pot odds, but checking is the best initial move.

River 9c pairs my 9h, giving top pair with Jack kicker on a board of 6d-8d-7c-6h-9c. This improves my showdown value significantly from gutshot/draw on earlier streets. Possible villain holdings from BB limp-call pre, calling flop (straight/flush draw heavy) and turn (paired 6s): includes made straights (54s, any 5x for 5-6-7-8-9), two pairs (e.g., 98, 87=7-8 tp, 67, 68=6-8, 69=6-9, 96), trips+ (66 full house), 99 sets; but also missed diamond draws (Axd, Kxd, etc.), weaker pairs like T9/JT (now pairs), 10x high, floats. History shows Flash passive: calls bets but checks streets, folds to repeated aggression sometimes (e.g., Hand 24 showdown after calls, Hand 30 checked down). I've bet three streets, repping strength (sets, two pair, str8s). Pot 2300, my stack 1250 (SPR ~0.54), jamming commits max value from worse (weaker 9s like Q9/K9/A9 low kicker? A9 strong but J good, missed draws pairing nothing, T9 pair+str8 draw). Straights/boats snap-call, but ~30-50% of calling range (bluffs/draws/weak pairs) calls, +fold equity vs pure air. Checking risks villain having similar/weaker or chopping, but pot-committed spot favors extracting. Thin value shove maximizes EV long-term stack growth.

The river 9c is a very coordinated card, completing straights for any hand with a 10 or a 5. My hand, which provides two pair (sevens and sixes), now loses to any 10, 5, 8, 9, or higher pocket pair. However, Elon is a short stack who has been aggressive throughout the hand. The pot is 3,550 and I only need to call 1,250, giving me odds of 2.84:1. This means I only need to be right about 26% of the time to break even. While the board is dangerous, a short-stacked player in a Cutoff vs Big Blind scenario could be shoving with a missed flush draw (like AdXd) or a pair smaller than my King kicker. Given my significant chip lead and the price offered, I will call to see his cards, as folding for such a small remaining portion of his stack at these odds is generally too exploitable.

6-handed UTG with Q4o is too weak to open: dominated by better queens, poor playability, and will frequently face 3-bets from aggressive stacks behind (Button/SB especially). With no compelling ICM pressure info and a healthy stack, best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better UTG spots.

T9o is a marginal speculative hand at best, but from middle position with deep stacks (48BB), it's not strong enough to open voluntarily. Standard play is to fold preflop to preserve chips for premium spots. No reads from history suggest otherwise for this spot, and with multiple players behind including button and blinds, risking a limp or raise is -EV.
A4o in cutoff with two folds before me. This is a reasonable hand to open from late position as a steal attempt. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

On the button with 5♣7♥ facing a standard cutoff open to 250. This is a weak, low offsuit connector with some straight potential but poor high-card/blockers — not worth a 3-bet. Calling is correct: I keep position, see a flop cheaply, and can fold to heavy aggression post-flop if I miss. Folding would be too tight given my positional advantage and the small cost; a raise isn’t justified by the hand’s equity. I will call 250 to realize equity and play the flop in position.

I am holding 2h 3c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Claude has raised from the cutoff and Minni has called on the button. I am in the small blind, which means I would have to play the rest of the hand out of position against two opponents. Given the hand strength and the disadvantageous position, there is no reason to commit any more chips to this pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2c, a very weak offsuit hand. The Cutoff raised to 250 and the Button called. Although I only need to call 150 into a pot of 650 (getting very good pot odds of roughly 5.3:1 or 18.75%), playing 92o out of position against two opponents is rarely profitable. This hand has very poor playability and is dominated by many hands in the opponents' ranges. I will likely fail to realize my equity and face difficult decisions post-flop. Folding is the disciplined choice.

Flopped top pair with Ace on a KKA board. I have strong showdown value with Ac - likely the best hand here. Minni just called preflop so probably has a medium-strength range. Standard c-bet around half pot to extract value and protect against draws/underpairs.

Board K♣ K♦ A♥ — I hold 5♣7♥, a complete miss. Claude was the preflop raiser from the cutoff and has now c‑bet a half‑pot 325 into the ~650 preflop pot; the pot is 975 and calling 325 would make the pot 1,300 (I need ~25% equity to break even). I don’t have showdown value and have only very remote backdoor outs, but I do have position. Claude’s CO open/c‑bet range is wide and his sizing is consistent with many continuation bets and missed hands. Raising as a bluff here is too risky (I don’t have good blockers and a reraise would force a fold vs a lot of value hands or commit a lot of chips). I’ll call the 325 to use position — float the flop and see the turn, look to take the pot away if he shows weakness, or fold to clear turn aggression unless I pick up real equity (a pair or a meaningful draw).

I have top pair (Aces) on a KKA5 board. Minni called both preflop and flop, likely holding a weaker ace or pocket pair. The 5s is a blank that changes nothing. I should continue betting for value - Minni will call with worse aces and possibly pocket pairs. Betting around 60-65% pot.

Claude opened from the cutoff, c‑bet the K♣ K♦ A♥ flop and now barreled the turn to 800 into a 2,100 pot. Heads‑up his line is heavily weighted toward value (Kx or Ax are primary candidates). I only have a small pair (5♣ with the 5♠ on board) — I’m dominated by K/A holdings and have just two clean outs (the remaining fives). Pot odds: calling 800 into a 2,100 pot requires ~800/2900 ≈ 27.6% equity to break even, while my chance to improve to a winning hand on the river is ~2/46 ≈ 4.3% (and my showdown equity versus a value range is far below the required 27%). Raising would be an overbluff (poor blockers and no fold equity against value), and calling to “see river” is unjustified vs Claude’s line. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

UTG position in a 6-handed game with Ac7h offsuit. This is a marginal ace-high hand with weak kicker and no suitedness or connectivity. Standard UTG opening range is tighter: 22+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways and strong suited connectors. A7o plays poorly post-flop, vulnerable to domination by better aces, and doesn't make strong hands often. Folding preserves stack for better spots, especially with deep stacks and tournament goal of long-term maximization. No steals viable from UTG with players behind.
K8o in middle position is a marginal hand. With 4 players still to act behind me (Minni, Flash, Pro, FiveTwo), this is too weak to open. Standard fold from MP.

Cutoff with T♠5♥ (Ten-five suited hearts) — a marginal but playable hand with backdoor flush/straight potential and good positional leverage versus the button and blinds. Two players already folded, so this is a perfect seat to attempt a standard steal. With a deep 150bb stack I want a clear, standard open-size that buys some fold equity and keeps multiway cheap calls less attractive. The table’s typical open sizes have been ~2.5–3x; I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB = total 300) to define the hand and take initiative. If called I’ll proceed cautiously post-flop: c‑bet in position on favorable textures, head towards pot control or fold to heavy resistance on dry boards. Fold to a large 3‑bet unless the sizing/players suggest a profitable call — this hand is not strong enough to commit to big 3‑bet pots out of position.

I am holding Jack-Seven offsuit, which is a weak hand. Minni has opened for 3x from the cutoff. Even though I have the button and a significant stack advantage, J7o is not a hand that plays well against an opening range, especially from an aggressive player. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o), which is a very weak hand. Minni, raising from the Cutoff to 300 (3BB), likely has a reasonable opening range. Playing this hand out of position against a strong range and a large stack is not profitable. The pot odds do not justify a call given the poor equity and playability of my hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Minni is opening from the cutoff and I’m getting a good price in the big blind (200 to win 450 ≈ 31%). Q4o is not strong enough to 3-bet (dominated too often when called/4-bet), but it has enough raw equity to defend as a call versus a likely wide CO range. I’ll take the pot odds and play postflop carefully (mostly check/evaluate, avoid bloating the pot when I make a weak top pair).

We defended the BB with a very weak offsuit hand and completely missed this A-high, dry flop. From out of position, our default is to check our range here: we don’t need to build a pot with no equity, and checking allows Minni to continuation-bet a wide CO range (giving us a chance to fold efficiently). Donk-betting Q4o here is too thin and risks getting raised or called by better hands very often.

I raised preflop and am in position against a single caller on a dry, rainbow A‑3‑7 board. T♥5♥ has no pair (only remote backdoor hearts/straights) so my hand itself is weak, but as the preflop aggressor I have initiative and good fold equity. A half‑pot continuation bet (~300 into 650) pressures many of the BB’s flatting hands (broadways, weak kings, small pairs) that missed, while keeping the pot manageable if I’m called. Plan: bet 300 to take the pot now or get value when missed; if called I’ll slow down and pot‑control on most turns unless I pick up real equity (heart, ten, five). I will fold to a sizeable raise — this is a probe for info and to win the pot outright when possible.

We defended BB very wide and this A-high, dry flop is a very poor texture for Q4o: no pair, no flush draw, and essentially no meaningful backdoor equity. Minni’s small c-bet (300 into 950) gives decent pot odds, but we still don’t realize anywhere near the ~24% equity needed to continue, especially out of position and with reverse implied odds when we spike a weak queen. Check-raising as a bluff is also unattractive here (we don’t block Ax and this board heavily favors the preflop raiser). Best play is to exit efficiently.
J5s is too weak to open from UTG. Poor position and marginal hand - will be dominated by better Jx hands and can't profitably continue vs 3-bets. Easy fold.

9♦5♦ is a marginal, low-suited hand — not a 3-bet candidate but it has some playability (backdoor flush/straight potential). The cost to continue is small (100 chips) relative to my 15,375 stack, so defending cheaply to see a flop is reasonable versus a single open from late/CO who is likely opening a wide range. Plan: call and play the flop cautiously — fold to heavy resistance unless I hit a pair, two pair, or a decent diamond draw; fold to a 3-bet preflop. Preserve chips otherwise and avoid bloating the pot out of position with a weak holding.

I am in the cutoff with Ace-Four offsuit. After Minni limped from middle position, I want to take the initiative. Raising here allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or isolate Minni to play the rest of the hand in position. The Ace serves as a good blocker against the players behind me. I'll raise to 400 to put pressure on the blinds and the limper.

I hold 4h 7d on the Button, which is a very weak hand (7-4 offsuit). Flash has raised to 400 over a limp from Minni. Facing a 4x big blind raise with a trash hand, I have no equity to call and no reason to bluff raise in this spot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

We’re in the small blind facing a large iso-raise (to 400) over a limper, and we’ll be out of position for the rest of the hand with 98o. While the immediate pot odds aren’t terrible, this hand realizes equity poorly OOP in a likely multiway pot (BB can come along and the limper is already in). A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive here because the sizing would need to be big (due to the limper and being OOP), and we don’t want to build a pot with a dominated, low-realization holding against strong continuing ranges. Best EV is to cut losses and fold the small blind.

6d9h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the BB facing an isolation raise to 4x from Flash in the CO after Minni's limp. Minni is likely to call or reraise, leading to a multiway pot where this hand has low equity and poor postflop prospects. Pot odds are not compelling (calling 300 into 950 effective pot), and with my 4700 stack, better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than speculate OOP with trash. Standard fold to avoid difficult situations.

9♦5♦ is a marginal suited speculative hand with backdoor diamond and straight potential. Facing a CO open to 4bb, calling the extra 300 (400 total committed this street) is a cheap way to see a flop given my 15,275 stack — the cost is small and Flash’s opening range from the cutoff should be fairly wide, so I have reasonable equity. I’m out of position, so I’ll proceed very cautiously: fold to any substantial 3-bet or large preflop aggression; on the flop I’ll continue only with a pair/two pair, a clear diamond/straight draw, or a favorable board, and otherwise fold to significant bets. This call preserves chips while keeping the option to realize implied odds if I connect.

I completely missed this flop. Board A♣ 2♣ 8♣ and I hold 9♦5♦ — no club, no pair and effectively only runner-runner outs. Flash opened from the cutoff so his range contains lots of clubs and A‑x that have already connected. I’m out of position and have very little equity, so betting as a bluff is high-risk (he can easily call or raise with clubs/Ax). I’ll check to conserve chips and plan to fold to any meaningful bet. If checked through to the turn I’ll re-evaluate only if I pick up a real draw or a pair.

I have top pair (Aces) with a 4 kicker and a very low flush draw (4 of clubs) on a monotone flop. Minni checked to me. While the monotone board is dangerous, I should bet for value and to protect my hand against single-club hands that might be drawing. If I face significant resistance (a large raise), I can re-evaluate, as my kicker is weak and the board is very wet. A bet of roughly half the pot should suffice to define my hand and extract value from weaker Aces or draws.

Board A♣ 2♣ 8♣ — I have 9♦5♦: no pair, no club, only hopeless runner‑runner outs. Flash opened from the CO so his range contains lots of clubs and A‑x that have already connected. He bets 450 into a 1,400 pot; calling 450 requires ~24% equity. My realistic equity vs his range here is very small (single digits), well below the pot‑odds threshold. I’m out of position and have no reasonable fold equity, so a bluff/raise is too risky. Following my preflop/flop plan to fold to meaningful bets with this holding, I fold to conserve chips.

UTG with 3♦5♣ is a very weak, unplayable hand — low, offsuit, poor connectivity and dominated by most calling/raising ranges. The pot is only the blinds (150) and calling 100 to play out of position against 4 players behind (including big stacks/likely-aggressive Flash and Pro) is a losing long-term proposition. Raising as UTG with this hand is unjustified; I should conserve my deep stack for better spots and stronger opening hands. Fold.

I am in middle position with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a weak starting hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening raise, especially with five players still left to act. As the chip leader, I want to maintain my stack and wait for better opportunities rather than playing marginal hands out of position. I will fold.

I am in the Cutoff position with A7s, which is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. With only the Button and Blinds remaining to act, raising puts pressure on them. A standard open size of 2.5x (250 chips) balances pot odds and fold equity. If 3-bet, I will likely fold, but if called, I have a suited ace which plays reasonably well post-flop, ideally in position.

CO opens to 2.5x and we have position on the Button with a reasonably connected hand that realizes equity well postflop. T9o is a bit thin for a 3-bet bluff (no suit, no strong blockers), but it’s comfortably within a BTN defend vs CO open at this sizing given the price and positional advantage. Calling keeps weaker hands in, avoids inflating the pot versus a range that can 4-bet, and lets us leverage position on many flops; if SB/BB squeeze big, we can comfortably release.

2d9s is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards, poor playability. Facing a CO raise by Pro and BTN call by FiveTwo, multiway OOP from SB is a nightmare. Pot odds ~3:1 but implied odds negative with weak range. Fold to preserve stack for premium hands in tournament.

Q2o is a weak hand, but I'm in the BB getting great pot odds - need to call 150 into a 650 pot (about 4.3:1). I'm closing the action with two players already in. This is a clear call to see the flop and play cautiously post-flop.

I flopped middle pair with Q2 on K-Q-8 board. With a King overcard and weak kicker, I should check here. Pro opened preflop and likely has hands that beat me (Kx, better queens, overpairs). Checking lets me control pot size and see what opponents do.

I raised pre-flop from the Cutoff with As7s and got called by the Button and the Big Blind. The flop comes Ks Qd 8s, giving me a backdoor flush draw and Ace high. This board texture (two broadway cards) connects very well with the calling ranges of my opponents (who likely have Kx, Qx, or pocket pairs). Being in a multi-way pot out of position to the Button, and with a hand that has low showdown value currently, a continuation bet is unlikely to get many folds from better hands and will only build a pot I am likely losing. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size and hopefully realize equity or see a free turn.

Multiway on a K-Q-8 rainbow flop, our T9o only has a gutshot to the J (4 outs) and no flush backdoor, so our equity and ability to barrel profitably are limited. Although Pro’s flop check can invite a stab, multiway reduces fold equity and both blinds/CO retain plenty of Kx/Qx that will continue. With position, the best EV line is to take the free card, keep the pot controlled, and realize our equity—then reassess on turns that improve us (J) or are favorable to bluff selectively.

I turned two pair (Queens and Deuces) on the 8s-Qd-Kc-2h board. This is a significant improvement from middle pair. Both opponents checked flop and turn to me, showing weakness. I should bet for value here - around 50-60% pot seems right to extract value from worse queens, Kx hands that might call, or draws.

I hold As7s on a board of 8s-Qd-Kc-2h. Despite having suited spades in the hole, the board is rainbow with only one spade (8s), meaning I have no flush draw. I possess only Ace-high with no straight draws on a board that hits opponent ranges heavily (Kings, Queens, Eights). The Big Blind leading out for 450 into an 800 chip pot (into two opponents) demonstrates strength, likely a made hand like top pair or better, or perhaps a straight draw like J-10. With no pair, no draw, and a player left to act behind me, I have zero reason to continue. I am effectively drawing dead against any value hand.

We only have a bare gutshot (J only, ~9% to hit) with no backdoor flush and no meaningful blockers. Claude’s small turn lead after the flop checks through is very often thin value/protection with Kx/Qx/8x (and some bluffs), and the price (450 to win 1700) requires ~26% immediate equity, which we don’t have. Floating needs massive implied odds on a J river (or a reliable bluff plan), and raising is a high-variance, low-equity bluff that risks getting called or jammed on by all Kx/Qx+ and better. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position with 6h 5d. This is a very weak starting hand and playing it from the earliest position at a 6-handed table is mathematically unprofitable. There are five players behind me who could have stronger ranges or choose to 3-bet. Given my current chip lead and the standard opening ranges for this position, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

I hold 2h 6s in Middle Position, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With four players left to act behind me, including the aggressive blinds, entering the pot would be a losing play in the long run. My stack is healthy (67 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with trash cards. Folding is the only logical decision.

We’re first in from the cutoff after two folds, so this is an open-spot, but J4o is near the bottom of the deck and performs poorly when called (dominated Jx, bad playability, reverse implied odds). With a big-stack BB likely to defend and an active BTN behind, forcing a steal with this combo isn’t worth the risk at 50/100. Best to preserve stack and wait for stronger opens/spots.

Preflop on the button with Qs9d offsuit. Three folds ahead (UTG, middle, cutoff), so isolation against blinds only. Button position is ideal for stealing blinds with a wide range. Q9o has decent equity (around 40-50% vs typical calling ranges), good playability, and fold equity vs blinds. Blinds have deep stacks (Claude 90BB, Minni 148BB), but my 46.5BB stack supports a standard open-raise. Historical opens are ~2.5BB (250), so raise to 250 for value/steal. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots.

AQo in the SB facing a button open from Elon. This is a premium hand that plays well as a 3-bet. Elon has been active with raises and I have position on the BB. Standard 3-bet sizing to around 750-800 to take control of the pot and extract value or take it down preflop.

I have Jd5c in the big blind facing a button open to 250 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 750. This is a weak, poorly‑playable offsuit hand that will be dominated by a typical SB 3‑bet range and is very difficult to play out of position (often multi‑way). Calling 650 more (750 total invested) only gets ~37% pot odds but my raw equity vs likely ranges is well below that, and 4‑bet/isolate options are spewy with this holding. With deep stacks and many better spots ahead, I fold and preserve my chips.

Preflop on button with Qs9d offsuit after opening to 250 as a steal. Claude 3-bets to 750 from SB (3x), Minni folds. Pot 1100, to call 500 (11.4% of my stack). Q9o has poor equity (~30-35%) vs typical SB 3-bet range (QQ+, AKo/s, some bluffs like Axs, KQs). Offsuit reduces playability OOP postflop. Position advantage doesn't outweigh hand strength; calling leads to difficult spots multiway or OOP. Raising requires 1250+ (too thin with marginal hand and committed stack). Historical aggression from Claude (e.g., frequent raises/3-bets) suggests strength. Fold preserves 44BB stack for better positions/hands in tournament with 105 hands left. Standard fold for this spot.

I am in UTG position with 2c 3h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, low connectors). With 5 players left to act behind me and a stack of 67BB, there is no justification for entering the pot. The risk of being dominated or facing a 3-bet is high, and the hand has very little post-flop playability. Folding is the only standard and correct play.

9♣3♠ offsuit is far too weak to open from middle position, especially with multiple aggressive/large stacks left to act (CO/BTN/SB/BB) who can isolate and force us to play dominated, low-equity spots out of position. Limping is also unattractive because it invites isolation and we realize equity poorly. Preserve stack and wait for better opening candidates.

5c7h is offsuit, gapped, and very weak with poor postflop playability and low equity multiway or against defenses. Even in cutoff after two folds, it's not in a standard RFI range (~25% hands from CO). Button Claude is aggressive and deep-stacked, blinds deep too. Folding preserves 44BB stack for premium spots in tournament setting with 104 hands left.
Button with 75o after 3 folds - decent steal opportunity. While the hand is weak, position is excellent and only blinds remain. Standard button steal raise to 250 (2.5x). If I get 3-bet, easy fold.

I'm in the small blind with A♣6♠ facing a BTN open to 250. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot, giving ~33% (2:1) pot odds — a cheap defend. A6o has decent showdown value (blocks many A-x combos) but a weak kicker and poor postflop playability to build a big pot OOP. I don't want to 3-bet this marginal offsuit Ace and face harder decisions out of position or invite a squeeze from the big blind. With deep stacks and a likely wide button opening range, the optimal line is to call, keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss; if I hit an Ace or get decent equity on the flop I can proceed. So I'll defend by calling the 200.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8h 7s. Claude (Button) raised to 250 and Minni (Small Blind) called. I have already committed 100 chips for the big blind, and it costs me only 150 more to call into a total pot of 600. This gives me pot odds of 4:1. While 8-7 offsuit is not a premium hand, it has decent connectivity for a multi-way pot. Given the excellent price and the fact that I am closing the action pre-flop, calling is the most profitable decision to see if I can hit a strong draw or two-pair on the flop.

I have A♣6♠ on A♠ T♣ J♠ — top pair but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position in a three‑way pot (Claude opened BTN, Flash defended BB). This board is highly dangerous (KQ makes Broadway straight, AJ/AT/TJ and sets are in their ranges), and I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal kicker. I’ll check to control the pot, gain information, and avoid committing chips unless I see a reasonably small c‑bet. Plan: call small-to-medium bets (~≤½ pot ≈ 350–400) to realize equity and fold to large bets or check‑raises that represent straights/two‑pair/sets; re-evaluate on the turn based on sizing and who remains in.

On a board of As-Tc-Js, my 8h-7s has zero connectivity and no showdown value. This board is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Claude) and the caller's range (Minni), as it consists entirely of broadway cards. Since I have no pair and no draw, there is no reason to bet or bluff into two opponents on such a coordinated texture. I will check and plan to fold if any significant betting occurs.

I have 75o and completely missed this AJT two-spade board. Both opponents checked to me. This board is extremely coordinated and hits many hands that would call a button raise (broadways, suited aces). With zero equity - no pair, no draw (my 5s doesn't help) - a c-bet is too risky. I'll take the free card.

I have A♣6♠ — top pair with a very weak kicker in a three‑way pot, out of position to the BTN opener and the BB. Flop checked through and the 9♥ on the turn makes the board even more dangerous: lots of better A‑x combos, two‑pair/TJ, sets and Broadway straights are in our opponents’ ranges. I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal kicker or get exploited by a check‑raise/large turn bet. With no bet to face, the correct, lower‑variance line is to check and control the pot: call small bets to realize value vs worse A‑x but fold to large sizing/raises that represent straights or two‑pair. If everyone checks to the river I’ll re‑evaluate for a small value bet vs worse A‑x or check back to showdown.

On the turn 9h, I have actually completed a straight (7-8-9-10-J). However, this is the lowest possible straight on this board texture (As-Tc-Js-9h). Any player holding a Queen has a higher straight (8-9-10-J-Q), and any player with K-Q has the Broadway straight. Since the flop (As-Tc-Js) was checked around by all three players, it is possible that no one has a powerhouse yet, but the board is extremely coordinated. By checking, I can see how the Button (the pre-flop raiser) reacts and potentially see a safe river card. If the river is a blank, I may be able to reach showdown or evaluate a small bet. I will check for now to control the size of the pot with the 'idiot end' of the straight.

Board is now A-T-J-9 with two spades - extremely connected. Any Q makes a straight (KQ has the nuts). I have 75o with absolutely no equity - no pair, no draw, nothing. Both opponents have checked twice, which could be weakness or trapping. With this scary board texture, a bluff is too risky. Check and hope to see a free showdown.

Check. I have top pair A♣6♠ but a very weak kicker on a highly coordinated river (A♠ T♣ J♠ 9♥ 8♣) in a multiway pot. There are many Qx/KQ combinations that make straights (8-9-T-J-Q and T-J-Q-K-A), plus possible two-pair/set combos (AJ/AT/TJ/99/88) that beat me. Everyone checked through earlier, but passive action doesn’t eliminate a large number of straight combos — and OOP I don’t want to inflate the pot or face a river raise that I must fold. The pot is small relative to stacks, so a small thin value bet could get called by worse A-x but also gets raised by straights; overall the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check to showdown and avoid turning a marginal hand into a big loss. If an opponent bets a small amount I’ll consider a call to realize equity vs worse A-x, but I’ll fold to a large raise.

The board has completed a very dangerous runout (As-Tc-Js-9h-8c). I hold 8h-7s, which gives me a straight (7-8-9-10-J). However, this is the lowest possible straight on this board. Any opponent holding a single Queen makes a higher straight (8-9-10-J-Q), and anyone with K-Q has the Broadway straight. Since the flop and turn were checked around by all three players, it is possible that no one has a high-ranking hand, but betting into this board is extremely risky. I will not get called by anything worse than my hand, and I will likely only be called or raised by a Queen or K-Q. Checking is the most prudent play to see a showdown for free or evaluate if another player bets.

Wait - I have 7d5s and the board is As-Tc-Js-9h-8c. The board has 8-9-T-J, so my 7 completes a 7-8-9-T-J straight! Both opponents have checked all streets showing weakness. I should value bet. Any Q beats me (makes a higher straight), but given their passive play, they likely don't have it. Betting ~1/2 pot for thin value against pairs, two pairs, or weaker holdings that might call.

Board: A♠ T♣ J♠ 9♥ 8♣. I hold A♣6♠ — top pair with a very weak kicker in a three‑way pot. Claude (BTN) bets 375 into a 1125 pot (≈1/3 pot), so calling 375 gives me ~25% equity to break even. Preflop and postflop action was passive and my pre‑stated plan was to check the river and call small value bets from worse A‑x; this sizing falls inside that threshold. Claude’s line is consistent with a thin value bet or a small bluff — yes, Qx/7x straights and two‑pair combinations beat me, but folding to a 1/3‑pot probe would be too nitty given the pot odds and my blocker to some A‑x. I’ll call the 375 to realize equity and go to showdown, but I will fold to a significant raise (e.g., a min‑raise to 750 or larger) since my kicker is very weak and a raise heavily indicates a straight/two‑pair.

On a board of As-Tc-Js-9h-8c, my 8h-7s has completed a straight (7-8-9-10-J). However, this is the lowest possible straight on this board texture. Any player holding a single Queen makes a higher straight (8-9-10-J-Q), and any player holding K-Q has the Broadway straight (T-J-Q-K-A). Claude (the pre-flop raiser) bet 375 on the river after checking the flop and turn, and Minni has already called that bet. This suggests they both likely have at least a piece of the board. However, the pot is 1500 and it only costs me 375 to call, giving me pot odds of 4:1 (or 5:1 including my own call). Given the passive action on the flop and turn, it is possible Claude is value betting two-pair or a set that is now afraid of the straight, or even bluffing. While my hand is weak relative to the other possible straights, the price to see the showdown is too good to fold a straight in this spot. I will call to close the action.

UTG in a 6-handed game, 7♠2♣ is the weakest offsuit holding with poor playability, dominated high-card equity, and terrible realization when called/3-bet. With reasonable stacks behind and no compelling ICM/steal dynamic to justify opening trash, the highest-EV line is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

53 suited is a speculative hand at best, with poor high-card strength and limited straight potential that requires perfect conditions. In middle position with 44BB effective stack in a tournament, facing 5 opponents behind including aggressive players like Claude and Minni, and potential for multiway pot, this hand has negative EV. Folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term growth over 150 hands.

Q2 suited in cutoff with two folds ahead. Marginal hand but suited with a queen gives some value. In position against blinds and button. Worth a standard raise to try to steal the blinds - folding is too tight here given position.

A2s on the button vs a CO open to 250 is a clear flat-call. Suited ace with position has excellent postflop playability and blocks a lot of Claude’s nutted ace combos. A min 3-bet to 400 is too small (it just invites squeezes/4-bets from the blinds) and a large 3-bet for isolation is unnecessary with this hand — I prefer to keep the pot controllable and use position. Plan: call 250, play aggressively postflop when I hit an ace or nut spade/draw, otherwise pot-control and fold to heavy action on dry boards without equity.

I am holding 63o in the small blind. Claude raised from the cutoff and Minni called on the button. This is an extremely weak hand to play out of position in a multi-way pot. Despite the decent pot odds to call, the hand has almost no playability or equity realization. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with [Qc Tc], a strong suited broadway hand. Facing a raise to 250 from the Cutoff and a call from the Button, I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 650). My hand plays well multi-way and can make strong flushes and straights. There is no need to 3-bet squeeze out of position against two opponents with reasonable ranges. Calling closes the action and allows me to see a flop cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with QcTc on a flop of 5c Ah Kh. The pot is 800. This board texture (Ace-King high) hits the pre-flop raiser's (Claude in Cutoff) range very hard, as well as the caller's range. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack) and a backdoor flush draw. Being out of position in a multi-way pot against ranges that likely connect with this board, leading out (donk betting) is inadvisable as I will likely be called by better hands or raised. The standard and prudent play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to see if they continue aggression and re-evaluate based on their sizing. I check.

I have Qh2h on a 5c Ah Kh board - that's a second-nut flush draw (9 outs). It's checked to me with two opponents. This is a great spot to semi-bluff: I can win immediately if they fold, or I have strong equity if called. Betting around 50% pot (400) is standard to build the pot and apply pressure.

I have top pair (A) with a very weak kicker (A2s) and I’m on the button — position is a big advantage. Claude’s CO open then small flop C‑bet (400 into a ~1200 pot) looks like a wide/relatively weak continuation range (AX, Kx, broadways, some bluffs). Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, risk running into AK/two‑pair combos, and needlessly polarize. Calling keeps worse hands and heart-draws in, preserves pot control and lets me use position to extract value or fold to clear strength on the turn. Plan: call 400; reassess on the turn (bet/raise if I improve or villain shows weakness, fold to large polarized aggression indicating two‑pair/trips).

I hold QcTc on a flop of 5c Ah Kh. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack) and a backdoor flush draw. The pot is currently 1550 after Claude bets 400 and Minni calls 400. I need to call 400, offering me pot odds of roughly 20.5%. My gutshot has 4 outs (approx 8-9% equity on the turn), and the backdoor flush adds minimal equity, putting my total equity around 12-13%. Since my direct equity is lower than the pot odds required, and I am out of position against two opponents who likely connect well with this Ace-King heavy board, calling is unprofitable. The implied odds are present but risky given the flush possibilities on board. Folding preserves my stack.

I just made the nut flush! Board: 5c Ah Kh Jh and I hold Qh2h. My flush is A-K-Q-J-2 of hearts. Since I have the Qh and the A-K-J are on board, no one can beat me - they'd need two hearts with one being higher than Q, but A/K/J are on board and I have the Q. This is a value betting spot. Minni called my flop bet, likely holding Ax, Kx, or a heart draw that may have gotten there with a lower flush. I should bet around 60% pot to extract value from worse flushes, two pair, and strong one-pair hands that might call.

I hold As2s — top pair but the worst kicker. Pot is 2600 and Claude (CO) just raised the turn to 1000, so I must decide between folding, calling, or raising. Claude opened pre, gave a small flop c-bet (400) that I called, and now increases on the Jh turn. That line is consistent with a lot of value: turned hearts-flushes (two hearts in hand), KJ (turning two pair), or strong A/K combinations — but it can also include thin value and some bluffs given his small flop sizing. Calling 1000 into 2600 gives me ~21.7% pot-odds, and with position I can realize equity and control the pot size going to the river.

Raising here is unattractive: it polarizes my range while I have a vulnerable top pair and will often get called by better hands (flushes, two pair) or fold out the weaker hands I want to keep. Folding now is too passive given the pot odds and the fact I beat many of Claude's Kx/weak broadway hands. The correct exploitative line is to call, keep worse hands in, and re-evaluate on the river. Plan after the call: if a heart lands or Claude fires a large river shove/bet I will heavily favor folding (he will have a lot of flush/two-pair value), but if the river bricks I will proceed cautiously — check/call small-to-medium value bets and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal kicker.

I have the nut flush with Qh2h - the board shows Ah Kh Jh, and I hold the Qh, making A-K-Q-J-x the best possible hand. No one can beat me. Minni has called both flop and turn bets, suggesting either a smaller flush, strong Ax, or two pair. I need to bet for value - sizing around 65-70% pot (2400-2500) should get called by worse flushes and strong made hands while extracting maximum value.

The river 2d pairs my 2s — I now have two pair (Aces and Deuces). Claude’s line (CO open, tiny 400 flop c‑bet, larger 1000 turn bet, 2400 river) is mixed: it contains Ax (AK/AJ/A5), Kx/broadways, some two‑heart combos that already make a nut heart flush on the turn, and some thin value or bluffs. Hands that beat me are AK/AJ/A5 two‑pair combos, any made heart flush, and rare sets/trips/full houses; many of Claude’s single‑pair Kx/Qx and missed broadway bluffs are now behind. Calling 2400 into an 8400 final pot gives ~28.6% break‑even equity — my read and card removal (I hold As2s) make it likely my two‑pair is ahead of his betting range more than that threshold. Raising would polarize me and risk getting called or shoved by hands that already beat me; folding is too passive with a made two‑pair. I’ll call 2400 and take it to showdown.

UTG in a 6-handed game with A4h suited. While suited aces have some postflop playability due to flush and straight potential, A4s is too weak from early position with 5 players behind and multiway pot risk. Facing aggressive opponents like Flash with deep stacks, better to preserve stack and wait for stronger holdings. Folding avoids difficult spots OOP with marginal equity.
J4 offsuit is a weak hand in middle position with multiple players behind. This is a straightforward fold - not worth opening or defending with such poor equity.

6♦3♣ is a very weak, offsuit, unconnected hand with poor playability even in late position. There are still three players to act behind (Button, SB, BB) who have shown willingness to defend and raise; open‑raising here risks facing a 3‑bet or playing a bloated multiway pot out of position with near‑zero equity. Limping/calling 100 would invite multiway action and is a losing long‑term strategy with this holding. With a healthy 102bb stack it’s better to conserve chips and wait for a much stronger spot — fold and open ranges to better hands.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit (Kd, Ts), and the action has folded to me. This is a standard opening hand in late position. I will raise to 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position with a solid hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Js8s, facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash), who is the chip leader. While J8s has some playability, defending from the Small Blind out of position against a 3BB open is generally loose and often -EV, especially given the risk of the Big Blind squeezing and the difficulty of realizing equity against an aggressive big stack. The larger open size reduces my pot odds, making a fold the standard GTO play for this specific hand in this configuration. I will preserve my 64BB stack for better opportunities.

Button open to 3bb is a wide range, so BB can defend fairly wide, but 7♠3♠ is near the bottom even suited: no high-card playability, poor domination potential, and we’ll realize equity badly out of position versus an active raiser with a big stack. With no relevant blockers and little postflop robustness, I’d rather preserve chips for better defend/3-bet candidates.
K2 suited from UTG is too weak to open. Even though it's suited, K2s doesn't play well from early position with 5 players behind. Standard fold here.

4♦8♥ offsuit is a very weak, disconnected hand with poor post‑flop playability and high chance of being dominated. With UTG folded there are multiple players still to act (button, cutoff, blinds) who have shown aggression in prior hands — calling 100 or raising to 200 would likely put me in a multiway pot out of position or face a reraise. With ~102bb I should preserve chips for a better spot rather than limp/raise a trash hand. Fold.

I am holding 8-3 offsuit in the Cutoff position. This is an extremely weak hand with no significant equity or playability. Even as the table chip leader, there is no strategic reason to open this hand with several active players behind me. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the Button with As9s, a strong suited Ace. The action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. A9s performs well post-flop and dominates many hands in the blinds' calling ranges. I will raise to 2.5BB (250 chips) to apply pressure and build the pot with a strong hand.

Facing a button min-open, A5o is a marginal defend from the SB because we’ll be out of position the whole hand and often dominated when we make an ace. Flat-calling also invites the BB to come along cheaply, creating a multiway pot where our hand realizes equity poorly. As a 3-bet bluff, A5 blocks strong Ax (reducing 4-bet/call continues), can make wheel straights on low boards, and leverages fold equity vs a likely wide BTN opening range. Plan: 3-bet and fold to a 4-bet; if called, play mostly as the aggressor with selective c-bets on favorable textures (low/paired boards, A-high boards) and keep pots manageable on bad runouts.

Preflop in the big blind with K4o, facing a button open to 250 and a small blind 3-bet to 900. Pot is 1250, to call 800 for pot odds of about 1.56:1 (needing ~39% equity), but K4o is a marginal king-high hand with a terrible kicker, offsuit, and poor playability postflop. Against a button opening range (wide) and SB 3-bet range (often strong or polarized), my equity is low multiway. With 43BB stack, no need to speculate; folding preserves stack for better spots. Standard fold.

I opened to 250 with As9s from the Button and faced a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo in the Small Blind. The 3-bet sizing is large (3.6x), offering me pot odds of approximately 34%. Against a typical Small Blind 3-bet range which includes many stronger Aces (AT+, AQ+) and pairs (88+), As9s is frequently dominated and has roughly 30-35% equity. Although I have position, the stack depth (approx 56BB effective) and the high price to call make this a marginal spot with significant reverse implied odds if I hit an Ace. Preserving my 60BB stack is preferable to playing a bloated pot with a dominated hand. I will fold.

UTG with 2♦T♠ — a very weak, unsuited, non-connecting hand that performs poorly out of position and in multiway pots. I’m first to act with five opponents yet to act (including aggressive players and large stacks), and the cost to see a flop (100) invites multiway action where my hand has little equity and playability. Limping or opening with this hand is unnecessarily speculative; raising would be a pure bluff from early position. With a healthy 102 BB stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am holding 3s 4c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given my middle position and the fact that there are still four players to act behind me, including two in the blinds and the aggressive players like FiveTwo and Elon, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff with 5d 7s, a very weak offsuit hand (7-5 offsuit). Although I am in late position, this hand is far below the threshold for a profitable opening raise, even for a steal. The potential to make a strong hand is low, and playing it out of position against the button or in position against the blinds with weak holdings is generally -EV. With 61 BBs, I should preserve my stack for better opportunities. The standard and correct play here is to fold.

Folded to us on the button with Ah4s. This is a profitable steal spot versus two blinds: we have an ace (good blocker to strong continuing ranges), decent playability in position, and we pick up 150 uncontested often. I want a standard open size that pressures the blinds without bloating the pot too much with a dominated A-x hand. Given the table’s typical opens (250–300), 250 (2.5x) is a solid, consistent sizing.

9c2h is one of the worst possible starting hands, with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity for straights or flushes. Facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB), which can be wide but still crushes my range, from the small blind out of position. Pot odds of calling 200 into 400 are about 3:1, but equity realization post-flop OOP with this hand is abysmal – dominated often, no bluff value. Folding preserves my 42.5BB stack for better spots in a tournament with 100 hands left. No value in raising or calling.

In BB with 96o facing a button min-raise to 250. Getting 550:150 or about 3.7:1 pot odds, needing roughly 27% equity. Against a button open, 96o has reasonable equity to defend. While it's a marginal hand, the price is right for a call from the BB.

I flopped bottom pair (6s) plus an open-ended straight draw (5 or T completes). The board is very coordinated with 678 and two hearts. I should lead out for value and protection - there are many draws out there (flush draws, straight draws) that I want to charge. A bet around 60% pot (~325) balances getting value from worse hands while protecting against the many draws.

Claude donk-leads small (325 into 875 ~37%). With Ah4s we’re very light, but we’re in position getting good immediate odds (~21% needed) and can realize equity plus leverage position on many turns (overcards, especially if he checks). The Ah also reduces his nut-heart semibluffs, making this sizing more likely to contain marginal one-pair/draw holdings that may slow down. Raising here would be a higher-variance bluff with poor equity vs a BB defend range that connects well with 7-8-6, so I prefer to peel and play turns in position.

I have bottom pair (6s) plus an open-ended straight draw (5 or T completes). The Jd on the turn is a bit of a bad card - it doesn't help me and could have connected with villain's range (JT, J9, etc.). I led the flop and got called. With my pair + OESD I have decent equity (~8 straight outs plus 5 outs to two pair/trips). Checking here makes sense to control pot size and see what FiveTwo does. If they bet, I can call given my equity. If they check, I get a free river card.

Claude’s small donk on 7h-8s-6h followed by a turn check on Jd is most consistent with a one-pair/medium-strength hand or a draw that’s now deciding whether to continue. With Ah4s we have essentially no real equity (no flush draw, no straight draw, and an ace is not a clean out versus two-pair/straights), so betting would rely almost entirely on fold equity. In practice, BB’s range that leads flop and then checks turn contains a lot of 6x/7x/8x/Jx and combo draws that won’t fold to a single turn stab at a good frequency, and we also risk getting check-raised by hands like T9 that improved. Best play is to take the free card, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate river (potentially winning at showdown versus busted draws if he checks again).

The river 5c completes my straight! I now have 5-6-7-8-9 for the nut straight. This is a great outcome - I need to bet for value. FiveTwo called my flop bet so they likely have some piece of this board. With straights possible and the board being very connected, I should bet around 65-70% pot to get called by worse straights (like 54, 94), two pairs, or sets. Betting 800 into 1200.

River completes our 4-5-6-7-8 straight (very strong vs Claude’s line). Claude’s 800 into 2000 looks like a thin value/blocker bet with a lot of 7x/8x/Jx/two-pair/sets and some missed-draw bluffs; we want to extract value from those hands. However, the main hand that beats us is any 9x making the higher 5-6-7-8-9 straight, which is definitely in his BB defend range—so we don’t want to bloat the pot too much. A small/min raise targets his medium-strength value range and keeps our risk controlled; if he 3-bets/jams, it’s heavily weighted to 9x and we can comfortably fold.

I have a straight (5-6-7-8-9) but FiveTwo raised my value bet to 1600. The only hand that beats me is T9 (makes J-T-9-8-7). Getting 4400:800 or 5.5:1 pot odds, I need ~15% equity to call. FiveTwo could have T9 (beats me), but also 94/45 (worse straights), sets, or two pair. Given the excellent pot odds and the possibility they're overvaluing a weaker hand, calling is correct.

I am dealt 7-6 offsuit in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand to open with, especially from the earliest position at a 6-max table.
