Standard strategy dictates folding such hands to maintain a solid range and avoid playing out of position with marginal holdings against multiple opponents yet to act. Given my healthy stack, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands.

I am in Middle Position with King-Four offsuit (Kd 4h). This is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and playability. With 61.5 BBs, I have a healthy stack and no reason to open speculatively with such low equity cards, especially with several players left to act including the chip leaders in the blinds. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand 100% of the time from this position.

We’re first-in from the cutoff with J2o, which is too weak to profitably open at these stacks against two capable blinds. SB (Claude) is deep and has shown willingness to fight postflop, and BB (Minni) can defend wide; getting 3-bet or called puts us in dominated, low-equity spots. Preserve stack and wait for better steal candidates/board coverage hands.

Unopened pot folded to the button with Q7o, a speculative hand with position. Standard play is to raise for value/steal against blinds. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) puts pressure on blinds who may fold weak hands, building the pot with position advantage. Q7 has straight potential and dominates weaker queens. My stack of 4250 (~42BB) supports this without overcommitting. History shows blinds defend but not always, and stealing adds up over 150 hands.

JQo is a solid hand to defend against a button raise. Elon opened to 300 from the button which is likely a wide range. I'm getting decent odds to call 250 into a 450 pot. While I'll be out of position, JQo plays well postflop with straight and pair potential. 3-betting is also viable but calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize my equity.

I will call (300 total). Rationale: 9♠8♥ is a marginal but playable connected hand from the big blind — especially against a button open that the small blind has just called, which gives me good pot odds to defend. The pot is ~700 and I only need to add 200 more (300 total) so the call is cheap (≈3.5:1 pot odds) and preserves implied odds on flops where I can make straights or pair the 9/8. Raising to isolate with an offsuit connector OOP into two players is unnecessary and bloats the pot with a hand that plays poorly out of position. Plan postflop: see the flop and proceed only with clear equity (pair, two pair, straight/strong draw); fold to heavy action on dry boards. Calling keeps options open and avoids committing too much stack preflop.

I completely whiffed this flop with JQo on 8s3h5h. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw (no hearts). The board favors the preflop raiser's range with low cards. Best play is to check and see what develops - I can fold to aggression or potentially bluff catch if checked through.

I have top pair (8s) with a decent kicker (9♠8♥ on 8♠-3♥-5♥). This is a three-way pot (BTN open, SB called, I defended) and the flop is relatively dry aside from two hearts. Betting here would bloats the pot out of position and invites a raise from the button or a call/raise from the deep SB — not what I want with a marginal top pair. I’ll check for pot control and to realize equity cheaply. If checked around I get to see a free turn; if someone leads a small-to-medium bet (~1/3–1/2 pot, ~300–450) I’ll call to see another card since I still have showdown value and backdoor heart equity. If faced with large aggression (a pot-sized or larger raise) I will fold unless I improve, because those sizes usually represent sets/strong made hands that dominate me.

Preflop, I raised Q7o from the button for value, position, and steal potential against blinds who defend wide but fold often enough. Flop is 8s-3h-5h, a drawy board with flush and straight draws possible (e.g., 4-6, 2-4, 6-9). I have no pair, no strong draw (Qs is a spade backdoor, 7c gutshot-ish to 4-6 but weak), but queen-high with position. Both opponents checked, showing weakness. Standard c-bet to continue representing strength, deny equity to draws, and take down the 900 pot often. Betting 2/3 pot (600) sizes well for value from worse or folds from air/draws. My stack (39.5BB) supports it; if raised, I can fold profitably given preflop investment (300). History shows blinds call but check-fold flops frequently when out of position.

I have JQo with no pair and no real draw on 8s3h5h. I have only one heart (Jh) so no flush draw. Elon bet 600 into a 900 pot - a reasonable c-bet. With just two overcards, no backdoor flush draw, and minimal straight potential, my equity is too low to profitably call 600 out of position. This is a clear fold.

I call 600. I have top pair (8s) with a good 9 kicker and it’s heads-up after Claude folded. Elon’s 600 is a fairly standard ~2/3-pot c-bet — large enough to be protective but still contains many worse hands and bluffs from a button continuation range. Calling gives me correct pot odds (about 2.5:1) and keeps the pot under control while OOP. Raising here would bloat the pot out of position and often commit me against a shorter-stacked opponent who can shove; I prefer to see the turn and re-evaluate. Plan: call to see the turn; if I improve (two pair/trips or a clear draw) I’ll extract value/raise, if I miss and face pot-sized or larger aggression I will fold unless the action and read suggest otherwise.

Board: 8s 3h 5h Ac (turn). I hold 9s8h — top pair with a decent kicker, but the turn Ace severely improves the button opener’s range (many A‑x, two pair, sets). Pot is 2,100 and Elon is short (≈3,350) and has shown aggression (preflop open + flop c‑bet). I’m out of position, and betting the turn would bloat the pot and frequently induce a shove or large raise from better hands that now dominate me. There are no meaningful draws for me to charge, so the upside of betting is small while the downside is committing chips OOP to a polarized response. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking. If Elon bets the river I’ll re‑evaluate (call small/medium bets for pot odds or fold to large overbets unless I improve). Action: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid getting forced off by his likely Ace‑heavy range.

Preflop raise with Q7o from button for initiative and position. Flop c-bet continuation on drawy board to deny equity and take it down, which got one fold but Minni called OOP. Turn ace bricks my hand completely—queen-high no pair, no strong draw (backdoor spade at best). Board now has ace, favoring her range after calling flop (Ax, pairs, stubborn draws). Minni checks OOP again, showing possible weakness, but betting into her as the preflop caller risks value from Ax/pairs or calls from draws. With 900 invested (~25% pot pre-turn), checking preserves stack, controls pot, realizes equity with queen-high (live sometimes vs bluffs/draws), and uses position on river. Bluffing turn is low EV given ace likely improves her and pot odds poor for her to fold better. Deep stacks allow patience; folding equity not great here. Check to showdown or river cheap.

I hold 9♠8♥ — top pair from the flop but the turn Ace (and now the river Jack) meaningfully improve button’s calling/raising range. Elon bet the flop (600) and then checked the turn, a line that shows either missed turns or pot control; there’s no bet to call now. Betting river for value is thin (I’m beat by any Ace, Jx, two pair or better) and risks creating a situation where the short-stacked button can shove and put me to a tough decision. There’s also little to gain from bluffing since I don’t block the nuts and a bet will often fold out worse hands that might call small but induce a shove from better. I’ll keep the pot under control and take it to showdown — check and see if he shows weakness or fires a small bluff I can call.

Preflop button raise with Q7o for initiative and position. Flop c-bet on drawy board denied equity and took it down vs one opponent, but Minni called OOP, assigning her medium strength like pairs, weak aces, or stubborn draws. Turn Ac likely hits her range (Aces now stronger, pairs still good), and her check suggests caution but not air. River Jc changes little—no flush completes obviously, pairs board weakly. Queen-high has minimal showdown value vs her calling range (beats pure bluffs/air, loses to any pair/Ax). Bluffing risks 2100 into 2100 pot (EV negative given her history of calling stations and check-check indicating trap/weakness balance). Checking realizes equity cheaply, preserves stack (3350 ~16.5BB post-pot), and exploits position for free card/showdown. Tournament stage favors patience over high-variance bluff with 100 hands left.

I am in UTG position with King-Two offsuit (K2o). This is a very weak hand to play from early position with 5 players left to act behind me. The hand has poor playability and is easily dominated. There is no strategic merit in entering the pot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Unopened pot with 6 players, we’re in middle position with J8o. This hand is too often dominated by the continuing ranges of players behind (button/CO/blinds), and with several aggressive stacks left to act (Claude/Flash/Minni) we’ll face a lot of 3-bets or tough postflop spots. With ~47bb, preserving stack and waiting for better open candidates is higher EV than limping or opening J8o here.

97o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against typical calling or 3-betting ranges from the button (Claude) or blinds. Position is decent in cutoff, but unopened pot doesn't justify entering with trash given the aggressive tendencies observed in opponents like Claude, Minni, and Flash from hand history (frequent raises and multiway pots). Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament with 98 hands remaining. No need to limp or raise this speculative hand.

On the button with 73 suited after 3 folds. This is a standard button steal spot. While 73s is on the weaker side, I have position and fold equity against the blinds. A small raise to steal the 150 in blinds is profitable here. If I face resistance, I can easily fold.

I’m in the small blind with Ks6s facing a button open to 250 (pot 400). K6s is a playable, suited K with decent equity against a wide CO/BTN opening range and the call gives ~2:1 immediate pot odds (need ~33% equity). I don’t want to 3-bet this weak K as a pure isolation bluff (min-raising to 400 is too small and creates awkward multiway pots OOP), so I’ll defend and see a flop. Plan: call to 250, play cautiously postflop — continue with top pair/strong draws or fold to heavy aggression; fold to a sizable 3-bet preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o. Claude raised to 250 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The pot is 600, and it costs me 150 to call, giving me 4:1 pot odds. While the odds are mathematically tempting, T4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position in a three-way pot. It is easily dominated by the Button's opening range and the Small Blind's calling range. I choose to fold to avoid being caught in a difficult post-flop situation with a marginal hand.

I’m heads-up vs Claude on the flop with Ks6s and the board 9s 2s Tc. I have a strong nut-ish spade draw (4 to a spade flush) — 9 outs (~19% to hit on the turn, ~35% by the river) plus a single high card (King) which gives some showdown value. Pot is 600 and Claude opened from the button (likely a fairly wide range). This is a textbook spot to semi-bluff and build the pot: a half-pot lead applies pressure to many of his broadway/air hands, charges his weaker draws, and still leaves me room to fold to a big raise or continue if called. A bet of 300 (≈1/2 pot) requires him to fold ~25% to make the semi-bluff immediately profitable; combined with my ~35% equity to river makes this +EV. Plan: bet 300 to take down the pot or see a turn with my draw; if he raises large I’ll re-evaluate (often fold to very large raises), if he calls I’ll proceed for value/bluff-catch on the turn.

I have 73 clubs on 9s 2s Tc - complete air with only a backdoor club flush draw. Minni donk-bet 300 into 600. I have no pair, no real draw, and 7-high. Floating or raising as a bluff against Minni who tends to be sticky isn't profitable here. Easy fold.

UTG 6-handed with 7-2 offsuit is the weakest holding: poor playability, dominated equity, and no good nut potential. With ~47bb effective, there’s no need to defend the blind amount or enter marginal multiway spots out of position. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better opens/3-bet spots.

Preflop in middle position with 6s Jh offsuit, a weak gapped hand with low playability and no domination potential. After UTG fold, but still early enough to fold trash like J6o to preserve stack (33.5BB) for premium hands in a tournament setting aiming to maximize stack over 150 hands. Opponents have shown aggression historically, but no need to speculate here.
ATo in the cutoff with two players already folded. This is a standard open-raise hand from this position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to take down the blinds or isolate against callers with position.

Holding 2♦7♥ on the button facing a cutoff raise to 250: this is the worst offsuit hand and is almost always dominated. The pot is 400 and it costs 250 to call (break-even equity ≈ 250/650 ≈ 38.5%), but my actual equity vs a standard cutoff raising range is far lower and will drop further if the blinds come along. I have no meaningful blockers and little fold equity for a successful 3-bet bluff, so there is no +EV line here. Stack (11,750) is healthy — better to wait for a playable spot on the button. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Claude has raised to 250 from the cutoff. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against a significant stack, and this hand has almost no playability or equity. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my chip lead.

I hold 6d 3c in the Big Blind, which is a very weak offsuit hand. The Cutoff (Claude) has raised to 250. Although the pot odds are reasonable (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550, requiring ~27% equity), my hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position against an aggressor. Realizing equity with 63o is difficult, and calling here is generally a losing play in the long run. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

Preflop, UTG in 6-handed with AhJd (33.5BB effective). AJoff is a strong opening hand from early position: high card strength, broadway potential, good blockers. Standard to open raise for value and to take initiative. Players frequently open to 250-300 in history, so sizing to 300 (3x BB) builds pot while maintaining fold equity. Position is tough but hand is too strong to fold. Limping suboptimal. Pot 150, min-raise 200, but 300 optimal.
T5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a UTG raise to 300, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such a marginal holding out of position.

Elon opened UTG to 300 (3x). I have 7♣8♣ in the cutoff — a suited connector that plays very well in position and has good implied odds with my ~117 BB stack. Versus an UTG open his range is tighter, so 3‑betting as a bluff is risky (and he’s only ~30 BB — he could shove). Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and use position post‑flop to apply pressure or fold to big aggression. If players behind fold I’ll be heads‑up in position; if I face a big 3‑bet I can fold and preserve chips. Standard, +EV play here is to call 300.

I am on the button with Kc 9c, a strong suited hand that plays well in multi-way pots. Elon (UTG) opened for 300 and Minni (CO) called. Given my position and the pot odds, calling is a standard play to see a flop. While 3-betting is an option, a call allows me to evaluate the flop in position against two opponents without bloating the pot against an UTG range.

I hold Js 4h in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Elon) and two callers (Minni and Flash). This creates a multi-way pot where I would be out of position with a very weak offsuit hand. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 250 to win over 1000), J4o has poor playability and reverse implied odds. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

We’re getting a price (200 to win 1050), but 8-4 offsuit is a very low-realization hand multiway and out of position versus an UTG raiser plus two callers. Even if raw equity is near the breakeven threshold, we’ll frequently be dominated when we make a pair and have poor playability with no suitedness/connectivity. Preserving stack and avoiding reverse-implied-odds spots is higher EV here than defending this bottom-tier holding.

Preflop as UTG raiser with AJh, now flop Jc Qd Ad gives me top two pair (AJs), an extremely strong hand on this coordinated board with diamond flush draw and broadway straight draw possibilities (KT). Two callers behind: Minni (CO) and Flash (BTN), who have decent calling ranges including Ax, broadways, suited connectors. As preflop aggressor first to act, standard c-bet for value against worse Ax (KQ, AQ, AJ without diamond blocker), pairs, and draws, while protecting against flush/straight draws and semi-bluffs. Pot 1050, bet 2/3 pot (700) builds pot, offers good fold equity vs draws, and gets value. My stack 3050 (~30BB), SPR low post-bet. Avoid check-raise vulnerability.

Fold. Elon (UTG) bets 700 into a 1750 pot on A♦ Q♦ J♣. Calling 700 to win 2450 requires ~28.6% equity. With 7♣8♣ I have no pair and only remote backdoor outs (pair outs = 6 → ~13% to hit a pair on the turn; runner‑runner straight/flush odds are negligible). UTG’s range and sizing on this wet Broadway/ace board is heavily weighted to Ax/broadways/two‑pair, so my actual equity is well below the break‑even threshold. Raising is reckless — Elon is short (~23 BB) and could shove, and multiway action (Flash still to act) further reduces my playability. Preserve stack and fold.

On a flop of Jc-Qd-Ad, I have a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight (needing a Ten). Elon, the UTG raiser, is leading into two players with a 700 bet into a 1050 pot. This board texture heavily favors an UTG opening range, which likely includes many pairs (AA, QQ, JJ), two-pair combos (AQ, AJ, QJ), and strong Ace-X hands. With only 4 outs to the nuts (approx. 8.5% chance to hit on the turn) and facing a 2/3 pot bet, I do not have the direct pot odds (22%) to call. Furthermore, Elon's remaining stack (1650) limits the potential implied odds. Since the board is highly coordinated and hits Elon's range so well, I will fold.
8c4d is a very weak offsuit hand from UTG - no connectivity, no high cards. Easy fold from this position.

Ad7o from middle with only blinds in — this is a clear call. A7o has decent playability: top-pair potential and an Ace blocker, and the price to see a flop is small (100 into 150). Raising here would be a marginal isolation attempt facing 4 players to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB) — likely to get callers or a 3-bet from aggressive stacks and bloat the pot out of position. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop to evaluate. Plan: call 100, play the flop based on texture (bet/raise if I hit top pair or strong draw, fold to big aggression with nothing).

I am holding 3s 2c in the Cutoff position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I have a significant stack advantage and the current bet to call is only 100 chips, playing such a weak hand out of position against a limper and three players yet to act is not a profitable strategy. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Js Jh) on the Button, which is a premium hand. Minni has limped in from middle position. My goal is to isolate the limper and build value while discouraging the blinds from entering the pot cheaply with random holdings. A standard isolation raise size is 3 big blinds plus 1 big blind for the limper. With the blinds at 50/100, a raise to 400 (4BB) is appropriate. This size punishes the limp, charges draws, and allows me to play a pot in position, likely heads-up against Minni.

K4o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position, especially versus a button iso-raise over a limper. Calling 350 gives tempting pot odds, but we’ll be OOP to Pro (and often still have Minni/BB involved), leading to low realizable equity and nasty reverse implied odds when we make weak top pairs. Squeezing with this hand is too loose given stack sizes and likely continue ranges. Best decision is to release and preserve stack.

98o is a marginal speculative hand in the BB facing an isolation raise from the button over a MP limp. While pot odds are ~3:1 (300 to call into 650 pot), playing OOP multiway (if Minni calls the raise) with no suited potential limits playability and implied odds. My 40BB stack doesn't support deep implied odds postflop, and Pro's button range is wide but profitable for them to iso. Folding preserves stack for better positions or stronger hands, aligning with long-term ICM and stack maximization in tournament.

Call 400 (call the additional 300). Ad7o is worth seeing a flop here: I already have 100 committed, and calling 300 into a 650 pot gives ~31–32% break-even odds — reasonable with an Ace-blocker and decent top-pair potential. Pro raised from the button after a limp, so their range can be fairly wide (isolation vs limps) rather than only premium hands. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with an offsuit Ace and a middling kicker — that bloats the pot out of position and can get me into uncomfortable spots against stronger Axs or 4-bets. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize equity postflop: if I hit an Ace or a strong draw I will proceed for value/continue; if I completely miss and face significant aggression I’ll fold. If Pro shows unusual weakness or checks the flop, I can take the lead. Overall calling preserves stack flexibility and lets me play a reasonable hand postflop rather than forcing a marginal preflop confrontation.

Flop: Qd 3d 6d — I hold Ad7c: nut (ace‑high) flush. Pro opened from the button preflop, so his range is wide and contains plenty of diamonds, Qx hands and single‑pair hands that will pay to see a flop. This is a value spot: lead here to extract from dominated diamonds, Qx/top‑pair hands and other worse holdings and to deny free cards. I choose a half‑pot bet (~475 into 950) — big enough to charge draws and get thin value from worse flushes and pairs, but not so large as to fold out too many calling hands. If Pro raises aggressively I’ll reassess (likely call or shove depending on sizing and reads), but for now bet for value.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Js Jh) on a dangerous monotone flop of Qd 3d 6d. I do not hold a diamond, which significantly weakens my hand as I have no flush equity and unblock my opponent's flush combinations. Minni's line of limp-calling pre-flop and then leading out (donk betting) 475 into a pot of 950 usually indicates a hand that connects well with the board, such as a made flush, a set, top pair (Qx), or a strong draw like the Ace of diamonds. Against a Queen or better, I am drawing extremely thin (2 outs). Even against a flush draw with an overcard, I am in a precarious spot. Calling creates a difficult situation on future streets, especially if another diamond or an overcard falls. Given the stack depth and the adverse board texture, preserving chips is the prudent play.

UTG with J♣2♠ — a very weak, unplayable hand out of position. With five players yet to act and several capable/aggressive stacks behind (Flash, Claude, Pro, Button), limp-or-raise both lead to marginal, often losing spots: limping invites raises/squeezes and an open-raise is likely to be 3-bet or played against out of position. Deep stacks don't justify spewing chips from UTG with such a hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better, positionally advantaged spot.

I am in Middle Position with King-Eight offsuit. In a 6-max game, this hand is generally too weak to open, especially with several aggressive and short-stacked players behind me who might 3-bet or shove. Given the stack sizes and position, folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play to avoid unnecessary variance with a marginal hand.

I hold Qd 6c in the Cutoff. This is an offsuit Queen with a weak kicker, which is generally below the threshold for a profitable open-raise, even from late position. The Big Blind (Claude) is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively; opening light into a large stack who defends widely or 3-bets is risky. With 56 big blinds, I am not desperate to steal and should preserve my stack for better opportunities. Therefore, folding is the correct decision.

Folds to us on the Button with a strong suited broadway (AcQc). This is well within a profitable open-raise range: we can win the blinds often, have good playability in position, and can continue versus many 3-bets (especially from the SB’s ~40bb stack). I’ll use a standard 2.5bb sizing to apply pressure without bloating the pot unnecessarily versus the big stack in the BB.

27h suited is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, with poor post-flop playability, low equity against a button raise range (which includes many strong hands and bluffs), and difficult to realize equity out of position in the SB. Pot odds are about 2:1 (200 to call into 400), but implied odds are minimal due to hand strength. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in the tournament.

54 suited in the BB facing a button open of 2.5x. Getting 2.7:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). This is a playable suited connector with straight and flush potential. Against a button open which is typically wide, 54s has decent equity and implied odds. With my large stack advantage, calling is profitable.

Flopped bottom pair (5s) with 54h on a Qh7c5c board. I have some showdown value but it's a marginal hand. Standard play is to check to the preflop aggressor. If FiveTwo c-bets, I'll need to decide whether to call based on sizing - my pair has some equity against their range. I also have backdoor heart flush potential. No reason to donk bet here.

We flop top pair/top kicker plus the nut flush draw (AcQc on Qh-7c-5c), which is a very high-equity, high-value hand on a dynamic board. Versus BB’s check, we want to c-bet for value against worse Qx/7x/5x and to deny equity to hands like 8c6x, Tc9x, small pairs, and random overcards with a club. Betting also sets up profitable turn barrels on many cards (clubs, A, K, T, 9, 8, 6) while we can comfortably continue versus a check-raise due to strong equity.

I have bottom pair (5s) with 54h on Qh7c5c. FiveTwo bet 325 into 550, giving me 2.7:1 odds (need ~27% equity). Against a button range c-betting this board, my bottom pair has reasonable equity. I also have backdoor heart flush draw. The sizing is standard and doesn't scream strength. Calling to see a turn is justified - if a scare card comes or they barrel big, I can release. Folding bottom pair getting these odds is too tight.

Turn brought the 3h which significantly improves my hand. I now have bottom pair (5s) PLUS a flush draw (5h4h with Qh3h on board). I also picked up a gutshot straight draw (need a 6 for 3-4-5-6-7). My equity has improved substantially. Checking here makes sense - if FiveTwo bets, I can call with strong equity (flush draw + pair + gutshot). If they check behind, I get a free river to complete my draws. No need to lead out and potentially face a raise that prices me off a strong drawing hand.

Turn is a brick (3h) and after BB check we should keep building a pot with a monster-equity hand: top pair/top kicker plus the nut flush draw. Betting gets clear value from worse Qx, 7x/5x, and all club draws, while denying equity to hands like 8x6x/T9/overcards with a club. A larger sizing also sets up a clean river jam for value on many rivers (clubs, A/K, and some bricks).

I have bottom pair (5s) plus a flush draw (4 hearts - 5h4h in hand, Qh3h on board) plus a gutshot (need a 6 for 3-4-5-6-7). Pot is 2800, calling 800 gives me 3.5:1 odds (~22% equity needed). My combined outs: 9 hearts for flush + 3 non-heart sixes + 2 fives for trips = ~14 outs = ~28% equity on river. I'm getting the right price to call with this strong draw combination.

River 7s paired the board but missed all my draws - no flush, no straight. I'm left with just bottom pair (5s). My previous notes were overly optimistic about outs. With a weak pair facing a player who's bet every street, checking is the only sensible play. I have showdown value but betting would only get called by better hands. If FiveTwo checks back, I might win; if they bet, I'll likely fold.

River pairs the 7, which is a slight range-shift toward Claude (any 7x just improved to trips), and our nut flush draw missed. Still, our line (bet/bet) plus Claude’s check/call, check/call, check leaves him with lots of bluff-catchers: worse Qx (QJ/QT/Q9), some 5x/underpairs that floated, and missed club draws that now give up. We should take thin value in position, but avoid a big sizing that gets called mostly by trips/boats or strong Qx. A small-to-mid value bet targets those worse queens and keeps our bet/fold clean versus a check-raise.

River 7s paired the board. I have bottom pair (5s). FiveTwo triple-barreled but with relatively small sizing - 1000 into 3800. I'm getting 4.8:1 odds, needing only ~17% equity. The club flush draws missed, so FiveTwo could be bluffing with busted draws. The small sizing suggests either thin value or a blocking bet. At these odds, my bottom pair is a reasonable bluff-catcher. I don't beat much value, but I don't need to win often to make this call profitable.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a marginal opening hand from Under the Gun in a 6-max game. However, given my significant chip lead and the need to maintain an aggressive image to keep opponents off-balance, I will put in a standard open-raise to 250. This allows me to take control of the pot early or potentially win the blinds immediately. If I face significant resistance, such as a large 3-bet, I have the flexibility to fold without damaging my stack significantly.

I hold 5d 3s, which is 5-3 offsuit, an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the Under-the-Gun (UTG) player, whose range is likely very strong, I am significantly behind. I am in Middle Position with players yet to act behind me. There is no justification for calling or raising with this holding given the stack depth (56BB) and the early position strength shown by the raiser. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Flash is opening UTG in a 6-handed game to 2.5bb, which is typically a reasonably strong range. A3o is too often dominated when called and doesn’t realize equity well multiway, and flatting also invites squeezes from the button/blinds given stack depths. A light 3-bet bluff here is higher variance and better done with suited wheel aces (A5s/A4s) rather than A3o. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Qh6c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raise from Flash, who has shown aggression in the past. On the button, position provides some advantage, but calling 250 into a 400 pot (needing ~28% equity) is not justified as my hand is dominated by Flash's strong UTG range (top ~15-20% like pairs, broadway, suited connectors). Raising is suicidal without premium strength. Folding preserves my 40BB stack for better opportunities in this multi-player tournament.

7h4s is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. Against an UTG raise from Flash, this is a clear fold from the small blind. I'm out of position with a hand that won't make strong holdings often enough to justify the call.

I have Q♠J♠ in the big blind facing an UTG open to 250. The pot is 400 and I need to put in 150 more (250 total). QJ suited is a strong, playable hand heads-up with good straight/flush potential and more than the ~27% equity required by the pot odds (150 to win ~550). A small 3-bet to the minimum (400) would be weak and a larger 3-bet commits more and invites 4-bets from a deep, aggressive opener. I’ll call 250 to keep the pot controlled, see a flop, and proceed depending on whether I hit a pair or strong draw (continue for value/bluffs), or check/fold to heavy aggression on bad A/K high boards.

I hold Q♠J♠ on A♠ 7♣ 2♠ — a very strong drawing hand: two spades on the board plus my two spades = 9 outs to a flush (~35% to hit by river) and some backdoor straight possibilities. We're heads-up to the UTG opener whose range contains a lot of A-x and broadway hands that will continuation-bet this flop frequently. With deep stacks and good equity, I prefer to let him c-bet and take my pot odds rather than lead out or commit with a check-raise. Checking keeps the pot under control, induces bluffs/c-bets I can call profitably, and preserves fold equity on later streets if I hit. Plan: check and call reasonable c-bets; re-evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy aggression only if the action and sizing make my equity insufficient).

I have top pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker on a very dry board (As-7c-2s). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me from the big blind, a continuation bet is the standard play here. This allows me to get value from weaker hands that might call (like a 7, pocket pairs, or a spade flush draw) and to protect my hand. A bet of roughly half the pot (275) is a standard sizing for this texture.

Call 275. Q♠J♠ on A♠7♣2♠ is a very strong drawing hand — 9 spade outs (~35% to hit a flush by the river) plus some backdoor/paired-card equity. Flash opened UTG and cbets a moderate 275 into a ~825 pot; that sizing gives me roughly 25% required equity to call, and my draw comfortably exceeds that, so this is +EV. Raising here inflates the pot and invites 3/4-bets from a deep, aggressive opener; folding would concede too much equity. Plan: call to realize my draw, then re-evaluate on the turn — if I hit a spade or improve I’ll pursue value; if I miss and face heavy turn pressure I’ll fold unless the odds/effective stacks justify a continue.

On the turn I’ve improved to a pair of jacks (Js) with a Queen kicker plus a strong spade draw (9 spades left) and two remaining jacks — ~11 outs total (~24% to improve to trips or a flush on the river). Flash (UTG) c-bet the flop and then checked the Jack on turn — that check is ambiguous (pot-control with Ax or a missed/medium holding). With a made hand plus substantial draw, betting here is +EV: it charges spade draws, extracts value from worse jacks/Qx/KQ-type hands and many floats, and denies a free river to parts of his range. I’ll make a sizing around half-to-two-thirds pot to get value but not overcommit (bet 600 into the 1100 pot). If he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large check-raise; call smaller aggression to realize equity).

On the turn, Minni leads out for 600 into a pot of 1100. The Jack of Clubs is a somewhat significant card as it completes AJ (if she defended with it) and adds some backdoor club draws to her possible spade draws. However, I still have top pair with an Ace, and my hand is too strong to fold to a single lead, especially given Minni's history of aggressive play in previous hands. Raising here is unnecessary as it would likely only get action from hands that beat me (AJ, sets) while folding out her bluffs. Calling is the best option to keep her range wide, control the pot, and evaluate the river.

River assessment: I have J♠Q♠ on A♠‑7♣‑2♠‑J♣‑8♣ — one pair (Jacks) with a Q kicker; my spade flush draw missed. Line: UTG (Flash) raised pre, c‑bet the A♠ flop, called my turn bet of 600 after checking the J‑turn. That line is heavily weighted toward A‑x and other showdownable hands that often beat a lone Jack. Betting the river would mainly fold out bluffs and worse Qx hands while getting called by many better A‑x and any made hands that already beat me, so it’s thin/inverse value. With the board pairing a club and no additional outs for me, plus a pot of 2300 and no bet to call, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to control the pot and take showdown. I check and take my one pair to showdown rather than risking extra chips when I’m likely behind to much of his calling range.

I have top pair with a 9 kicker on a board that has become somewhat coordinated. Minni led into me on the turn after check-calling the flop, which usually indicates either a semi-bluff or a hand that improved (like AJ or a set). Her check on the river suggests she may have been on a draw that missed (like spades) or has a marginal Ace and is looking to get to showdown cheaply. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, but I could easily be called or raised by hands that have me outkicked or have improved to two pair (like A8 or AJ). Checking back is the safest play to realize my showdown value.

I am in UTG position with Ac3h (Ace-Three offsuit). At a 6-handed table with 50/100 blinds, opening A3o from early position is generally a losing play (-EV). The hand is easily dominated by better Aces, plays poorly post-flop, and I will be out of position against any callers. With 56 big blinds, I have plenty of chips and do not need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands in bad position. Standard strategy dictates a fold here.

We’re first-in after UTG folds, but Kh7c is a dominated offsuit king that performs poorly when called and doesn’t realize equity well multiway. With deep-ish stacks and several opponents who have shown willingness to defend/3-bet (especially the big stack in the BB), opening this hand from middle position is a low-EV steal attempt. Preserve stack for better spots.

Preflop in cutoff with 9d4c offsuit, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. UTG and MP have folded, but blinds and button behind with deep stacks. No reason to open or call; folding preserves stack for better spots. Standard tight play early in hand to maximize long-term EV.
On the button with Q5o after 3 folds. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and a Queen high hand. Raising to 250 (2.5x) to steal the blinds.

I'm in the small blind with A♣9? (Ah9c) facing a button open to 250. With ~108 BB effective and an Ace blocker plus a decent kicker, this is a hand worth defending but marginal to 3-bet out of position. I prefer to call 250 (total) to keep the pot manageable OOP, realize equity on many flops, and avoid getting squeezed by the big blind. If Flash (BB) 3-bets or Claude 4-bets I will fold unless I hit the flop or get a strong read. Re-evaluate on the flop and proceed aggressively if I pair the Ace or pick up favorable draws.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4h 6s, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude opened with a raise from the button and Minni called from the small blind. Although the cost to call is only 150 chips to win a pot of 600 (4:1 odds), this hand has very poor equity and playability post-flop, especially when out of position against two players. Given my current chip lead, there is no reason to speculate with garbage hands in this spot.

Check. I defended preflop with A♥9♣ and the flop (8♠ 2♠ 7♦) is very dry — I only have Ace-high and no spade. Out of position I want to control the pot and gather information rather than lead with a marginal hand. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, lets me call small/medium continuation bets to realize equity, and avoids bloating the pot versus Claude's likely c-betting range. Plan: call small c-bets (~≤1/2 pot ≈300) and fold to large overbets unless I improve; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and bet for value/protection only if I pair the Ace or pick up a strong draw. For now the safest + most +EV line is to check.

Missed the flop completely with Q-high. Board is 8s 2s 7d - dry but with a flush draw present. Minni checked to me showing weakness. As the preflop aggressor with position, this is a standard c-bet spot. Betting around 1/3 pot to maintain pressure and potentially take it down.

Claude's flop bet is small (200 into 800). Calling 200 gives me 20% break-even equity (200/(800+200)=20%), which is an easy price with A♥9♣ — I block many A-high combos and I beat a lot of his bluff/air and weak broadway hands. The board is dry and I'm out of position, so raising as a bluff is thin with no spade backdoor and would bloat the pot unnecessarily. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to a large turn-sized raise/overbet if I don't improve, and become aggressive only if I pair the Ace or pick up a clear draw or actionable read.

Board on the turn: 8♠ 2♠ 7♦ 2♦. I hold A♥9♣ — still only Ace-high with no spade draw and the board just paired a deuce. Claude opened BTN and made a small flop c-bet (200 into ~800), a line consistent with a wide BTN c-betting range that includes many Ax, missed hands, and some small pairs or a deuce. I did not improve on the turn and I am out of position, so betting here is thin EV: it will often get called by hands that already beat me (pairs) and will fold the worse Ax that I’d like value from. Raising as a bluff is unattractive with no backdoor equity. I will control the pot and see the river — check now, plan to value-bet/raise if I pair the Ace or otherwise re-evaluate on the river; otherwise fold to large river aggression and call only reasonable sizings.

Minni called my flop c-bet, showing some interest in the pot. The turn 2d paired the board but doesn't improve my Q-high. I have no showdown value and limited bluffing equity against a player who called the flop. Firing a second barrel here with complete air against someone who showed resistance is likely burning chips. Better to check back, take the free card, and potentially improve on the river or make a decision based on Minni's action.

I have A♥9♣ on 8♠ 2♠ 7♦ 2♦ 3♦. I have exactly Ace-high — no diamond, no backdoor — so I only beat pure air/missed broadways and the occasional weaker Kx/Qx. Claude’s line (BTN open, small flop c-bet, check turn) is consistent with a wide range but includes many hands that currently beat me: pocket pairs (2x, 7x, 8x), small pairs, and any two-diamond combo that now makes a flush. Betting the river is thin: I will rarely get value from worse hands and will get called by almost every hand that actually beats me. As a bluff I have limited fold equity because a lot of Claude’s continuing range will simply call. With pot control and showdown value prioritized, the +EV line is to check and see a showdown — force opponents to bluff to win. Plan if faced with a river bet after my check: fold to large overbets; consider a call only to small/medium bets if I assign significant bluff frequency. For now: check.

River completed with 3d, bringing three diamonds on board. I have Q-high with no pair, no flush, nothing. Minni checked again. My Q-high actually has some showdown value here - it beats busted spade flush draws and complete air. Betting would be a pure bluff that only gets called by better hands. Checking back to see if my Q-high is good against missed draws is the best play.

6-handed UTG with J8 suited is a playable open at some frequency: it has good equity realization (suited, connected), can flop strong draws, and benefits from taking initiative rather than limping and facing isolation. With ~70bb effective vs most stacks, we can open and comfortably fold to large 3-bets (especially from the BTN/CO/SB big stacks), while still having postflop playability when called by blinds or a single position.

Hand #59, pre-flop, holding 4d2s, which is one of the worst possible starting hands: uncoordinated, offsuit, low cards with poor playability and low equity against any UTG raising range. FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 (2.5x BB), indicating a strong range (likely 15-20% top hands like pairs, suited broadways, Ax). I'm in middle position with 39.5BB stack, pot 400, calling 250 for ~3.2:1 implied odds needed, but this hand has no post-flop potential—no straight/flush draws, dominated vs any pair or better. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to speculate against UTG strength. Long-term EV positive to muck trash.

AQs is a premium hand in the cutoff facing an UTG open of 250. This is a clear 3-bet spot - I have position on the raiser and AQs plays very well as a value 3-bet. Standard sizing would be around 3x the open, so 750.

Facing an UTG open (FiveTwo) and a CO 3-bet to 750. Qc8h is a weak, often-dominated holding vs a 3-bet range and has poor postflop playability, especially multiway or against a likely strong 3-bettor. A small 4-bet to 1250 wouldn’t be credible and a larger 4-bet shove is far too thin with this hand. With a healthy 114bb stack it's better to preserve chips for a clearer spot on the button — fold here.

I am holding Jack-Nine offsuit in the small blind. There has been an open raise from FiveTwo (UTG) and a 3-bet from Claude (Cutoff). J9o is a weak hand that is significantly behind the ranges expected for these positions and actions. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against potentially two opponents with stronger holdings. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am facing a UTG open to 2.5bb and a Cutoff 3-bet to 7.5bb. My hand, Ks6s, is easily dominated by the ranges involved in this action (e.g., AK, KQ, stronger flush draws, overpairs). Additionally, the original raiser (FiveTwo) is still left to act and could 4-bet. Calling 650 chips (6.5bb) cold out of position is a losing play given the reverse implied odds and the strength demonstrated by my opponents. I should preserve my stack of 55bb for better spots.

We opened UTG and face a CO 3-bet to 750. Although J8s has reasonable raw equity and the price (500 to win 1650) is tempting, we’ll be out of position versus a stronger, more condensed range, and J8s is often dominated (AJ/KJ/QJ, ATs+, etc.) with poor equity realization at ~65–70bb effective. This is not a good candidate to 4-bet bluff either, and defending here creates too many tough postflop spots for the stack depth. Best is to preserve stack and fold UTG open vs this 3-bet.

76o is a very weak offsuit hand from UTG in 6-handed play. Standard tight opening range doesn't include this; it's dominated often and plays poorly postflop multiway. Pot has blinds (150 total), to call 100 suggests possible early limp but irrelevant—clear fold to preserve stack (39.5BB effective) for premium spots in tournament with 90 hands left.
J6s is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. Not strong enough to open-raise profitably. Easy fold.

T5o is a marginal, easily-dominated hand. With UTG and MP folded I’m in the cutoff, but there are three players left to act (button + SB + BB) and both the button and blinds have shown they can defend/3-bet often. Raising to try and steal invites a 3-bet from deeper/aggressive stacks and calling just to limp multiway leaves me out of position with a weak hand. With a deep but finite stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a much better spot — fold preflop.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand. Since the action has folded to me, I am in a prime position to open for a raise to steal the blinds or build a pot with a very strong range. Standard opening size for me has been 250-300; I'll choose 300 here to put pressure on Pro and FiveTwo in the blinds.

I hold 2h 3c in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. Facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from Flash on the Button, I am out of position with a garbage hand. The pot odds do not justify calling with such low equity and playability. Folding is the only standard and correct play here to preserve my stack of 54.5bb.

Button open looks like a wide steal spot, and A4o is a good 3-bet bluff candidate because it blocks many of Flash’s strongest continues (Ax) while still having playable equity when called (wheel/ace-high potential). Calling is fine, but we’ll be out of position and give Flash a lot of profitable c-bet opportunities; applying pressure preflop should win the pot outright often and helps prevent Flash from freely opening. I’ll use a standard BB vs BTN 3-bet sizing that discourages a call but doesn’t overcommit my stack.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a very strong hand in this position. After my initial raise to 300, the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has 3-bet to 950. While I could 4-bet here, calling is a very solid option that allows me to play a pot in position with a strong range. 4-betting might force out his bluffs and leave me isolated against only better hands like AK or pocket pairs JJ+. By calling, I maintain flexibility and can evaluate the flop. FiveTwo has a stack of about 58 big blinds, so I don't need to rush to get all the money in pre-flop with AQo.

This is a 3-bet pot BB vs BTN on an AQT two-spade flop. As the preflop aggressor I retain a strong range advantage (lots of Ax/strong broadways), and Ac4s is top pair that benefits from betting for thin value and protection versus Flash’s continuing range (worse Ax like A2–AJ, Qx/Tx, Jx, and spade draws). I don’t want to polarize or bloat the pot with a weak kicker at ~SPR 3, so I’ll use a small c-bet that keeps worse hands in while denying equity to overcards/draws. If I face a big raise, I can comfortably fold this kicker.

I have top two pair (Aces and Queens) on a highly coordinated board of Ts-Qs-Ad. This is a very strong hand, but it is also vulnerable given the abundance of straight (KJ, J9, Kx) and flush draws (two spades on board). FiveTwo's lead of 650 into a pot of 1950 is quite small (about 1/3 pot), which could represent a range of hands from AK/AJ to semi-bluffs or draws. While FiveTwo could theoretically have a straight with KJ or a set of Tens, my hand is too strong to simply call on such a wet texture. I will raise to 1700 to extract value from one-pair hands like AK/AJ and to charge flush and straight draws. This also helps define FiveTwo's range: if he shoves or reraises, I have a difficult decision but can better assess if he has the nuts.

Flop is AQT with two spades in a 3-bet pot (BB vs BTN). My small c-bet got raised; versus this line Flash’s range is heavily weighted to hands that have me in very bad shape: AQ/AT (domination), sets (QQ/TT), and strong draws that also have good equity (KJ, spade combos). With Ac4s specifically I have a weak kicker and no spade, so I don’t block his best semi-bluffs and I’m rarely ahead when he’s raising for value.

The big issue is stack-to-pot: if I call 1050, the pot becomes huge and SPR drops below 1, meaning I’ll often face a turn jam and be forced to stack off with a marginal top pair. That’s not a profitable plan against a competent/active button who can raise this board with strong value and high-equity bluffs. Best play is to fold now and preserve the remaining stack for better spots rather than “call and pray” into a near-commitment situation.

A6o from UTG is a marginal hand. In early position with 5 players to act behind me, this hand is easily dominated by better aces and doesn't play well multiway. The disciplined play is to fold here rather than risk building a pot out of position with a weak ace.

3♥7♦ is one of the weakest offsuit holdings — virtually always dominated and has very poor post‑flop playability out of position. There’s no raise in front of me and the call is 100 into a 150 pot, which requires roughly 40% equity to justify a call; 3‑7o doesn’t come close against typical calling/defending ranges and will be crushed by any raise. With multiple players to act behind (Button, SB, BB) who can defend or 3‑bet and with deep stacks at the table, neither limping nor a light open is worth the post‑flop headaches and potential multiway trouble. Preserve stack and wait for a real hand — fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Four offsuit. While I have a large stack and have been active recently (winning Hand #60), this hand is too weak to open from this position, especially with three players behind me who have relatively short stacks (38-54 BBs) and might look to shove or 3-bet. I will wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with A5o. The action has folded to me. With approximately 54 big blinds, this is a standard spot to raise and attempt to steal the blinds. A5o blocks opponents' Aces and has decent equity if called, plus I will have position post-flop. The standard open size at this table has been around 2.5x, so I will raise to 250.

We’re in the small blind facing a competent-looking Button open to 2.5bb with 8♠2♣ offsuit. This hand is far too weak to defend out of position: it realizes equity poorly, makes dominated pairs, and has almost no suited/connected playability. A 3-bet bluff is also unnecessary here given the hand’s lack of blockers and our decent 50bb stack—better to preserve chips for higher-EV spots. Folding and surrendering the small blind is the best option.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 after SB folds. My hand is 5c8c, suited connectors with good playability. Button opens are wide (likely 35-50% range including many speculative hands). Pot odds are excellent: calling an additional 150 into a 400 pot gives about 3.67:1 odds (need ~21% equity). 58s has strong equity (~38-42%) against a typical button opening range and great implied odds for hitting straights, two-pair, or flush draws postflop. Folding is too tight from BB; 3-betting is suboptimal as it's not strong enough for value and marginal as a bluff OOP with 38BB stack. Calling to realize equity and play multiway potential if others wake up, but likely heads-up.

OOP first to act heads-up vs button raiser on a drawy flop (3c4s7h) with 5c8c: strong club flush draw (10 club outs), gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs), totaling ~28% non-overlapping equity plus 8 as a live overcard for equity realization. Button range is wide postflop (Ax, broadway, suited connectors, pairs), many of which have showdown value or weaker draws we dominate. Leading (donk) risks folding out better equity realizations and is suboptimal OOP; checking induces bluffs/bets from their range (~60-70% c-bet freq expected), allows check-call/call down profitably given pot odds/implied, or check-raise as semi-bluff if oversized bet. Pot control if they check back. With 37BB effective, preserve stack for better spots while realizing equity multi-street.

The flop is 4s 7h 3c, a low rainbow board that connects better with the Big Blind's defending range (which includes sets, two pairs, and connected low cards) than my high-card heavy opening range. I hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw. Checking back is the prudent play here for several reasons: 1) It allows me to realize my equity (seeing the turn) for free without risking a check-raise. 2) My hand has some showdown value that benefits from pot control. 3) I can delay a bluff or value bet to later streets depending on the runout. Aggression here provides little value as better hands (pairs) will likely call and worse hands (air) might fold but have little equity against me anyway.

Turned a strong 9-high flush on a board with two clubs (3c9c), holding 5c8c. This is a very strong hand, likely best or close to best given Pro's flop check-back on a drawy 3c4s7h flop, suggesting a weak range: Ax, broadway, small-mid pairs, missed draws, or weak clubs/Ace-high flush draws we dominate. Board has straight draw potential (e.g., 56 for wheel/straight) but limited; no obvious pairs. OOP first to act, betting extracts value from their worse hands (top pair, two-pair unlikely, smaller flushes/sets rare), protects against river scares (e.g., higher flush, straight), and denies equity to draws (spades, 56). Half-pot bet ~275 too small, induces raises only from nuts; 2/3 pot (350) sizes well for value/call-down, represents strength without overbetting 37BB stack. Checking risks free card/check-raise or pot control issues OOP. Pro's button range wide but checked flop caps strength; time to get value multi-street.

I hold Ace-high with a double gutshot straight draw (outs: 2, 6) on a 4-7-3-9 board. The 2 completes the wheel (A-2-3-4-5) and the 6 completes the 3-4-5-6-7 straight. This gives me 8 outs, roughly 16-17% equity, plus my Ace might be live as an overcard. The opponent's lead on the turn after a checked-through flop is consistent with a range of pairs or draws trying to pick up the pot. Given the pot odds of 28% and the implied odds if I hit my disguised straight, calling is the most profitable play in position. Raising is unnecessary and folding relinquishes too much equity.

River pairs the 9c with 9d, bricking most draws and not completing obvious straights on this disconnected 3c4s7h9c9d board. Holding the 9-high flush (9c8c5c3c), this is very likely the best hand given Pro's weak history: checked flop on drawy board, checked turn, and called my 350 turn bet after completing my flush. Their range is capped at mid-pairs (77,44,33 unlikely given sizing), overcards, weak Ax/9x, or dominated club draws/smaller flushes. No full houses likely (99 unlikely preflop OOP check), higher flushes rare as we'd have noticed villain tendencies. OOP first to act into 1250 pot with 3350 stack, value betting extracts from worse pairs (7x,4x,9x), two-pair (97,94 etc.), and any stubborn Ax. Checking risks them checking back weak hands or bluffing, but betting polarizes correctly and denies equity. 2/3 pot (~833) sizes well for value/call-down without overbetting, getting called by top of their range ~20-30% freq profitably. Larger risks folds, smaller underrepresents.

I hold Ace-high (As5h) on a board of 4s 7h 3c 9c 9d. My opponent, the Big Blind, defended pre-flop, checked the flop, led the turn, and is now firing a substantial bet (850 into 1250) on the river. The board runout paired the turn card (9) on the river. This line (Lead Turn, Bet River) is typically strong, representing trips (9x) or a straight (56), both of which are well within the Big Blind's defending range. While there are missed draws (flush draws, 58, 68), my hand beats nothing but pure bluffs. I hold a 5, which blocks the straight (56) but also blocks some straight draw bluffs (58). Given the large bet size and the polarized nature of the opponent's line, calling with just Ace-high requires a very high frequency of bluffs which is not standard. Preserving my stack of roughly 48 big blinds is prioritized over a marginal hero call.

8♠5♦ is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand and I’m UTG in a 6-max game — too early to open with junk. There’s a full ring of players to act (including big stacks/likely 3-bettors like Flash and Claude), so raising or even limping invites isolation or multiway pots where my hand has very poor equity. I have a deep, healthy stack (≈114bb) so there’s no need to gamble from early position; I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position or with stronger cards.

I am holding Ace-King of hearts, one of the strongest starting hands in poker. I am in middle position and have the largest stack at the table. My goal is to build a pot with this premium hand and ideally thin the field. A standard open-raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to exert pressure and extract value.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo). The player in Middle Position (Flash), who is the chip leader, has opened with a raise to 300 (3 big blinds). In a 6-handed game, facing an open raise from MP, ATo is a marginal hand that is often dominated by the opener's range (which likely includes AK, AQ, AJ, and pairs). Calling creates a difficult situation where I might be squeezed by players behind or play a bloated pot with a hand that has reverse implied odds. 3-betting is an option but risky against a deep-stacked opener. Folding is the standard and most prudent play to preserve my 48bb stack for better spots.

Flash opened to 3bb from middle position into two blinds. With T4s (Td4d) we have very weak high-card value, poor playability versus a relatively strong opening range, and no useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. Even on the button, calling would put us in many dominated, low-equity spots postflop at ~45–50bb effective. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

5h8h is a marginal speculative hand suited connectors. Preflop, facing a raise to 300 from Flash (aggressive player per history) after UTG fold, in SB OOP. Pot odds ~2:1 (250 into 700), but postflop OOP vs raiser limits playability. Hand performs better multiway/IP or vs limpers. Folding preserves stack for better spots; implied odds insufficient against deep-stacked aggressive opponent who often c-bets.

AJo is a premium hand in the BB facing a single MP raise. Flash raised to 300 and everyone folded. I have a strong hand that plays well as a 3-bet for value. Calling is also reasonable but 3-betting allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win it preflop or build a bigger pot when ahead. I'll 3-bet to around 900.

I started the action with a standard open to 300 with Ace-King suited, a premium hand. Claude in the Big Blind has 3-bet to 900. Given our stack depths and my positional advantage, I will 4-bet to 2400. This exerts pressure on Claude's 3-betting range, allows me to take control of the pot, and builds value for my strong holding. If Claude continues, I have a powerful hand that plays excellently post-flop in position.

Flash 4-bet to 2400 after my 3-bet. This is a strong move that typically represents a premium range (QQ+, AK, sometimes JJ/AQs). AJo plays poorly against this range - I'm dominated by AK/AQ and crushed by big pairs. Calling 1500 more out of position with a hand that will often be dominated is a losing play. While I've invested 900, that's a sunk cost. Folding is the disciplined decision here.

K3o is a very weak hand to open from Under the Gun. Even with a substantial chip lead, it's important to maintain a disciplined opening range to avoid being exploited or bleeding chips with marginal hands out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in Middle Position with Pocket Sevens (7s 7d). The UTG player has folded. My stack is roughly 48.5 BB, which is a comfortable depth for standard play. Pocket Sevens is a strong enough hand to open-raise from this position to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. Limping is weak and invites multi-way action which devalues my hand. Folding is too tight. A standard open size of 2.5 BB (250 chips) is appropriate to balance my range and limit risk if 3-bet.

Pro’s MP open to 2.5x puts us up against a range that’s still relatively strong. Q7s is too loose here: it’s frequently dominated when we make top pair, and with ~46bb effective we don’t have great implied odds to justify chasing flushes/weak pairs. 3-betting would create a low SPR pot where our hand performs poorly when called, and flat-calling also risks getting squeezed by the Button/blinds. Best is to pass and preserve stack for better CO/Button spots.

Preflop on the button with 5c2c, a very weak suited connector. Pro raised to 250 from middle position after UTG fold, indicating strength. 52s plays poorly postflop, especially multiway with blinds to act behind. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament; wait for premium spots to build stack.
T2o is a very weak hand. Out of position against a middle position raise, this hand plays poorly and will rarely connect well with the board. Not worth calling 200 more with such a marginal holding.

Fold. Holding J♥4♣ in the big blind facing a standard MP open to 250 (pot 400, 150 to call). Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires ~27% equity (150/(400+150) ≈ 27%), and J4o has poor playability and is frequently dominated by the raiser’s likely range. I’d be out of position postflop and would face tough decisions vs c-bets. 3-betting as a bluff is unattractive with this hand and these effective stacks. Preserve the healthy stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG (Under the Gun) position with King-Jack offsuit (KJo). In a 6-max game, this hand is at the very bottom of an opening range or, more commonly, a standard fold from early position. The primary risks are reverse implied odds (being dominated by AK, KQ, AJ) and playing out of position against 5 opponents, including deep-stacked aggressive players who have demonstrated 3-betting capabilities. With a comfortable stack of 50 big blinds, there is no need to force marginal spots. Folding preserves chips for better hands and positional advantages.

Qs5h is a weak offsuit queen that plays poorly multiway and is often dominated when we make a queen. From middle position in a 6-max setup, opening this hand is too loose, and limping invites isolation from the bigger stacks in the blinds (especially Flash) and from late position. With ~50bb effective, we preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Qh5d is a trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, dominated by any queen or better. Position is cutoff, which is strong, but this hand has poor playability postflop, low equity against calling ranges from button/SB/BB, and doesn't warrant a limp or raise. Early folds from UTG/MP suggest no strong hands there, but limping invites multiway pots where this hand struggles. With 45BB stack in a tournament, fold to preserve chips for premium spots. Opponents are deep/aggressive but no need to get involved here.
On the button with Q6 suited, everyone folded to me. This is a standard button steal spot. Q6s has decent playability with flush potential if called. Raising to 250 to steal the blinds.

A♥T♠ is strong enough to defend the small blind vs a BTN open but it's borderline as a 3-bet candidate out of position. Claude's raise to 250 is a standard BTN open (pot 400, I must add 200). With deep stacks and a big blind behind who can squeeze, I don't want to bloat the pot or face isolation OOP with a marginal kicker. Calling preserves pot control, realizes equity, and keeps Claude's range wide so I can play flops that favor my Ace. Pot odds are attractive (call 200 into 400 -> win 600), and I can fold to heavy aggression post-flop or continue when the board is favorable. I'll flat-call and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 4d. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (150 to win a 600 pot), 5-4 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position in a three-way pot. I have a substantial chip lead and prefer to wait for better situations rather than defending with marginal holdings that are easily dominated or difficult to play post-flop.

Check. I have Ace-high (A♥T♠) on 9♠-3♥-J♥ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart draw. I’m out of position vs Claude’s BTN open, so I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Checking keeps his range wide and lets me call small c-bets (~≤1/3 pot ≈200) to see a turn, while folding to heavy aggression unless I improve. If he checks back I take a free turn to re-evaluate.

Flopped nothing - just queen high. Board is 9s 3h Jh with a heart flush draw possible. I raised preflop so a small c-bet is standard here to apply pressure. Minni checked, suggesting weakness. A ~40% pot c-bet should fold out hands that missed this board.

Call 250. On 9♠-3♥-J♥ my A♥T♠ is only Ace‑high with a very weak backdoor heart possibility — not strong enough to bluff‑raise out of position. Claude’s bet (250 into 850) is small, and the pot odds (~250 to win ≈1100, ~22–23%) make a call cheap to see a turn. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot controllable OOP, and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to large turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear draw, or decide to raise for value if I improve.

Check. I’m out of position vs Claude after his BTN open and flop c‑bet; I have A♥T♠ — no pair but I hold the A♥ (a blocker) and after the 6♥ on the turn I have a one-card nut‑flush draw (9 hearts left) plus three remaining aces as pair outs → roughly 12 outs (~26% to improve on the river). With no bet to me, betting OOP with only Ace‑high is thin: I have little fold equity and will be called by lots of worse hands while still losing to any pair/set. Checking keeps the pot (1,100) under control, lets me realize equity and choose on the river. Plan: check the river if he checks back; if he bets, call small/medium bets (break‑even call ≈600 chips given ~26% equity) and fold to large river aggression unless I pair or hit the heart.

I hit bottom pair with the 6 on the turn, but the board now has three hearts (3h, Jh, 6h). This is a dangerous board - any heart gives Minni a flush if they're on a draw, and they could already have one. My bottom pair has some showdown value but I don't want to build a big pot here. Checking to control pot size and see a cheap showdown is the prudent play.

The river 8♥ completes a heart on the board and with A♥T♠ I now have the nut (ace‑high) flush. Claude’s line (BTN open, small C‑bet on the flop, check on the turn) is wide — he can have Jx/9x/pocket pairs, missed bluffs, and some heart combos that now make inferior flushes (K♥/Q♥/T♥) or strong one‑pair hands that will still call a value bet. I should extract value rather than check and give a free showdown. A moderately large value bet (~2/3 pot) balances getting called by worse hands (pairs, second‑best flushes, some K/Q hearts, Jx) without folding out too many callers; it also prices calling bluffs out of the river. Pot = 1100, so I’ll bet 750 into it. If Claude raises big I’m facing the nut — I can call (or shove) comfortably. Action: bet 750.

Four hearts on board (3h, Jh, 6h, 8h) and I have Qd6d - no heart at all. My bottom pair is essentially worthless here. Any single heart in Minni's hand makes a flush, which is extremely likely given they called both preflop and the flop c-bet on a board with two hearts. Paying 750 to see a likely flush is burning chips. Easy fold.

6-handed and 50bb deep, A3s is a reasonable UTG open in a modern range: it has nut-flush potential, wheel-straight equity, and strong blocker value (reduces opponents’ premium Ax/AA combos). Limping invites multiway play where dominated Ax performs poorly; raising lets us win the blinds/antes immediately or take initiative postflop with position disadvantage. I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing to pressure the blinds without bloating the pot too much versus stacks that can 3-bet.

A9o is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands but likely has a strong range here (top pairs, broadways, strong aces). My stack is 45.5BB, sufficient to wait for better spots with 85 hands remaining. Calling risks domination postflop (e.g., vs AQo+, AK) and poor playability multiway potentially. Raising is a bluff that's easily countered by stacks behind. Folding preserves stack and avoids tough decisions.
72 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a UTG raise, this is an easy fold. No reason to get involved with such a weak holding.

Facing an UTG open to 250 on the button with A♥T♦ and a 11.9k stack (~119BB), this is a solid 3‑bet spot. I want to leverage position and fold equity to isolate the opener and take the lead postflop against a likely wide-ish UTG opening range. I will 3‑bet to 1000 (≈4x the open) — big enough to discourage loose callers and build a pot when I have a high-card, playable hand in position. Plan: if I get called, play aggressively in position (C‑bet into dry boards, control size on coordinated boards); if I face a large 4‑bet shove from the remaining players (shorter stacks), ATo is usually behind and I will fold to preserve my deep stack.

I am holding 2d 6c in the small blind, which is an exceptionally weak hand. There has been an opening raise from FiveTwo (UTG) and a significant 3-bet from Minni on the button. I am out of position and have zero incentive to involve myself in this pot with such low equity. Folding is the only viable strategic move here.

I am in the Big Blind with As6s. The action began with a UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo, followed by a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni on the Button. This is a strong line; a Button 3-bet against a UTG open typically indicates a strong range (premiums, strong broadways). Additionally, the 3-bet size is large (4x). I have 4900 chips, so calling 900 represents nearly 20% of my stack. Furthermore, the UTG player is still left to act behind me and could 4-bet shove. A6s is easily dominated by hands like AK, AQ, or high pairs. Out of position and facing significant strength, I cannot profitably continue.

We opened UTG with A3s and got a large Button 3-bet to 1000. Being out of position with a low wheel Ace, our hand is often dominated when we make top pair, and the 4x sizing worsens our price (750 to win 1400 ≈ 35% immediate) while our equity realization OOP in a 3-bet pot is poor. A 4-bet jam as a bluff is high-variance and likely gets called by a fairly strong range versus an UTG shove. In a 50bb tournament spot, this is a marginal/losing defend, so we conserve stack and move on.

K5h is a marginal suited hand at best, but from UTG in a 6-handed game, it plays poorly post-flop with reverse position on multiple players. Even with suited potential for flush draws or straight draws, the king high is vulnerable to domination by better kings (KQ, KQ suited, etc.), and 5 is a poor kicker. Stacks are around 45BB for me, others deeper; no need to speculate early with a weak range. Pot is small (150), to-call 100 (likely an early raise or limp-raise), but folding preserves stack for 150-hand tournament where position and strength matter more. Standard UTG fold to build stack long-term.
Q5o is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players left to act behind me. This hand plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to open or limp. Standard fold.

Cutoff, action folded to me. I hold Q♠ T♥ (QTo) with a healthy stack (~123 BB) and both blinds to act. QTo is a standard open from the CO — good chance to steal the blinds and play post‑flop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind): large enough to gain fold equity and discourage light squeezes from the button, but a standard size that keeps weaker callers and post‑flop maneuvering manageable. Plan: if called, use position to c‑bet favorable flops and extract value; on dry boards apply pressure, on highly connected/ace/K boards proceed cautiously and fold to heavy resistance when missed. If I face a substantial 3‑bet (large re‑raise or shove), I’ll fold QTo; I’ll only continue vs small 3‑bets from overly loose players or when pot odds and positional post‑flop play justify it.

I am on the button with King-Eight suited, a solid speculative hand. Minni raised from the cutoff to 300. Given my position and significant chip lead, calling is a standard play here to see a flop. This allows me to use my positional advantage throughout the rest of the hand without committing too many chips pre-flop against a potentially wide range from the cutoff. A 3-bet is possible but might isolate me against stronger hands or invite shoves from the short stacks in the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 9d, a very weak off-suit hand. There has been a raise to 300 by Minni in the Cutoff and a call by Flash on the Button. Even with pot odds of roughly 4:1 if I call, playing 94o out of position in a multi-way pot is extremely -EV. I should fold and preserve my stack of ~48BB for better opportunities.

We’re getting a decent price (200 to win 750), but this is a multiway spot (CO open + BTN flat) and we’ll be out of position with an offsuit, easily dominated hand. J7o has poor playability, makes a lot of second-best top pairs, and doesn’t realize equity well multiway. A squeeze bluff would be high-variance against two players (including the chip leader on the button) and risks running into strong ranges that continue. Best EV is to release and preserve the stack for better defend/squeeze candidates.

I have top pair (T) with a strong Q kicker (QsTh) on a fairly dry 7c‑Td‑6h board. I was the preflop aggressor and we’re heads‑up vs the button caller, so a continuation bet is standard: it extracts value from worse tens, pocket pairs and coaxes folds from equity hands (small pairs, flush/straight draws). I’ll size about half‑pot to get value while denying too much equity to draws and keeping combos that will call (Kx, Qx, weaker Tx, small pairs) in range. If I get called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (proceed to value‑bet safe cards, be cautious to A/K/7/6/8/9 or heavy aggression). If I face a large check‑raise I’ll assume a stronger made hand (sets/two‑pair) and fold to big raises; I may call small raises to navigate the turn given position dynamics.

On a board of 7c-Td-6h, I currently hold King-high with an inside straight draw (needing a 9 for a ten-high straight). Minni, the pre-flop raiser, has led out for 400 into a 750 pot, which is a significant continuation bet. While I have position and a massive chip lead, the board texture is quite coordinated and likely connects with a wide variety of hands in Minni's opening range from the cutoff, including pocket pairs, top pair (Tens), or straight draws. My equity with just K-high and a gutshot is relatively low (roughly 16% to hit the straight by the river), and calling a 53% pot bet doesn't offer the right immediate price. Given that Minni is the second-largest stack at the table, I prefer to avoid unnecessary variance and fold a hand with limited potential on this specific texture.
Q5o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand has poor playability and I'm in the worst position at the table. Easy fold.

QcTc (suited) in middle position is a playable, value-plus hand with good postflop playability (flush + straight + two‑pair potential). There is no raise yet and the pot is just the blinds (150), so limp would invite multiway action and reduce fold equity. A min-raise to 200 is too small — I want enough fold equity to isolate and take initiative, but I don’t need to bloat the pot out of position. I open to 3x the BB (300 total). If called I can play postflop for position and implied odds; if I face a significant 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies (often folding to large, polarized 3‑bets, or calling small/medium 3‑bets).

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin. Minni has opened from Middle Position with a 3x raise. My hand, King-Four offsuit (K4o), is well below the standard defense range for a raise from this position, even as the chip leader. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position or against a range that likely dominates my King. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack and wait for higher equity opportunities.

I am on the Button with Qs 4d. The action has folded to Minni in Middle Position who raises to 300 (3bb). Q4o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by an opening range from MP. Despite having position, the hand quality is too poor to justify a call or a bluff 3-bet, especially against a deep-stacked opponent. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my 48bb stack for better opportunities.

Td7o is too weak to defend from the small blind versus a middle-position open, especially being out of position to Minni and with Elon still able to squeeze from the big blind. It has poor playability and is dominated when we make top pair, and it’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate (no blockers, low equity when called). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.
