Pocket 9s is a strong premium hand preflop, well ahead of Minni's middle position opening range, which includes many broadway hands, suited connectors, and Ax that we dominate. With folds to her, this is an isolation spot. Calling OOP is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots and difficult postflop play; 3-betting takes initiative, builds the pot when ahead, and applies pressure. Sizing to 900 (3x her raise) is standard, leaving fold equity and good SPR if called. My stack of 4450 supports this without overcommitting. Minni's history shows aggression, but 99 crushes her likely calling range.

I opened to 300 with QcTc (good playability: flush/straight/two‑pair potential). Elon 3‑bet to 900 from the big blind. QcTc has reasonable equity vs a 3‑bet range and calling keeps pot controllable while allowing me to realize post‑flop equity. Calling 600 more (900 total committed) requires ~32% equity (600/(1250+600) ≈ 32.4%), which QcTc will generally have versus a mixed/aggressive 3‑bet range. I don’t want to 4‑bet polarized or fold away significant equity — a 4‑bet would either bloat the pot out of position or force a tough decision if he jams. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with top pair/strong draws or favourable runouts, and give up to heavy polarized aggression or a shove that I don’t have the equity to call.

Flopped top set of 9s on a dry board (2d 6h 9s) with no strong draws or coordinated straight possibilities. My hand is way ahead of Minni's 3-bet calling range, which includes overpairs (TT-88 crushed), weaker pairs, broadways, and some suited connectors Ax. As first to act OOP postflop, betting is mandatory for value from weaker hands (top pair, two pair) and protection against overcards or runner-runner draws. Pot is 1850; betting ~70% pot (1300) builds the pot while leaving a good SPR (~0.75 behind) if she raises, allowing me to stack off profitably. Minni's history shows she calls bets but folds to pressure sometimes; this size extracts max value without overbetting early. Stack of 3650 supports this aggression to maximize EV in tournament.

Board is dry (2♦-6♥-9♠). With Q♣T♣ I have two overcards and a backdoor club flush — no pair but reasonable showdown equity. Elon was the preflop 3‑better so his range is weighted to strong hands, but he c‑bet a relatively small 1,300 into a 3,150 pot. Calling 1,300 gives ~29% pot odds (1300/(3150+1300) ≈ 29%) and my hand has roughly mid/high‑20s% equity to improve by the river (≈24% to hit a Q/T plus a few % backdoor/runner possibilities), so a call is marginally +EV. Raising to 2,600 would apply pressure but risks committing a lot versus a 3‑bettor who can shove or call with many hands that dominate me. Plan: call 1,300 to see the turn and re‑evaluate — press when I pair or pick up clear equity; fold to heavy polarized aggression or a shove that I don’t have the equity to call.

Turn brings the 10s, completing spade flush draws and some straight draws (e.g., 78). Board: 2d6h9sTs remains favorable for my top set of 9s, crushing Minni's likely range of overpairs (JJ-QQ, KK, AA less likely given preflop call), two-pair combos (e.g., 62, 96 unlikely), or draws like JTs, 8sXs, QJs. Pot is 4450 with my effective stack of 2350 (SPR ~0.5), mandating commitment. Checking risks free cards to draws or check-raises; shoving extracts max value from worse (overpairs, TP/TK) while denying equity to ~20-30% equity draws. Minni's calling station tendencies (e.g., Hands 27,32,47) suggest she calls lighter with medium strength. Tournament stage favors stacking off profitably here to maximize stack growth.

I have QcTc and just paired the Ten on the turn (top pair, decent kicker). Elon 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the flop and now committed a large turn bet/all‑in for 2350. The pot is 6800 so calling 2350 requires ~25.7% equity (2350 / (6800+2350) ≈ 25.7%). Villain’s line is value‑heavy (AA/KK/QQ/JJ and some sets are possible) but also includes a meaningful portion of missed broadway hands (AK/AQ/KQ types) that I currently beat. Versus overpairs/sets my river outs are limited, but versus the missed Ax/Kx/AK portion my equity is large (I only lose to the stronger pairs). A realistic weighting of his 3‑bet/c‑bet/shove range puts my equity roughly in the low‑30% range, which clears the ~25.7% break‑even threshold. With a made top pair and correct pot odds, folding is too tight and raising isn’t an option (he’s effectively all‑in). I call the 2350 more to realize equity and extract value from worse hands/semi‑bluffs.

J♦T♦ in a 6‑handed game is a strong, playable UTG open — suited connector with good equity and postflop maneuverability. Stacks are deep (~82bb), so I want the initiative and fold equity but keep the pot manageable. A standard 3x open to 300 (total) is appropriate here: it pressures the blinds, defines ranges, and preserves room to maneuver postflop. If I face a standard 3‑bet from Button/SB I’ll consider calling to play postflop; versus very large 3‑bets or tight 4‑bets I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack. If called, I plan to continuation‑bet favorable flops and plan lines based on opponent tendencies and flop texture.

Minni opened from UTG to 3x. My hand, 8s 6h, is extremely weak and does not play well against an early position opening range. I am in middle position with several players yet to act behind me. There is no reason to defend or bluff in this spot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff with Kd Jh. Minni opened to 300 (3BB) from UTG. A UTG opening range is typically strong (often containing AK, AQ, KQ, pairs), against which KJo is frequently dominated. Additionally, calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the Button or Blinds. My stack is 48.5BB, so I am not desperate to make marginal plays. Folding is the standard disciplined decision here to preserve chips for better spots.

Minni is opening UTG to 3bb in a 6-max game and her range should be relatively strong, so 8c6c isn’t a great 3-bet candidate at ~47bb effective because we’d be risking too much of our stack and often get forced to fold to a 4-bet. On the Button we have excellent position, a suited connector with good playability, and a reasonable price (300 to win 450 with implied odds when we make strong draws/straights/two pair). Calling also keeps weaker hands from the blinds in and lets us realize equity in position; if we face a squeeze from SB/BB we can re-evaluate based on sizing and player tendencies.

Preflop in SB with Ah7s facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni (strong range) and a button call from FiveTwo (wider but still decent). Pot is 750, calling 250 more gives good odds (~3:1), but postflop OOP multiway (BB to act) with a marginal suited ace that plays poorly against strong ranges. A7s has some equity but dominated often by better aces, and hard to realize value. No strong blockers to villain's continuing range. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament mid-stage; avoid speculative multiway pots OOP.

I have QTo in the BB facing a min-raise from UTG that Button called. I'm getting 750:200 = 3.75:1 pot odds to close the action. QTo is a playable hand with decent high card strength and straight potential. At these odds, closing the action, this is an easy call.

Completely whiffed this flop with QTo. Ace-high board with no pair, no draw for me. I have two overcards to the 8 and 5, but the Ace is problematic since Minni (preflop raiser) likely has an ace in their range. Standard check here - if someone bets, I'm folding.

Flop A♣ 5♣ 8♦ after my UTG open (J♦T♦). This is a multiway pot (BTN + BB) and the board heavily favors callers — lots of Ax and club hands in their ranges. I have two overcards and a very small backdoor diamond draw but no club backdoor and no made hand. Against two opponents on an Ace‑high, two‑club flop, c‑betting will often run into pairs/Ax or club draws and bloats a pot I’m not ahead in. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking. Plan: check back to see a free turn; if faced with a small probe bet I’ll call to see a turn (I have ~6 outs to pair plus backdoor possibilities) but fold to large pressure or if a club hits and I face a meaningful bet. Re‑evaluate on the turn and look to bluff only when fold equity and texture justify it.

Flop checks from BB and the preflop raiser (UTG) cap their ranges and give me a good spot to probe in position. With 8c6c I have a marginal made hand (pair of 8s) that benefits a lot from denying two-overcard equity and from picking up the pot immediately when they’ve whiffed. A small bet also gets thin value from worse pairs/5x and some floats, while keeping the risk low multiway; if I face significant check-raise pressure on this A-high texture, I can comfortably fold.

I have QTo with no pair, no draw on Ac5c8d board. FiveTwo bet 350 into 950. I have zero equity here - no clubs for flush draw, no straight draw, just two overcards but the Ace blocks top pair combos from my range. Easy fold.

I opened UTG with J♦T♦ and checked this A♣5♣8♦ flop. I have no pair and no club backdoor, but I do have two diamonds (a two‑card backdoor flush) and ~6 clean outs to pair (≈24% to hit by the river). FiveTwo’s bet (350 into a ~1300 pot) is small — pot odds are roughly 21% — so a call to see a cheap turn is correct: it lets me realize equity and keep the pot manageable. Plan: call now, reassess on the turn; if I pair (J or T) I will extract value, but I will fold to significant turn aggression or to a bet after a club that completes obvious club draws. I won’t raise here — I lack blockers and raising would bloat the pot when likely behind.

Turn brings 9♣ making the board A♣ 5♣ 8♦ 9♣. I hold J♦ T♦ — I still have no pair but I now have an open‑ended straight draw to a 7 or Q (8 clean outs, ~17.4% to hit the river) and additional pair outs (J/T) that increase raw equity but are often second‑best. The 9♣ is a dangerous card because it brings a three‑club board and significantly increases the chance that FiveTwo’s flop betting range contains a made club flush or club+Ace hands. FiveTwo bet the flop small (350) — a line consistent with clubs/Ax/pairs — so I should not bloat the pot out of position with only drawing equity and no blockers. Decision: check. Plan: keep the pot under control and realize equity; call a small turn bet to see a cheap river (I’ll call sizes up to a reasonable fraction of the pot), but fold to large turn/raise which likely represents a made club flush or top pair. If the river gives me a straight or a safe pair, I will seek value; if it’s another club or an Ace, I’ll be very cautious.

Turn brings the 9c giving us a made flush (A-high via the board, with 9/8/6/5). This is strong versus Minni’s flop check/call range (a lot of Ax, some 8x/5x, and club draws that now often call as worse made hands), so we should value-bet. At the same time, because the board has 3 clubs, a higher flush is possible when she specifically has two clubs (e.g., KcQc/JcTc type suited combos), so I don’t want to polarize too large and get forced into a high-variance stack-off. A medium-small sizing keeps Ax in, denies equity to hands that might peel one street, and lets us comfortably fold to a big check-raise that is heavily weighted to better flushes.

Board: A♣ 5♣ 8♦ 9♣. I hold J♦T♦. FiveTwo’s small turn bet (650 into 2300) gives me ~22% pot‑odds to call. I have an OESD to Q/7 (8 outs) plus J/T pair outs (~6 more) — ~14 raw outs (~30% to improve) though some outs are “dirty” against Ax/club hands. The 9♣ makes a three‑club board so raising OOP with only draws and no club/A blockers would bloat the pot and is likely a losing line; a min‑raise won’t fold many better hands and risks committing chips out of position. Calling is +EV: it realizes equity, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me reassess on the river. Plan: call 650; if I hit a straight I’ll extract value, if I pair I’ll proceed cautiously vs big bets, and if a club/Ace comes and he fires big I’ll likely fold. If he raises now I’ll fold (represents a completed club or strong value).

I have J♦T♦ and the river (J♠) gives me top pair with a ten kicker on a very wet board: A♣ 5♣ 8♦ 9♣ Js. Pot is 2,950 and I am out of position to FiveTwo, who led the flop and turn (small flop bet then a larger turn bet) — a line consistent with Ax, clubs, and some made pairs. This board is dangerous (three clubs and an ace), so I don’t want to bloat the pot, but I do have thin value vs worse one‑pair hands (QJ/KJ, some Jx, 9x/8x) and can also get called by some missed draws turned into bluff-catchers. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) extracts from those worse hands and keeps the pot manageable if I’m called; checking risks giving a free showdown to some worse hands but also concedes the initiative and might allow a river thin‑value bet from stronger hands. I’ll make a modest value bet of 1,000 to get called by worse and fold out some pure air; if raised I will fold (raise would strongly represent a completed club flush or Ace/strong two‑pair).

We have an A-high flush (A♣-9♣-8♣-6♣-5♣), which is a very strong hand in a single-raised pot, but it’s not the nuts on this 3-club board (she can show up with K♣Q♣/Q♣J♣/J♣T♣ etc). Minni’s 1,000 lead into ~3,950 is a small “blocker/value” sizing that can easily be Ax (including AJ/two pair type hands) trying to set a price because she’s worried about clubs. Given the price (1,000 to win ~4,950 after calling ≈ 20%), folding is too tight. Raising is unattractive because worse hands (most Ax) often fold and the hands that continue are disproportionately better flushes, putting us in a nasty spot with our remaining stack. Best play is to just take the good pot-odds call and realize our showdown value.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a premium starting hand in a 6-max tournament. I will open for a standard 3x big blind raise to build the pot and narrow the field. Given my chip lead, I want to maintain pressure while playing solid ranges from early position.

I am holding 7s 6h in Middle Position facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from the UTG player (Flash). 76 offsuit is a very weak hand to play against an Early Position open, as my equity is low against their range and I lack position on the opener. With a stack of ~48.5BB, I should not be splashing chips on low-probability speculative hands. The standard play is to fold.

Flash is opening UTG in a 6-max game, which should be a relatively tight range. 5♠3♣ offsuit has very poor playability, is frequently dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well versus an UTG range even with position. The pot odds (300 to win ~750) aren’t enough to justify continuing with such a weak hand given reverse implied odds and low ability to apply pressure postflop. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with K6o facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash, who has a large stack and plays aggressively but UTG opens indicate strength. My hand has poor playability, dominated by any Kx better or pairs/Ace-high. Pot is 450, calling 300 gives poor implied odds given the dead money is minimal and postflop SPR low with multiple players behind. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase.

J2o is a very weak hand. Flash raised from UTG which typically indicates a strong range. I'm out of position in the small blind with a hand that has poor playability and will be dominated by most of Flash's UTG range. Easy fold.

Flash opened UTG to 300 — an early-position raise, often a fairly strong range. I have Tc8h: marginal but playable in the big blind facing a 3bb open with 58bb effective. Calling 200 more (300 total commitment) is the right play: the open size gives me decent pot odds (~30% equity required) and implied odds to realize hands and play post‑flop. A 3‑bet bluff OOP is too thin against Flash’s stack and tendencies, and folding surrenders too much dead money (the 100 BB already posted). Plan post‑flop: proceed cautiously — continue with connected/straight/flush draws or good pair boards, fold to heavy aggression on A/K/Q heavy boards, and try to keep pots controlled OOP.

Preflop I called a 3bb UTG open with Tc8h — marginal but fine given pot odds. On 7c Qs Qc I have no pair and only a runner‑runner club flush draw (needs both turn and river, ≈4% equity) — essentially zero showdown value. Flash opened UTG so his range is weighted to strong pairs, Qx, and big broadways; this is a Q‑heavy board that disfavors my hand. I don’t want to bluff OOP into a strong opening range or bloat the pot with air, so the prudent line is pot control and to see a turn card. I’ll check and plan to call small turn bets to realize backdoor equity but fold to meaningful aggression; if he checks back I get a free card to re‑evaluate on any club/ten/8 or a safe blank.

I have flopped trip Queens with an Ace kicker on a very dry board (7c-Qs-Qc). Since the board is so disconnected and I block the Queen, it is unlikely Minni has a strong hand. Checking back is an option to induce bluffs on later streets, but a small continuation bet (approx 1/3 pot) is standard here to extract value from pocket pairs, 7x, or even Ace-high hands that might peel one street. This bet size also protects my checking range for hands that missed the flop. I will bet 225 into the 650 pot.

Board: Qs Qc 7c. I hold Tc8h — no pair, only a runner‑runner club flush possibility (need both turn and river clubs, ~4%); I do have ~6 immediate outs to make a pair by the river (~24%) so combined ~28% to improve to something showable. Flash opened UTG (strong, Q‑heavy/overpair/big broadway range) and now makes a small c‑bet (225 into the 650 pot) — a probe sizing that gives me good pot odds. Calling 225 to win 875 ≈ 25–26% pot odds, so a call is justified to realize equity and see a turn. I won’t bluff‑raise OOP into an early‑position raiser with almost no showdown value; if Flash applies significant pressure on the turn (large raise/shove) I’ll fold. Plan: call the 225, keep the pot controlled, re‑evaluate on the turn (look to continue with a club, a T/8, or a very blank; fold to heavy aggression).

I’m OOP with Tc8h vs Flash’s UTG open and small flop c‑bet. On the turn (Qs Qc 7c 9h) I still have no made hand and only imperfect outs (T/8 to pair, J/6 to complete a straight) — roughly ~25–30% raw chance to improve, but many of those outs are non‑clean against an early‑position raiser. Flash’s line (UTG raise, small c‑bet on a Q‑heavy board) is consistent with strong Qx/overpairs or pot‑control with good hands. There’s no bet to call now, so the clear +EV line is to check and realize my equity rather than bluff into a deep, early‑position range OOP. If Flash now bets a small amount (thin probe), I can call for pot odds to see the river; I will fold to sizable turn aggression (big bet/raise/shove) because my hand has poor showdown value. Checking preserves stack and keeps options for river evaluation.

I have trip Queens with an Ace kicker on a 7c-Qs-Qc-9h board. My hand is extremely strong, essentially the nuts given the pre-flop action (it is very unlikely Minni has 77 or 99 given the check-call on the flop). After Minni checks the turn, I want to continue extracting value from her calling range, which likely includes pocket pairs (88, TT, JJ), 7x hands, or perhaps a straight draw like JT. The 9h doesn't change much of the board texture. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot (650 into 1100) is a good size to keep her in with mediocre hands while building a pot for a potential river shove or value bet.

I’m out of position with Tc8h on Qs Qc 7c 9h vs an UTG opener who has shown a value‑oriented line (small flop c‑bet then a larger turn bet). The turn bet is 650 into a 1750 pot so I need ~27% equity to justify a call (650 / (1750+650) ≈ 27.1%). My raw outs to improve (3 tens + 3 eights + 4 jacks = 10 outs) give ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit the river — below the pot‑odds threshold — and many of those outs are not clean (a Jack can give a villain with Qx a full house; even making a T or 8 often still loses to A/K or Qx). Raising is unattractive: UTG’s range is strong and unlikely to fold, and a raise risks a shove or call from better hands. With poor showdown value and reverse implied odds, folding preserves 5400 chips for better spots.

I am in UTG position with J4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. With a stack of roughly 48.5 big blinds, I have plenty of depth to wait for better spots. Opening from early position requires a strong range, and this hand falls well outside of that. Folding is the standard and correct play.

We’re in middle position with T4o in an unopened pot. This hand is too weak to open-raise profitably from MP (poor playability, dominated by better Tx, and high reverse implied odds), and limping just invites multiway trouble against the blinds and late-position players. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

J4o is a trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, low cards, poor playability post-flop. In cutoff after two folds, the pot is small (150), but opening this risks playing OOP with a weak range against button/Claude who can 3-bet wide, or blinds who defend aggressively based on history. Equity realization is low vs calling ranges. Tournament phase mid-way (hand 70/150), 9100 stack ~91BB, no need to speculate with marginal holdings. Folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV.
On the button with J6o after everyone folds to me. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and only need to get through the blinds. J6o is playable here as a steal. Raising to 2.5x BB.

Facing a button open to 250 from the button with K♣9♦ in the small blind and ~53 BB effective, calling is the most +EV line. The open is small (2.5bb) so defending a wide range from the SB is standard — K9o has reasonable equity and useful blockers. Folding is too tight given the pot odds (calling 200 into a 400 pot), and a 3-bet is thin here (not enough value, mediocre blocker, and likely to face calls/4-bets from deeper stacks). I’ll call to 250, see a flop, and play postflop depending on texture: continue with top pair/strong draws, pot-control or fold to heavy action on dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs 6s, which is a solid hand to defend with given the pot odds. The Button (Claude) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Minni) called, making the current pot 600. I only need to call 150 more to see the flop, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Q6s has sufficient equity against a wide button opening range and a typical small blind calling range to justify seeing a flop, especially since I am closing the action pre-flop.

I called preflop with K♣9♦ to defend a small open; on this flop (2♠ 2♦ 5♠) I have no pair, no spade, just K-high. The board is paired and has two spades — it’s a relatively dry, low-equity spot for bluffing out of position. Betting here would commit chips with very little fold equity (Claude opened wide and Flash defends a lot), and I’m out of position to two opponents, so pot control is the priority. I’ll check to see how the others behave: call small continuation bets to realize equity, fold to large raises, and re-evaluate on the turn for either a bluff or a showdown attempt if checked to me again.

I have a Queen-high flush draw on a paired board (2s 2d 5s). Minni, the small blind, has checked. Claude, the button and original pre-flop raiser, is still to act behind me. Checking is the most strategic move here to see if Claude decides to continuation-bet. This allows me to realize my equity by seeing a turn card or gives me the option to check-call or check-raise depending on Claude's bet size and the table dynamics.

Dry paired board (2-2-5) that rarely connects with calling ranges. Both opponents checked to me showing weakness. This is a perfect c-bet spot - I can represent an overpair or any pocket pair. Small sizing around 1/3 pot should be enough to take it down.

I defended the small blind preflop with K♣9♦ vs a small button open; on the flop (2♠ 2♦ 5♠) I have no pair and no spade draw — just K-high. Claude’s flop bet is tiny (250 into a 1000 pot), which gives ~20% pot odds to call. Raising as a bluff into two opponents out of position is a losing line here and folding would be too tight given the price. Calling keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize my (limited) showdown equity cheaply, and preserves stack flexibility. Plan: call the 250, fold to a substantial raise or aggressive turn barrel, and re-evaluate on the turn if checked to me (possible bluff or showdown attempt).

I am in the Big Blind holding a Queen-high flush draw on a paired board [2s 2d 5s]. After I checked, the Button (Claude) placed a continuation bet of 250 into a 750 pot, and the Small Blind (Minni) called. The pot is now 1250, and it costs me only 250 to call. This gives me 5:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to justify calling with a flush draw (roughly 35% equity to hit by the river, and about 18% to hit on the turn). Given the paired board, there is a slight risk of a full house, but Claude's small sizing and Minni's flat call suggest neither is necessarily holding a monster like a 2 or 55. Calling allows me to realize my equity cheaply and see a turn card.

I'm on the SB with K♣9♦ on 2♠-2♦-5♠-3♦ after calling Claude's small flop bet in a multiway pot. I have no pair and no draw, and there's very little fold equity vs two opponents. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position with essentially no equity and is a losing line; pot control is the priority. I check to see how my opponents react. If someone leads a small turn bet I'll consider a call for pot odds, but I'll fold to large pressure. If checked to the river I'll re-evaluate (only consider a bluff if it's heads-up and the river texture and reads justify it).

I have a Queen-high flush draw on a board of 2s-2d-5s-3d. On the turn, the small blind has checked to me. I am in the big blind and the original aggressor (the button) is still to act behind me. Leading out here doesn't make much sense as I haven't improved yet and I would prefer to see the river as cheaply as possible. By checking, I allow the button to either check behind, giving me a free card, or bet, which I can then evaluate based on the pot odds and the action from the small blind. Given the pot size and the strength of a flush draw, I am looking to realize my equity.

Both opponents called my flop c-bet on this paired board. The 3d doesn't improve my hand or change the board texture meaningfully. With J-high (no showdown value) against two callers, continuing to barrel is risky - at least one of them likely has something. I should check and give up on this bluff rather than invest more chips with air.

Board is 2♠-2♦-5♠-3♦-2♥ — trips on the board and any player holding a 5 or a 3 now has a full house (and someone could even have the fourth deuce). I hold K♣9♦ with no pair or draw, in a three-way pot (Claude + Flash called the flop raise). I have essentially zero showdown value and very little fold equity bluffing into multiple opponents, so betting is a negative-EV play. Minimize risk: check and fold to any river aggression. If checked through I’ll concede the showdown (muck) — no chips to invest here.

The river brings a third deuce to the board, making the final community cards 2s-2d-5s-3d-2h. My hand is now three-of-a-kind (deuces) with a Queen-high kicker (2-2-2-Q-6). The turn was checked around by all players, which suggests that nobody likely has a deuce for quads or a strong pocket pair for a full house. However, many hands in the opponents' ranges, specifically any Ace (creating 2-2-2-A-5) or any pocket pair (creating a full house), would beat me. While I have some showdown value against other missed high-card hands or lower kickers, there is little to no value in betting as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands. Checking is the best play to realize my showdown value as cheaply as possible.

River brought a third deuce, giving everyone trip deuces with the board. My J-high kicker gives me some showdown value - I beat any kicker lower than Jack. Betting here would be a mistake since I'd only get called by better kickers (Q, K, A) or full houses (pocket pairs). Checking behind to showdown is optimal - I might win against worse kickers.

9♦5♠ offsuit is well below an UTG opening range in a 6-max lineup, especially with several opponents showing willingness to raise/3-bet (Flash, Claude, Minni). Limping invites isolation and forces us to play a weak, dominated hand out of position with poor playability and reverse implied odds. Preserving stack and waiting for better UTG opens is highest EV.

Jd5h is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, low equity against typical calling ranges, and significant domination risk (e.g., to Jx with better kicker). In middle position with 91BB stack, folding preflop preserves chips for better spots. No steals or limpers indicated yet (only UTG fold shown), so no incentive to play this trash hand. Prioritizing discipline to maximize long-term stack growth over 150 hands.
7c4h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. From cutoff with 3 players behind, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such a weak holding.

Button with K♥T♥ — a strong suited broadway hand with good postflop playability and fold equity. Folds to me with only SB (Flash) and BB (Pro) to act, so I want to seize the initiative and pressure the blinds. A standard 3x open to 300 both denies correct odds to the blinds and keeps pot-to-stack (SPR) workable given my ~64bb and the BB’s ~47bb. Plan: play postflop in position if called; re-evaluate against a large 3-bet (call smaller 3-bets, fold to heavy shoves or huge 4-bets).

I am in the Small Blind with 3-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised from the Button to 300. Although I have a significant chip lead and calling the 250 would not significantly impact my stack, playing this hand out of position against a Button open is a losing proposition. There is no strategic reason to defend such a weak hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c4c. Minni opens to 300 (3bb) from the Button. The pot odds are 200 to win 450, requiring about 30.7% equity. However, being out of position with a weak suited gapper against a 3x open makes realizing that equity difficult. 94s is typically outside the profitable defense range against a 3bb raise. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack of ~47bb.

UTG in a 6-handed game with Kc8d offsuit is a standard fold. This hand has poor playability, dominated by better kings, and low equity against calling ranges. Raising would require a strong hand, and limping is weak and exploitable. Folding preserves our deep stack (91BB) for better spots, aligning with tight-aggressive strategy to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.
A4o in middle position with Elon already folded. This is a playable hand that I should open-raise with to take initiative. With 111 BBs, I'm comfortable. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Claude opened to 250 from MP (about 2.5x). I’m on the cutoff with J♠9♣ and a healthy stack (~6,550 ≈ 65bb). Calling 250 into a 400 pot gives ~38% immediate pot odds and leaves a comfortable SPR (~9–10) to maneuver post‑flop. J9o is playable in position but too weak to 3‑bet for value — a min‑raise to 400 would bloat the pot out of position vs the button/blinds and commit chips with a hand that’s often dominated. I’ll call 250 to see a flop: if I hit a pair or a strong draw I’ll continue; if I miss and face heavy action (especially a sizable 3‑bet or a big c‑bet with callers behind) I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Claude opened from middle position and Minni called from the cutoff. Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by an opening range from middle position (hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, AJ, AT). While I have the button, calling would likely lead to a multi-way pot where my hand has poor playability and low 'nut' potential. Additionally, with a caller already in the pot, a 3-bet bluff is less likely to succeed against these opponents who have shown some stickiness in previous hands. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jc 8c. Facing a raise to 250 from Middle Position (Claude) and a call from the Cutoff (Minni). The pot offers decent odds (calling 200 to win roughly 650), but playing a marginal suited one-gapper out of position against two opponents is generally a losing proposition due to difficult playability and reverse implied odds. I faced a nearly identical situation with the exact same hand in Hand #10 and folded. Maintaining discipline and avoiding tricky OOP spots with dominated hands is the correct strategy.

We’re getting a decent price (150 to win 650), but J4o is a dominated, low-realization hand in a multiway pot (raiser + caller) from the big blind. The reverse implied odds when we make a weak jack and the difficulty of profitably continuing postflop out of position outweigh the pot odds. No good squeeze candidates here either with this holding. Preserve stack and wait for a better defend/3-bet spot.

I completely missed this flop with A4o. The board is 8-7-6 with two spades - very connected and coordinated. This hits Minni's calling range hard (suited connectors, pocket pairs, straight draws). I have ace high with no draws. C-betting here would be burning chips as I'll get called or raised frequently by better hands and draws. Check and give up if faced with aggression.

Board 8♥ 6♠ 7♠, my hand J♠9♣. I have 8 clean outs (any 5 or any 10) — roughly ~31% to make my straight by the river (~17% to hit the turn). Claude was the preflop raiser and checked the flop, so his range contains a lot of missed overcards, medium pairs, and some draws — a half‑pot semi‑bluff here can win the pot outright or get called by worse draws/pairs where I still have good equity. With position and a strong draw I want to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable, so I’ll bet about half the pot to get folds from pure overcards and to build value when I hit. Plan: if called I'll proceed cautiously — barrel for value if I complete (5/10) or if the turn significantly improves my tactical equity; fold to strong check‑raise since that line typically shows a very strong made hand. Betting 325 gives good fold equity while preserving stack depth (I have ~65bb).

I have ace-high with only a weak gutshot (need a 5 for a straight). This 8-7-6 two-spade board is extremely coordinated and hits Minni's calling range hard. Calling 325 with minimal equity is burning chips - I have no pair, no flush draw, and a weak gutshot. Clear fold.
KJo from UTG is a borderline opening hand but playable in a 6-max game. With a healthy stack of 108bb, I should open raise to around 2.5x. Standard open to 250.

Claude opened UTG to 250 (about 2.5x BB), which is an early‑position raise and represents a fairly strong, relatively tight range. I hold K♣3♠ — a weak, often dominated hand with poor postflop playability and few backdoor prospects. Calling 250 into a 400 pot commits a large portion of my stack with little equity and opens me up to multiway action or significant aggression from players behind. A 3‑bet bluff from my middle position against an UTG opener and several deeper stacks behind is not attractive either. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Claude has raised to 250 from UTG. My hand, King-Six offsuit (Ks 6h), is quite weak against an UTG opening range in a 6-max game. Additionally, I am in the Cutoff with three players still to act behind me who could potentially 3-bet. Given my significant stack lead, I should avoid playing marginal hands out of position or in situations where I am likely behind. K6o is an easy fold in this spot.

I am on the Button with 9s7h, a weak off-suit one-gapper. An early position player (Claude, UTG) has opened to 250 (2.5bb). Against a UTG opening range, 97o performs very poorly, even with position. My stack is 47bb, which is comfortable but not deep enough to speculate with trash hands against strong ranges. There is no strategic merit to calling or bluff-raising here. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Small blind versus a 6-handed UTG open is one of the worst positions to defend from: we’ll be out of position against the raiser and the big blind can come along or squeeze. Jh8h is playable in looser spots, but versus an UTG range it’s frequently dominated (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, better suited broadways) and doesn’t realize equity well from the SB. The 200 chip price is tempting, but the reverse-implied-odds and positional disadvantage make this a losing defend. I’ll pass and preserve stack for better spots.

Qs3d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by any raising range from UTG Claude, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Pot odds are roughly 2.7:1 (400 pot to 150 call), but the hand lacks equity, implied odds, or fold equity if raising. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to defend this trash hand out of position against an early position raise in a 6-handed game.

UTG in a 6-max game is an early position and I should open a fairly tight range. K♦8♥ offsuit is a marginal hand — easily dominated by callers/raisers behind and difficult to play postflop out of position. Limping (calling 100) is passive and will invite aggression from big stacks (Flash, Claude) and the button/SB, while opening to isolate is fine with stronger Kx or suited hands but not with K8o. With ~69 BB I can afford to wait for a better spot with more fold equity or suited connectivity. I fold to avoid getting stuck in a dominated, low-equity spot and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in Middle Position with 6c 4c, which is a very marginal suited connector with a two-gap. Given the current table dynamics and the fact that I have a significant stack lead, there is no need to play such a weak hand from this position. Standard strategy in a 6-max game suggests folding 64s from MP, especially with active players like Claude and FiveTwo behind me who have shown a willingness to 3-bet or play aggressively post-flop.

I am in the Cutoff with 7h 2d, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite having a decent stack of 47 BBs and being in late position, there is no strategic merit in playing this hand. The risk of losing chips with such poor equity outweighs any potential steal equity, especially with three players left to act behind me. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Folded to us on the button with A♠K♠, a premium that benefits a lot from building the pot in position and denying the blinds cheap realization. I don’t want to limp and invite multiway play; opening applies pressure to the blinds’ wide ranges and keeps initiative. A standard ~2.5x sizing performs well here and doesn’t overinflate while still punishing loose defenses.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 250 (200 to call into 400 pot). My hand is K8o, which is offsuit, king-high with a weak kicker, and plays poorly out of position postflop. Button ranges are wide in late position, especially with 6 players and steals common. Pot odds are decent (~3:1 effective), but realizing equity OOP with this hand is difficult without strong draws or hits. No history on FiveTwo suggests extreme weakness here; standard play is to fold marginal hands like this to preserve stack for better spots in a tournament setting with 77 hands left. 3-betting is not justified as K8o lacks blockers or fold equity leverage.

Q8o in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting 400:150 pot odds (2.67:1), and I'm closing the action. This is a standard defend - Q8o has enough equity to call here, and I'll have position information on FiveTwo's actions postflop. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds.

Missed flop completely with Q8o on 5-5-2 paired board. I have Q-high which has some showdown value. No reason to donk bet - standard check here and evaluate FiveTwo's action. If they bet, I can decide whether to float or fold based on sizing.

Heads-up in position after opening the button; Claude defended BB and checked a very dry paired low flop (5h-2s-5d). This texture heavily reduces strong made hands overall and is a good spot to use a small range c-bet: we have the preflop initiative and can fold out a lot of his unpaired overcards/broadways and some random floats, while keeping the price low when he continues with 5x/pairs. With A♠K♠ we have two clean overcards but little backdoor equity, so betting small to realize fold equity is higher EV than checking back and giving free realization.

Q-high on a 5-5-2 paired board with no draws. FiveTwo's small c-bet (200 into 550) is either value with a 5/pocket pair or standard continuation. Floating with Q-high is too marginal - I have very few outs to improve, and even if I pair I may still be behind. The paired board also limits my bluffing potential on later streets. Pot odds of ~3.75:1 aren't enough for Q-high with minimal equity. Clean fold.

I am Under The Gun (UTG) with King-Six suited. In a 6-max game, this hand is generally too weak to open-raise from the earliest position, as there are still five players left to act who could easily hold stronger Kings or premium pocket pairs. While I am the chip leader and can afford to play some speculative hands, doing so out of position with a marginal K-high hand is not ideal for maximizing my stack in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I hold [4h, Th] in Middle position. The action is on me after the UTG player folded. This is a very weak hand (T-4 suited) that does not justify an open raise from early/middle position, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against potential callers on the button or cutoff. With 47 BBs, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Folded to us in the cutoff, but Kh7d is a weak offsuit king that plays poorly postflop (dominated by better Kx and most calling ranges). With no antes, the immediate steal value is modest, and we have three players behind who can defend/3-bet (notably Elon on the button has shown willingness to 3-bet). Given stack depth (~75bb) and low realization with this hand when called, it’s a disciplined fold.

Unopened pot folded to the button. A2o (Ad2c) is a profitable open-raise from the button due to position, ace blocker for nut potential, and fold equity against blinds. Standard sizing is 3x BB (300). Blinds have shown folding tendencies to button opens in history. Builds pot or steals blinds to maximize stack growth in tournament.

54 offsuit is a weak hand to defend out of position against a button raise. While it has some straight potential, playing this OOP for 250 more chips is -EV. With a healthy 10,712 stack, no need to get involved with marginal holdings. Easy fold.

Facing Elon's button open to 300 from the big blind with 2♦3♥. Pot is 450 and it costs 200 to call, so calling requires ~30.8% equity (200/(450+200)). 2-3 offsuit has very poor raw equity vs a standard button opening range and is extremely difficult to play out of position postflop. A min 3-bet to 500 would be a weak, small bluff with little fold equity and no meaningful blockers. Stacks are deep enough to wait for a better spot (I have ~68.5BB), so preserving chips and folding is the best play.

I am in UTG position with A2o (Ace of Diamonds, Deuce of Clubs). With 47 big blinds, I have a healthy stack and no need to take unnecessary risks. A2o is a weak hand to open from early position as it plays poorly post-flop, is often dominated by better Aces, and faces five opponents left to act who could wake up with premium holdings or 3-bet me. The standard play here is to fold.

We’re first in after UTG folds, and Tc2h offsuit is well below a profitable 6-max opening range from middle position. It plays poorly multiway, is dominated by better Tx holdings, and has weak postflop realization out of position versus the blinds. No need to limp or create a marginal spot at 50/100 with our stack depth.

Cutoff position with UTG and MP folded, giving a good stealing opportunity. 3s8s is suited with some playability for flush and straight draws postflop, and deep stacks allow for implied odds. SB has 68BB, BB deep; raise to build pot or take it down preflop. Raising 3x BB to 300 is standard size here to pressure blinds while maintaining fold equity.

Q5s on the button facing a cutoff raise. While I have position, Q5 suited is a weak hand that doesn't play particularly well postflop. The queen is easily dominated by better queens, and the 5 provides little support. With two players still to act behind me, I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m in the small blind with 2♦6♠ facing a cutoff open to 300 (I’ve already posted 50, need 250 more). This is a very weak, easily-dominated hand and plays terribly out of position. Pot odds to call (250 into a 450 pot) are ~35%, but my equity vs a standard CO opening range is well below that and I’d be forced into difficult postflop decisions with little fold equity. A raise is unreasonable. With a healthy 68BB stack I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding K‣ 3‣. Elon raised to 300 from the Cutoff, which has been a frequent opening size for him. With a stack of over 21,000, I have plenty of chips to defend. The price to call is 200 to win a pot of 450, giving me roughly 3.25-to-1 odds. K3s is a reasonable hand to defend against a late-position raise, as it has decent playability post-flop and sufficient equity against a wide opening range. I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of 6h-Js-9d, I have completely missed with my Kc-3c. I have no pair, no real draw, and no backdoor flush potential. As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) here makes little sense given I have no equity to protect or semi-bluff with. I will check and see how Elon reacts, though I am likely folding to any significant bet.

Preflop raise with suited connectors in cutoff was standard for position and stealing potential. Flop of 6h Js 9d misses completely: no pair, no strong draw (only backdoor spade flush and straight draws). Flash checked, giving me a chance to check back and realize equity cheaply or see turn for free. C-betting into a somewhat coordinated board (straight draws like 78, T7 possible) risks getting called or raised by better hands, and with no showdown value, checking maintains pot control and avoids bloating the pot OOP response. Position allows seeing turn card cheaply. Deep stacks mean folding to aggression postflop is fine with this weak hand.

On the turn 8c, the board is now 6h-Js-9d-8c, which is extremely coordinated and connects with many potential straights and two-pair combinations. My hand (Kc 3c) has no pair and no draw. Since Elon checked back the flop, he might have a marginal hand or is also playing cautiously. However, I have no equity to justify a bet as a bluff here, as it's difficult to represent a specific strong hand that would have checked the flop and now wants to lead. I will check and plan to fold to any bet, as King-high is unlikely to be the best hand.

Preflop raise with 3s8s from cutoff was standard for position and playability. Flop 6h Js 9d missed entirely (no pair, weak backdoor draws), so checking back was correct for pot control and to realize equity cheaply in position. Turn 8c pairs my 8s for middle pair with a terrible 3 kicker. The board is now 6h-Js-9d-8c, which completed some straight draws (e.g., 7x for 6-7-8-9-J) and is somewhat coordinated, but no flush draws completed obviously. Flash has checked flop and turn, representing possible weakness, draws that missed, or traps. With deep stacks (my 88BB, Flash 208BB), position, and initiative from preflop, betting thin value protects against river draws (spades, hearts) and gets value from worse pairs (6x, weaker 8x), floats, or bluffs. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing Flash to bluff river. Half-pot bet of 350 balances value/protection while keeping pot manageable OOP response if raised. Folding equity against air post-oop check-check.

On a highly coordinated board of 6h-Js-9d-8c, I am holding Kc-3c which is just King-high with no pair and no draw. Elon checked the flop, which could signify a marginal hand, but his bet on the turn now puts pressure on my air. While I have the chips to call, doing so with no equity on a board that connects so well with an opponent's range is fundamentally unsound. There are too many straights and two-pair combinations possible, and I have no way to win the hand without improved cards or a high-risk bluff. I will fold and preserve my stack.

We’re first to act 6-handed with 4♣3♥ offsuit, a very weak, disconnected hand that plays poorly out of position and doesn’t realize equity well versus the field’s raising ranges. Limping invites isolation and multiway pots where we’re dominated and rarely able to continue. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Jd9d is a strong suited connector with great playability, straight and flush potential, and positionally sound in middle position with folds ahead. Pot is 150 (blinds), folded to me, ideal spot to open-raise for dead money steal or to play post-flop profitably against a call. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 builds pot and applies pressure. My stack (94BB) and opponents' stacks allow for deep play. Aggression early in tournament builds stack over 150 hands.

5d7d is a weak suited connector. Facing a raise from middle position, this hand doesn't have enough equity to profitably call or 3-bet. Even though it's suited, 57s plays poorly against a typical MP opening range. Easy fold from the cutoff.

Elon opened to 300 from middle; pot is 450 and my call would cost 300. I’m on the button with K♦2♠ — a weak, offsuit hand that’s dominated by most of the raiser’s range and has poor post‑flop playability in multiway pots. Calling requires ~40% equity versus the current pot odds and K2o doesn’t reach that against a standard open. A small 3‑bet to 500 is an option as a bluff, but the small blind (Flash) is deep and the big blind (Pro) can defend or 3‑bet; I don’t have the hand or reads to expect reliable fold equity. With ~68bb I’ll preserve chips for a better spot and fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Elon has raised to 300 (3x the big blind) from middle position. Although I have a significant chip lead, there is no strategic reason to defend this specific hand out of position against a raise. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid losing chips in a low-equity situation.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 8d. Elon raised to 300 (3bb) from Middle Position. While 98o has some connectivity, it is an off-suit hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a 3x open which indicates a stronger range than a min-raise. The pot odds are approximately 31%, but my equity realization will be low playing OOP. Defending here is likely -EV in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket Kings is one of the strongest preflop hands, especially from UTG. With 6 players and deep stacks, I want to raise to build the pot while thinning the field and charging draws or weaker hands. Opponents have shown aggression in past hands, but KK dominates most calling ranges. Standard UTG raise size of 3x the BB (300 total) balances value and fold equity. Pot is 150, so this commits 300 chips.

4c3c is a very weak hand to defend against a UTG raise. Even suited, this hand doesn't play well against a strong UTG opening range. Poor implied odds and reverse implied odds if I hit a weak pair. Easy fold from middle position.

Fold. I have 3♠5♦ in the cutoff facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450). This hand has very little equity vs an early-position raising range and is easily dominated. Calling 300 into 450 (300/(450+300)=40% required equity) is unjustified — 3-5 offsuit is far below that. Button and the blinds still to act make a marginal call or a bluff 3-bet unattractive; with a healthy 68BB stack I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am holding Kd Qd on the button, which is a premium broadway hand that plays excellently in position. Elon raised to 300 from UTG. While UTG ranges are typically tight, Elon has been quite active in recent hands. I have a massive chip lead (over 20,000 chips), allowing me to apply pressure. A 3-bet here allows me to isolate Elon, potentially take the pot down immediately, or build a pot with a very strong hand while having positional advantage. I will raise to 900.

I am in the Small Blind holding AcJh with a stack of roughly 45bb. The action involves a UTG open from Elon and a 3-bet to 900 from Flash on the Button. This represents a very strong range configuration. AJo is significantly dominated by a standard UTG opening range and a Button 3-betting range (which includes AK, AQ, JJ+). Calling would commit nearly 20% of my stack out of position with a dominated hand, and Elon (UTG) is still left to act behind me, posing a threat of a 4-bet. A 4-bet shove from me is also -EV given the strength shown. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Facing an UTG open and a button 3-bet, 8♣2♥ has extremely poor equity and playability, no useful blockers, and we’d be out of position in a bloated pot (often 3-way if we call). The pot odds aren’t sufficient to justify defending with this trash hand, and a 4-bet bluff is too spewy here given ranges and stack depths. Best is to preserve stack.

Pocket Kings remains extremely strong facing a button 3-bet after my UTG open and folds around. Flash's button 3-bet range is wide, including Ax, broadway hands, smaller pairs, and bluffs, against which KK has excellent equity. Calling 600 OOP would be suboptimal due to poor position postflop, where Flash can realize equity easily. Folding is never correct with KK here. 4-betting for value and fold equity is standard; sizing to 2700 (3x the 3-bet) builds a pot while denying equity to worse hands and pressures Flash's range. Deep stacks allow this without overcommitting (2700/9300 ~29%). History shows Flash aggressive on button but folds to pressure sometimes.

Elon's 4-bet from UTG to 2700 is a very strong show of strength, representing about 40% of his starting stack for the hand. Typically, a 4-bet range from early position is extremely tight, consisting of JJ+, AKs, and AKo. My Kd Qd is a strong hand, but it is significantly behind this range. I have blockers to KK and QQ, but even against AK I am a 2:1 underdog, and I am crushed by AA. Given the pot odds (calling 1800 into a 5550 total pot), I need roughly 32% equity to break even. Against a standard UTG 4-bet range, my equity is likely between 25-29%. Despite my large stack, there is no strategic reason to gamble in a dominated spot against a pot-committed opponent. I will fold and maintain my chip lead.
AJo is a solid opening hand from UTG. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x) to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against weaker hands.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — a strong early-position range. Q♠5♣ is a weak offsuit queen with poor playability and is often dominated by UTG calling/raising ranges. 3-betting is a mistake with this holding, and calling would leave me out of position against likely stronger hands for a marginal price (250 into 400 requires ~38% equity). Preserve my 6800 stack and wait for a better, positionally advantaged spot — fold.

I am in the Cutoff with 9s 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude opened from UTG with a raise to 250. Given the strength of an UTG opening range and my poor hand, there is no reason to call or raise. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold 4c 7c on the Button. The action began with Claude raising to 250 from UTG. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, consisting of pocket pairs, strong aces, and broadway cards, against which my hand performs poorly. While being suited provides some playability, 7-4 is a wide gapper and speculative. Calling risks a squeeze from the blinds and puts me in a spot where I need to hit the board hard to continue against an uncapped range. My stack is 45.5BB, so preserving chips is preferable to playing marginal hands against early position strength. Folding is the standard and correct play.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG 2.5bb open with the big blind still to act. 63o has very poor playability and is dominated by UTG’s opening range; defending here OOP will realize equity badly and often puts us in tough multiway spots if BB comes along. Not a good candidate for a squeeze bluff either. Best is to release and preserve stack.

Facing a standard UTG raise from Claude to 250 (2.5BB) in the BB with KTo offsuit. Pot is 400, calling 150 more for odds of approximately 21.4% (150 to win 550). KTo offsuit has reasonable equity (~30-35%) against a typical UTG range (pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited connectors/broadways) and plays well postflop with king high and broadway potential. While OOP postflop, the implied odds are good with deep effective stacks (~100BB), and Claude has shown aggression in past hands, suggesting a polarized range I can exploit. Defending wide in BB vs single raise is standard to realize equity and deny dead money. Folding a hand this strong would be too tight in a tournament setting where we need to accumulate chips over 150 hands. Calling keeps the pot small and maintains fold equity for later streets.

Heads-up pot of 550 on a Jd6d7s flop, OOP with KTo offsuit. This board hits Claude's UTG range strongly (JJ+, JTs+, AJ suited/diamond draws, etc.), while completely missing my hand—no pair, no straight draw (T is live but gutshot at best to Q for broadway), backdoor diamond flush draw at best. King-high has showdown value only against bluffs, but Claude's UTG open is tight and value-heavy. Betting OOP would be a pure bluff with poor equity realization and fold equity against a strong range. Checking is mandatory, and I expect to fold to any reasonable bet size given my lack of equity (~20-25% or less against his range). Prioritizing stack preservation in tournament with 71 hands left; not spewing chips with air.

Flopped top pair top kicker (TPTK) on J76 with two diamonds. This is a strong hand that needs protection against flush and straight draws. Elon checked to me - I should bet for value and to deny equity to draws. Betting around 60% pot (330) is a good sizing.

Facing a 330 bet into 550 pot (~60% pot) OOP on a Jd6d7s flop with KsTd (king-high, no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw, backdoor diamond at best). Claude's UTG raising range (tight: 66+, AJs+, KQs+, broadways) connects well here (Jx, sets 66/77/JJ, two-pair combos like J7/J6, diamond draws AdXd/KdQd etc.), giving him ~70-80% equity vs my air. My equity is minimal (~15-20% at best, mostly from runner-runner broadway or diamond miracles, but unrealistic). King-high has little showdown value against his value-heavy range and blocks few calling hands. Defending wide preflop is standard, but this is a clear fold spot postflop to preserve stack in tournament (71 hands left, deep effective ~100BB vs Claude). His sizing is small, but calling OOP invites further barrels on turns/rivers where boards worsen (straight/flush/draw completes), and pot control is irrelevant with zero equity. Folding realizes no equity loss and avoids spew against a strong player like Claude who has shown value aggression from early position (e.g., Hand 47 top pair top kicker bets, Hand 41 KK flop bet). Prioritize survival and chip accumulation over marginal hero calls.

UTG in a 6-max game with T6o (T♦6♣). This is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor postflop playability and not part of a standard UTG open range. Raising from early position invites 3-bets or multiway pots against better ranges (several deeper, aggressive stacks behind), and limping is also a losing line since I won't realize equity and will face pressure. With a healthy 68bb stack and many better spots to pick, fold and conserve chips for premium opportunities.

74s is a weak starting hand in 6-max from Middle Position. While it has some post-flop potential being suited, it is too weak to open with four players left to act behind me, including several stacks between 45 and 100 big blinds. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my chip lead.

Current hand is 9h 8s in the Cutoff position. Stack size is 45.5 BB. Action has folded to me. 98 offsuit is a weak connector that is typically outside the standard opening range from the Cutoff in a 6-max game, usually being a fold. Looking at table history, the players behind me (FiveTwo, Elon, Claude) have shown willingness to 3-bet my opens (Hands #7, #15, #49). Opening a marginal hand like 98o into aggressive stacks is -EV. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folds to us on the Button with blinds in play, so this is a prime steal spot. Even though 54o is weak, position lets us realize equity and apply pressure postflop, and both blinds will have to defend with relatively wide ranges. I’ll use a small standard open to keep risk low while still generating fold equity and building initiative.

K2s is a marginal hand from the small blind facing a button raise. The button (FiveTwo) often raises wide from late position as a steal attempt, given their history of frequent opens. However, my hand plays poorly post-flop out of position, especially with the weak deuce kicker, making me vulnerable to domination if a king hits or to floats/bluffs. Pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into 400), but equity drops if BB defends wide. With 103 BBs deep and tournament mid-stage, folding preserves stack for premium spots rather than speculative play OOP. FiveTwo's stack is covered, no need to engage lightly.

Facing a button raise with Q6o in the BB. Getting ~2.7:1 odds to call 150 into a 400 pot. Q6o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. While button opens wide, I'll have position disadvantage throughout the hand. Folding is the disciplined play here rather than defending with a marginal hand that will be difficult to play profitably OOP.

I am in the UTG position with 7d 9s, which is a weak offsuit connector. In a 6-max game, opening such a wide range from early position is generally unprofitable, especially when there are active players behind me. With a comfortable stack of nearly 200 big blinds, there is no need to force a play with a sub-optimal hand. Folding is the standard disciplined move here.

I am in middle position with 3c 8c, a very weak suited hand with a large gap. There are four players left to act behind me, including the aggressive button and blinds. With a stack of roughly 45 big blinds, there is no incentive to play this speculative hand. Folding is the standard and correct strategic decision.

Cutoff 6-handed is a prime steal spot after two folds. A3o is comfortably within an aggressive CO opening range: it blocks strong Ax/AA, can make top pair, and has some wheel/straight potential. With ~75BB effective vs blinds, I prefer raising over limping to deny equity and pick up the blinds/maintain initiative. If we face a sizeable 3-bet from Button/Blinds, this hand will usually fold rather than continue dominated.

Preflop on the button facing a cutoff raise to 250 into a 400 pot. My hand is 8d5c, offsuit and poorly connected with low cards, placing it at the bottom of any playable range. FiveTwo's cutoff raise is standard and likely wide but strong enough that 85o has poor equity (~30-35% vs a typical open range) and plays poorly postflop without position advantage outweighing the dead money. Pot odds require ~38% equity to call, which this hand lacks. No suitedness or playability justifies a call or 3-bet bluff given deep stacks (~100BB effective) and tournament stage (hand 81/150). Folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV.
96o is a weak hand in the small blind facing a cutoff raise. Out of position with poor playability. Easy fold.

I have As8s in the big blind facing a 250 CO open. This is a defensible hand — suited ace with decent playability and blocker value — so I prefer to call the additional 150 (250 total) rather than 3-bet. A small 3-bet to the min (400) would be too weak and a larger 3-bet would bloat the pot and commit me out of position versus an unknown opener. Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity (nut-flush and top-ace possibilities), and lets me evaluate the flop before committing more. Postflop plan: continue/raise with top pair or strong draws; check/fold to heavy pressure on dry boards where I miss.

I missed this flop with As8s (board 5h 5s Qc). I have no pair and only a backdoor spade possibility — the Ace is a useful blocker but doesn’t make this a value spot OOP. FiveTwo’s CO opening range contains many Qx hands and some pocket pairs/sets that already beat me, and betting here would bloat the pot out of position for little value. With ~65BB effective and the pot only 550, I prefer to control the pot: check and see how the opener reacts. If he checks back I get a free turn; if he bets a small c-bet I’ll consider a call to realize equity and probe on the turn; if he bets big or check-raises I’ll fold to avoid committing chips with a marginal hand.

Paired flop (Q55) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range: I have all the strong Qx/overpairs and some 5x, while BB has lots of air and marginal hands that don’t want to continue versus a small c-bet. With A-high I also have decent blocker value (Ah blocks some strong Ax continues) plus backdoor hearts. I’ll fire a small, range-ish continuation bet to pick up the pot immediately at high frequency and keep my risk low. If Minni check-raises, I’m comfortably folding this combo.

Flop: 5h 5s Qc. I hold As8s (no pair, backdoor spade/runner-runner nut possibilities, and a useful Ace blocker). Preflop CO opened and now fires a small c-bet of 175 into the ~550 pot (pot is 725 now). Calling 175 requires roughly 175 / (725+175) ≈ 19.4% equity — a low threshold. FiveTwo’s flop range will include many Qx hands, some 5x/sets, value pairs and a fair number of air/bluffs given the small size. My hand is priced well to continue: I block many Ace combos, have respectable showdown value against bluffs, and a tiny backdoor flush chance plus the possibility to improve to top pair on an Ace. Raising OOP with Ace-high here is poorly disguised and will often bloat the pot or get called by better hands; folding surrenders +EV equity for too little. Plan: call 175 to realize equity and keep the pot controlled. On the turn I will reassess — proceed if I pair the Ace or see a cheap card and Villain checks or bets small; fold to large turn aggression when I miss to avoid committing with marginal holdings.

Check. Board is 5h 5s Qc 6c and I have As8s. The turn (6c) kills the backdoor spade draw so my only clean way to improve is pairing the Ace (3 outs) or the 8 (3 outs) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% raw to hit the river, and many of those outs are ineffective vs sets/5x or Qx (if Villain has Qx only Aces are clean). FiveTwo’s tiny flop c-bet suggests a wide range that includes many Qx and some bluffs, but also a lot of hands that will call a turn bet. I’m out of position and betting here would bloat the pot with Ace-high and be vulnerable to calls by better pairs or folds only by air. With ~64BB effective and the pot controllable at 900, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and see the river, then re-evaluate: value-bet if I pair an Ace, bluff selectively on a favourable river card or if Villain shows weakness, but fold to significant aggression now or on the river if I miss.

Minni’s flop check-call on Q55 after a very small c-bet keeps their range wide (lots of A-high floats, underpairs, some Qx/5x, occasional backdoor clubs). The turn 6c adds some 6x and club draws, but this board texture still heavily favors the preflop raiser, and my A-high has almost no showdown value if I check back. I want to apply a second barrel with a more meaningful size to fold out the floating/weak portions (A-high, K-high, underpairs without a club) and deny equity, while still keeping my line consistent with value hands (Qx/overpairs/boats) that want to build the pot. If I face a check-raise, I’ll fold this combo; if called, I’ll look to pressure again on favorable rivers (A/K/J/T or club runouts).

Villain bets 550 into a ~1450 pot — I must call 550 to win ~2000, so I need ≈27.5% equity. I have As8s with no pair on 5h 5s Qc 6c and only ~6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 eights) ≈ 13% to improve on the river; much of that equity is poor versus likely parts of FiveTwo’s continuing range (Qx, 5x, sets and medium pairs). Raising as a bluff OOP here is poorly balanced and risky — I’ll often get called by better hands or face a shove. Given the pot odds, my raw equity, and being out of position, calling is -EV and the correct low-variance line is to fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Qh Jc. In a 6-handed game, QJo is generally considered too weak to open from Under the Gun. It is easily dominated by hands like AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ and plays poorly out of position against 5 opponents. With a stack of 45.5 BB, there is no need to force action with marginal holdings. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot or position.

52o has very poor playability and reverse-implied-odds, especially from middle position with three players still to act who can isolate/3-bet. With stacks deep, entering this pot is -EV versus competent ranges; better to preserve stack and wait for stronger steal spots (CO/BTN) or better hands.

Preflop in cutoff with 6c9s offsuit, an unopened pot after UTG and MP folds. This hand has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and is easily dominated. Position is good but not worth entering the pot with speculative trash versus button, SB, and deep BB stacks. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. No steal incentive here with players behind.

QJs on the button with everyone folding to me. This is a premium hand in late position - suited broadway cards with good playability. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to pick up the blinds or build a pot in position.

I'm in the small blind with Tc4d facing a Button open to 250 (pot 400, I already have 50 posted; calling would cost 200 more / 250 total). This is a weak, poorly connected offsuit hand with bad post‑flop playability and frequent domination by the button's opening range. I'm out of position against Claude and still have the big blind behind who can defend or 3‑bet, so a shove/3‑bet bluff or even a small reraise is unnecessarily high variance here. With a healthy ~63BB stack and the tournament to play, preserve chips for better spots — fold.

K3o is a very weak hand to defend the Big Blind with, even when getting decent pot odds against a button open. Playing out of position with a hand that is easily dominated and lacks connectivity makes post-flop play difficult and often leads to unnecessary chip loss. With a comfortable chip lead, I choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot.

UTG in a 6-max game with 3♣2♠ (offsuit) is the very bottom of the range: terrible high-card value, poor playability, and dominated when we make a pair. There’s no profitable open-raise or limp strategy here, and defending creates reverse implied odds against larger stacks in later positions. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Preflop, unopened after UTG fold, in middle position with A2o (offsuit). This hand has poor postflop playability, dominated by better aces, and low equity against typical calling ranges. With deep stacks (103BB effective), strong opponents behind (Claude in CO is aggressive, Minni on button, deep SB), folding preserves stack for premium spots in this tournament stage (hand 83/150). No steal value here given positions and histories of raises from late positions.

6d9h offsuit is a weak hand from the cutoff. Not suited, poor connectivity. With 3 players behind including the button, this is a clear fold. No need to open marginal hands when I have a healthy stack of 110 BBs.

I’m on the button with Q♦4♣ (marginal hand) and both blinds still to act. The pot is 150 and the cost to play is only 100. With 63bb and two opponents to my left I’ll attempt a standard button steal: raise to 300 (3x BB). This sizing is large enough to pick up the blinds often but small enough that I can fold without significant damage if Flash (SB) or Pro (BB) 3-bets. If I get called I’ll play postflop in position — c-bet on favorable/dry textures and proceed cautiously vs heavy aggression. If either blind 3-bets big, I’ll relinquish the hand and preserve the stack.

I am holding Ace-Ten of Diamonds in the Small Blind. The action has folded to the Button (Minni), who raised to 300. ATs is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind or blind-versus-button situation. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller and play post-flop, a 3-bet is generally superior here to take initiative, capitalize on the strength of my hand, and potentially end the hand immediately. Given Minni's stack of roughly 60 big blinds and my significant chip lead, a raise to 1,000 puts appropriate pressure on the opener and helps define their range while remaining well-balanced for my overall strategy.

I hold 3d Th in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action involves a raise from the Button (Minni) to 300 followed by a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Flash) to 1000. My hand has virtually no playability or equity in a 3-bet pot, especially facing this level of aggression. Calling 900 chips (nearly 20% of my remaining stack) with T3o would be a significant mistake. I must fold and preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 as a standard button steal with Qh4c. Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1000, putting 700 more to call into a 1400 pot — I’d need ~33% equity to break even. Q4o is a marginal, highly-dominated holding versus a typical SB 3-bet range (lots of Qx, broadways and pairs) and won’t have the required equity or post‑flop playability to justify calling or 4-betting here. I planned to relinquish the hand to a big 3-bet to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot, so I’ll fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game requires a tight opening range to avoid difficult post-flop situations out of position with speculative hands. 9h5h is a suited connector but too weak and low for UTG; it plays better in late position or multiway pots. Folding preserves stack and avoids variance with a marginal hand facing potential 3-bets or calls from the field.
3s6c is a very weak offsuit hand with no real potential. No connectivity, no high cards, not suited. Easy fold from middle position.

Unopened pot in cutoff with AKs — a premium hand and ideal spot to take the initiative. I have ~60BB, the pot is 150 and to call is 100. Standard open here is ~3x the big blind to build the pot and get fold equity while keeping post‑flop flexibility. I’ll open-raise to 300. If I face a 3‑bet I’ll evaluate by size and opponent (call or 4‑bet/shove vs short stacks, call or 4‑bet vs deep stacks).

I am on the button with 3h 4d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has opened from the cutoff for 3x the big blind. Even though I have position and a large stack, this hand has zero playability and very little equity against a standard opening range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jh 2h. Minni raised to 300 from the Cutoff. While my hand is suited, J2 is extremely weak and dominated by most of the opener's range. Playing this hand out of position is not profitable. The pot odds do not justify calling with such poor equity and playability. Folding is the standard decision here.

Minni is opening from the cutoff and has shown a fairly active raising frequency. Getting good immediate price (200 to win 650) and 9-8 has decent playability in positionless defense (can make strong pairs/straights and some two-pair combos). A light 3-bet here risks bloating the pot OOP versus a stronger continuing range with an offsuit hand, so the best EV line is to defend by calling and realize equity postflop.

Heads-up BB defend vs CO open on a J♣ T♥ 7♦ flop, 9♠8♦ gives us a strong open-ended straight draw (6/Q) but no made hand and no flush backdoors. Out of position, the highest-EV baseline is to check to the preflop raiser: we keep our range protected (we have many strong hands here), allow Minni to continuation-bet frequently, and then we can choose between check-calling (to realize equity) or check-raising (as a semi-bluff) depending on her sizing and tendencies. Donk-leading is unnecessary and risks isolating ourselves versus a stronger continuing range while not improving realization much.

I opened to 300 preflop with A♠K♠ and got one caller (FiveTwo in the BB). Flop J♣ T♥ 7♦ is a relatively dry, rainbow texture where I hold two overcards and the range advantage as preflop aggressor. A standard continuation bet will fold out lots of weak pairs and equity hands from his wide defending range while preserving post‑flop maneuverability. I'll c‑bet roughly half the pot (325). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn — continue with an A/K or showdown‑value lines, be cautious and fold to a large check‑raise representing a strong made hand.

We flopped an open-ended straight draw (8 clean outs to a strong straight) versus a small ~1/3-pot c-bet. Calling 325 to win 1300 gives us ~25% immediate pot odds, and our draw has ~31% equity to improve by the river, plus some implied odds when we hit. A check-raise semi-bluff is possible, but with no flush backdoors it’s a weaker raising candidate and it also risks getting jammed on by Minni’s stronger continues (Jx/Tx/sets/KQ), forcing us to fold and burn extra chips. Best EV is to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and retain the option to apply pressure on favorable turns (6/Q or high cards that shift range advantage).

Turn 3♦ is essentially a brick for both ranges: we still have the same strong OESD (6/Q) but no added backdoors, and donk-leading doesn’t credibly represent much while risking getting floated/raised by her Jx/Tx/overpairs/strong draws. Checking keeps our range protected, invites her to continue barreling with her high c-bet frequency, and lets us realize equity and choose between check-call (good price) or check-raise on a bet depending on sizing.

I opened preflop with A♠K♠, c‑bet the dry J♣T♥7♦ flop (325) and got called. The 3♦ on the turn is a blank — it doesn’t meaningfully improve most of his calling range. As the preflop aggressor I still have a range advantage and decent fold equity vs the big blind’s wide calling range (marginal pairs, floats, missed broadways). A half‑pot second barrel will fold out lots of unpaired hands and weak pairs while keeping the pot manageable with only two overcards. I’ll bet ~50% pot (650). If he answers with a large check‑raise I’ll give it up — I have no pair and limited equity vs strong made hands. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (look to pot‑control or extract value if I pair up).

Turn is a brick (3♦) and we still have the same OESD with 8 outs (6/Q). Minni’s 650 into 1300 (~1/2 pot) gives us ~25% immediate pot odds, while our raw river equity is only ~17%, so it’s not a pure-price call.
