However, the implied odds versus Minni’s remaining stack (she’ll have ~3475 behind if we call) are strong: when we hit a straight it’s well-disguised and we can often win an additional ~1k+ via a river bet/call or her value-betting into us. A check-raise semi-bluff is less attractive here with no flush backdoors and likely low fold equity versus her turn-barreling range (Jx/Tx/sets/KQ), risking getting jammed on and burning equity. Best EV is to call and realize equity; on 6/Q rivers we can lead or check-raise for value depending on sizing, and on bricks we mostly check-fold versus significant pressure.

River Q♥ completes our open-ender: 9♠8♦ now makes the Q-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q). After check-calling flop and turn, our range is draw-heavy, so checking risks Minni checking back a lot of one-pair/two-pair hands on this scary runout. We should lead for value versus Qx/KQ/QJ/JT/sets that will still pay, while choosing a sizing that’s big enough to build the pot but not so big that it only gets action from better straights (AK/K9). A ~60% pot lead accomplishes that and also improves our price if she responds with an all-in.

I opened the pot with A♠K♠, c-bet the J♣T♥7♦ flop (325) and barreled the blank 3♦ turn (650). FiveTwo has check‑called both streets, which is a very sticky/passive line consistent with medium‑strength made hands (Jx, Tx, slow‑played sets) or a wide defending range that will call down. The Q♥ on the river now gives a lot of hands clear value (KQ, AQ, Qx) that beat my Ace‑high. He bets a small 1,600 into a 4,200 pot — a sizing consistent with thin value or a blocking/targeted value bet. Calling 1,600 only needs ~21.6% equity (1,600 / 7,400) to break even, but my read is his river is value‑heavy and he bluffs far less than that frequency after check‑calling flop and turn. Raising as a bluff (min‑raise to 3,200) would be high variance and I have poor fold equity versus Qx/KQ. I preserve ~47.5BB and fold this river facing a value‑leaning bet.
K9s in UTG - marginal but playable suited hand. Has flush potential and decent high card strength. Standard open to 2.5x is appropriate here. Will fold to significant 3-bet pressure.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I have K♣9♠ (K9o) in middle position — a marginal hand that is often dominated by an UTG opening range (AK, KQ, KJ, K10, mid pairs). I would be out of position to the opener and there is still action behind (cutoff, button, blinds), increasing the chance of multiway action and tough postflop decisions. Calling 250 to play OOP with a mediocre holding gives poor implied odds and a min‑raise to 400 is a thin isolation that can get 3‑bet or called. With ~47.5bb I should preserve chips for better spots — fold here.

I am holding 2d 3c in the Cutoff position. Claude, who is the UTG player, has opened with a raise to 250. This is a very weak hand with almost no post-flop potential or showdown value. There are four players behind me yet to act. Folding is the only sensible play here as I am out of position against the initial raiser and my hand is essentially garbage.

I am on the Button with Q7o. The player in UTG (Claude) has opened to 250. An UTG opening range is generally strong and likely dominates my hand (which is weak unsuited broadway trash). Q7o does not play well post-flop, even with position. Calling is -EV, and bluffing (3-betting) is ill-advised against an UTG range without better blockers or playability. The disciplined and standard play is to fold.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG 2.5x open with J4o. Against a tight UTG range this hand is heavily dominated (Jx/Qx/AX) and plays very poorly out of position. Flat-calling also invites the big blind to come along, creating a multiway pot where our equity realization is even worse. A 3-bet bluff is not attractive versus UTG’s stronger continuing range and we’d be bloating the pot OOP with a hand that can’t comfortably continue to aggression postflop. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 into a 400 pot, requiring a call of 150 from the BB. My hand is T3o, a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card value. UTG ranges are strong (high pairs, strong aces, broadway cards), and T3o has minimal equity against it (around 25-30% at best heads-up). Pot odds are about 2.67:1, but implied odds are poor due to the hand's lack of playability post-flop. SB has folded, but no need to defend wide here with trash. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Long-term tournament goal prioritizes not spewing chips.

A♣J♥ UTG in 6-max is a clear open — strong, playable hand that wants to take the initiative. I have ~47.5BB, so a standard 3x open is appropriate to build the pot, gain fold equity vs the blinds, and protect my hand from giving cheap flops to speculative holdings. Limping or just calling 100 is too passive and invites multiway action; the minimum raise (200) is a bit small and will invite frequent 3-bets from deep/aggressive stacks (Flash, Elon). I’ll open to 300. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold to very large 3-bets out of position, consider call/shove vs smaller 3-bets depending on opponent and effective stacks).

Minni has raised to 300 from under-the-gun. I am holding Ten-Six suited in middle position. While it's a suited hand, it is significantly below the calling range against an UTG open, particularly with several players still to act behind me who could squeeze or have stronger holdings. Given the stack sizes and the 3x open, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qd 2s) in the Cutoff position. The player under the gun (Minni) has raised to 300, indicating a strong range. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly against an UTG opening range. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me. Calling or raising with this hand would be a significant mistake. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Button with 99 versus UTG 3bb open (Minni ~44bb). This is strong enough to 3-bet for value and isolation: we’re ahead of her opening range, we deny equity to overcards, and we reduce the chance of a squeeze from the deep stacks in the blinds. I’ll use a size that pressures her stack while still getting calls from worse pairs/broadways. If she 4-bet jams, it’s likely very tight (JJ+/AK-type) and we can comfortably fold; otherwise we play an in-position 3-bet pot with a manageable SPR.

78o is a weak offsuit hand with marginal playability. Facing a 3-bet to 950 from FiveTwo on the button over Minni's UTG open to 300. FiveTwo's range is strong (premiums, bluffs maybe, but still ahead). OOP in SB, pot odds are poor (900 to win 1400, ~39% equity needed, but 78o has far less vs. this range). BB still to act. Clear fold to preserve stack for better opportunities with 65BB effective.

J6o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. I need to call 850 into a pot of 1400, requiring about 38% equity. J6 offsuit has terrible equity against a 3-betting range and poor playability post-flop. Clear fold.

I opened UTG to 300 with A♣J♥ and the Button (FiveTwo) 3‑betted to 950. AJo is strong but vulnerable (dominated by AK/AQ) and I'm out of position — with ~44.5BB effective I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal 4‑bet or shove. The 3‑bet size offers ~2.2:1 immediate pot odds to call; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity and fold to extreme action on unfavorable flops. Plan: call to 950, see the flop, continue when I hit (A/J or strong draws) and be cautious / surrender vs heavy aggression on dry boards.

I called FiveTwo’s 3‑bet preflop with A♣J♥ intending to see a flop and only continue when I connected or had a real draw. The flop (4♠ T♣ 2♠) is very dry for my hand — I missed: no pair and no realistic draws (with one club in my hand and only two cards to come I cannot make a 5‑card club flush). I’m out of position against the 3‑bettor and the pot is already large (≈2050) relative to my stack, so barreling with air would be reckless. I’ll check to control the pot and evaluate: fold to large pressure, consider calling a small c‑bet as a float to take the pot later, and only invest further if I pick up a pair or a clear fold equity card on the turn.

We’re the preflop 3-bettor with a clear range advantage (overpairs, big broadways) on T♣4♠2♠. 99 is a medium-strength hand: often ahead of her AK/AQ-type floats and some smaller pairs, but behind her continuing value (TT/JJ/QQ+). With her short remaining stack and low SPR, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a big c-bet that forces me into a miserable call vs a shove; instead I’ll use a small continuation bet to (1) fold out unpaired overcards, (2) deny equity on a two-spade board, and (3) keep my loss small if she check-jams (which from UTG-call-3bet is likely strong and I can fold to).

I called FiveTwo’s 3‑bet preflop with A♣J♥ and checked this very dry flop (4♠ T♣ 2♠). His c‑bet is small (450 into the ≈2050 pot pre‑bet; pot ≈2500 now), so calling is cheap and consistent with my preflop plan to see flops and only continue with reasonable equity. I have no pair and limited immediate outs (A/J outs → ~24% to hit by river), but calling lets me float, realize equity, and fold to significant turn aggression. Raising as a bluff out of position is too risky against a 3‑bettor’s range, and folding to a small c‑bet would be overly nitty. Plan: call the 450, fold to a large turn shove/raise, but extract value or apply pressure if I pair up or the turn presents a clear bluff opportunity.

I have A♣J♥ out of position vs FiveTwo who 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet a very dry flop. The turn 8♥ is a blank for my hand — I still have only ace‑high and no real draws. The pot (~2950) is nearly my entire stack (3350), so this is effectively a commitment decision and I must avoid bloat or an ill‑timed bluff OOP. Betting here would risk committing most chips with little fold equity (villain’s 3‑bet/c‑bet line will call many turn bluffs), while checking controls the pot and preserves options. Plan: check and see how FiveTwo reacts. If he checks back we go to the river and can realize equity or bluff only with better blockers; if he bets a small amount (~≤1/3 pot) I can consider a float/call to try to take the river; if he bets large (pot or shove) I’ll fold. Checking is the prudent, EV‑positive play.

Turn is 4♠ T♣ 2♠ 8♥ in a 3-bet pot. With 99 we’re in a medium-strength spot: we beat missed overcards and some smaller pairs, but we’re behind any Tx, overpairs, and sets (and her UTG call vs 3-bet can contain 22/44/TT that may have trapped flop). With Minni’s remaining stack (3350) and the pot already 2950 (SPR ~1.1), betting risks getting check-jammed and we’d be forced to fold a hand that still has decent showdown value. A turn barrel also doesn’t fold out the hands that beat us (Tx/overpairs) and may still get called/reshoved by spade draws. Taking the free card keeps our loss controlled and lets us bluff-catch some reasonable river sizes while comfortably folding to a polar river jam if needed.

Check. I only have A♣J♥ (Ace‑high) on a paired river (4s Tc 2s 8h 4d). Villain 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet a very dry flop, then checked the turn — his line contains many hands that beat me (tens, deuces, pocket pairs, slow‑played strong hands) and some medium‑value hands that will not fold to a river bluff. The pot (~2950) is large relative to my 3350 stack, so bluffing here has poor fold equity and risks committing most of my chips for little expected value. Checking preserves my stack and avoids making a low‑EV bluff; if FiveTwo bets into me I’ll fold to sizable bets and only consider calling very small bets with correct price.

River goes check/check on turn then Minni checks again on 4♠ T♣ 2♠ 8♥ 4♦ in a 3-bet pot. That line usually caps her range toward pocket pairs below TT (55–77), some A-high floats (often with a spade), and occasionally slow-played Tx/boats. With 99 we have solid showdown value and don’t want to face a big polar bet, but we can still go for thin value because (1) Minni has shown a tendency to continue/call in prior hands, and (2) a small sizing should get looked up by worse pairs that don’t believe us after we checked turn. I’ll bet small and comfortably fold if she check-jams (that’s almost always Tx/full house/quads at this SPR).

FiveTwo 3‑bet me preflop so his range is weighted to value (TT+, sets/2s, AK/AQ and strong Axs). The flop c‑bet then turn check is consistent with pot‑control/strong made hands or thin value hands — not a line that promises a big bluff frequency. He now bets 750 into a 3,700 pot; calling 750 into a 4,450 total pot gives me ~17% break‑even equity (750/(3700+750) ≈ 16.9%). On this river my A♣J♥ is only ace‑high and beats almost exclusively pure bluffs or very weak non‑pair hands. I’m behind nearly all realistic value combos in his 3‑betting range, so I don’t expect him to be bluffing ≥17% of the time. Raising as a bluff OOP here is too risky and would commit most of my stack with poor blockers. Fold to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I am UTG with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. In a 6-max game, opening this hand from early position is a massive mistake regardless of stack size. The standard and correct play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in Middle Position with King-Four offsuit (K4o). This is a weak hand that falls well below the standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game (typically top 20-25%). With four players left to act, including large stacks behind me, opening this hand would be a negative EV play. My stack of 44 BB is comfortable enough that I don't need to take unnecessary risks. The correct play is to fold.

CO with A8o is strong enough to open in 6-max: we have positional advantage, block a lot of Ax continues, and can often win the blinds immediately. Limping is unnecessary and invites multiway play; folding is too tight. I’ll use a standard open size that still keeps my risk controlled versus an aggressive Button and a shorter BB (and I’m comfortable folding if faced with a 3-bet/jam).

On the button with 9h5h suited connectors, facing a standard 2.5x raise from the cutoff (FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands like frequent preflop raises and postflop betting). My stack is deep (102BB), pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400, ~1.6:1 direct, better with position and implied odds). Suited hand has good playability for straights/flushes/draws in position, especially multiway potential with SB/BB to act. Folding is too tight from BTN; 3-betting is unnecessary with this speculative hand. Calling exploits position and hand potential while keeping pot small.
T7o is a weak hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position. Even with 3.25:1 pot odds, this hand lacks the playability needed from the small blind against two opponents. Easy fold.

I’m in the big blind with Q♥7♥ facing a cutoff raise to 250 and a button call (multiway). Pot is 650 and it costs me 150 more (total commitment 250) to see the flop — very cheap relative to my stack (~32.5BB). Q7s has reasonable multiway equity (flush + overcard + straight potential) and my pot odds (~650:150 ≈ 4.3:1 — need ~18.8% equity) justify defending. A tiny 3-bet to 400 won’t reliably isolate and would put me OOP vs two players while risking more chips, so I’ll call and evaluate the flop: continue with hearts/top pair/draws, fold to heavy aggression when missed.

Check. I missed this flop — Q♥7♥ has no pair and only a backdoor heart (very small, ~5% to complete by river) and only ~12–13% to pair Q/7 by the river. This is a multiway pot (cutoff raised, button called), so my fold equity as a bluff is very low and many hands in their ranges (Jx, pocket pairs, any 3) dominate me. Pot is 800 and it’s checked to me; with ~31BB I want pot control and a free card to see the turn. I’ll check and only continue if I pick up real equity (a heart, pair Q/7) or face a very small bet I can justify calling — otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful aggression.

Multiway on a paired J-high flop, my A8o has little showdown value but I still retain a range advantage as the preflop raiser (overpairs, strong Jx, some 3x). This texture also allows a high-frequency small c-bet: it pressures the BB’s wide preflop defend (random floats, small pairs without a J, undercards) and keeps me balanced with my value hands that also like betting small here. I’ll use a small sizing to risk little versus a check-raise and to fold out hands with equity denial (two spades on board). If I get called in one or two spots, I’ll proceed cautiously and improve mainly on A turns (and selectively on good barrel cards).

Preflop call with 9h5h was speculative from the button for position, implied odds, and multiway potential. Flop 3d3sJs completely misses my hand: no pair, no straight draw (95 needs 6-7-8 or 67-8-T etc., none here), no flush draw. Pure air. FiveTwo's CO raise and flop bet (250 into 1050, ~1/4 pot) represents Jx, overpairs, sets, or draws. Calling gives poor odds (~19% needed vs likely dominated), no fold equity on raise given board, and risks multiway with Minni behind. Deep stacks but no playability; folding preserves stack for better spots. Consistent with GTO: fold marginal speculative hands when bricked.

Call 250. The 250 into a 1050 pot is a very small c‑bet (≈23–24% pot) giving me ~4.2:1 pot odds (I need ~19% equity). With Q♥7♥ on 3♦3♠J♠ I have ~24% to pair Q/7 by river plus ~5% backdoor flush equity — roughly 25–30% total vs a continuing range — so a call is +EV. Elon folded so it’s heads‑up; FiveTwo’s small bet is consistent with a wide c‑bet range so I’d rather realize equity cheaply than bluff‑raise OOP with no pair. I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn: continue aggressively only if I pair or pick up a strong draw, otherwise fold to significant turn aggression to protect my stack.

I’m heads‑up with Q♥7♥ on 3♦ 3♠ J♠ A♣. I missed — no pair and after the turn there’s no realistic flush/straight draw left (only one card to come). I have ~6 outs to pair (≈13%), so very little equity to justify building the pot. FiveTwo led the flop and then checked the Ace on the turn, which looks like pot‑control with a marginal hand or caution to the Ace — not a spot I want to barrel into OOP with air. Pot is 1300 and it’s checked to me: betting here as a bluff has limited fold equity (one card left) and betting for value is impossible. Best line is to control the pot and see the river for free. Plan: check turn, re‑evaluate on the river — call small probes to realize equity, fold to large aggression unless I pair, and if I hit a Q/7 on the river extract thin value.

Turn Ace is a great barrel card: it improves us to top pair and also heavily favors our preflop-raiser range (lots of Ax). BB’s flop-calling range contains many Jx/pairs/spade draws that will continue at least once, so we can get value and deny equity without bloating the pot too much with a weak kicker. Given BB’s short stack and the paired board, I’ll use a small sizing that keeps us flexible (and allows a disciplined fold if they check-jam, which is usually strong here).

Fold. I defended preflop and checked the turn as planned, but FiveTwo’s turn lead (450 into a 1,300-ish pot, now 1,750) gives poor pot odds. Calling 450 to win a 2,200 pot requires ≈20.5% equity (450/2200). With Q♥7♥ on 3♦3♠J♠A♣ I only have 6 clean outs (three queens + three sevens) → 6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river. That is well below the break‑even threshold, and even if I pair I can still be behind (Jx, A, or better pairs). Raising as a bluff is unattractive OOP — it risks a lot of chips and has limited fold equity against a preflop-raiser who’s shown multi-street aggression. Preserving my ~28.5BB stack for better spots is the correct EV play here, so I fold.

I hold [As, Qs] in UTG position with a stack of 4400 (44 BB). AQs is a premium starting hand that is well within the top of my range and a mandatory open from early position in a 6-handed game. My goal is to build the pot for value and seize the initiative. Looking at my previous history (Hand #1, #3, #7, #15, etc.), my standard opening size has consistently been 250 chips (2.5 BB). I will stick to this standard sizing to balance my range and extract value without committing too much of my stack pre-flop. If 3-bet, I am in a good position to call or 4-bet depending on the opponent.

A8o is too weak to flat versus a UTG open in a 6-max lineup: it’s often dominated by better aces, plays poorly multiway, and invites squeezes from the button/blinds. However, it has a valuable Ace blocker, and Pro has shown a tendency to open/fold to 3-bets, especially at these stack depths. I’ll take the higher-EV aggressive line and 3-bet as a semi-bluff to deny equity and win the pot preflop often, with a clear plan to fold versus a 4-bet/jam.

Facing an UTG open-raise from Pro (tight range likely) and a 3-bet to 850 from FiveTwo in middle position (even stronger range), Kc9s offsuit has poor playability and dominated equity against their ranges. My cutoff position doesn't justify a call or 4-bet with this marginal hand. Pot odds are insufficient (calling 850 into 1250 gives ~2.5:1, but equity too low), and folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this multi-table dynamic.
87 offsuit facing a 3-bet is a clear fold. This hand lacks the equity to profitably call 850 against a 3-bet range, even with position. Not suited, not connected enough to justify the investment.

Pro opened from UTG to 250 and FiveTwo 3-bet to 850. I’m in the small blind with Q3o — a hand that’s largely dominated by a typical UTG open + 3-bet range and has very poor post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 800 would commit ~29% of my 2800 stack into a multiway pot (and leave me with awkward SPR) for very little equity; a min‑raise would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity against these stack sizes. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Pro opened from UTG and FiveTwo 3-bet from middle position. J9o is a weak hand that does not play well against two potentially strong ranges, especially out of position. Given the 3-bet, the price to continue is too high for the equity this hand holds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold AQs in UTG and opened to 250. FiveTwo in Middle Position 3-bet to 850. FiveTwo has shown aggression previously (e.g., Hand #49 where they 3-bet me and I folded), so their range is likely wide enough to include weaker pairs or suited connectors, though it certainly contains premiums. With a stack of ~4150 (41.5 BB), 4-bet shoving is a viable option to deny equity and collect the pot, but it increases variance significantly as I am only called by better hands or flips. Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range in and see a flop with a strong hand, despite being out of position. The SPR post-flop will be approximately 2.2, which simplifies decision-making (essentially commit on top pair or strong draws). I choose to call to control variance and realize equity.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with Ace-Queen high on a Jack-Nine-Three rainbow board. The pot is 1850 and I have 3550 behind, creating an SPR of just under 2. The board texture favors the pre-flop 3-bettor's range (which includes JJ, 99, AA, KK, QQ, AJ). As the pre-flop caller, my standard line is to check to the aggressor. I have two overcards and some backdoor equity, but no made hand. Leading out (donk betting) would not be strategic here as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

In this 3-bet pot versus a short-stacked UTG caller, the J-9-3 rainbow flop connects reasonably well with his continuing range (Jx, 99/TT, some 9x, pocket pairs) and my A8 has very little immediate equity (no pair, no real draw). A small c-bet is likely to get peeled a lot due to his price and SPR, which sets up uncomfortable turn decisions and risks donating chips when he’s already somewhat committed.

Checking back keeps the pot controlled with a weak hand, protects my checking range (I can still have AK/AQ/overpairs), and lets me take stabs on favorable turns (A/K/Q, or if he checks twice) while comfortably folding to significant aggression on later streets.

Pre-flop I called a 3-bet with AQs. The flop J-3-9 checked through. The turn is a King (Kd), creating a board of J-3-9-K. This card favors the 3-bettor's range (AK, KQ, KK) significantly more than mine. While I have picked up a gutshot straight draw (need a Ten) and hold an overcard, I am currently losing to any pair. Leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to get folds from better hands (Kings or Jacks) and folds out worse hands. Checking is the standard line to potentially see a free river to hit my straight or Ace, or to evaluate the opponent's sizing if they decide to bet.

Pro’s double-check in a 3-bet pot caps him heavily toward pocket pairs (TT–QQ), some Jx/9x, and missed AQ-type hands. The K♦ is a strong range-advantage card for my 3-bet range (AK/KQ/overpairs) and is also a very credible delayed c-bet after checking back the flop. With A♣8♥ I have little showdown value, so the best way to realize equity is to apply pressure now and try to fold out his underpairs and some Jx that hate the king. I’ll use a medium sizing that actually creates fold equity versus his short stack without committing myself; if he check-jams, it’s almost always value and I can fold comfortably. If he calls, I’ll mostly shut down on blank rivers and continue only when improving (A) or on very favorable runouts.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with Ace-Queen high on a board of Jc 3h 9d Kd. I checked the flop and the opponent checked back. The turn brings a King, which hits the 3-bettor's range (AK, KQ, KK) hard. My opponent now bets 1000 into a pot of 1850. I only have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and potentially live Aces, but many of the opponent's value hands (AK, AA) dominate me. My equity is likely around 9-15%, which is not enough to call a bet of this size given the pot odds of roughly 2.85:1 (needing ~26% equity). Preserving my remaining stack of ~3550 is more important than chasing a thin draw in a spot where I am likely behind.

UTG 6-max, Q8o is too weak and dominated by the continuing ranges (Q9+/KQ/AQ, better eights) and plays poorly out of position in multiway pots. With several capable/aggressive opponents behind (and a couple of shorter stacks who can 3-bet jam), opening or limping creates tough, low-EV spots. Preserve stack and wait for better UTG opens.

69o is a marginal speculative hand at best, offsuit with a gap, from middle position. The pot is 150 with 100 to call (likely a limp or small action), offering ~2.5:1 but poor implied odds potential due to hand strength. No strong equity against calling ranges or position advantage. Stacks are deep (I'm at ~100BB), so fold to preserve stack and wait for premium hands or better spots in this multi-way limped scenario implied by pot size. Consistent with tight-aggressive tournament strategy to maximize long-term EV.
5h7s offsuit is a weak hand from the cutoff with 3 players still to act behind me. Not suited, poor connectivity, no high cards. Easy fold - no reason to get involved with this marginal holding.

Button with Ah8s and a 2,800 stack (~28bb). This is a strong, playable suited Ace in position and a clear open-raise spot against only the blinds. I want fold equity and to play postflop in position, but keep the pot controllable given a very deep SB (Flash) and a competent BB (Pro). A standard 2x open to 200 total accomplishes that: it denies cheap flops to the blinds, isolates or takes the blinds down, and preserves stack depth if I face resistance. Plan: if both fold I take the pot; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and play straightforwardly; if I face a large 3-bet/shove I’ll fold to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-3 suited. The Button (Minni) has raised to 200. Although I have a massive chip lead and it only costs 150 more to call, 8-3 suited is a very weak hand to play out of position against a short-stacked opponent who might be looking to commit their remaining 2,600 chips. Additionally, there is a player left to act in the Big Blind. Folding is the most prudent play to maintain my stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Deuces (2s 2d). The player on the Button (Minni) has min-raised to 200. The Small Blind folded. I need to call 100 chips into a pot of 350, giving me pot odds of 3.5 to 1 (requiring ~22% equity). Against a standard wide button opening range, 22 has sufficient equity (~45-50%) to justify a call, despite being out of position and the hand being difficult to play post-flop. The price is too good to fold a pocket pair. I will call and look to set-mine; if I miss the set on the flop, I will likely check-fold to aggression.

I have flopped bottom set (2s 2d) on a dangerous monotone board (7c 9c 2c). While my hand is very strong, the presence of three clubs makes it vulnerable to flushes or strong draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni on the Button), the standard play is to check. This allows Minni to make a continuation bet with her bluffs or value hands. If I were to lead out (donk bet), I risk folding out her air range that might bluff, and I isolate myself against made flushes or strong draws. Checking keeps her range wide and allows me to evaluate her action. If she bets, I can decide whether to call or raise based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the optimal starting action.

I opened to 200 from the button with Ah8s and Pro (BB) just called. On 7c9c2c I have only Ace-high and no club, so this is a marginal holding — but as the preflop aggressor I can apply pressure and pick off many of the BB’s defending hands (overcards, weak pairs, unsuited broadways). I’ll make a small continuation bet ~1/3 pot (150 chips) to buy the pot often while keeping the size manageable. This sizing gives fold equity vs wide calls but lets me fold to a sizable check-raise or shove without committing too much. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn: continue only on safe, non-club bricks or when I improve (Ace), otherwise proceed cautiously or check back and realize equity in position.

I have flopped a set of Deuces on a 7c-9c-2c board. This is a very strong hand but vulnerable on a monotone board. Facing a small c-bet of 150 (1/3 pot) from Minni on the button, I have two main options: call or raise. Raising (check-raising) would charge draws (single high clubs) and get value from top pairs, but it risks isolating me against made flushes or higher sets, while folding out her bluffs. Calling keeps her range wide, allowing her to continue bluffing or value-betting worse hands. Given the stack depths (SPR ~3) and the wetness of the board, I prefer to call to keep the pot manageable and reassess on the turn. If the board pairs, I have a monster; if a fourth club hits, I can proceed cautiously. The price is excellent to see a turn card.

I hold bottom set on a board that is very wet (monotone clubs). The King on the turn hits the opponent's range well (Button opening range), making it likely they will bet if they have Top Pair or better, or continue semi-bluffing with a high club. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs and isolate me against flushes or higher sets. Checking allows the opponent to hang themselves with bluffs or value bet a King, keeping the pot manageable if I am behind a flush while retaining the opportunity to fill up to a full house.

Check. I have only Ace-high with no club on a 7c-9c-2c flop; Pro called my small c-bet which means their range includes lots of club flushes, one-pair hands and some draws. The Kd on the turn doesn’t help me and actually improves many of Pro’s calling hands (Kx); betting here would be a bluff into a range that beats me too often. Pot control and position are the highest EV lines: I’ll check the turn to see the river and preserve chips. If Pro fires a sizeable bet I’ll give up (they’ll often have a flush or a pair); if the river pairs my Ace I can look to extract value.

I hold a set of Deuces on a board of [7c 9c 2c Kd 3s]. The river 3s is a brick, changing nothing. The opponent (Minni) checked back the Turn, which strongly suggests she does not hold a flush or a very strong hand, capping her range to mostly one-pair hands (like Top Pair with a King, a 9, or a pocket pair) or missed draws. Given the scary nature of the monotone flop, she is unlikely to value bet these one-pair hands if I check, likely opting to check back for showdown. Therefore, I must bet to extract value. A bet of around half-pot (350 into 750) targets her Top Pair (Kx) hands and perhaps some sticky mid-pairs, while being small enough to look like a potential blocking bet or bluff.

Pot is 1100 after Pro’s river bet of 350; calling 350 to win 1100 gives ~24% break-even equity. My hand (Ah8s) is just Ace-high and I have no clubs, so I don’t block the many club-flush combos. Pro’s line (call preflop, call small flop c-bet, check-turn, small river bet) is much more consistent with thin value (Kx, 9x/7x, sets, or completed two-club combos) than with a large bluff range. I only beat pure air/missed bluffs and a few turned-down marginal hands — likely well under the ~24% I need. Raising as a bluff is unattractive because I have no club blockers and would risk a sizable portion of my ~24.5bb stack for little fold equity. For stack preservation and long-term EV, fold this river bet.

Th6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, dominated by most calling ranges, and low equity multiway. Acting UTG in a 6-handed game, folding is standard to avoid playing out of position post-flop. Pot odds to limp (100 into 150) are poor given the hand's playability issues, and no raise incentive exists. Prioritize stack preservation for premium hands in tournament with 60 hands left.
32 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. No reason to play this from middle position with players behind me.

Cutoff with Q♠9♦ (offsuit) in an unopened pot. This is a hand with decent heads-up playability and good blocking value to Qx hands, so I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds. I’ll open-raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to reduce multiway action and build a pot when I’m likely ahead; my stack (~24.5 BB) still leaves room to play postflop. Plan: continuation bet favorable flops, play cautiously if I face a substantial 3‑bet (fold to large 3‑bets, consider calling small 3‑bets with positional advantage and strong draws or top pair).

I am on the button with T7o. Minni, who is currently the table's shortest stack with about 21 big blinds, has raised from the cutoff to 300. T7o is a very weak hand that does not play well against a raising range, even from late position. Given the stack sizes and the fact that I have the chip lead, there is no reason to get involved in a pot with a marginal hand against a short stack who is likely to be committed to the pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 4c 2d in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni in the Cutoff, I am getting poor pot odds (calling 250 to win 450) and will be out of position. Even against a wide stealing range, my equity is too low to justify a call, and 3-bet bluffing a short stack (Minni has ~21BB) is risky as they may be committed. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Minni is opening 3x from the cutoff with only ~21.5bb, which generally means a fairly value-heavy/stronger range and she can comfortably jam over any 3-bet. Q4o is dominated by her Qx/Kx/Ax opens and plays very poorly at the low SPR we’d create by calling. The pot odds (200 to win 650) aren’t enough to justify defending this offsuit trash versus a short-stack raiser, so I’ll decline the spot and preserve chips for better BB defenses.
8d5s is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. From early position, I need a much stronger hand to open. This is an easy fold.

UTG folded and I’m in middle with K♥3♥ and ~26BB. This is a very weak, easily‑dominated hand that performs poorly out of position and in multiway pots. There’s 100 to call preflop; calling 100 just to limp invites raises from the big stacks behind (Flash/Pro) and leaves me with difficult post‑flop decisions. A tiny raise to 200 is thin and would get 3‑bet or commit too many chips with a poor kicker. I’ll conserve chips and fold — wait for a better spot or position to open‑raise.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Nine offsuit. With the players in early and middle positions folding, I have a potential opportunity to steal the blinds. However, Ten-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand that doesn't perform well against a 3-bet or when called out of position (if the Button calls). Pro, the Button, has a stack of 3,900 (39 BB) which is a prime size for a re-shove over a late-position open. Additionally, FiveTwo and Elon are competent deep stacks. I will choose the disciplined path and fold this hand, looking for a better spot to exert my chip lead.

I hold Pocket Tens [Th Td] on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. The action has folded to me. With 39 BBs, this is a clear value open. I want to build the pot and play in position against the blinds or take down the pot pre-flop. A standard raise size of 2.5 BB (250 chips) is appropriate to balance my range and offer a poor price to the blinds while extracting value.

Button open to 2.5bb is wide and we’re getting a decent price (200 to win 400 now, with implied odds when we make a flush/straight). 6h4h has good playability and can realize equity via strong draws, and I don’t want to 3-bet a hand with poor blockers and then have to fold to a short-stack 4-bet jam from Pro or get played back at by the BB. Calling keeps the pot manageable and protects our SB defending range versus frequent steals.

Preflop in BB (out of position) with K6o, facing a button raise to 250 by Pro and a flat call by FiveTwo in SB. Pot is 600, call 150 for ~20% pot odds, but K6o is a weak offsuit king-high hand with poor kicker. Button range is wide, SB defend range also wide, but multiway OOP postflop playability is terrible. Equity too low against two opponents, better to fold and wait for better spots with deep stack (9850). Preserve chips for tournament survival.

We’re out of position versus the Button’s wide opening range on a fairly dry 9-5-2 flop that still favors the preflop raiser (lots of overpairs, 9x, and high-card hands that can c-bet). Our 6h4h has only a gutshot to the 3 plus very thin runner-runner heart equity, so it’s not strong enough to inflate the pot by donk-leading. Checking keeps our range protected (we can have sets/two pair/strong 9x) and lets us respond appropriately to Pro’s sizing (continue versus small c-bets at a good price, and fold versus larger pressure).

I hold an overpair (Tens) on a dry, unconnected flop of 9-5-2. This is a very favorable board for my hand. My opponent in the Small Blind likely has a wide range including pairs like 66-88, top pair 9x, or overcards. I want to bet for value to extract chips from these worse hands and to protect against the roughly 30% of the deck (J, Q, K, A) that are overcards to my pair. A bet of around 40% of the pot (250 chips) is large enough to build the pot but small enough to keep their calling range wide.

Pro’s small c-bet (250 into ~600) is likely range-wide from the Button. With 6h4h on 9c-2s-5h we have a gutshot to the 3 (4 outs) plus backdoor-heart potential and some ability to win later when he checks back turns. We’re getting a good price (250 to win ~1100 after we call) and have strong implied odds versus his shorter stack when a 3 arrives, so folding is too tight. Raising is poor because our draw is weak and he can jam over it, forcing us to burn chips. Best is to call and continue only on favorable turns (3/heart/7, etc.) while check-folding to heavier pressure on bricks.

Turn pairs the 2, which is a relatively good card for our SB defending range (we can have more 2x than the Button) but we still have very little equity with 6h4h—just the gutshot to a 3 (and no meaningful flush draw). Leading as a bluff is unattractive because we don’t block his continuing range, and if we bet and get raised/jammed by his short stack we’re forced to fold after investing chips. Checking keeps our range protected, lets Pro continue barreling his air (or check back and allow us to realize our small equity), and preserves our ability to respond based on his sizing.

I hold Pocket Tens on a board of [9c, 2s, 5h, 2c]. The turn pairs the board with a Deuce. This card is generally safe for my range and keeps my overpair ahead of most of the opponent's holdings (9x, 88-66, floats). Betting here might fold out weaker hands that I want to keep in, such as random overcards or small pocket pairs that are wavering. By checking back, I control the pot size (SPR is roughly 3), disguise the strength of my hand, and induce potential bluffs on the river from missed floats. It also allows me to comfortably call a river bet or value bet the river myself if checked to. There aren't many scary river cards besides an Ace or King, so protection is less critical than pot control and extracting value from a wide range.

Rivered complete air with 6-high; checking just gives up and we almost never win at showdown after calling a c-bet. Pro’s line (small flop c-bet, then check back the paired turn) weights him toward unpaired overcards and medium-strength hands that didn’t want to build the pot, so this is a good spot to apply pressure. Our story is credible: from the SB we can easily have slow-played trips (A2s/32s/42s/62s), boats (22/55), or value like 98/9x that checked turn for pot control on the paired board. A ~3/4-pot bet targets folds from his AK/AQ/KQ/JT type hands and some underpairs, while still looking like value rather than a desperation stab.

The river card (8c) is unfavorable for my hand (Pocket Tens), as it completes the 6-7 straight and brings in a backdoor club flush. My opponent's line of check-calling the flop, checking the turn, and then leading out for a large bet (900 into ~1100) on the river is polarizing. This line typically represents strong value (flush, straight, or trips) or a bluff exploiting my turn check. Without a club in my hand to block the flush, and given the wet board texture favoring the caller's range, my hand is effectively a bluff-catcher. The bet size is significant (~26% of my remaining stack), and calling here is high-risk. I choose to fold and preserve my 34BB stack for a better spot.

2♠5♦ is one of the worst hands to play, especially UTG. There are five players to act behind me and my stack (~26bb) means I don’t want to get involved with a dominated hand or limp into a multiway pot. There’s no raise yet (pot is just the blinds), so the only reasonable options are limp, raise, or fold — limp/raise with 2-5 offsuit is a negative-EV play. I’ll conserve chips and fold, waiting for a much stronger spot or position to try stealing blinds.

Ace-Four offsuit is generally too weak to open from Middle Position in a 6-max game. With several aggressive players still to act behind me, including some shorter stacks who may be looking to jam, folding is the most prudent play to maintain my chip lead.

I am in the Cutoff with 2h 4d, which is 4-high unsuited and one of the weakest starting hands in the deck. With 34 big blinds, I am not in a desperate push/fold situation, but this hand has virtually no playability or equity. Even in late position, opening this hand is a losing play against three players yet to act. The only standard and correct decision is to fold.

We’re on the button after three folds with A♠T♠, a strong, playable hand that performs well in position and benefits from fold equity against the blinds. With only the SB/BB left to act, opening is clearly higher EV than flatting (which would invite multiway and under-realize equity). I’ll use a standard small open to pressure their ranges while keeping the pot manageable versus potential 3-bets from two capable stacks.

Preflop from SB facing a button raise to 2.5bb (250). My hand Qc5h is offsuit, unconnected, and queen-high with a weak kicker, making it unplayable post-flop even against a wide button opening range. Pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 into 400), but implied odds are poor due to reverse position and low equity/multiway potential with BB behind. FiveTwo has been active (multiple raises in history), but folding trash preserves stack in a tournament with 58 hands left. Standard fold to avoid spew.
J9o in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting good pot odds (150 to win 400) and this is a standard defend against a wide button opening range. J9o plays reasonably well postflop. Easy call.

Flopped top pair with J9 on Jc3c6h. I'm first to act. With the flush draw on board and top pair, I should lead for value and protection. Betting around 1/3-1/2 pot to extract value from worse pairs and charge draws.

Claude donk-leads very small (200 into 750) on Jc-3c-6h. With A♠T♠ we have two overcards to many BB hands (pairs/weak Jx), position, and can realize equity well. Raising would be a pure bluff that risks getting played back by Jx/sets and doesn’t improve our hand’s equity realization as much as simply floating in position versus a likely wide/weak blocker bet. Calling keeps his bluffs and weaker made hands in, and we can take it away on many turns if he checks or his sizing stays weak.

Turn is 8s, board is Jc3c6h8s. I have top pair with J9. FiveTwo flatted my flop bet which could be a club draw, a weaker jack, a pocket pair, or a float. The 8s doesn't complete any obvious draws. I should continue betting for value against worse Jx and to charge the flush draw. Betting around 50-60% pot.

Claude continues the donk line with a smallish turn bet (500 into 1450). Our raw river equity with A♠T♠ is only ~6 outs, but we’re in position and this sizing keeps his range wide (one-pair hands, 8x that just paired, underpairs, and club draws). Raising would need a lot of folds and gets called too often by Jx/8x and any club draw. Calling lets us realize our overcard equity and retains the option to win on many rivers when he checks (or to value when we spike A/T).

River is 7d completing the board Jc3c6h8s7d. I have top pair with J9. The 7d completes straights for T9 (7-8-9-T-J) and 54 (4-5-6-7-8). It also gives 77 trips. The club draw bricked. FiveTwo has called flop and turn bets - their range includes club draws (now busted), worse Jx, pocket pairs, and possibly straights/sets. Betting here for value is risky as I only get called by better. Checking for pot control is best - I can call a reasonable bet or check back.

Claude’s river check after leading small on flop/turn looks like a capped, marginal one-pair range (6x/8x/weak Jx) and some give-ups. With A♠T♠ we have essentially no showdown value versus any pair, so checking back only wins when he has pure air. Our line (BTN open, call-call) credibly represents strong Jx, sets (33/66/77/88), and the occasional straight, so we can apply pressure to his medium pairs that don’t want to face a sizable river bet. A ~2/3 pot sizing targets folds from 3x/6x/8x and some thin Jx while not risking an unnecessarily large portion of our stack.

River 7d completes straights for T9 (6-7-8-9-T) and 54 (4-5-6-7-8). FiveTwo bet 1300 into ~1950. I'm getting 4550:1300 = ~3.5:1 odds, needing ~28% equity. Their value range includes straights (T9 very common in button range) and two pairs (87, J8, J7). However, they also have many busted club draws that could be bluffing here (AcXc, KcXc, QcXc, etc.). Given the pot odds and the frequency of busted draws in their range, this is a marginal call but the price is too good to fold top pair.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 6d 3s. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em and should never be played from this position, especially in a 6-max game. My stack is currently the largest at the table, and there is no strategic reason to risk any chips with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the only sensible move.

I am holding 7s Td in Middle Position. This is a weak off-suit hand with very low potential to make strong hands like straights or flushes, and it is easily dominated by higher cards. With 34 Big Blinds, my stack is healthy enough that I do not need to take desperate risks. Acting early with several players behind me, including aggressive stacks, makes this an easy fold.

Folded to us in the cutoff with only three players left to act, so this is a good spot to apply pressure and try to pick up the blinds/antes-equivalent dead money. 7c4c isn’t a hand I want to limp with (it plays poorly multiway), but as a late-position open it can show a profit via fold equity and positional advantage. I’ll use a standard-but-slightly-larger-than-min open to discourage loose blind calls, especially vs the BB who is shorter and may play more straightforwardly. If faced with a 3-bet or shove, I’m folding.

On the button facing a standard cutoff open-raise to 2.5BB from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in previous hands. Ts7d is offsuit but has straight potential (78TJ9), decent playability, and good position postflop. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400+250=650, ~2.6:1 immediate). Defending wide in position against a likely wide CO range maximizes EV by realizing equity and using position to bluff/catch. Folding too tight here; 3-betting marginal as hand doesn't block strong hands well. Plan: call, see flop, play aggressively if hits or as float.

J8o is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a raise and a call. I'm out of position against two players with a marginal hand. This is an easy fold - the hand doesn't play well multiway OOP and isn't strong enough to 3-bet.

Facing a cutoff open to 250 with the button calling, I'm in the BB with Jc3c and a 2,500 stack (25BB). The pot is 650 and calling costs 150 more, so I need ~18.8% equity (150/(650+150)) to make a pure call profitable. J3s is a weak, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated by much of the raiser/caller ranges and loses significant equity multiway and out of position. A small raise to 400 isn’t a value play and would only bloat the pot without a real chance to win it; shoving is too spewy given our holding and stack. With better spots to pick off and the need to protect my tournament life/chips, the correct play is to fold and wait for a stronger situation.

We’re heads-up but out of position versus the Button on an A-Q, two-heart flop. While this texture is generally good for the preflop raiser’s range, our exact hand (7c4c) has essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no heart, no meaningful straight draw) and very poor ability to continue versus a bet/raise. C-betting here will get called a lot by Elon's wide preflop continuing range (Ax, Qx, any heart draw, pairs), and we’ll be forced to give up too often after investing chips. Checking keeps our range protected, avoids lighting money on fire with pure air, and allows us to realize our slim backdoor equity if he checks back; versus a bet we can comfortably check-fold.

Flop is Ad Qh 3h, a coordinated board with flush draw potential and A-Q top pair blocker. FiveTwo (CO raiser) checks to me on the button. My Ts7d has no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (guts not even present), and minimal equity against his continuing range, which likely includes Ax, Qx, broadway, and some draws. Bluffing here is suboptimal as my hand blocks few strong hands and has zero showdown value. Checking maintains pot control, realizes any tiny equity for free, and keeps position for turn play. If he bets turn, I can fold easily. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better, folding out his worse nothing unnecessarily given tournament stage and stack sizes (effective ~10k). Preflop defend was positional, but postflop discipline avoids spew.

Turn brings the 5c, giving us only a small gutshot (a 6 makes 3-4-5-6-7) and otherwise we’re still pure air with no flush draw. After flop went check/check on A-Q-two-hearts, Elon’s range is often weighted to hands with showdown value (Ax/Qx), plus some hearts that can comfortably continue versus a bet. Bluffing here will get called too frequently and forces us into ugly river decisions with very little equity. Best line is to keep the pot small, realize our limited equity, and be ready to check-fold to meaningful pressure.

FiveTwo, the CO preflop raiser, has checked twice on a flop of Ad Qh 3h (ace-high with heart draw) and turn 5c (blank completing some gutshots but rainbow). His range likely includes missed Ax, weak Qx, broadway that missed, or cautious medium strength, as strong hands often bet flop for value/protection. My Ts7d is pure air: no pair, no draws, minimal showdown. However, with button position, double-checked-to-me spot, deep stacks (~95BB effective), and tournament stage (93/150 hands), a bluff bet exploits his likely capped range. Ts blocks some broadway/fold equity hands like KT/KJ. Bet 1/3 pot (~325 rounds to 300 min raise but sizing 400 for fold equity) to fold out his worse (Jx, weak draws, missed overs). If called, shut down river; if raised, fold easily. Checking realizes no equity but misses stealing opportunity against passive line. Bluffing maximizes EV by denying equity and building stack.

Elon’s 400 bet into 1050 gives me ~27.6% pot odds, but with 7c4c on A♦Q♥3♥5♣ I only have a gutshot to a 6 (4 outs ≈ 8–9% equity) and no flush draw. Calling is -EV unless I can reliably win a big extra chunk when I hit, which is unlikely because many of his betting hands (air, missed hearts) won’t pay, and Qx may not continue.

Turning this into a bluff-raise also isn’t attractive: after I’ve checked flop and turn, a raise needs very high immediate fold equity to justify the risk, and his range contains plenty of Ax that won’t fold. The disciplined, highest-EV line is to check-fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in UTG position with ATs (Ace-Ten suited), which is a premium suited connector and strong enough to open from early position in a 6-handed game. My stack is 34 BB, allowing for post-flop play. Limping invites aggression and folding is too conservative for this hand strength. A standard opening raise of 2.5x BB (250 chips) is consistent with my previous play style (e.g., raising 250 with 55, KQ, etc.) and balances my range. I intend to play the hand aggressively if I hit the board or check-fold if I miss against significant resistance.

Facing an UTG open to 250 in a 6-handed game, 6-2 offsuit is far too weak to continue from middle position. It plays poorly multiway, is frequently dominated when it makes a pair, and has minimal high-card/straight potential. Even with decent immediate pot odds, the reverse implied odds versus a strong UTG range make this a clear fold.

3c5h is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a standard UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) from Pro, who has been shown to raise with a decent range from early position based on hand history. My position in cutoff is good for stealing blinds but not for calling or raising into an early position raiser with this holding. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 400), and implied odds are minimal given the hand's weakness postflop. With 102BB deep stack and tournament late stage (hand 94/150), folding preserves stack for better spots. No exploitative reads on Pro loosening up UTG here justify playing.

KQo on the button facing UTG open of 250. This is a premium hand in position - ideal spot for a 3-bet. Pro has a shorter stack (3150) so I want to build value but not blow him off. Standard 3-bet sizing to around 750.

I have 3♠3♥ in the small blind facing a button 3‑bet to 750 after an UTG open to 250. My stack is ~2450 (~24.5bb), the pot is 1150 and it costs me 700 to call. Calling here would leave me ~1750 behind and produce an SPR ≈ 1, so I won’t have room to realize set equity post‑flop and I’ll be playing OOP against a 3‑bettor (and possibly the original raiser if he calls). A min‑4bet to 1250 is too small to generate fold equity and a shove is too high variance with a small pair against likely stronger ranges. Standard, lower-variance play is to fold and wait for a better spot rather than committing 700 chips into a multiway/OOP situation with marginal set‑mining prospects.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. There has been an UTG raise from Pro and a 3-bet from Claude on the Button. My hand is very weak and I would be playing out of position against potentially strong ranges. This is a straightforward fold to preserve my stack.

I opened to 250 from UTG with AhTh and faced a 3-bet to 750 from Claude on the Button. Claude has been an active 3-bettor in this tournament. I am getting roughly 2.3 to 1 pot odds (calling 500 to win 1150), needing about 30% equity. Ace-Ten suited plays reasonably well against a wide Button 3-bet range and retains potential for strong draws and top pairs. Folding is too tight given the price and opponent profile, and 4-bet jamming is an overplay with this specific hand. I will call and evaluate the flop, noting the SPR will be around 1.6, making post-flop decisions relatively straightforward.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with an SPR of approximately 1.6. The board is low (8-4-2) with a flush draw. I hold Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. It is standard strategy to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) to see if he continues. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much here as I likely fold out worse hands and get called by better. I will check and evaluate his bet sizing.

Flop is 8s 2h 4s - completely missed my KQo but this is a very dry board. Pro checked to me after calling my 3-bet. As the preflop aggressor with position, I should c-bet this board that favors my range. Pro has only 2650 behind with 1650 in pot - he's fairly committed. A standard 1/2 pot bet of ~825 puts pressure while leaving room to fold to a check-raise.

I opened pre-flop with AhTh and called a 3-bet from Claude. The flop comes 8s 2h 4s, which misses my hand completely, leaving me with just Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw. Claude bets 825 (half-pot) into the 1650 pot. With only 2650 chips behind, calling represents committing over 30% of my remaining stack with a marginal hand out of position. My equity against a typical 3-bet range (which includes overpairs like 99+, and strong hands like AK/AQ which dominate me) is poor. While Claude is aggressive, floating out of position with just overcards and a runner-runner draw is not profitable. Preserving my 26.5 BB stack is the better strategic play for the tournament longevity.

6-handed UTG with ATo is strong enough to open for value and initiative. Our stack is deep (~106bb) while several opponents are 24–26bb and may reshove; opening keeps our range strong, and ATo has good blockers/realization while still being able to fold to very tight 3-bets or continue versus reasonable short-stack jams depending on player/range. Use a standard open size that pressures blinds without overcommitting versus reshoves.

J6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by any competent UTG opening range from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive historically. In middle position with 6 players, calling or raising offers no equity advantage, poor implied odds, and risks playing out of position post-flop. Folding preserves our deep stack (102 BB) for better spots in a tournament with 55 hands left to maximize stack growth.

Pocket 5s in the cutoff facing a UTG open of 250. With deep stacks (14k effective), this is a good set-mining spot. The implied odds are favorable - if I hit a set, I can potentially win a big pot. 3-betting is an option but 55 plays better as a call to see a flop cheaply.

Facing an UTG open to 250 with the cutoff calling, I’m being asked to act on the button with 4♠T♣ — a weak offsuit hand against an UTG range. There are still the small and big blinds to act behind (one with a very deep stack, the other with a similar short stack), so any raise risks a 3-bet or isolation against stronger hands. With ~24.5bb effective I should conserve chips for better spots and avoid playing a dominated hand multiway out of position postflop. Folding preserves my stack and waiting for a better opportunity on the button.

I am holding 3s 2d in the small blind, which is the weakest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. FiveTwo has raised from UTG and Claude has called from the cutoff. Although the pot odds are somewhat attractive to call (200 to win 650), playing 32 offsuit out of position against an early position raiser and a caller is a losing proposition in the long run. My goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands, and wasting chips on bottom-tier hands in poor position is counterproductive.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 7s (67o) facing an UTG raise to 2.5BB and a Cutoff call. The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot that will be 800, requiring ~18.75% equity). However, 67o is a very weak hand to play multi-way out of position. My equity against reasonable opening and calling ranges is likely around 20-25%, but my equity realization factor will be poor due to being out of position and the hand's lack of playability (offsuit). With a stack of 25.5BB, preserving chips is preferable to engaging in a marginal to negative EV spot. The prudent play is to fold.

Heads-up OOP vs a CO flat, A55 is a board where ranges are ace-heavy and our exact hand (AT) is strong but not a great value-better: any AJ+/boats have us in rough shape, while worse hands often won’t put much money in if we lead. By checking we (1) keep the pot controlled with a medium-strength showdown hand, (2) protect our checking range (we can have strong aces/trips too), and (3) give Claude room to stab with missed broadways, pocket pairs, and spade floats/draws that might otherwise fold to a small c-bet. Plan is to check-call most reasonable bets and re-evaluate turn sizing/textures.

I flopped QUADS with 55 on a 55A board! This is the absolute nuts. FiveTwo opened UTG and checked to me. I need to extract maximum value. A small bet looks like a probe/steal attempt and may induce a call or raise from an Ace or pocket pair. Betting around 200-250 keeps them in while building the pot.

Claude’s 200 into 850 is a small stab that can come from a very wide range (underpairs, broadway floats, spade draws, some weak aces). With AcTs on A55 we have a strong bluff-catcher/showdown hand but not a hand that wants to build a big pot: when we raise, worse hands often fold and better aces/5x/slowplayed monsters continue. Calling keeps his bluffs in, controls pot size, and lets us re-evaluate on turns (especially spades/broadways) while still being able to value-catch on bricks.

Turn 8c doesn’t meaningfully improve our AcTs: we still have a medium-strength top pair on a paired board (A55) where villain can have a lot of stronger Ax (AJ+), some 5x, and occasional slowplays. If we lead, worse hands (broadway floats, underpairs, missed spades) often just fold, while better hands continue—so betting is mostly isolating ourselves versus the top of his range. Checking keeps the pot controlled, protects our checking range (we can have strong Ax/boats too), and gives Claude the chance to keep bluffing or value-betting worse Ax. Plan: check and bluff-catch reasonable river/turn barrels, folding only to very large/polar sizing from a tight line.

I have QUADS - the absolute nuts. FiveTwo called my flop bet, likely holding an Ace, pocket pair, or spade draw. I need to continue extracting value. A medium-sized bet (~50% pot) looks like standard value betting and should get called by Ax hands or draws. Betting around 500 keeps them engaged without scaring them off.

Claude’s line (flat pre, small flop stab, then ~1/2 pot turn) can still be very wide: a lot of Ax (including weaker A2–A9), some slowplayed 5x/boats, occasional 88, plus bluffs/semi-bluffs from spade holdings and random broadways/pairs that took a stab on the flop. With AcTs on A55-8 we’re a strong bluff-catcher but not a hand that wants to raise—raising mostly folds out the bluffs/worse and gets action from better Ax/5x. Getting ~3:1 (525 to win 1575) we only need ~25% equity, which we should have versus his betting range. Plan: call and evaluate river; continue versus small/medium bets, but be willing to fold to a large polarized river barrel.

We’re first to act on the river after check-calling flop and turn on A55. With AcTs we have a decent showdown hand but it’s not strong enough to build a bigger pot: Claude can easily have better Ax (AJ+), occasional 5x/boats, and his bluffs will often fire when checked to. Leading risks (1) getting raised off our equity by polarized value, and (2) folding out the very bluffs we want to capture while only getting thin calls from worse Ax. Best is to check, keep his range wide, and bluff-catch versus reasonable sizing.

I have QUADS - the absolute nuts. FiveTwo has checked to me on the river with a pot of 2100. They've called flop and turn bets, suggesting they likely have an Ace or possibly made a full house with A8, A3, 88, or 33. I need to size my value bet to maximize extraction - too small leaves money on the table, too big might fold out weaker Ax. Around 65-70% pot (~1400) should get called by most Ax hands, and if they have a boat they might even raise.

We’re getting 1400 to win 4900 (~28.6% needed). On A55-8-3 with AcTs, our hand is effectively AA55 with a T kicker: we beat all the suited/weak Ax (A2–A9) that Claude can plausibly flat pre and then value-bet, and we also beat all his missed spade/broadway stabs. We mainly lose to AJ+ (and occasional A5/5x/slowplayed monster), but his line (small flop stab → medium turn → 2/3 pot river) can contain plenty of thin value and bluffs, and our hand is near the top of our bluff-catching range after checking all streets. Folding would be too tight given the price and how many worse Ax/bluffs exist; raising just isolates vs better. Call.

Pocket 4s UTG in a 6-handed game with deep stacks (102BB effective for me). Standard play is to open-raise small pairs like 44 from early position to take initiative, build the pot for set-mining value, or steal dead money from blinds. Minni and Pro are short-stacked (~24-25BB), so they might shove light, but overall range supports a raise. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is sized appropriately to discourage multiway pots while keeping fold equity. Limping is too passive UTG; folding is too tight.
K7o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. UTG typically has a strong range, and K7o plays poorly against that range. Not worth calling or 3-betting here - easy fold.

Call 300. A♥2♣ is marginal but worth defending from the cutoff against an UTG 3bb open with ~24.5BB effective. Calling keeps me in position postflop (I act after Elon), preserves stack depth and lets me realize equity (can make top pair or use position to bluff/counterplay). A tiny 3‑bet to 500 is too thin and will often just invite a 4‑bet or a squeeze from the deep button; shoving ~24BB is overcommitting with a dominated Ace. Plan: see the flop, play aggressively if I hit an Ace or favorable draw, pot‑control on dry boards, and fold to large 4‑bets/squeezes.

I am holding Queen-Five suited on the button. While I have a significant chip lead and the button position, an UTG raise from Elon generally represents a stronger range than Q5s can comfortably play against. Minni has already called and is very short-stacked (roughly 21 BBs total), which increases the likelihood of her being pot-committed or shoving on the flop. Pro in the Small Blind is also short-stacked and might look for a squeeze opportunity. Given the strength of an UTG opening range and the potential for multi-way action with short stacks, Q5s is a fold here to avoid unnecessary variance.

I hold [4d 9d] in the Small Blind facing a 3BB raise from UTG (Elon) and a call from the Cutoff (Minni). My hand is a weak suited two-gapper. Although the pot odds are somewhat tempting (calling 250 to win a pot that will be 1000+), playing this hand out of position against an UTG opening range and a caller is a losing play in the long run due to poor equity realization and reverse implied odds. With a stack of 25BB, I cannot afford to bleed chips in marginal spots. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

We’re getting a very good price: 200 to win 750 (~21% needed). QJs suited has plenty of equity versus an UTG open plus a caller and realizes well with strong top-pair potential, straight/flush draws, and good playability. A squeeze 3-bet is higher variance here because (1) UTG’s range is stronger, and (2) Minni’s short stack can shove over the top, putting us in an awkward spot with a hand that’s good but not great versus a jam/call range. Best is to defend and take a flop, then proceed aggressively on favorable textures (spade boards, broadway-heavy boards) and more cautiously when we miss.

Multiway pot versus an UTG opener and a caller on 3h-4h-2s. With QsJs we have two overcards plus backdoor spades and some backdoor straight potential, but no immediate draw and we’re out of position. Donk-betting here risks getting raised (including by Minni’s short stack) and forced to fold equity, while UTG’s range contains many overpairs and strong heart combos that will continue. Best is to check and realize our equity, evaluating versus sizing/frequency if Elon c-bets, and taking stabs later on favorable turns if it checks through.

Flopped top set of 4s on a highly draw-heavy flop (3h 4h 2s) with heart flush draw and wheel straight draws possible (e.g., 5x, A5). FiveTwo checked from BB, which is standard. Pot is 950, I'm first to act post-flop among active players (Minni still to act). Must bet for value against weaker pairs, floats, and overpairs, and to protect against draws. Checking is too passive and invites free cards to draws. Betting 2/3 pot (~630) balances value and protection; Minni short-stacked (~18.5BB effective after pre-flop) may shove light but our hand crushes her range. FiveTwo deeper but checked weak. This builds the pot while denying equity.

I have A♥2♣ — a pair of deuces with the nut ace kicker on a 3♥-4♥-2♠ flop. Elon opened UTG and now makes a modest c‑bet (600). Pot is 1550; calling 600 gives me roughly 2750 to win, so I need ~22% equity to break even. My hand is often ahead of a large portion of his c‑betting range (overcards, many missed hands and semi‑bluffs), I block A♥ combos, and I have useful turn/river equity (wheel with a 5, backdoor hearts). Raising to 1200 is too committal from my ~21.5BB stack and exposes me to costly 3‑bets/squeezes from deeper stacks; folding is too weak given my showdown value and pot odds. Plan: call 600, keep the pot manageable and use position to fold to heavy turn aggression if I don't improve, or press for value if I pair the Ace/2 or pick up favorable action.

We’re getting an excellent price: 600 to win a pot of ~2150 (need ~22%). Even multiway on 3h-4h-2s, QsJs has 2 clean-ish overcards plus backdoor spade/straight runouts that can pick up equity on many turns (spade, broadway, 5/6/A/K/T). Raising is high variance and unlikely to fold out UTG’s overpairs/strong heart combos, and Minni’s short stack can easily jam, forcing us into an awkward spot with a marginal semi-bluff. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Best is to call and re-evaluate turns—continuing aggressively when we improve/pick up strong equity, and exiting on heavy pressure on bad runouts (hearts/low cards that connect with their ranges).

Turn Th is a very bad card for our exact hand: we still have pure overcards with no heart, and the third heart completes many draws that both Elon (UTG c-bettor) and Minni (short-stack caller) can easily have. Donk-bluffing here is high variance without a heart blocker and risks getting called/raised by overpairs with a heart, made flushes, sets, or getting jammed on by Minni (even if small). Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and be prepared to fold versus meaningful pressure unless we improve on the river.

Top set of 4s remains extremely strong on this turn board (3h4h2sTh), crushing overpairs, two-pair combos, and most flush draws that called flop (as many would have raised if nutted). The 10h completes heart flushes but also adds straight possibilities (e.g., J5), yet pre-flop ranges from callers make nuts unlikely. FiveTwo's double check indicates weakness, unlikely to have flopped monster and checked it down. Minni short (~1550 left, pot-committed), likely has draw/marginal hand; we target her stack for value while protecting. Pot 2750, bet 1550 sizes perfectly to put Minni all-in (pot becomes 4300), offers good price if called by worse (e.g., overpairs like QQ, two-pair like 3T), and has fold equity vs FiveTwo's weak range. Checking risks giving free river to draws or induces minimal bluffs; betting maximizes EV multiway with monster.

Elon (UTG) opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and now barrels the turn for 1,550 into a 4,300 pot. Calling would commit my entire stack (1,550) and the pot after a call would be 5,850, so I need ~26.5% equity to break even (1,550 / 5,850 ≈ 26.5%). I currently have A♥2♣ on 3♥-4♥-2♠-T♥: just a small pair (pair of twos) with an Ace kicker. My clean outs to improve on the river are the remaining hearts (9) plus the one remaining non‑heart deuce (2♦) — 10 unique outs → 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit. That is below the break‑even threshold versus a value hand. Elon’s line (UTG open + flop c‑bet + turn bet) polarizes toward value (sets, tens, paired 3s/4s, strong A hands) and there is still the big blind (FiveTwo) behind who called the flop — a multiway pot would further reduce my equity. I also planned to fold to heavy turn aggression if I didn’t improve. Given the pot odds, my limited outs, the opponent’s range and the risk of being crippled/eliminated by calling my last chips, folding is the prudent play.

Turn brings the 3rd heart and we hold no heart, so our hand drops to mostly two overcards with very poor realization. Elon’s line (UTG open, c-bet multiway, then barrel on a flush-completing card after Minni folds) is heavily weighted toward (1) made flushes/two-heart hands, (2) overpairs often with a heart, and (3) strong one-heart hands that now have a high-equity redraw. Our only realistic improvements are pairing Q or J, and those are frequently not clean (still lose to overpairs/flushes, and even when good we won’t get paid much on this texture). With no heart blocker, raising is a low-percentage bluff into a range that continues too often. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

J6 suited from UTG is too weak to open. Even though it's suited, the hand lacks high card strength and playability needed for early position opens. With 16,687 chips I'm well-stacked and should wait for better spots. Standard fold.

I have Js2s in middle with a 1,550 stack (~15.5 BB). Pot is 150 and it would cost 100 to see a flop. This is a weak, often-dominated hand with poor playability out of position and several deep stacks yet to act who can 3-bet or isolate. Limping or a small open will put me in difficult post‑flop spots; shoving is too loose for J2s. With my stack depth I should preserve chips and wait for a much stronger or shove-able spot — fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Six offsuit. Since the action folded to me, this is a standard opening spot in a 6-max game to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Ace-Six is at the lower end of my opening range, but the presence of the Ace blocker makes it a viable hand to raise. Pro on the button is relatively short-stacked (25 BBs), so I must be prepared to fold if he shoves, but against the blinds, I have a significant stack advantage and position. I will raise to 300, which has been a consistent opening size for me and others at this table.

Current hand is K7o on the Button. The player in the Cutoff (Flash) has opened to 300 (3 BB). Flash is the chip leader and has been active, but K7o is a weak hand that is often dominated by an opening range (KQ, KJ, KTs, Ax, pairs). With a stack of 25 BBs, I cannot profitably flat call a 3x raise with this hand, as it plays poorly post-flop and I would be committing >10% of my stack. A 3-bet shove is also too loose given the action. The standard and most +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Flash’s late-position open is fairly wide, but 9♣2♦ offsuit is near the bottom of the deck and performs terribly from the small blind: dominated when we make a pair, no real connectivity, and we’ll be out of position the entire hand. With Elon in the big blind capable of squeezing, calling becomes even worse. This is a clear spot to preserve chips and fold.

AKo is one of the best hands in poker, especially from the big blind facing a cutoff isolation raise after two folds. Flash's range from cutoff is wide, including many hands I dominate like Ax, Kx, suited connectors, etc. Calling risks multiway pots OOP and playing postflop awkwardly, while folding wastes equity. 3-betting to 900 (3x the raise) builds the pot for value, puts pressure on Flash's wide range, and gains initiative heads-up. My stack is deep (119BB effective), so this size denies equity to floats and sets up postflop play. If Flash 4-bets, I can reassess based on sizing.

I opened from the Cutoff with Ace-Six offsuit, and Elon has 3-bet me from the Big Blind. While I have position on Elon, Ace-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet. It is often dominated by his value range (better Aces, big pairs) and lacks the post-flop playability or equity to justify a call. Additionally, 4-betting as a bluff is risky against an active opponent like Elon. The most disciplined and correct play here is to fold and preserve my chip lead.

I'm UTG with Js/Qc (QJ offsuit) and a 1,550 stack (~15.5 BB). This hand is playable but marginal from early position — I want to take the initiative rather than limp and invite isolation or multiway pots against several deep/aggressive players behind. A standard 3BB open (raise to 300 total) balances fold equity and pot-building without overcommitting. Plan: fold to a large, polarized 3-bet (preserve my short stack), consider shove or call a small 3-bet depending on opponent and sizing, and continue postflop if called (c-bet favorable textures, proceed cautiously OOP on dangerous boards).

Minni raised to 300 from UTG but only has 1,250 chips remaining. This raise represents a significant portion of her stack, suggesting she is likely looking to commit. My hand, Jack-Seven of diamonds, is a speculative suited one-gapper that does not play well against an UTG opening range, especially one coming from a short stack who is effectively pot-committed. Furthermore, there are four players left to act behind me, including two with moderate stacks and one with a very large stack. Calling here would invite a squeeze or put me in a difficult position post-flop with a marginal hand. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack and wait for a higher-equity situation.

Current hand is 6c 7s (off-suit connector), which is weak. The action started with Minni raising to 300 from UTG.
