Minni is short-stacked (starting around 12.5 BB), implying their opening range from early position is likely quite strong (high pairs, strong Aces). I am in the Cutoff with a 25 BB stack. Calling speculative hands against a strong UTG range, particularly one that is pot-committed, is losing strategy. There are also players left to act behind me. The only prudent play is to fold.

Minni is very short (12.5bb) and opened UTG to 3bb. Flat-calling A2o invites the blinds in and puts us in a tough multiway pot with a dominated ace. Instead, I’ll 3-bet to isolate the short stack, maximize fold equity, and simplify decisions: if Minni jams, we’re getting excellent pot odds and our ace has reasonable equity; if she folds, we pick up dead money. The Ac also blocks some of her strongest continuing hands (AA/AK/AQ).

Preflop in SB with 5c8d, a complete trash hand. Facing a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo on the button over Minni's UTG open to 300. Pot is 1350, to call 850 (already in for SB 50). Equity vs 3-bet range is abysmal (~20-30% at best, likely less), and OOP postflop with no playability. Pot odds require ~28% equity to call profitably ignoring future streets, but hand is too weak. No history suggests FiveTwo is super loose here; standard fold to avoid spew. Preserve stack for better spots with 52BB effective.

4h5s is a weak hand facing a 3-bet to 900. Even though I'm in the BB and getting some pot odds (calling 800 into 1350), 45 offsuit is too weak to continue against FiveTwo's 3-bet. I'll be out of position postflop with a marginal holding. Easy fold.

I opened UTG to 300 with QcJs and got 3‑bet by the Button to 900. The pot is 1,350 and I must call 600 more (or shove my full 1,250). My effective stack is ~12–13 BB, so calling would leave me ~6–7 BB behind and requires roughly 31% equity to break even immediately. Versus a button 3‑bet over an UTG open the opponent’s range is likely value‑heavy (AK/AQ, high pairs, strong broadways) and QJo is often dominated and has marginal equity. I would also be playing out of position with a very shallow stack postflop, which makes postflop maneuvering unprofitable. An all‑in shove here risks being called by many better hands and is not +EV. Given the sizing, my stack depth, and the range disadvantage, I fold to preserve chips and wait for a better spot to accumulate.

I am in the UTG position with 4d 6c, which is a very weak hand. Given the 6-handed table and my chip-lead status, there is no strategic reason to open such a poor hand from early position, especially with short stacks like Minni and Pro behind me who might shove. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in early/middle position (UTG+1) with A4o and a stack of 25 Big Blinds. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position and has significant reverse implied odds if dominated by better Aces. With aggressive deep-stacked players behind me and a short stack in the Big Blind who might shove, opening this hand is risky and likely negative EV. Preserving my 25BB stack for a better spot is the prudent strategy.

We’re in the cutoff 6-handed with a reasonable connected hand that plays well in position and benefits a lot from fold equity. With UTG and MP folded, this is a good spot to attack the blinds—especially with the BB very short (likely to defend by shoving fairly wide, which 98o can realize decent equity against) and to potentially pick up dead money uncontested. I’ll use a small late-position open to risk little while applying pressure and keeping my range wide.

Preflop on the button with 4c5c suited connectors. FiveTwo raised to 250 from cutoff after UTG and MP folds. Excellent position postflop. Pot 400, calling 250 for ~1.6:1 direct odds, but strong implied odds due to suited straight potential, flush draws, and deep stacks (mine 12350, FiveTwo 7450, Claude 16537). Minni short at 1150 may shove, but multiway pots favor speculative hands like this. Folding too tight; 3-bet not ideal as it's not strong enough for value/protection. Standard call to realize equity.

QcTc is a solid suited broadway hand with good playability. Getting 3.25:1 pot odds to call 200 into a 650 pot with two players already in. This hand has flush potential and straight potential, making it profitable to see a flop multiway. Minni in BB is short-stacked so may squeeze, but I can fold to significant aggression.

Facing a 250 open from the cutoff with the button and small blind already in, this is a multiway pot. Pot is 850 and I must call 150 (about 15% pot odds), but I’m effectively a short stack (1150 ≈ 11.5bb). T9o is a speculative hand that loses a lot of value multiway and will often be dominated by the raiser/callers’ ranges. Shoving or raising here is thin — fold equity is low with two callers and I’d be committing a large portion of my stack into marginal equity. Calling 150 leaves me with ~10bb in a tough postflop spot against aggressive players. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better, cleaner spot to shove or defend. Fold.

I have QcTc on a monotone spade flop (5s2s7s). I have zero spades and no made hand. My overcards are essentially worthless here. Should just check and fold to any bet - no reason to put money in without any piece of this board or a flush draw.

Monotone flop multiway is a texture where pure range-betting is too aggressive, but having the 8s gives us a strong combo-draw: 9 spade outs to a flush plus a gutshot to the 6 (with 6s already included), and we can credibly represent strong A♠x / K♠x holdings as the preflop raiser. A small c-bet pressures the many one-spade / no-spade overcard hands that called pre, denies equity, and starts building a pot for when we improve, while keeping our risk controlled since the Button still has position and can raise. If we get raised large, we can comfortably let it go; versus calls we can continue selectively on good turns (spade/6/high cards) and take free cards otherwise.

Flop is 5s 2s 7s, a highly coordinated monotone spade board favoring flush draws, straight draws (3-4, 3-6, 4-6, 4-8, 6-8), sets, and overpairs. I have bottom pair (5s with 4c kicker) using the 5c, providing minimal showdown value and no draws (not spaded, straight draw weak at best with 45 vs 257). FiveTwo's preflop raise from CO and flop bet of 300 into 1150 (3.8:1 odds) after Claude's check smells like a polarized range: value (spade Ax, pairs 77/22/55? unlikely, sets, str8/flush draws) or semi-bluff. Calling risks too much equity to villain's range that dominates or draws outdraw me; implied odds poor due to reverse and vulnerability. Position on button allows floating, but hand too weak for profitable bluff-catch or thin value later. Pot odds decent, but EV negative long-term vs aggressive villain. Preserve deep stack (12100 >100BB) for better spots in late tournament.
I have QcTc with no spades on a monotone 5s2s7s board. I have no made hand and no draw - my clubs are useless here. Anyone with a single spade has me crushed. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG position with a stack of 25 BBs (2500 chips). My hand is 3c 6d, which is 6-3 offsuit. This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, with no connectivity or high card value. Given my position and stack size, there is no justification for entering the pot. The only correct play is to fold immediately.

Unopened pot but Q3o is too weak to open from middle position in a 6-max game. It has poor playability, is dominated by most calling/3-betting ranges (Qx, broadways, suited aces), and doesn’t realize equity well postflop. With stacks deep and decent players behind (CO/BTN/BB), the EV of entering is negative—best to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Folded to me in the cutoff with K5s, a suited king-high hand with good playability and position. Standard open-raise spot to steal the dead money from blinds (pot 150 already) and apply pressure. Minni is very short (~11BB) and may shove wide, but K5s has decent equity vs shoving ranges and good fold equity overall. Claude on button may 3-bet, but we can fold if needed. Raising standard 3x BB (300 total) balances value, bluff, and position.
Holding 4c6h - a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. Facing a 3x raise from Elon in the cutoff. While I have position on the button, 46o is too weak to call or 3-bet profitably here. Easy fold.

I’m on the short side (1,100 ≈ 11 BB) in the small blind facing a 3 BB raise to 300 from the cutoff. My hand, 9♥4♦ offsuit, is very weak and has poor equity against a standard opening range. With ~11 BB the correct framework is push-or-fold — but 9-4o is outside a profitable shove range here (I’d be called by many hands that dominate me). Calling 250 also isn’t attractive because it invites the big blind to act and creates a multiway pot where my hand fares poorly. Better to conserve my stack for a stronger holding or a better spot to shove. Action: fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. Elon raised from the Cutoff to 300. With the rest of the table folding, I am getting decent odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) to defend my blind. While Ace-Six is not a premium hand, it has enough equity against a late-position opening range to warrant a call and see a flop, especially given my chip lead.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Six) on a board of Ah-2c-Qd. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Elon, who raised from the Cutoff, the most standard and effective play is to check. This allows Elon to continue with his wide range of bluffs or semi-bluffs. Donk betting (leading into the aggressor) would likely only be called by hands that beat me (better Aces or Queens) and would fold out his bluffs. I intend to check-call most standard bet sizes to see how the turn develops, as my hand is too strong to fold but too marginal to build a massive pot with right now.

Preflop, raised K5s from cutoff as a standard open with position, playability, and fold equity against blinds, especially short-stacked Minni. Now heads-up vs Flash (BB caller) on a dry A-Q-2 rainbow board. My king-high has some showdown value but no pair, draw, or strong blocker. Flash checks, suggesting weakness or trapping. C-betting thin risks folding out better but getting called by worse/Ax; however, with 11800 stack (deep effective) and 50 hands left, better to check for pot control, realize equity if ahead (vs missed draws/bluffs), and maintain balance by not always c-betting. Will induce bluffs on turn/river or check behind strong hands. Checking exploits Flash's wide defending range while protecting my stack from tough spots ahead.

Following the check-check on the flop (Ah-2c-Qd), the board remains relatively static with the 3s on the turn. My top pair of Aces with a 6 kicker is a marginal made hand that benefits from pot control. Elon's check on the flop suggests he either has a medium-strength hand (like a Queen or a mid-pocket pair) or is giving up with air. By checking again, I am allowing Elon to bluff with his air or take a stab at the pot with a weaker made hand. I am not looking to build a large pot with a weak kicker, so check-calling is the most prudent line here to reach showdown efficiently.

Preflop raised K5s suited from cutoff as a standard steal/position play. Flash defended wide from BB. Flop A-2-Q rainbow is dry, checked behind to pot control with no equity. Turn 3s brings a spade, completing my nut flush draw (Ks5s + 3s = 9 outs to nuts) while adding coordination (gutshots). Flash checks again, showing continued weakness or trapping. With position, initiative, deep stacks (~118BB effective), and nut flush draw + K blocker (blocks AK), this is a prime semi-bluff spot. Betting builds pot for river shove if flush hits, gets value from Ax/Qx/draws that call once, folds out pure air/missed draws, and denies equity to Flash's floats. Checking risks giving free card to his draws or allowing check-raise trap, but bet exploits his weak range (preflop caller OOP). Size ~2/3 pot (425ish, but round to 400 for simplicity; pot 650) applies pressure without overbetting. Maximizes EV by turning draw into semi-bluff equity realization.

I have top pair with an Ace-Six offsuit on a board of Ah-2c-Qd-3s. Pre-flop, Elon raised from the Cutoff and I defended my Big Blind. On the flop, both of us checked, which suggests Elon might not have a powerhouse hand like AK or AQ, as those would typically continuation bet on an Ace-high board for value. His 400-chip bet on the turn looks like a delayed continuation bet or a value bet with a Queen. Given my chip lead and the fact that I have top pair, I have enough equity to call this bet and see a river. Raising would likely fold out his bluffs and only get called by hands that have me beat (better Aces). I will call to keep the pot manageable and aim for a showdown.

I have top pair with an Ace and a 6 kicker. On the river, the King of Clubs is an overcard to the Queen but doesn't significantly change the board texture for my specific hand. Given that I have been playing a check-call line since the flop, and Elon showed aggression on the turn after checking the flop, my hand is a classic showdown-value hand. It is too weak to bet for value, as I am unlikely to get called by many worse hands, and betting might force me to fold to a raise. By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply if Elon checks back, or I can decide whether to call a final bet based on his sizing. Checking is the most standard and safe play here to manage the pot size.

Preflop opened K5s suited from cutoff for standard reasons. Flop Ah-2c-Qd checked behind for pot control with no strong equity. Turn 3s gave nut flush draw + K blocker, semi-bluffed 400 (2/3 pot) exploiting Flash's wide BB defending range OOP; he called, assigning him Ax (non-spade), Qx, weaker pairs, missed draws/floats. River Kc pairs my King for top pair (KK with 5 kicker), bricks my draw but massively improves hand from air to decent value. Board A-Q-3-2-K rainbow (clubs/spades), no flush completes, possible straights minimal (QJT unlikely in his range). Flash checks third time (flop/turn/river), indicating capped range without strong Ace/Queen. With position, initiative, deep stacks, and history of thin value/semi-bluff, bet for thin value vs worse top pair (Qx, mid pairs calling), Ax that calls light, bluffs/missed draws. Size 800 (~55% pot) gets value without pricing in draws/sets, denies free showdown to worse, balances previous check. Checking risks losing to pure air or allowing hero call, but betting maximizes EV by extracting from broadways/Ax.

I have top pair with an Ace and a 6 kicker. On the river, the King of Clubs is an overcard to the Queen but doesn't significantly change the board texture for my specific hand. Elon's line (Raise PF, Check Flop, Bet Turn, Bet River) often indicates a marginal hand like a Queen or a delayed bluff, as premium Aces (AK, AQ) would typically continuation bet this flop. Given the pot odds of approximately 3.8:1, my top pair is a strong candidate for a bluff catcher. Raising would only get called by hands that beat me, so calling is the most profitable decision to reach showdown.

6-handed UTG, A3s is a standard open in a relatively wide 6-max UTG range: it has nut-flush potential, wheel/straight equity, and reasonable playability. With several players behind (including shorter stacks who may shove), I prefer raising rather than limping to build initiative and avoid going multiway too cheaply. If faced with a large 3-bet/jam from a deeper stack I can comfortably fold, but I can evaluate shorter-stack jams (e.g., BTN) based on price and ranges.

K8s is a weak hand facing a UTG raise from FiveTwo, who acts first and typically has a strong range there. In middle position with 106 chips BB effective (10600 stack), folding is standard to avoid difficult postflop spots, especially with deep stacks behind (Claude 163 BB, Flash 211 BB) and short stack Minni who might shove. Preserve stack for better opportunities in tournament.

8c9h offsuit is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise. UTG ranges are typically tighter, and this hand doesn't play well enough out of position to justify calling. With a healthy stack of 163BB, no need to get involved with a speculative offsuit hand here.

I am short (1100 ≈ 11BB) on the button facing an UTG open to 250. At this stack depth I should be in a push-or-fold mode — calling 250 or making a small reraise (to 400) leaves me with little fold equity and awkward post‑flop decisions. 9♣6♣ is a reasonable shove candidate on the button: it has suit/blockers and plays well if called, and shoving maximizes fold equity against the opener and isolates action. If I get called by the opener alone the break‑even equity is high (~1100 / 2350 ≈ 47%), but the realistic profit comes from folding out better hands or getting called by worse from the blinds. Given my short stack and position, I will jam to either take the pot now or double up if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four of spades. The action in front of me includes an open-raise from FiveTwo (UTG) to 250 and a significant 3-bet from Minni (Button) to 1,100. Queen-Four suited is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a 3-bet. My stack is currently the largest at the table, and there is no reason to risk a large portion of it on a marginal speculative hand when facing two opponents who have already shown strength pre-flop. Folding is the most prudent and standard professional play here to preserve my stack lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 5c and a stack of 2400 (24 BB). FiveTwo opened UTG to 250, and Minni 3-bet to 1100 from the Button. Facing a large 3-bet and a UTG open, my hand is far too weak to continue. Q5s is easily dominated by the ranges of both opponents. Calling would commit nearly half my stack with a marginal hand out of position, and shoving has very low fold equity given the action. Folding is the standard and correct play.

We opened UTG with A3s, but the BTN 3-bet to 1100 is a very large sizing versus a 250 open, which typically skews toward a strong/value-heavy range. Calling 850 OOP needs decent realization and ~36% raw equity; A3s performs poorly versus a tight 3-bet range and will struggle to realize equity out of position (often dominated when we make top pair). A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive with this sizing because we’d risk too much and face a range that won’t fold enough. Best play is to preserve the 80bb stack and fold.

UTG with T7o (7s Tc offsuit) in a 6-handed game at 50/100 blinds. This is a speculative trash hand with poor playability, low equity against ranges, and gets dominated frequently by better tens or sevens. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack and avoid tough spots OOP. Deep stack (106 BB) but no need to speculate here; wait for better spots with 48 hands left.
4s3c is a very weak offsuit hand in middle position. With players still to act behind me including an aggressive Minni in cutoff, there's no value in playing this hand. Easy fold.

6♠9♦ offsuit is a weak, easily dominated hand from the cutoff. There are two players to act behind (an aggressive button and the blinds who defend often), so any small open is likely to be called or 3-bet and I'll be in multiway pots with poor equity. With only ~15BB (1500) I don't want to open-shove a marginal hand and calling 100 is just limp-play with little upside. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with K6o. Action has folded to me. With King-high, this is a standard opportunity to attempt to steal the blinds. The small blind (Pro) is relatively short-stacked with about 23.5 BBs, and the big blind (FiveTwo) has a healthy stack. K6o is at the lower end of a standard button opening range, but the positional advantage and the folding of the earlier players justify a raise to pick up the pot now or outplay the blinds post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 2d, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (Flash). My stack is 2350 (23.5 BB). While suited connectors can be playable, 3-2 suited is the lowest possible connected hand and has very poor playability, especially out of position with a stack depth that limits implied odds. Calling 200 chips (8.5% of my stack) would be speculative and likely -EV as I will often miss the flop and be forced to fold to a continuation bet. A 3-bet shove is too loose given the hand strength. Preserving my stack for a better spot is the optimal strategy here.

Button opens to a standard 2.5bb and we’re getting a very good price (150 to win 400). JsTs is a premium BB defend: it plays well postflop with strong top-pair potential, straight/flush draws, and good implied odds. With Flash covering and having position, I’d rather not bloat the pot OOP with a marginal 3-bet that can face 4-bet pressure; calling keeps dominated hands in and lets us realize our equity efficiently.

We’re OOP in a single-raised pot vs the Button, and this Qd-8c-4c texture favors the preflop raiser’s range advantage (more strong Qx, overpairs, and good club combos). JsTs has no made hand and no immediate straight draw; we mainly have two overcards to 8/4 plus some backdoor equity (K/9 turns for straight potential, backdoor spades). Leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting raised off our equity, so the best line is to check and realize equity, then respond to Flash’s likely c-bet based on sizing/turn cards.

I raised pre-flop with K6o and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes Q-8-4 with two clubs. While I haven't hit anything, as the pre-flop aggressor, this is a good board to fire a continuation bet. A Queen is more likely to be in my opening range than the Big Blind's defensive calling range. I will bet 275 (half the pot) to represent strength and try to take the pot down immediately.

Flash’s small c-bet (275 into 550) is giving us a very good price: 275 to win 825 now (~25% needed). With JsTs we have two overcards to the 8/4 (6 clean-ish outs twice is ~24% to improve by river) plus additional backdoor equity (running spades, and some K/9 runouts that can create straight pressure). This isn’t a good check-raise bluff candidate (no club to represent/continue well, and he can continue wide in position), so the highest-EV line is to peel and re-evaluate turns—continuing on favorable cards (spade, J/T, sometimes K/9) and comfortably folding versus strong turn barrels on bad runouts.

Turn brings the 9c completing our straight (8-9-T-J-Q). This is a strong value hand but the 3rd club significantly increases draw/value density (made flushes with two clubs now, plus many one-club hands that can improve on a club river). Since we’re OOP, I don’t want to give Flash a free river with his air/one-club hands that may check back; I’d rather start building the pot and charge equity.

I’m choosing a medium sizing to (1) get called by a lot of his Qx/overpairs and club holdings, (2) deny correct odds to pure one-club floats, and (3) avoid over-polarizing and folding out everything except flushes. If raised large, his range becomes very flush-heavy; we can continue cautiously (often call) and reassess river sizing/runout.

On the turn, the nine of clubs completes several potential draws, including a possible flush and straight (J-10). FiveTwo has led out with a 'donk bet' of 600 after calling my flop continuation bet. Since I have King-high with no clubs and no significant draw, I have very little equity to continue in this hand. Calling would be a pure float with no showdown value, and raising into a board that likely improved my opponent's range is too risky. Folding is the most prudent action.
J2 suited from UTG is a weak hand. While it has some suited potential, it's not strong enough to open raise from early position. Folding is the correct play here.

Facing an open (pot 150, I must call 100) with 2♦3♥ in middle position. This hand has very poor raw equity and plays terribly out of position vs an opening range and potential callers behind. With a 1,500 stack (~15 BB) it's too weak to 3-bet shove and calling to see a flop gives low implied odds and a bad postflop playability. Preserve my chips for a better spot — fold.

I am the chip leader by a significant margin. From the Cutoff with K5 suited, this is a standard opening range hand to put pressure on the blinds and the remaining short stacks. Both Pro and Minni are quite short, so I need to be prepared for an all-in shove from them if I open. However, opening to 300 (3x) is a standard play to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ah Ac) on the Button with a stack of 23.5 big blinds. The Cutoff (Flash), who is the chip leader, has opened for 3bb. Given my stack depth, a 3-bet shove is the most effective play. It balances my range (as I would make this move with medium pairs like 88-JJ and hands like AK/AQ to fold out equity or get value) and allows me to fully realize the equity of my hand immediately. A small 3-bet commits a significant portion of my stack anyway and might look stronger (sucking out fold equity), while a flat call risks a multi-way pot where Aces are vulnerable. I want to isolate the opener and get maximum value if they look me up with a worse pair or broadway hand.

We’re in the small blind facing a very large 3-bet (300 open → 2350). With Tc6c we’re dominated when called, and calling puts us out of position in a bloated pot with shallow SPR and poor realization—especially with the big blind still to act and potential for a 4-bet/continue behind. A 4-bet (or shove) is too optimistic into a range that’s heavily weighted to strong hands given sizing. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

Preflop in the big blind with 7s8c offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability. Facing a cutoff open to 300 followed by a massive button 3-bet to 2350 (over 7x), which indicates significant strength from Pro. Pot is 2800, calling 2250 commits nearly 25% of my 10,500 stack into a bloated pot with dominated equity and reverse implied odds. No position postflop, and villain's sizing suggests premium range (AA, KK, AK, etc.). Folding is the only playable option to preserve stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 103/150).

Pro has shoved for 23.5 big blinds from the button. King-Five suited is a speculative opening hand that is likely dominated by a standard button shoving range (pairs, high Aces, and better Kings). I need roughly 42% equity to call profitably, but K5h typically has closer to 30-35% against this range. Folding allows me to maintain my significant chip lead without taking a high-variance, negative-EV risk.

UTG with 9♣5♠ and a 1,500 stack (≈15 BB). Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop. 9‑5 offsuit is a very weak hand from early position — poor equity, often dominated, and painful to play post‑flop out of position. A small open to 200 gives almost no fold equity and just induces 3‑bets or larger pots I don’t want to play with this holding; shoving ~15BB from UTG is too loose with 9‑5o. Best play is to fold, protect my short stack and wait for a better shove/call spot or a stronger hand in position.

I am in Middle position with K2 offsuit. This is a weak hand to open with in a 6-player game, as there are still four players to act behind me. K2o has poor playability and is easily dominated. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Current stack is 28 BB. K6s is a marginal hand from the Cutoff. While it has some blocker value and flush potential, it is easily dominated by the ranges of the players behind me (Button and Blinds), who are all deep-stacked and capable of applying pressure. Opening here risks losing chips to a 3-bet or playing a difficult post-flop pot with a weak kicker. With 28 BB, I am not desperate and can wait for a better spot to enter the pot. Folding is the standard disciplined play.

Folded to us on the Button and blinds are the only players left. Q6o is not a premium, but in position it’s a profitable open as a steal: we can win the 150 dead chips often, and when called we still realize equity well with position and a queen blocker to some strong holdings. I’ll use a standard small-ish open that keeps my risk down while still applying pressure to the blinds; plan is to play postflop in position and comfortably fold to a large 3-bet.

Preflop in small blind facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB). My hand 3s8c is trash: offsuit, unconnected low cards, dominated by any calling range. Button ranges are wide but profitable against this holding. No fold equity if 3-betting. Big blind yet to act but irrelevant for this decision. Deep stack (104BB), no need to speculate with garbage. Standard fold to preserve chips for better spots.

Holding 3d8h offsuit - one of the weakest hands in poker. Getting 2.7:1 pot odds to call 150, but 83o has virtually no playability post-flop. No straight potential, no flush draw, rarely makes strong hands. Easy fold despite the discount.

I am in the UTG position with 42o, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker. Opening with such a weak hand from early position is generally a losing play in 6-max, especially as the chip leader. There is no reason to commit chips with this hand when I can wait for a much better opportunity.

I am in middle position with [8c 6h], a weak off-suit one-gapper. With 28 big blinds, I have a playable stack but am one of the shorter stacks at the table. Opening with this hand from MP is too loose given the players left to act, particularly the aggressive stacks on the button and the short stack in the big blind who might shove. It is a trivial fold.

2♠5♥ offsuit is too weak even for a CO open, and the player dynamic makes it worse: the BB is very short (1400 ~14bb) and can profitably jam over a raise, forcing us to fold and burn chips, while SB/Button can also apply pressure. With little postflop playability and poor blocker value, this is a clear spot to preserve stack and pass.

Folded to the button with Qs6c, a speculative queen-high hand that is well within my wide button opening range in 6-max play. Minni in BB is very short (1400 chips, ~14BB) and may fold or shove, but my hand has decent equity against shoving ranges and blocks queens. Claude in SB is deep but acts last preflop. Standard steal raise size of 2.5x the BB to build pot or take it down immediately, maximizing fold equity while keeping stack committed low (250/10450 <3%). Limping or folding is suboptimal on button.

AcTc is a strong suited ace in the small blind vs a button open. This is a standard 3-bet hand for value. Elon's stack is 10200 and he's been active. I'll 3-bet to around 750-800 to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop.

I have 3♦4♣ — a very weak, unsuited, low-connected hand. Claude has 3-bet to 800 after Elon opened, so the pot is 1150 and it costs me 700 to call (I would need roughly 38% equity to break even). This hand has negligible equity vs a typical SB 3‑betting range and plays terribly out of position; if I call I’m committing half my stack with poor flop playability and I can still face action from the button (including a 4‑bet). A shove or a cold‑call here would be high variance and largely speculative; preserving my 1400 stack for better, constructive spots is the correct long‑term play. Fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Claude in the SB after my button open-raise with Qs6c. This hand is queen-high offsuit with poor connectivity and playability. SB 3-bet ranges vs BTN opens are strong and polarized (QQ+, AKo/s, some bluffs but mostly value). Equity realization OOP is low, and calling 550 into 1150 pot commits ~5% of stack into a marginal spot postflop where I'm OOP. Shoving over is overkill with 10200 stack (40BB) and poor equity vs calling range. Standard fold preserves stack for better spots late in tournament with 45 hands left.

I hold Pocket Tens [Td, Th] in UTG position with a stack of 2800 (28 BB). This is a strong premium hand that warrants an opening raise to build the pot and define ranges. Limping is generally weak and invites multi-way action which devalues my hand. A standard open size in this tournament has been around 2.5x BB. I will raise to 250. If the short stack in the Small Blind shoves, I have an easy call. If deep stacks 3-bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, but TT is strong enough to play for stacks at this depth if necessary.

Facing an UTG open from Pro in a 6-max configuration, 6♠2♠ is far too weak to continue from middle position. It has poor high-card value, gets dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position against a strong UTG range. It’s also not a good 3-bet bluff candidate here (no relevant blockers, poor playability). Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing a standard UTG raise from Pro to 250 into a 150 dead money pot (blinds). My holding Ks5d is offsuit, king-high with a weak kicker, poor postflop playability, and dominated by Pro's likely strong UTG range (pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc.). No position advantage justifies calling or 3-betting. Deep stack but no need to speculate with trash. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

Q8s on the button facing UTG raise to 250. This is a marginal hand but I have position and a healthy stack (16587). Pro is short-stacked (2550) so if I 3-bet he might shove, putting me in an awkward spot. Calling in position to see a flop is reasonable - I can play the hand profitably postflop with position. Pot odds are decent at 250 into 400.

I’m in the small blind with 8♣4♥ — a weak, unsuited, off‑suit hand. Pro opened UTG to 250 and the button called; pot is 650 and it costs me 200 to call (min‑raise to 400). My stack is 1,350 (~13.5bb). Calling 200 into the 650 pot (post‑call pot ≈850) requires ~23–24% equity, but 8‑4o has very poor equity against an UTG raising range and a caller and I’ll be out of position for the rest of the hand. With a shortish stack the correct preflop strategy is generally shove or fold — this hand isn’t strong enough to shove for fold equity and a shove would be easy to call by better hands. I’ll conserve chips and fold here rather than invest 200 into a low‑equity situation out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 6d. Pro raised from UTG and Claude called on the Button. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win 650), 63 suited is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Given that I have a significant chip lead and we are in the final third of the tournament, I should avoid marginal situations that could bleed chips. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here.

I hold Pocket Tens on an Ace-high flop (4s 8d Ah) out of position against the Button (Claude). The Ace connects well with a Button flat-calling range (Axs, broadways). C-betting here turns my hand into a bluff catcher if called, or forces me to fold to a raise. Checking allows me to pot control, realizing my showdown value cheaply. If Claude checks back, I am happy to see a free turn. If he bets, I can evaluate based on sizing, as he might be stabbing with air or a worse pair, but I prefer to keep the pot small given the overcard on the board.

I flopped middle pair with Q8s on a 4s-8d-Ah board. Pro opened UTG and checked to me. His check on an Ace-high board is interesting - could be giving up with underpairs or trapping with an Ace. With middle pair I have showdown value. A small bet (1/3 pot) protects against overcards and extracts thin value from underpairs like 66, 77. If he check-raises, I can fold easily.

I hold Pocket Tens on an Ah-8d-4s flop. After checking to the aggressor (Claude on the Button) to control the pot, he bets 225 into a pot of 650 (about 1/3 pot). The Ace is certainly in his range, but a small bet like this is often used with a wide range including lower pairs (99, 77, 66), draws, or just air trying to take the pot after my check. Pot odds are very favorable (calling 225 to win 1100, requiring ~20% equity). Folding here to a small single bet is too exploitable, and raising accomplishes little as it isolates me against better hands (Ax). I will call to evaluate the turn, keeping his bluffs in the hand.

The turn card is the Jack of Spades (Js), making the board 4s 8d Ah Js. This is not a good card for my Pocket Tens. It is a second overcard to my pair, and it connects well with the Button's flat-calling range (hands like QJ, KJ, JT, J9s) as well as the existing Ace. Since I am out of position, leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from hands that beat me. My best option is to check again. This allows me to see what my opponent does. If he checks back, I get a free river and realize my equity. If he bets, I will have to re-evaluate based on the sizing, though folding becomes a much stronger consideration given the deteriorating board texture. My primary goal is to get to showdown as cheaply as possible.

Turn brings Js, another overcard to my 8s. My middle pair is now 3rd pair on board (A-J-8-4). Pro called my flop bet and checked again. With showdown value but facing a board that's gotten worse for me, betting again is thin - I'm only getting called by better hands (Ax, Jx, overpairs). Check back to control pot size and try to realize equity at showdown.

I hold Pocket Tens on a board of 4s 8d Ah Js 2h. After calling a small flop bet and seeing the turn go check-check, my hand essentially functions as a bluff-catcher with some showdown value. The turn check by the opponent suggests they likely don't have a strong Ace or two pair, capping their range to weak Aces, Jacks, mid-pairs, or air. The River 2h is a brick. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like 8x or 99) often enough to justify the risk of being called by better (Ax, Jx), and it folds out the bluffs I beat. Checking is the optimal line to realize my showdown value cheaply. If the opponent checks back, I win against lower pairs. If they bet, I can evaluate, but checking is the first step.

River completes with 2h. Board is A-J-8-4-2. I have third pair (8s) with Q kicker. Pro has checked three times now (flop, turn, river after calling my flop bet). His passive line suggests either a weak made hand trying to get to showdown or giving up. Betting for value here is terrible - I only get called by better (any Ace, any Jack, overpairs). My hand has pure showdown value. Check and hope to win against missed draws or worse pairs like 66-77.

We’re 6-handed UTG with Q5o, a dominated offsuit queen that plays poorly out of position and will often make second-best pairs. With several deep stacks behind who can 3-bet pressure and a short stack on the button who can reshove wider, opening or limping is -EV. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Pocket Kings (KK) is one of the strongest preflop hands, behind only AA. Position is middle, pot is 150 with a small open raise to 200 (to call 100), min raise 200. Standard play is to 3-bet for value and to isolate against the raiser. Sizing to 600 (3x the open raise) is appropriate given deep stacks (mine 102k effective ~100BB) and builds the pot without overcommitting. Opponents include short stack Minni (13BB) but primarily deep Flash (200BB) and others; this pressure isolates and denies equity to speculative hands. Folding or calling is suboptimal as it misses value and allows multiway pots.
8c5s is a very weak hand facing a 6x raise from Elon in middle position. No playability, no blockers, and poor reverse implied odds. Easy fold.

I have 8♦5♥ on the button and only 1,350 chips (≈13.5 BB). Elon opened to 600; the pot is 750 and calling 600 would commit ~44% of my stack and leave me with ~7.5 BB behind. Break-even equity to call here is 600 / (750+600) ≈ 44.4%. 8-5 offsuit has poor raw equity and very poor postflop playability versus a typical MP open (dominated by Ax/Kx, broadways, and pairs). A min‑3bet to 1,100 would be essentially an all‑in with almost no fold equity and is too thin with this hand. With many hands left in the tournament and my stack short but not desperate, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot (or a clean steal against the blinds) is correct. Fold.

Elon has opened for 600, which is a 6x big blind raise. This is significantly larger than the standard 2.5x or 3x opens we've seen throughout the tournament. With pocket fours (4s, 4c) in the Small Blind, I am in a difficult position. While I have the chip lead and could afford the call to set-mine, the implied odds are less favorable against a 6x raise, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Furthermore, Pro (Big Blind) is relatively short-stacked with 3325 chips and could easily shove over my call, forcing me to fold and lose the 600 chips. Given the large raise size and the positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

The opponent Elon opened to 600 chips (6bb) from middle position, which is an unusually large sizing compared to the standard 2.5bb-3bb. The pot odds offered are 500 to call into a pot of 750, requiring 40% equity. My hand, T8o, is a weak offsuit gapper that typically has around 30-35% equity against a strong opening range and realizes equity poorly out of position, especially with a low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR ~2.6). Given the poor price and the difficulty of playing this hand post-flop against a large opener, folding is the only profitable option.

9c4s is a trash hand: offsuit, low cards, large gap, no straight or flush potential worth playing. UTG in 6-handed game requires a tight opening range. Folding preserves stack for premium hands and avoids tough post-flop spots, especially deep-stacked at 103BB. Even with short stacks behind, no value in limping or raising this.
23 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With 4 players still to act behind me and no positional advantage, this is a clear fold.

I have T7o in the cutoff with a 13.5BB stack. This hand is marginal for a shove but fine to open-raise to take the initiative and steal the blinds. A standard 3x open (300) is consistent with the table, preserves fold equity, and keeps my stack flexible — if Flash or a blind 3-bets large I'll fold and preserve chips; if I'm called I'll play postflop for position and pot control. Raising to 300 maximizes my short-term expectation here compared with limp or a risky all-in.

I am on the button with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni, who is extremely short-stacked with only about 10 big blinds remaining in her total stack, has raised to 300 from the cutoff. This action often signals a willingness to go all-in given her stack size. There is no reason to risk chips or get involved in this pot with such a poor hand. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 8s. Minni, who is effectively short-stacked with only 10.5 BBs, has raised to 300 from the Cutoff. This raise size represents nearly 30% of Minni's remaining stack, signaling commitment. While I am getting decent pot odds, playing 78 offsuit out of position against a desperate short stack who will likely shove the flop is not a profitable spot. My hand is too weak to defend, and I preserve my 32 BB stack for better opportunities.

Minni is very short (1050) and opened to 300, giving me a great price in the BB (200 to win 450). With an SPR of ~1 after calling, I don’t want to 3-bet and essentially commit 1050 with 76o versus a short-stack get-in range that will have me in bad shape. Calling keeps flexibility: I can continue on favorable flops (pairs/strong draws) and comfortably fold when I miss and face a jam, while still defending wide due to pot odds.

We flop big: two pair (7s and 6s) on a wet J-7-6 board versus a very short stack (Minni has ~750 behind into a 650 pot). I want to (1) get value from Jx/7x/6x and straight draws (T9/98/85), and (2) deny a free card when an overcard/straight card can change action. A medium bet also cleanly sets up stacks so that if she calls, she’s basically committed on the turn, and if she jams, we snap it off.

Call 350. I have T♠7♥ for a pair of sevens on J♥6♦7♠ — decent but vulnerable (middle pair, weak kicker). FiveTwo called preflop and now leads out a small bet (350 into a 1000 pot). Calling is the best option: with position I can control the pot and get to see a turn for a reasonable price (call 350 to win ~1700, so I need ~20.6% equity). My pair is ahead of many hands in his calling/c-bet range (overcards, floats, some pocket pairs, and draws), and raising to 700 would commit too much of my 1050 stack and put me in tough spots vs Jx or bigger pairs. Folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call and reevaluate on the turn — if I improve (T or 7) or he shows weakness I can extract value; if a Jack or big overcard arrives and he applies pressure I’ll fold to large bets.

Minni is extremely short (700) into a 1350 pot, so SPR is ~0.5. With top two pair on a dynamic J-7-6 board, we want maximum value and to deny equity from straight draws (T9/98/85), overcards, and now spade-draw holdings that picked up additional outs on the 3s. There’s no reason to give a free river when any Jx/7x/6x and most draws are priced in anyway; jamming cleanly realizes our equity and captures full value from her continuing range.

Pot = 2050, to call 700 (my remaining stack) I need ~700/(2050+700) ≈ 25.5% equity. I hold T♠7♥ — middle pair (sevens) with a weak kicker. BB (FiveTwo) called preflop, led small on the flop (350) and again with a modest-sized turn bet (700). That line contains value (Jx, some pairs) but also a fair number of floats/overcards and bluffs. My pair is behind Jx and bigger pairs but ahead of many of his bluffs/overcards, and the price is correct to see a river given short-stack dynamics — folding here surrenders >2k chips I can win for ~25% equity. I can’t extract more by raising (this call is effectively all‑in), and folding is too nitty. Call 700 to try to double up and realize equities.
T3o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is far too weak to open from the earliest position - poor connectivity, not suited, and I'll be out of position against the entire table. Easy fold.

I have J♣3♣ in middle position with a ~27.5bb stack (2750) and it's folded to me with only the blinds in. J3 suited is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability out of position. Calling 100 would likely get me multiway and put me in marginal spots postflop; raising to try and steal is too risky with several deep, aggressive stacks behind who will defend or 3-bet. With this stack depth I'd rather wait for a much stronger spot. Fold and preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am holding KQo in the Cutoff. Two players have already folded. KQo is a strong opening hand in this position. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Button and the blinds.

I am on the Button with Ace-Five offsuit (A5o). The player in the Cutoff (Flash), who has a large chip lead, has raised to 300 (3bb). My stack is 3275 (approx 33bb). A5o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a Cutoff opening range. While having an Ace blocker is useful for 3-bet bluffs, a 33bb shove is an overbet that risks too much, and a smaller 3-bet commits a significant portion of my stack awkwardly against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent. Calling is also unattractive as A5o plays poorly post-flop even in position due to kicker issues. Given the action and stack depths, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve chips for a better spot.

Pocket deuces play best as a set-mine rather than a 3-bet at this depth. Calling 250 more to see a flop is cheap relative to the effective stacks (~7.0k vs Flash), giving good implied odds when we flop a set, and we can comfortably fold if the BB squeezes big. A 3-bet turns our hand into a bluff with poor playability OOP, and folding is too tight getting a good price.

Facing a cutoff raise from Flash and a small blind call from FiveTwo, with J5o offsuit in the big blind. This is a marginal hand with poor post-flop playability out of position against two opponents. Pot odds are about 3.5:1 (200 to call into 900 effective pot post-call), but equity against raiser's likely strong range (top 20-30%) and SB's calling range is insufficient long-term, especially OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 41 hands left in tournament.

We whiffed our set-mine on A75 and 22 has very little equity (only runner-runner wheel/backdoor spades). Against the preflop raiser’s range this board heavily favors Flash (lots of Ax), so leading would mostly get called/raised and just burns chips. Best line is to check OOP, let Flash c-bet his range, and continue only versus a very small stab with clear plan; otherwise we can comfortably fold.

I raised pre-flop with KQo and was called by the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The flop comes Ah-7h-5s, giving me nothing but overcards. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace-high board strongly favors my perceived range. FiveTwo has checked to me. A standard continuation bet here often takes down the pot immediately if the opponent does not have an Ace or a strong heart draw. I will bet 350, which is exactly half the pot, to represent the Ace and apply pressure.

Flash’s 1/2-pot c-bet on A♥7♥5♠ hits his CO opening range hard (lots of Ax, strong broadways with hearts, and overpairs). With 2c2s we missed our set-mine and have very little immediate equity (basically two outs to a set plus very thin backdoors), nowhere near the ~25% required by pot odds, and we’re OOP with poor ability to realize equity if we call and face turn pressure. Turning this into a bluff check-raise is also unattractive here: we have no relevant blockers (no A/heart), and as the big stack Flash can continue wide and put us in tough spots. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I'm UTG with T♥9♣ and a 2750 stack (~27.5BB). In 6-max this hand is marginal but playable — it's connected and has decent postflop playability. Limping UTG is passive and invites multiway pots I don't want; folding is too tight with this stack depth. I will open to the standard 2BB (200) to take the initiative, plan to c‑bet favorable flops and play cautiously out of position. If I face a sizable 3‑bet or a lot of aggression, I'll fold — preserve my stack and avoid bloating pots out of position with a marginal hand.

Minni is the shortest stack at the table with only 25.5 BBs and has opened with a min-raise from UTG. Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to contest this in a 6-handed game. By raising to 550, I aim to isolate Minni, potentially winning the pot immediately or forcing her to commit more of her small stack. This also discourages the players behind me (Pro, FiveTwo, Elon, and Claude) from entering the pot with marginal hands. As the chip leader, I can afford to apply this pressure.

I hold 8c 3s in the Cutoff. The action started with Minni raising to 200 from UTG, followed by Flash 3-betting to 550 from Middle Position. My hand is extremely weak (unsuited junk), and facing a raise and a re-raise puts me in a terrible spot to enter the pot. I have no equity to justify calling or raising. My stack of 3275 is valuable, and I must fold without hesitation to preserve chips for a playable hand.

We’re facing an UTG min-open and a 3-bet from Flash, with both blinds still to act behind us. 9♦5♦ is a very weak suited hand in a 3-bet pot: it’s often dominated when we make a pair, and the implied odds are reduced because the 3-bet creates a low SPR (hard to realize equity without getting pushed off). Additionally, Minni is only ~25bb and can jam, which would frequently force us to fold and forfeit our call. With no strong squeeze/4-bet leverage and poor realization, the best stack-maximizing play is to fold.

9s3h is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness that helps, and poor connectivity. Facing an UTG open from Minni and a 3-bet from Flash, the range is strong. As SB, out of position with 10.2k stack (102bb), folding is straightforward to avoid spew and preserve chips for better spots late in the tournament (hand 110/150). No bluff equity or implied odds justify calling or 4-betting.

Holding 6h5h in BB facing a 3-bet from Flash (550). I need to call 450 more into a 900 pot. While suited connectors have some implied odds potential, 65s is too weak to defend against a 3-bet out of position. Flash has been showing strength with 3-bets and has a large stack. Fold is the disciplined play here.

I opened UTG to 200 with T♥9♣ and face a 3‑bet to 550 from Flash. Calling 350 more to see a flop would commit a large portion of my stack and leave me out of position against a likely strong or aggressive 3‑betting range; the pot odds (350 to win ~1250) require ~28% equity which T9o won’t reliably have vs Flash’s range. A 4‑bet to 900 is too speculative and overcommits with a marginal hand. Stick to the preflop plan: fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 9 of clubs and 3 of spades. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and should be folded from almost any position, especially UTG, to avoid playing out of position with a marginal holding. With five players left to act behind me, there is a high probability of a raise or a better hand being out there.

I am in Middle Position with [9h 4d], which is a very weak off-suit hand. With 32.75 big blinds, I should not be opening this hand from this position as it has very low equity and playability. The action folded to me, but there are still four players left to act. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Unopened pot in the cutoff, but 8♦3♥ offsuit is well below a profitable CO opening range: it has poor playability, makes dominated pairs/weak two-pairs, and realizes equity badly when the blinds defend (especially with Minni short in the BB and Claude deep in the SB who can 3-bet or apply postflop pressure). With little upside and meaningful reverse implied odds, the best EV decision is to simply pass and preserve stack.

Folded to me on the button with As6s, a strong suited ace hand in late position. Excellent spot to raise for value and to steal the blinds/pot. Standard button open-raise size of 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against the blinds. Position allows control post-flop, and As6s plays well against typical blind defending ranges. Minni's short stack (24BB) may shove wide, but A6s is live and has good equity.

7s4c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a button raise of 300, I'd need to call 250 more. This hand has terrible equity against a standard button opening range and plays poorly postflop. Easy fold.

We’re heads-up vs the Button’s 300 open with AdJd — a strong, playable hand that performs well both for value and as a 3‑bet bluff catcher. Calling 200 (300 total) keeps us OOP and gives Elon cheap flops; the min 3‑bet to 500 is too small and won’t buy much fold equity. I want to seize the initiative and isolate his wide BTN opening range, so I’ll 3‑bet to a standard size of 900 total. That builds value against worse A/K/KQ type hands, gains fold equity, and still leaves me ~1,550 behind to play postflop. If he 4‑bets big we’ll re-evaluate, but 900 here maximizes expected value in this spot.

A6s is a premium suited ace suited hand with great playability from the button facing a short-stack BB 3-bet. Pot odds are excellent: 600 to call into 1250 (over 2:1 immediate odds), requiring only ~30% equity, which A6s crushes as it has strong equity against Minni's polarized short-stack 3-bet range (premiums like QQ+/AK where we have ~25-35% equity, or weaker Ax/pairs/bluffs where we dominate). Position post-flop is huge for realizing equity. Minni has ~16BB left (~1650 total stack), so she'll often commit anyway, but calling keeps our deep stack (99BB) protected while inducing action from worse Ax/KQo etc. Folding is too tight given blockers to AA and suited connectivity/flush potential. 4-betting risks bloating pot OOP against her jam range with a hand not strong enough to stack off deep; prefer to flop best hand or fold safely. Standard call to play post-flop profitably.

We have Ad Jd on a flop of 6c Tc Ac — top pair with a good kicker. The board is somewhat wet (three clubs), but a made club-flush requires two clubs in villain’s hand (relatively rare). Elon called our 3-bet preflop, so his calling range contains a mix of Ax (AK/AQ/AT/AJ), broadway hands (KQ/KJ/QJ), and some pocket pairs and suited connectors. With a short effective stack behind, I don’t want to give free cards or allow his wide holdings to realize equity on the turn/river. Shoving maximizes fold equity, charges worse A’s, T’s and broadway hands that will call, and avoids being priced-in to stack off on a later street when dominated. I’m willing to risk getting called by AK/AQ or AT two‑pair combos because jamming now is higher EV than checking and turning the hand into a coinflip. Commit all chips (all‑in).

Facing Minni's all-in bet of 1650 into a 3500 pot on a 6c Tc Ac flop with As6s, giving top two pair (Aces and Sixes). This is an absolute monster hand on a flush draw board. Minni's BB 3-bet pre-flop range is polarized: premiums like AA/AK/AQ suited, sets, or bluffs/draws. We dominate most Ax hands (except AA, which we block with As), crush pairs and worse draws, and even vs nut flush draws like KcQc or Jc9c, two pair has ~70-80% equity heads-up. Pot odds are excellent at ~2.1:1 (32% equity needed, we have 70%+ vs realistic range). Position irrelevant post-call as all-in. My deep stack (99BB) risks nothing significant; calling builds the stack massively +EV with 40 hands left. No fold equity concerns. Standard call to realize huge equity.

5-handed ranges widen, but UTG is still the tightest seat and K4o is too dominated (crushed by most Kx opens/calls and a lot of broadways). With ~70bb we don’t need to take thin, low-playability spots that invite 3-bets/isolations behind, especially with an active table and a short BB who can defend/jam more readily. Best EV is to pass and wait for better open candidates.

Qs3h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability from early position (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game. It has low equity against calling ranges, bad postflop potential due to lack of coordination and suitedness, and is easily dominated by better queens. Folding is standard to preserve stack and wait for better spots, especially with deep stacks and many hands left (112/150). No steals needed here with position and pot unraised.

92o is a very weak hand. Even on the button with only blinds to act, this is bottom of any reasonable opening range. Folding is the standard play - the hand has no playability and stealing blinds isn't worth the risk.

I have a strong starting hand (QJo) in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since the action folded to me in the Small Blind, I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, who is currently the short stack at the table.

Blind vs Blind scenario. The Small Blind (Flash) raises to 300 (3BB). I am in the Big Blind with 9s6s (Nine-high suited two-gapper). I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 400, offering 33% pot odds. While 9s6s has some playability, defending against a 3x open from out of position with a marginal hand is risky, especially against a deep-stacked opponent. My stack is roughly 32BB, which is healthy enough to wait for better opportunities rather than bleeding chips in marginal spots out of position. The 3x sizing makes the price to call unattractive for this specific hand strength.

UTG in a 5-handed game with 5h8h suited connectors. This hand has potential for implied odds and multi-way pots, but starting out of position makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop. My stack is healthy at 128BB, while Pro is short at ~31BB and may shove over raises. Opponents behind are deep-stacked (Claude and Flash very deep). Standard UTG range doesn't include 58s; it's too weak to open profitably here given the positional disadvantage and risk of multi-way action or 3-bets. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 38 hands left.
I have pocket 9s in UTG+1 after UTG folded. This is a solid hand to open raise with. Standard sizing is 2.5x BB = 250.

I am on the button with King-Queen of Spades, a premium drawing and high-pair hand. Claude opened for 2.5x from middle position (UTG+1 in 5-max). KQs is strong enough to 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot in position. By raising to 750, I put pressure on Claude and the blinds. Pro is quite short-stacked in the Small Blind, so I must be prepared for a potential shove from him, but KQs is likely ahead of a wide shoving range from a 30BB stack. If Claude calls, I will play the rest of the hand in position with a strong range advantage.

I am holding 7s 4c in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise to 250 from Claude and a 3-bet to 750 from Flash on the Button, I have absolutely no reason to get involved. The cost to call is too high relative to my stack and hand strength, and I am out of position against two strong ranges. Folding is the only correct play to preserve my 31BB stack for better spots.

We’re facing an UTG+1 open and a fairly large button 3-bet (250 → 750). JTo offsuit is heavily dominated by both players’ continuing ranges (AJ/KJ/QJ, AT/KT, big pairs), and calling would put us out of position with poor playability and reverse implied odds, potentially even multiway if Claude continues. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here because JTo has weak blocker value (doesn’t block A/K) and risks too much of our ~70bb stack if Flash responds aggressively. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack for better spots.

Pocket 9s facing a 3-bet from button. Flash could be 3-betting light from BTN. I'm getting ~2.3:1 odds (500 to win 1150). 99 is too strong to fold here but not strong enough to 4-bet. Calling to set mine with good implied odds - Flash has a big stack. If I hit a set, I can potentially win a big pot.

I have pocket 9s on Q73 rainbow. I called Flash's 3-bet preflop. Now I'm first to act with an underpair to the queen. My 9s have showdown value but are likely behind if Flash has any Q, overpair, or even AK that could pair up. Checking to the 3-bettor is standard here - I can evaluate based on his action. If he checks back, I might have the best hand and can re-evaluate on later streets.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a very dry board (3s 7d Qc). My pre-flop 3-bet has narrowed my range to strong hands, and Claude's call indicates a range that likely includes medium pocket pairs (88-JJ), suited Broadways, or perhaps a weaker Queen like QJ. Since Claude checked, I should place a continuation bet for value and to protect my equity against overcards. A bet of around one-third to one-half of the pot is standard on such a dry texture to keep his marginal hands in the pot while extracting value.

Flash's c-bet of 600 into 1650 is a small sizing (~36% pot). I'm getting 3.75:1 odds (600 to win 2250). With pocket 9s I'm behind Qx and overpairs but ahead of unpaired broadways like AK/AJ. Flash's small c-bet doesn't commit him strongly - this could be a probe with air. I'll call and reassess on the turn. If he fires again big, I can fold; if he checks, my 9s might be good.

Turn pairs the Queen, making the board 3s7dQcQd. This is actually a good card for me - with two Queens out, Flash is less likely to hold a Queen. My 99 now beats all unpaired hands (AK, AJ, KJ etc) that he might have 3-bet with. However, I'm still behind overpairs (AA, KK, JJ, TT) and any Qx. Checking here to control pot size and get to showdown cheaply. If Flash checks back, my 9s are likely good. If he bets again, I can reassess on river.

The turn Queen improves my hand to three-of-a-kind with a King kicker. Given the dry board texture (3s 7d Qc Qd) and the fact that I've already shown aggression pre-flop and on the flop, checking back here is a strong tactical play. It serves two purposes: first, it protects my range and hides the strength of my hand; second, it induces Claude to potentially bluff on the river or call a value bet with a marginal hand like a medium pocket pair (JJ-88) that might have folded to a second barrel on the turn. Since there are no draws to worry about, I am happy to give a free card and aim for value on the river.

River brings 7h, creating QQ77 on the board. My 99 plays as QQ779. Flash checked back the turn showing weakness, which is good. However, most of his 3-bet range (AK, AJ, KJ, TT+) has higher kickers than my 9. Betting accomplishes little - better hands call, worse hands fold. Checking to showdown is optimal since I beat bluffs and lower pairs like 88/66 but lose to most of his value range.

I have a full house, Queens full of Sevens (Qs-Qc-Qd-7d-7h). The board texture is very favorable as I have top set/full house and have played the hand with aggression as the pre-flop 3-better. After Claude checked the flop and river, and I checked back the turn to induce a bluff or value on the river, it's clear I must bet now. Claude's range likely includes medium pocket pairs (88-JJ), a Seven, or another Queen. If Claude holds a Queen, we will chop the pot since the five-card hand (Q-Q-Q-7-7) is already complete. However, I can extract value from a Seven or a pocket pair that might suspect a missed draw or a thin value bet. A bet of 1450, approximately half the pot, is designed to get called by those marginal hands.

Board is QQ77 - my 99 plays as QQ779. Flash checked back turn showing weakness, now bets 1450 into 2700 (54% pot). Getting 3.97:1 odds, need to be right ~25%. His 3-bet range is heavily weighted to high cards (AK, AQ, KQ, AJ, JJ+) which all beat my 9 kicker. However, his turn check was weak - if he had a strong hand he'd likely bet. This river bet could be thin value with overcards or a bluff with missed hands. I beat all his bluffs and low pocket pairs he might have. Given the pot odds and his weak turn check, I'll make the crying call.
AKo in UTG is a premium hand. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB to build the pot and isolate. With 131 BBs effective, I want to play this aggressively preflop.

Claude has raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. My hand, King of Diamonds and Trey of Diamonds (K3s), is very weak to be calling or raising with in early position against an UTG open. Even though I have a significant stack lead, there is no strategic reason to get involved in this pot with such a marginal hand, especially with three players yet to act behind me. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) facing an opening raise from Claude in the UTG position. In a 5-handed game, an UTG open typically signifies a reasonably strong range (often AJ+, 88+, KQs), against which ATo performs poorly due to dominance issues (kickers). My stack is approximately 31 big blinds, which is a playable stack size that I should protect rather than bleeding chips in marginal spots. Calling invites the blinds to squeeze or enter the pot, and playing ATo multi-way is difficult. 3-betting is risky as I cannot comfortably call a 4-bet shove. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot as the aggressor or against a wider range.

Claude is opening UTG to 2.5bb into 5 players, which is typically a relatively strong range. From the small blind we’ll be out of position the entire hand and still have the big blind behind who can squeeze. 3♠4♥ offsuit has very poor playability and realization versus an UTG range, and defending it here will bleed chips long-term. Best is to conserve stack and wait for better spots.

54s is a weak suited connector. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who often raises but has a strong early position range (likely 22+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways, etc.). Equity realization OOP is poor post-flop. Pot odds are 400:150 (~2.67:1, ~27% equity needed), but my hand has ~25-30% equity at best against that range and plays poorly OOP. Late tournament stage, preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.

I am dealt 8c 6d in the UTG position. This is a very weak starting hand and is not worth playing from any position, let alone under the gun in a five-handed game. My priority as the chip leader with 35 hands remaining is to avoid unnecessary risks and focus on high-quality openings or exploit situations where I have a positional advantage. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Current hand is J9o in UTG+1 (Cutoff in this 5-handed configuration). With a stack of roughly 31 BBs, this hand is below the standard opening threshold for this position. The players in the blinds (Elon and Claude) have deep stacks and have shown capability to defend or 3-bet, making a steal attempt with a dominated hand like J9o unprofitable. Preserving chips for better opportunities is the prudent play here.

On the button this is normally a prime steal spot, but 82o is near the very bottom of playable holdings and has poor postflop realization when either blind defends (and both blinds have shown willingness to call/3-bet versus opens, especially against an active image). Limping is unattractive, and raising invites tough defense/pressure from larger stacks with little equity to continue. I’ll pass and preserve stack for higher-EV opens.

4c9h is a trash hand with poor connectivity, no suitedness, and dominated in most scenarios. Preflop from SB vs BB OOP, post-flop will be difficult with no initiative and bad position. Everyone folded behind, but BB (Claude) defends wide and has been aggressive historically (frequent raises and calls). Stealing attempt risks too much with unplayable hand; pot odds to complete 50 into 150 are decent but equity and playability low (~35% vs loose BB range, but OOP nightmare). Late in tournament (hand 115/150), preserve stack for premium spots rather than spew chips. Standard fold.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with Qs9s and a stack of 31.25 BBs. While suited queens have potential, Q9s is generally below the standard opening threshold from UTG, especially with several deep-stacked opponents behind who can apply pressure or flat call in position. My stack size is in a zone where preserving chips for higher EV spots is prudent rather than opening marginal speculative hands out of position. Therefore, I will fold.

5-handed (with UTG folded) we should be opening a reasonable range from UTG+1, and 44 is comfortably inside it: it can win unimproved in position, and it has strong implied odds when we flop a set. Limping invites multiway play and lets the blinds realize equity too cheaply, especially against strong/aggressive stacks behind. I’ll make a standard open that pressures the blinds while keeping risk controlled versus a 3-bet from the deep stacks behind.

J8h is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. On the button, I have excellent position to realize equity against FiveTwo's UTG+1 raise. FiveTwo has shown aggression in prior hands but often calls down light or makes mistakes post-flop. Deep stacks (my 12.6k effective vs their 6.7k) provide strong implied odds for set-mining or hitting draws. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 into 400 (2.6:1 direct, better implied). Folding is too tight multiway potentially; 3-betting risks too much with marginal hand vs early raiser. Call to play post-flop in position.

A5s in the small blind with FiveTwo raising and Elon calling. Getting good pot odds (200 to call into 650) with a suited ace that has flush and wheel straight potential. This hand plays well multiway. Calling is the best option here.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 3d. FiveTwo raised from UTG+1, and both Elon and Claude called. Although I am getting very good pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 850), Q3o is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against three other players. Calling here likely leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am dominated or forced to fold. Given my large stack and the stage of the tournament, I will make the disciplined fold and look for better spots.

Flopped a gutshot straight draw (need a 6 for the wheel) and backdoor spade flush draw with one spade on board. No pair, just ace high. Against two opponents, my hand has limited equity. Best to check and see if I can improve cheaply on the turn or take a stab if checked around.

We flop bottom set in a 3-way pot on a fairly dynamic board (7-9-4 with straight draws like 56/68/78, plus plenty of 9x/7x that can pay). After SB checks, we should bet for value and protection rather than slowplay multiway—there are many turn cards that either kill action or give opponents equity. A medium-large c-bet targets one-pair hands and draws while building a pot for stacks.

Sizing: ~70% pot puts real pressure on draws and starts extracting from 9x/7x/overpairs while still keeping their calling range wide enough to continue.

Facing a 600 bet into 1450 pot on the flop (pot odds ~2.4:1, requiring ~29% equity). My J♥8♥ holds a strong combo draw: nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity) plus gutshot straight draw to the 10 (4 outs, ~9% additional), totaling approximately 40-45% equity against a typical UTG+1 c-bet range which includes many overcards, pocket pairs, and weak made hands. FiveTwo's history shows wide pre-flop ranges and frequent post-flop aggression, but also calling down light and folding to pressure. Claude checked flop, likely weak or trapping but position suggests fold to raise. Deep effective stacks (FiveTwo 6075) provide fold equity and future implied odds. Raising builds pot with superior equity, denies equity to draws like 56, T6, 56 suited, and takes down weaker hands like Ax, overpairs without set value. Min raise too small; sizing to 1800 (3x bet, ~60% pot) balances value/bluff, isolates if Claude folds, and leaves FiveTwo pot committed relative to stack. Calling is viable in position but risks multiway pot on turn; raising maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

I have As5s on a 7h9c4s board. My previous read was overly optimistic - I don't actually have a gutshot (I'd need both a 2 and 3 for the wheel). All I have is ace high and a backdoor flush draw with only one spade on board. Facing a bet of 600 and a raise to 1800 from Elon, I'm in a terrible spot with no made hand and minimal equity. Easy fold.

We have bottom set on 7-9-4 in a pot that’s already very bloated (pot 3250, we have 6075 behind). Elon’s raise over our c-bet can be strong value (9x/overpairs/two pair/sets) but also plenty of protection/semi-bluffs (65/86 type double-gutters, 8x with equity, occasional air). With this low SPR, we don’t need to 3-bet shove the flop to get stacks in; calling keeps his range widest and lets him continue barreling with bluffs and dominated one-pair hands. Plan: check most turns to let him bet, then jam; on very dynamic straight-completing turns (5/6/8/T), adjust by leading/jamming more often to deny equity and avoid giving free cards.

Turn is a very dynamic card: the 6 completes 58 (and improves 65/86-type hands) and adds additional equity to a lot of his flop-raising range. With SPR ~1 (pot 4450, we have 4875), there’s no real room for bet/fold lines—our goal is to get maximum value while denying a free river to draws that might check back in position. Shoving leverages the low SPR, gets called by his strong value (two pair/overpairs/straights/sets) and a chunk of his semi-bluffs, and prevents him from taking a free card with hands that have significant equity against our set.

On the turn, the board is 7h-9c-4s-6c, completing club flush draws and adding straight draw possibilities like 5-8 (for 4-5-6-7-8 or 5-6-7-8-9) or 78/T5 types. My Jh8h holds a strong nut flush draw (9 hearts outs, ~19% equity to river) with backdoor straight potential via 5 or 10, but no made hand. FiveTwo, an UTG+1 raiser who bet flop and called my 1800 check-raise (representing strength), now shoves 4875 all-in first to act (effective stack committed, shown as 0). Pot odds: 4875 to call into 9325 + 4875 = 14200 (~2.9:1, needing ~26% equity). Against their calling range (sets 44/77/99 unlikely but possible, overpairs QQ+, two-pair like 97/74/94, strong draws like clubs or completed straights), my equity is ~20-25% at best, lacking fold equity heads-up all-in. Tournament stage (116/150 hands, 5 players, my 10.6k stack decent), folding preserves stack for better spots vs aggressive history but value-heavy turn jam into raiser. Calling risks 46% stack with marginal equity; no future streets to realize implied odds post-river.

5-handed UTG, J2s is too weak to open profitably and plays poorly out of position versus multiple opponents. With a short stack in the BB (Pro) we also face increased reshove pressure and reduced implied odds when we limp/call. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger spots.

K6s is a marginal suited hand, but from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game, it is too weak to open raise or call. Folding preserves my deep stack (106BB) for better spots. Players behind like Claude on the button and short-stacked Pro in BB could punish a limp or raise. Standard play is to fold early position speculative hands to avoid difficult post-flop situations with position disadvantage.

I have 3s7h on the button - a very weak offsuit hand. While I have position and it's folded to me, 73o is simply too weak to profitably open or even limp. This hand has poor playability and limited potential to make strong hands. Better to wait for a more reasonable spot.

Everyone has folded to me in the small blind. I am holding Ace-Trey suited (Ac 3c), which is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. My opponent in the big blind, Pro, is the short stack at the table with approximately 30 big blinds. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and try to take down the pot immediately or play a heads-up pot where I likely have the range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with [8d 9d], a strong suited connector. The action folded to the Small Blind (Flash) who raised to 300 (3BB). This is a standard Blind vs Blind situation where opening ranges are wide. I have position on the opener post-flop. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 200 to win a pot of 400, offering 2:1 odds (33% equity required), and 89s has significantly better equity than that against a typical SB opening range. With a stack of ~30BB, I am deep enough to play post-flop effectively rather than shoving pre-flop. Folding is too weak given the hand strength and positional advantage. Therefore, I will call to see a flop.

I have the initiative as the pre-flop raiser and am holding Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw on a Jack-high board. This is a standard spot for a continuation bet to maintain pressure on the Big Blind, who is currently the short stack. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is effective for folding out his air while keeping the pot manageable if he has a piece of the board like a Jack or a draw.

I hold 8d9d on a Js7c2s flop. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten to make 7-8-9-T-J). The Small Blind led out for 300 (half pot). While I have position, my immediate equity is low (4 outs, approximately 8-16%) and the board contains a flush draw which I do not hold, devaluing my potential outs (Ten of spades) and making the board texture dangerous. Pot odds are 3:1, requiring ~25% equity to break even, which I do not have purely on the draw. Given my stack size (~28BB), it is prudent to preserve chips rather than chasing a weak draw against the chip leader.

Preflop UTG in a 5-handed game with 95s suited connectors.
