This hand has poor playability early position: low high-card strength, vulnerable to raises, and dominated often by better suited hands or pairs. Standard UTG range is tight (top 15-20% hands like pairs, broadways, suited aces). Folding avoids difficult postflop spots OOP with a speculative hand on deep stacks. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are marginal (+EV limp? unlikely vs villain ranges), better to wait for stronger spots with 106BB effective.

Q6o in UTG+1 is a weak hand. With 4 players behind me including the blinds, this is an easy fold. The hand lacks playability and has poor equity against ranges that would continue. No need to get involved with marginal holdings from early position.

I am on the button with Q5o. While the button is a prime stealing position, Q5o is a very weak hand to open with, especially since the Small Blind (Pro) is relatively short-stacked at 27.75 BBs and has shown a willingness to play aggressively. Raising here with such a weak hand risks a re-steal from either blind. Given my significant chip lead in the tournament, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with sub-par hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 3d, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. Action folded to me. While I am getting decent pot odds (3:1) to complete the blind, the hand has extremely poor playability and very low equity realization out of position. My stack is short (around 28 BBs), so preserving chips is better than bleeding them in marginal or losing spots. Folding is the standard play.
6c4h is a very weak hand - unsuited and poorly connected. From UTG position, I need a much stronger hand to open. Easy fold.

I am holding 7h 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. Positionally, I am in the middle of a 5-handed table. Since UTG has already folded and I have the largest stack, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with a sub-optimal hand. I will preserve my stack and fold.

I am on the Button with 8h 3d, which is a very weak hand (bottom 15%). The blinds (FiveTwo and Elon) have significant chip leads over me (93+ BB and 105 BB respectively vs my 27.75 BB). Although I am in position, attempting to steal with such a poor hand against two deep-stacked opponents who can defend widely or 3-bet is not profitable. My stack is not in the danger zone where I need to shove any two cards, so I can wait for a better spot.

Folded to me in the small blind heads-up versus the big blind. A7 suited is well above the bottom of my steal/open range here: it has blocker value, good playability, and benefits greatly from having the initiative. I’ll apply pressure with a standard small-blind open size that keeps my range wide while still generating fold equity and building a pot when I have the best of it.

Preflop in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from the small blind after all others folded. My hand is 9s2h, which is extremely weak with no high card value, no suitedness, poor connectivity, and dominated by any reasonable raising range. Pot odds are 200 to call into 400 (2:1), but the hand has negligible equity against a steal-raise range from SB and plays terribly out of position post-flop. With 105 BB effective stack and late in the tournament (hand 119/150), folding trash preserves chips for better spots rather than spewing post-flop.

I am holding Qh Th in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. This is a strong starting hand with good playability and post-flop potential. As the chip leader with over 240 BBs, I want to play aggressively and take control of the pot. A standard open raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to narrow the field and build the pot for a hand that flops well.

I am holding 8d 7h in UTG+1 facing a 3x raise from the UTG player (Flash). With a stack of roughly 28 BBs, I need to be selective. 87 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against an UTG opening range which is typically strong. Calling would bleed chips and leave me vulnerable to squeezes behind. The correct play is to fold.

5-handed on the Button versus a single UTG open, Kh9c is marginal but playable in position. Flash has shown a fairly active opening frequency, so folding is a bit too tight, but K9 offsuit isn’t a great 3-bet bluff (domination risk when called/4-bet, and we don’t want to bloat the pot deep). Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our position/equity versus his wider opens, and we can comfortably fold if one of the blinds squeezes big.

Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who tends to be aggressive but UTG suggests a strong range (likely 88+, AJo+, ATs+, KQs+, AKo). FiveTwo called from the button with a wider range, making it multiway. My JTs is a speculative suited connector with decent playability, but I'm out of position in the SB post-flop, which is tough multiway. Pot odds are 750:250 (3:1), requiring ~25% equity, but realized equity OOP multiway is lower due to difficult spots ahead. Tournament is late (hand 120/150), prioritize premium hands and better positions to build stack. Folding preserves chips for stronger situations.

Ad7s in the BB facing a UTG open to 300 and a button flat. Getting 3.75:1 on a call (200 to win 750). A7o is a reasonable defend here - the ace provides some showdown value and I'm closing the action with good pot odds. Not strong enough to 3-bet but definitely worth a call.

Flopped top pair with A7o on Ah-Td-9h. Draw-heavy board with flush draw and straight draws possible. My hand has showdown value but weak kicker. Standard play is to check to the preflop aggressor (Flash) and see what develops. If they bet, I can call with my top pair. No need to donk lead here.

On a board of Ah Td 9h, I have middle pair (tens), a queen kicker, a heart flush draw, and a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board hits my range well. With such significant equity and multiple ways to improve, betting here is the standard play to maintain initiative, build the pot, and potentially take it down immediately. A bet of roughly 60% pot is appropriate given the wet texture of the board.

We have second pair (9x) with a decent kicker and a relevant backdoor heart (Kh) in position versus a smallish multiway c-bet. This sizing is consistent with Flash range-betting a lot of unpaired broadways and some weaker Tx/9x that we beat, and folding here would be too tight given we need ~27% equity to continue. Raising mostly isolates ourselves against stronger Ax/Tx and better draws while folding out bluffs, so the best line is to call and re-evaluate turns (continue on 9, heart turns, and some K turns; be more cautious versus large barrels on blanks).

I have top pair weak kicker on a very draw-heavy board (flush draw, straight draws with T-9). Flash bet 550 and FiveTwo called. Getting about 3.7:1 on a call (550 to win 2050). While my A7 is vulnerable on this coordinated board, folding top pair here is too tight given the pot odds. I'll call and reassess on the turn - if a heart or straight-completing card comes and there's significant action, I can fold. But for now, top pair is worth continuing.

Turn is 5c - a blank that doesn't complete any draws. I have top pair weak kicker with Ad7s. The flush draw and straight draws are still possible. With two opponents still in (Flash who bet flop, FiveTwo who called), checking is optimal. My hand has showdown value but betting into two opponents on this draw-heavy board only gets called by better hands or strong draws. Check and let Flash potentially continue, or take a free card if it checks through.

On the turn 5c, which is a relative brick, the situation remains complex with two opponents still in the hand. I have a pair of Tens and a strong heart flush draw. Since both FiveTwo and Claude called my flop bet on an Ace-high board, there is a high probability that at least one of them holds an Ace. By checking here, I am exercising pot control and looking to realize my equity. Betting again into two callers on this texture is risky, as I am unlikely to fold out an Ace, and I don't want to get raised off my draw. If it checks around, I get a free look at the river; if someone bets, I have a very easy call given my pair and substantial draw equity.

Turn checks to us in a 3-way pot on Ah Td 9h 5c holding K9 for second pair with a good kicker and some backdoor-ish equity already realized. Flash’s check after c-betting multiway often caps him toward medium-strength Ax that wants pot control, Tx/9x, or give-ups (broadways/heart draws). Claude checking twice is also a lot of weak Ax, 9x, Tx, and draws. Checking back keeps our showdown, but it also gives a free river to hands like QJ/J8/heart draws that have decent equity and can put us in tough spots on many rivers. A small protection/value bet targets worse 9x, heart draws, and some sticky hands, while keeping the pot controllable and allowing us to fold comfortably to a big check-raise (which is heavily weighted to Ax/sets/two pair).

Top pair weak kicker facing a small turn lead from FiveTwo (about 25% pot) after Flash checked. Getting 5:1 pot odds (850 to win 4300). FiveTwo's small bet after Flash checked could be a draw betting for a cheap card, medium strength hand, or slowplayed strong hand. My A7 beats bluffs, worse aces, and draws. Folding top pair to this sizing is too tight. Flash still behind but unlikely to raise after checking. Standard call.

Flash holds middle pair (Tens) and a high heart flush draw (9 outs). After checking the turn to control the pot, FiveTwo bet 850 and Claude called, providing Flash with excellent pot odds of approximately 5:1. With 14 total outs to improve to a flush, trips, or two pair, Flash has roughly 30% equity, which significantly exceeds the 16.5% equity required by the pot odds. Calling is the most profitable play to realize the hand's high potential while maintaining pot control as the chip leader.

River brings Jc, completing several straight draws (Q8, KQ, 87 all have straights now with J-T-9 on board). My Ad7s is just top pair weak kicker on a very connected final board. Checking is clearly correct - betting only gets called by better hands (better aces, two pair, straights, sets). If someone bets, I'll have to decide whether to call with my bluff catcher. For now, check and hope to see a cheap showdown.

On a river board of Ah-Td-9h-5c-Jc, my hand (pair of Tens) has significantly diminished in value. The Jack of Clubs is a particularly bad card for me, as it makes my Tens third pair (behind any Ace or Jack) and completes potential straights for hands like KQ, Q8, or 78. FiveTwo showed aggression on the turn by betting into two players, and Claude called, suggesting at least one of them holds an Ace or has now improved to a better hand with the Jack. Betting here provides no value as I will only be called by better hands, and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against two opponents who have shown persistent interest. Checking is the only viable option to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

River goes check-check to us on Ah Td 9h 5c Jc holding K9 (third pair). The J improves a lot of the flop/turn continues: it completes KQ to the broadway straight (a very natural float/call on the turn after Flash’s check), and it also upgrades hands like QJ/J9 to a pair/two pair that now beat us. After we bet turn and got called by both players, their ranges are already weighted toward Ax/Tx/two pair and sticky broadways—i.e., hands that either still beat us or just improved. A thin value bet doesn’t get called by many worse hands (worse 9x are limited, and most Tx/Ax is better), while a bluff needs *both* opponents to fold in a big pot, which is low-frequency. Best play is to take the free showdown.

I am in UTG position with a stack of approximately 28 big blinds (2775 chips). My hand is 9s Th (Nine-Ten offsuit), which is a weak hand to open from early position given the number of opponents left to act and the likelihood of being dominated or playing out of position. With my stack size being the shortest at the table, I need to preserve chips for higher equity spots or better stealing opportunities in late position. Therefore, folding is the standard and correct play here.

We’re 5-handed (Pro already folded) and 77 is well within an UTG+1 opening range. Raising takes initiative, can win the blinds outright, and keeps us from playing a capped limped pot where overcards realize too easily. A standard ~2.5x size also reduces the chance we go multiway while still leaving room to maneuver postflop with ~78bb effective against most opponents.

2c8h is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low card values, and no speculative value like suited connectors or broadway potential. FiveTwo's raise to 250 from UTG+1 (early position) suggests a strong range, and calling 250 into a 400 pot offers terrible equity and implied odds. Position on the button doesn't justify playing this trash preflop in a late-stage tournament (hand 121/150) where stack preservation is key for better opportunities. Folding is the disciplined, +EV decision to maximize long-term stack growth.

K5o in the SB facing an UTG+1 raise. This is a weak hand out of position - K5o doesn't play well postflop against an early position range. With 16k stack and 30 hands remaining, no need to defend marginal holdings OOP. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. FiveTwo has raised to 2.5x from UTG+1. While the price to call is relatively cheap (150 more to win a pot of 400), T3o is an extremely weak hand with very poor post-flop playability, especially out of position against an early position raiser. As the current chip leader with only about 30 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to defend the Big Blind with such a weak hand and risk losing chips in a marginal spot. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

5-handed UTG with AQs is strong enough to open for value and to take initiative. I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing to 250 to keep the pot controllable versus the deeper stacks (Claude/Flash/Elon) while still pressuring the blinds and setting up profitable postflop play against the shorter BB (Pro).

Preflop from UTG+1 facing a UTG raise to 250 into a 400 pot. My hand is 4d5h, an offsuit low connector with poor playability, minimal equity against a likely strong range from FiveTwo's early position raise. FiveTwo has been aggressive historically but folding trash preserves stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 122/150) with deep stacks. No position advantage postflop, high chance of domination or multiway pot. Standard fold to avoid spew.

56 offsuit is a weak hand. Even with position on the button, calling an UTG raise with this speculative hand isn't profitable. The implied odds aren't good enough and I still have the blinds to act behind me. Easy fold.

In this hand, FiveTwo raises from UTG (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 5-handed game). I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit (A6o). While Ace-high has some value in short-handed play, A6o is a marginal hand to play out of position against a raise from an early-position player. Folding avoids the risk of being dominated by better Aces or pairs while playing out of position. With a healthy stack and being close to the end of the tournament (Hand 122 of 150), there is no need to defend with a weak Ace against a raise from UTG.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 2h. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot that will be 550, approx 27%), K2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly against a UTG opening range. Being out of position with a hand that has low playability and is often dominated makes calling -EV. My stack is 26.75 BB, which is playable, but preserving chips for better spots is preferable to bleeding chips with marginal defenses OOP.

9h8h is a strong suited connector with excellent playability and implied odds given deep effective stacks (mine 104BB, others deep except Pro at 26BB). In 5-handed play late in the tournament (hand 123/150), UTG opening ranges widen to include hands like 98s for their post-flop potential in multiway pots or to isolate limpers/blinds. Limping is weak OOP; folding misses value. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) applies fold equity vs blinds/short Pro, builds pot OOP, and has good equity realization if called. If 3-bet, can fold profitably.
8c4s is a very weak hand - not playable against an UTG raise. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand in a 5-handed game. Elon raised from UTG to 300. While UTG represents strength, in 5-max this is essentially the hijack/cutoff position. I want to 3-bet here to 900 to take initiative, leverage my position, and simplify the hand. This also forces the short-stacked Small Blind (Pro) to make a tough decision. If Elon calls, I play the pot in position with a range advantage.

I hold Jack-Six offsuit (Jc 6h) in the Small Blind. The action involves an opening raise from UTG and a 3-bet from the Button. My hand is extremely weak and completely dominated by the ranges of the opponents involved. I have 26 BBs and cannot afford to waste chips cold-calling a 3-bet out of position with a trash hand. This is a clear fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet, 4♦2♣ has extremely poor equity and plays terribly out of position in a bloated pot. The pot odds (800 to win 2150) don’t justify a call versus the combined strong ranges, and a 4-bet bluff (even as a shove) has too little fold equity multiway with UTG still behind and our hand blocking nothing relevant. Best EV is to preserve the stack and fold.

Facing a button 3-bet after my UTG open-raise with 98s suited. Button ranges are polarized: value (QQ+, AKo/s) or bluffs (suited connectors, Ax suited, broadway blockers). 98s has ~35-40% equity vs typical button 3-bet range, excellent implied odds deep-stacked (101BB eff vs Flash's 213BB), and plays well postflop OOP with flush/straight potential. Pot odds are favorable (600 into 1950 effective, >3:1). Folding is too tight late (hand 123/150, 5-handed); 4-betting risks too much OOP with a mediocre 4-bet candidate and poor postflop if called. Calling keeps pot manageable, realizes equity, and invites further aggression from aggro button for thin value.

Flop AdKc5h completely misses my 9h8h suited connector. I have no pair, no strong draws (only backdoor flush and insignificant straight potential). Flash's button 3-bet range is strong and polarized, heavily favoring Ax, Kx, QQ-JJ, with this board hitting his value range hard (AK, AQ, AT, etc.). Equity is poor (~25-35% vs his range), and reverse implied odds are terrible OOP deep-stacked. No showdown value. Pot odds irrelevant since first to act. Donking weak; check-raising bluff lacks equity/blockers. Best play is check-fold to his likely continuation bet, preserving stack late in tournament (123/150, 95BB deep). Calling any bet would be spew.

I have top pair (Aces) with a Jack kicker on a dry board that heavily favors my pre-flop 3-betting range. Elon checked to me after calling my 3-bet pre-flop. I should bet here to extract value from weaker Aces (like ATs or A9s), pocket pairs that might be sticky, or a King. Betting also maintains my initiative and prevents Elon from realizing equity with hands like QJ or JT. A bet of around 1/3 pot (700 chips) is effective on such a static board.

Facing Flash's 700 bet into a 1950 pot on AdKc5h flop with 9h8h, which completely whiffs. No pair, no strong draws (weak backdoor FH/SD), pure air. Flash's button 3-bet range is nutted/polarized (AK, AQ, KQ, sets, bluffs rare), board smashes value portion. Deep-stacked (95BB), OOP check-call commits too much with <30% equity, poor reverse implied odds, no fold equity. Pot odds ~3.7:1 insufficient given playability. Preserve stack late (hand 123/150, 5-handed ICM pressure); check-fold optimal vs aggro button c-bet.

J2o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, and UTG is the worst position to open from. With a healthy 165BB stack, no reason to play marginal hands out of position.

I am holding Queen-Five offsuit in the UTG+1 position. In a 5-handed game, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for this position. Given that I have a very healthy stack and there are several active players behind me, including a short stack on the button, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Current stack is 26.25 BBs, which is the shortest at the table with 5 players remaining. The blinds (FiveTwo and Elon) have massive chip leads (79 BBs and 94 BBs) and can apply significant pressure. While Q9o is often an open from the Button in a vacuum, doing so here risks bleeding chips against active big stacks who can defend wide or 3-bet effectively. Since Q9o plays poorly against calling ranges and cannot withstand a 3-bet, folding to preserve the stack for higher equity spots is the disciplined decision.

Folds to me in the small blind, so this is a prime spot to attack the big blind and pick up the dead 150 chips. 8-7 offsuit isn’t a premium, but it has decent playability (can make straights/two pair) and benefits a lot from having initiative. I’ll use a smaller SB open size to keep my risk down while still applying pressure and allowing profitable postflop c-bets and barrels versus a wide BB defending range.

Ac6c is a strong defensive hand in the BB facing a standard raise from the SB. FiveTwo's range from SB is wide due to position and steal attempt. Pot odds are excellent: 150 to call into 350 pot (~3:1 implied odds, plus postflop playability with suited ace potential for nut flushes, straights, and ace high). Effective stacks allow multi-street play. No need to 3-bet light here as call extracts value from worse hands and maintains balance. Folding is too tight given history of FiveTwo's aggression.

Heads-up SB vs BB on 4c-8c-3d, our 8s7h is top pair with a weak kicker and no backdoor flush. This board hits BB’s defend range (pairs, straight draws like 56/67/75, and club draws), so we want to bet for value vs worse 8x/4x/3x and pocket pairs, while denying equity to overcards and draws. A small c-bet keeps the pot controlled and leverages our preflop initiative without committing us vs a check-raise-heavy line.

Facing a 200 bet into 500 pot (now 700) on 4c8c3d flop with Ac6c nut flush draw OOP vs SB's wide range. Equity is excellent (~40-50% vs his continuing range, including vs sets/top pair/draws). His small 40% pot bet suggests weakness or thin value, consistent with his aggressive SB history (steals, frequent c-bets like hands 50,87,91). Raising builds pot when ahead in equity, applies pressure to fold worse draws/pairs/overcards, and denies equity. Calling OOP risks turn discomfort if he checks back or bets larger; folding spews equity. Raise to 700 (3.5x his bet) balances value/bluff, good SPR post-raise (~3:1), leverages deep stacks but late tourney (124/150) demands aggression to maximize stack. Blocks nut flush.

Elon’s check-raise on 4c-8c-3d can be plenty of value (sets like 33/44/88, strong 8x) but also a lot of natural semi-bluffs from BB defend (club draws, 56/67/75, A5, etc.), and he’s shown aggression across prior hands. With top pair (8) we’re too high in our range to fold getting ~26% immediate pot odds (500 to win 1900). I don’t want to 3-bet and bloat the pot out of position with a marginal kicker—raising mostly gets action from better hands and folds the bluffs we want to keep in. Best line is to call, re-evaluate on the turn, and be prepared to proceed on safe cards while slowing down or folding to heavy pressure on bad runouts (clubs/straight-completing cards).

After betting and getting check-raised on the flop, Elon has seized the betting lead and his range is polarized between strong value (sets/strong 8x) and natural semibluffs (club draws, 56/67/75/A5 type hands). The turn Th adds an overcard to our pair and creates more potential equity for his semibluffs, but our 8x with a weak kicker is not strong enough to start building a bigger pot out of position. If we bet, we mostly get called/raised by better hands and fold out the bluffs we want to keep in; checking keeps the pot under control and allows Elon to continue barreling with his bluffs, letting us respond based on sizing and river runout.

Turn card Th is a relatively neutral card for my nut flush draw with Ac6c; it doesn't complete the flush but adds some straight draw possibilities for villain (e.g., 56, 79, J9), though unlikely to have hit given his SB calling range. FiveTwo's line: preflop SB raise (steal attempt vs limpers? No, positions: UTG folds etc., SB raise, BB call), small flop c-bet 200 into 500, then calls my 700 raise (total call 500 after initial 200), now checks turn. This suggests medium strength: pairs like 88/44/33/set, top pair weak kicker, draws (clubs, gutshots), or floats planning check-raise. His history shows aggression (e.g., Hand 50 bluff river, Hand 87 value with sets, Hand 91 floats) but folding to pressure sometimes. Pot 1900, my stack 8600 (86bb), his 7025 (70bb), late tournament (124/150), need to accumulate chips. Equity remains high (~35-45% vs calling range with 9 outs to nuts + fold equity). Checking risks giving free card to his draws or letting him realize equity; betting continues aggression, extracts value from worse pairs/draws (77-TT, club draws, 52 etc.), protects, builds pot for river jam if flush hits. Size 2/3 pot (1267 rounded to 1300) for balanced SPR ~2.5:1 post-call, pressuring short stacks while allowing fold equity. Cannot check-fold spew; value-bet best.

We’re getting ~29% pot odds (1300 to win 4500 after we call). After Elon’s flop check-raise, his range is polarized: strong value (sets/two pair/strong 8x) plus a lot of natural semi-bluffs (club draws, 56/67/75/A5 type hands) that should continue barreling on this turn. Our 8x is too high in our range to fold to this sizing, but it’s not strong enough to raise—raising mainly gets action from better hands and folds out the bluffs we want to keep in. Best is to call and make a river decision based on runout/sizing (more comfortable on bricks, more cautious on clubs/straight-completing cards or big jam sizing).

River brings the 7d giving us two pair (8s and 7h) on 4c-8c-3d-Th-7d. After Elon’s flop check-raise and turn barrel, his range is still polarized: strong value (sets like 33/44/88, turned sets like TT, and now the 56 straight) plus missed club-draw/semi-bluff combos that can give up if checked to. If we check, we risk him checking back a lot of worse one-pair hands (8x/4x/PPs) and we also reopen the door to a polarizing shove that puts us in a tough bluff-catch spot. A small value/block bet targets calls from worse pairs and some stubborn bluff-catchers, while setting the price versus his stronger hands; if he responds with a big raise/jam, it’s heavily weighted to straights/sets and we can comfortably bet-fold.

River 7d misses my nut club flush draw completely (9 outs turned into 0), leaving me with ace-high no pair on a board with possible straights (5-6), two-pair combos (87/34/48), sets (33/44/88), and made club flushes (AcX, KcX, etc.). FiveTwo's line is very strong: SB steal-raise pre, small flop c-bet on draw-heavy board, calls my polarized raise (suggesting not folding draws/weak), checks turn (trap or medium strength), calls my value-range turn bet (now pot-committed-ish with ~70bb effective pre-river), then donk-leads river for 25% pot (1500 into 6000). This isn't a bluff line—bluffs would check or smaller earlier/overbet; instead, it's classic value/thin value after inducing. His history shows aggression (steals like #122, value bets like #116) but folds to pressure when beat (#50, #87); here he persisted and now leads, repping made hands that beat missed draw. Pot odds ~4:1 (1500 into 7500) require ~20% equity, but A-high crushes vs air/draws but <15% vs his calling/leading range (pairs+). Late tourney (124/150, 5-handed), deep effective (~70bb but his 42bb post-bet), no need to hero-call thin; folding preserves stack for better spots to ladder/ICM. Cannot raise (no leverage/polarization), calling spews EV.

I am UTG with 5h 6s. This is a very weak starting hand, especially for opening from early position. Even though I have a substantial chip lead, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a low-equity hand when I have aggressive players and a short stack (Pro) behind me. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am in UTG+1 (MP) with 26 BBs. A2o is a weak hand to open from early/middle position, even in a 5-handed game. It plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated by calling ranges (better Aces, pairs). With large stacks behind me who can apply pressure or call wide, opening here is -EV. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the correct play.

We’re on the Button with A9o in a folded-to spot versus only the blinds. This is a profitable steal candidate: position advantage, decent high-card playability, and the As blocks many strong 3-bet/value continues from the blinds. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity too cheaply; folding is too tight given the dead money and our stack depth. I’ll open-raise to a size that pressures the blinds and keeps our range credible without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in Hold'em. Facing a button raise from FiveTwo, who likely has a wide range including suited connectors, Ax, broadway, etc., I need to 3-bet for value and to isolate. The BB (Claude) has a deep stack and may defend wide, but my hand dominates most of their calling ranges. Pot is 400, I've posted 50 SB, so raising to 900 total (about 3.6x the raise) is a standard aggressive sizing that builds the pot while denying equity to worse hands. With 72BB effective vs FiveTwo's 99BB, this commits appropriately without overplaying early. Late tournament stage (125/150), maximize value from premium while protecting stack.

9d5d is a weak suited hand. Facing a 3-bet from Elon (900) after FiveTwo's open, I'm out of position with a speculative hand that doesn't play well in 3-bet pots. Even with the suited aspect, 95s lacks good straight potential and a 9-high flush is vulnerable. The 800 to call is too much for this hand quality. Easy fold.

Elon’s small blind 3-bet versus my Button open should be reasonably wide 5-handed, and I’m getting a good price with position: 650 more to continue in a pot of ~1250. A9o isn’t strong enough to 4-bet for value, and a 4-bet bluff would need a lot of immediate folds while risking getting flatted or jammed on by a player who has shown aggression. Calling keeps his range wide, lets me realize equity in position, and I can play pot-control on marginal top-pair runouts (especially A-high boards where I can be dominated) while still having decent equity versus his broadway-heavy 3-bet range.

Flop of Qs 7h 2c is rainbow and Q-high, which largely misses FiveTwo's wide button calling range after my 3-bet (suited connectors, broadway, mid pairs, Ax). AA remains way ahead as an overpair, crushing most of his continuing range including weaker Qx, pairs below Q, and draws. Very few sets possible (QQ/77/22, unlikely given pre-flop action; QQ might 4-bet). No strong straight draws, minor flush draw potential. As pre-flop aggressor out of position, c-bet ~2/3 pot (1300 into 1900) to build pot for value from Qx/floats/draws, deny equity to overcards/draws, and maintain initiative. Late tournament (125/150), but with 64BB effective and premium hand, extracting value is key to growing stack. Check risks giving free cards to improvers.

Elon 3-bet from the SB and then fired a big c-bet (1300 into ~1900) on a dry Q-7-2 rainbow board. My hand (A♠9♣) has little showdown value versus his value region (Qx/overpairs), but it does have good blocker properties (A♠ reduces some AQ/A♠X continues) and a small backdoor nut-flush path. In position, this is a good spot to apply pressure because my preflop call range contains strong hands that can raise here (sets 22/77, Qx), while a large part of his 3-bet range is unpaired broadways that hate facing a raise at this SPR. I’ll use a small raise to maximize fold equity versus his air/overcards while keeping my risk controlled and comfortably folding if he jams.

Flop raise from FiveTwo after calling my 3-bet preflop on this dry Qs7h2c board (rainbow, Q-high, minimal draws). His range: polarized between value (Qx like AQ/KQ, unlikely sets QQ/77/22 as QQ likely 4-bets, 77/22 rare callers), medium strength (overpairs TT-JJ crushed), or bluffs (Ax, broadway missed, suited connectors). AA has excellent equity vs this: crushes bluffs/Ax/floats, live vs Qx (dominated if AQ/KQ, but races vs QJ/QT), only behind sets. Raising flop sizing 2x my c-bet into small pot suggests polarization. Folding AA OOP in 3-bet pot too tight, especially late tournament (125/150) where variance low, stacks allow (5100 eff ~72BB). Shoving all-in risks getting called by better (rare) or isolating bluffs but overplays if behind Qx. Calling 1300 into 5800 pot (becomes ~7100, SPR ~1 with 3800 behind) controls pot, realizes equity, induces bluffs on turn/brick rivers, sets up shove on scary turns or value on clean. FiveTwo history shows aggression (pots won bluff-heavy like H20, H87) but also value (H116 shove). Exploit by calling, re-eval turn.

Board: 2c 7h Qs Th. Turn 10h adds a second heart, completing some speculative heart flush draws (e.g., from suited connectors or Ax h), gutshots (KJ, 89, J9), and broadway possibilities, but no obvious straights complete and AA blocks top sets. My AA remains an overpair, crushing FiveTwo's likely polarized flop raise range: value (Qx like KQ/AQ, medium pairs TT-JJ now vulnerable), rare monsters (QQ/77/22, Q10s unlikely caller pre), bluffs (missed Ax, broadway, suited gappers). Flop call was correct for equity realization and trap potential. Now OOP first to act in 7100 pot with 3800 behind (SPR ~0.7 eff vs his 6725), checking induces bluffs/draws from aggressive FiveTwo (history: H116 flop shove, H87 river jam, H124 river value), allows value extraction if he bets worse Qx/draws (I jam/call), while avoiding bloating pot OOP with bet/jam risking fold-out bluffs. Late tourney (125/150, 5 players), 38BB stack: preserve fold equity post-turn while maximizing vs his wide aggression. No fold/call needed (to-call 0). Check optimal.

Turn goes check to me after I raised flop and got called. With A♠9♣ on Q♠7♥2♣T♥ I have essentially no equity (no real draw) and very little showdown value, so checking back just concedes a big pot and lets Elon realize equity with all his missed broadways. The best way to realize the value of my flop raise is to follow through and maximize fold equity while the SPR is low: my line credibly represents strong hands from my BTN-call range (sets 22/77/TT, AQ/KQ, QTs), and the T is a good barrel card that improves my perceived range. Elon’s check also contains give-ups (AK/AJ-type hands) and some overpairs that can still find folds facing an all-in. Therefore I apply maximum pressure and put him to the decision for his remaining stack.

Facing an all-in call for 3800 into 10900 (pot odds ~2.9:1, needing ~26% equity), AA remains a massive favorite against FiveTwo's polarized range. His button 3-bet call preflop (wide but decent: JJ+, AQ+, suited broadways/connectors), flop raise on dry Qs7h2c (value: Qx like AQ/KQ/QJs, sets QQ/77/22 rare; bluffs: Ax/broadway misses), and turn overbet 3800 (~53% pot) after my check suggest strength but also fits his aggressive history (e.g., H116 flop shove with air/showdown, H87 river jam, H124 river value bet after check). Turn Th completes hearts (hits suited callers like JTs/9Ts/Kh), gutshots (KJ/J9), and QT straight, but AA blocks AA/sets, crushes overpairs/bluffs/draws/floats/weak Qx (QJ/QT crushed or live), and races QQ (2 outs). Equity >50% vs QQ/AQ/KQ/JTs/QTs mix, >90% vs bluffs/Ax. Low SPR (~0.5 effective post-call), late tourney (125/150, 5 left, my 38BB stack), variance manageable with 25 hands left; folding premium OOP too tight, risks blinded out. Calling realizes equity, exploits his aggression, grows stack if ahead (likely). No raise possible (all-in).

I am in UTG position with a stack of roughly 26 BBs. I hold Queen-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Given my early position and the poor playability of this hand, I must fold immediately. There is no reason to enter the pot voluntarily with this holding.

5-handed from UTG+1 with T5o, we’re too early to enter: the hand is dominated by any Tx that continues and plays poorly postflop, especially against larger stacks in position (Button) and the blinds. With ~29BB, preserving stack and avoiding marginal, dominated spots is higher EV than open-limping or opening and getting played back. Fold and wait for better steal/reshove candidates.

Preflop on the button with Qs4d after two early folds (Pro UTG and FiveTwo UTG+1). Excellent position with deep stacks. Q4o is within a standard button opening range (wide ~50% due to fold equity against blinds). Blinds are deep (Claude 16362, Flash 23238), but stealing blinds adds to stack late in tournament (hand 126/150). Postflop playability decent from position with queen-high. Raising builds pot with initiative or takes it down now (~60-70% fold equity est.). Limp or call weak; fold too tight. Size to 250 (2.5x BB) standard and effective.

Pocket 99 is a strong hand vs a button open from Elon. 3-betting for value is the standard play here - I want to build a pot in position against a likely wide button range. A 3-bet to around 800 is appropriate sizing.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 4s. The action opened with a raise from the Button (Elon) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Claude). 64o is a very weak hand and does not justify calling 700 more, especially when facing a 3-bet. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Preflop on button with Qs4d facing a 3-bet to 800 from Claude in SB after my open to 250 (pot 1150, to call 550). Q4o is a weak speculative hand with poor postflop playability, low equity vs typical 3-bet ranges (e.g., ~25-35% vs polarized range), dominated often, and bad reverse implied odds even if hitting queen. Position is good (act last postflop), but calling OOP for Claude first risks tough spots with trash; pot odds ~3:1 but realization low deepstacked (~140BB). 4-betting as bluff possible but risky with no blockers, large sizing needed, and Claude can 5-bet shove or call wide. Late tournament (hand 126/150), preserve stack vs aggressive SB; folding marginal opens is standard GTO, exploits if Claude loose by not bloating pot OOP. Opponent history shows Claude raises 3-bets but sizing decent; better spots await button with deeper stacks.

5-handed UTG is effectively a middle/late position spot, so we can open a reasonably wide range. 6d5d is a suited connector with good playability (can make strong disguised straights/flushes) and benefits from having the initiative. Limping invites isolation from the deeper stacks behind and puts us in tougher multiway spots; folding is a bit too tight given the blinds we can win uncontested. I’ll open to a small-but-standard size to pressure the blinds while preserving our 29bb stack; if we face a strong 3-bet/jam from the big stacks, we can comfortably fold.

83o is a complete trash hand from early position (UTG+1) facing an UTG open raise from FiveTwo. Poor playability post-flop, dominated equity against any reasonable raising range, unfavorable pot odds (calling 250 into 400 is ~2:1 but requires 33% equity which this hand lacks), no suitedness or straight potential worth speculating. With deep stack and tournament nearing end (127/150 hands), preserve chips for premium spots. Standard fold.

I have 7c3h - one of the worst hands in poker. Even though I'm on the button with position, calling a UTG raise with 7-3 offsuit is a clear fold. This hand has very poor playability and almost no equity against a UTG opening range.

I am holding 6-4 offsuit in the small blind, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo has opened from early position for 2.5x the big blind. Both FiveTwo and Pro (in the big blind) are short-stacked with approximately 25-27 big blinds. Calling here would put me out of position with a marginal hand against players who are likely looking for opportunities to commit their stacks. Folding is the most efficient play to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six suited (Kc 6c). The UTG player (FiveTwo) raised to 250 (2.5BB). The pot is currently 400 chips (including blinds and ante structure if implied, but visible chips are SB 50 + BB 100 + Raise 250). I need to call 150 more chips to see the flop. The pot odds offered are 150 to win 400 (approx 2.66:1 or 27% equity required). While I am out of position against an UTG raise, the game is 5-handed, making UTG effectively a Hijack open, which widens the range slightly. FiveTwo has been active recently. My hand has flush potential and top pair potential. With a stack of ~25BB, I can afford the 1.5BB call to try and hit a favorable flop. Folding is slightly too tight given the price and suit. Raising is unnecessary. I will call.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc6c. The flop is As-5c-2s. This board favors the pre-flop raiser's range (UTG) which contains many Ax hands. I have King high with a backdoor flush draw. Out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) makes no sense here as I fold out worse hands and get action from better hands. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on A-5-2, this board strongly favors my opening range (lots of Ax) and I have a made hand (pair of 5s) that benefits from denying equity to overcards and charging spade/straight backdoors. Checking back lets Pro realize for free and can invite tough turn leads. I’ll c-bet small to keep his continues wide (worse 5x, pocket pairs, floats) while risking little; if raised, I can comfortably fold this weak pair.

I hold Kc6c on a flop of As5c2s. I have missed the flop, holding only King-high with a weak backdoor flush draw (needing runner-runner clubs) and virtually no straight potential. The opponent (UTG) has bet 200 into a pot of 550. The Ace on the board connects well with an UTG opening range, making it likely they have top pair or are representing it effectively. Floating out of position with just King-high and backdoors against a likely strong range is not profitable, even with the relatively small bet size. Preserving my 23BB stack for better opportunities is the prudent play.

UTG in a 5-handed game with Qc3h offsuit, a very weak queen-high hand with poor playability and no suited or connected value. Standard pre-flop range from UTG does not include Q3o. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 128/150). No information from previous actions this hand warrants entering the pot. Pot is blinds only (150), to call 100 for BB, but not worth it.
7c 8d offsuit from UTG+1 is a weak hand. Not in my opening range from early position. With 22 hands left and a healthy stack of 167 BBs, I should remain disciplined and fold marginal hands like this.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight suited, a premium hand for this position. The two players remaining in the blinds, Pro and FiveTwo, are both quite short-stacked (23.25 BB and 31.25 BB respectively). With the action folded to me, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to attack the blinds. Given their stack sizes, I must be prepared for a potential shove, but Ah8h is strong enough to play against most of their defense or shove ranges.

Current hand is 9d 2c, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I am in the Small Blind with a stack of 23.25 BBs. The Button (Flash) has raised to 3 BBs. Even against a wide button opening range, 92o has poor equity (approx 29%) and suffers from being out of position. The pot odds (requiring ~36% equity) do not justify a call, and the hand is too weak for a 3-bet bluff given my stack depth. Folding is the only standard play to preserve chips for better spots.

Button opens to 3bb and we’re getting a very good price in the BB: call 200 to win 450 (~31% needed). Versus Flash’s likely wide button raising range, K2o has enough raw equity and we shouldn’t overfold our blind, especially with ~31bb where preserving defend frequency matters. 3-betting (or shoving) is too high-variance and unlikely to generate enough folds with this holding, so the best EV line is a defend and play postflop cautiously when dominated.

Flop 6-7-8 is a dynamic board that hits the BB defending range, so leading is possible in theory. But with K2o we have essentially zero equity (no pair, no straight draw, no meaningful backdoors) and no useful blockers, so turning it into a donk-bluff is too fragile—Flash can call/raise with a wide range and we’ll be forced to give up often. Best EV is to check, realize any chance of a free card, and respond to his c-bet based on sizing.

On the flop of 6h 7d 8c, I have top pair (Eights) with an Ace kicker and a backdoor flush draw. This board is quite connected, offering many straight possibilities (9-5 or 10-9). However, I have top pair and FiveTwo checked to me. In a blind-versus-button battle, my range includes many strong hands, and this specific hand is strong enough to bet for value and protection against various draws. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to charge draws and get value from smaller pairs or Ace-high hands that FiveTwo might be defending with.

Flash’s c-bet is small (325 into 975), so the price is tempting, but K2o on 6-7-8 has essentially no real equity: no pair, no gutshot, no flush draw, and no meaningful backdoors or blockers to the strong continues (9T, T9, sets, two-pair, pair+draws). We’d need ~25% to call and we’re well short versus a reasonable button c-bet range. Turning this into a check-raise bluff is also poor with our stack depth (~29bb) because we have no blockers and will get played back at (call/raise/jam) too often on this dynamic board. Best EV is to preserve chips and fold.

Pocket 4s in UTG at a 5-handed table. Small pairs have set-mining value with my deep stack (167 BB). Standard open raise to 2.5x to build a pot and potentially flop a set. Folding is too tight 5-handed.

I am holding 5s 9c in UTG+1. Claude (UTG) has opened with a raise to 250. 5s 9c is an extremely weak hand and does not play well against an UTG opening range. Additionally, there are several players behind me, including two short stacks (Pro and FiveTwo) who might look for an opportunity to shove. There is no strategic reason to call or raise here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with a stack of 23.25 BBs holding 5c3c. The UTG player (Claude, massive stack) has opened to 2.5 BBs. While 53s has some playability, calling here leaves me with a short stack-to-pot ratio post-flop and susceptible to squeezes from the blinds. My stack is too shallow to profitably set-mine or speculate with low suited gappers against a UTG opening range. The risk of domination or being forced to fold post-flop is high. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

Facing a 2.5bb open from Claude (effectively early position in 5-handed), K2s is too dominated to defend profitably from the small blind. Even with a decent immediate price, we’ll be out of position postflop, and Elon in the big blind can overcall/squeeze with deep stacks, worsening realization. A 3-bet bluff is also poor versus a relatively strong opening range and with our ~29bb stack. Best play is to conserve chips and avoid reverse implied odds.

Facing a min-raise from Claude UTG in the BB with JTo offsuit. Pot is 400, calling 150 more (total 250 committed) for ~2.67:1 odds. Late in tournament (hand 129/150), stacks are deep (143BB effective). UTG range is tight but raise size is small (2.5x), and JTo has straight potential, broadway playability, and ~30-35% equity vs a reasonable UTG range (e.g., 12-15%: pairs 77+, ATo+, KJo+, suited connectors). Defending BB wide OOP is standard here as fold equity postflop and implied odds if we hit. Folding too tight given pot odds; raising risky OOP with marginal hand. Call to realize equity and control pot size OOP.

OOP on flop as BB defender vs UTG raiser with TJo which completely misses the Ks3s7d board. No pair, no straight draw (broadway gutshot not present), no flush draw (backdoor spade with Js), pure air with two overcards but likely dominated by Claude's tight UTG range that hits this board well (AK/AQ/KQ, 77+, sets possible). Deep stacks late in tournament, no need to bluff OOP into strong range on coordinated board (spade draw, possible straights like 456 but unlikely). Pot odds favorable but equity <20% vs range; check to realize fold equity if he bets or hope for check-behind, but prepare to fold. Betting risks raise and no fold equity given position.

Missed my set on Ks 3s 7d flop with pocket 4s. Elon checked to me from the BB. I have minimal showdown value on this K-high board with a flush draw possible. A c-bet here could work to take it down, but if called I'm in a tough spot. Checking back preserves pot control and lets me see a turn cheaply - if I spike a 4 I can win a bigger pot, otherwise I can make decisions based on Elon's action.

OOP on turn with TdJs (jack-high, no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw) on a Ks3s7d7c board vs UTG raiser Claude. Complete air despite live J and backdoor potential, equity ~10-20% vs his strong range (KQ/KT/AK/77+/sets/draws). Pot 550, deep stacks (140BB effective). First to act: betting risks raise from his capping range and poor post-turn playability; fold equity low OOP on paired/drawy board where he has nut advantage. Checking realizes equity (check-behind possible with paired board scaring overpairs/AK), controls pot, and sets up cheap river if he weak bets. Late tournament, prioritize survival over marginal bluffs. Standard check-fold spot.

Turn paired the 7s on Ks 3s 7d 7c. I have pocket 4s - an underpair with minimal showdown value. Elon has checked twice showing weakness, but betting here accomplishes little. I only get called by better hands (Kx, 7x, higher pairs). Checking keeps pot small and I might win at showdown if he has complete air like missed suited connectors.

River board Ks3s7d7c7h gives the board trip 7s (7d7c7h). My TdJs is jack-high air with no pair, draw, or showdown value; pure fold to any bet. OOP first to act after mutual checks on flop and turn (dry Ks3s7d then paired 7c). Claude's UTG raising range (tight, e.g., 88+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways/connectors) polarizes post-checkdown: monsters like any 7 (quads, 7s left), KK (full house), 33 (full house), or medium strength like AK/AQ/KQ/TT/99/88/66 which beat me but may check behind fearing board strength; total air unlikely given position/range but possible. Pot 550, deep effective stacks (~140BB). Bluffing poor: low fold equity OOP on scary capped board (he hero calls wide with medium hands, raises value); no blockers to nuts (no K/3/7); history suggests check-check showdown likely. Checking realizes equity (free showdown or cheap river if weak bet), controls pot, preserves stack late (hand 129/150, 5 left). Bet risks losing more to calls/raises without upside. Check standard, fold to bet.

River brought the third 7, giving me a full house 7-7-7-4-4. This is a strong hand that beats all Kx (trips), lower pocket pairs (22-33), and any hand playing the board. However, Elon has checked all three streets showing extreme weakness. If I bet, he likely folds anything I beat. Higher pocket pairs (55+) beat me. Given his passive line, a bet won't get called by worse often. Checking to showdown is optimal - I'm likely winning already.

I am currently the chip leader with 23,538 chips. In Hand #130, I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position at a 5-handed table. I am holding Kd 8h, which is a marginal hand to open from early position. There are two very short stacks behind me (Pro with 2,325 and FiveTwo with 2,875) who are likely looking for opportunities to shove. Raising with K8o would put me in a difficult spot if either of them shoves, as the hand doesn't play particularly well against their jamming ranges. To maintain my lead and minimize unnecessary variance in the final stages of the tournament, I will fold this hand and wait for a more advantageous position or a stronger holding.

Current stack is 23.25 BBs (2,325 chips). Sitting in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed) with A2s. This is a standard opening hand from late position to steal the blinds and antes. While the stack is somewhat short, it is too deep to open-shove, and folding is too passive given the need to accumulate chips in the late stage of the tournament (Hand 130/150). The Ace blocker reduces the likelihood of opponents holding strong Aces. I will make a small raise to 2.25 BBs (225) to balance fold equity with stack preservation in case of a 3-bet.

Pro opens from UTG+1 with a short ~21bb stack, which is typically a relatively strong/tight range. 6♠4♦ offsuit has poor raw equity and plays badly even in position (domination/reverse implied odds). Calling needs ~37.5% immediate equity (225 to win 600 after calling) and we won’t realize that often enough versus this range plus two blinds behind. A 3-bet bluff/jam risks running into a calling range that crushes us and doesn’t justify the tournament/stack risk with this hand. Best decision is to conserve chips and wait for a better spot.

T6o is a weak offsuit hand with gaps, unsuitable for calling a raise OOP from the SB against an early-position raiser like Pro, who likely has a strong range. Pot odds are decent (175 to call into 375), but postflop playability is poor out of position, and implied odds are limited with Pro's short stack of ~21BB. No reason to 3-bet bluff here either. Folding preserves my deep stack (141BB) for better spots late in the tournament with 20 hands left.

T6o is a weak hand facing an UTG+1 raise. While I'm getting decent pot odds (125 to win 375), T6o plays poorly postflop and I'm out of position. Pro has a short stack (21BB) but is still raising from early position. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in UTG with a very weak hand (94o) and a stack of roughly 25 big blinds. Opening from early position with trash hands is a losing strategy, especially with several players left to act behind me. The correct play is to fold immediately.

5-handed and with Pro folding UTG, A9o is comfortably ahead of the folding threshold from UTG+1 and performs well as an open, especially with an Ace blocker. With ~29bb, I want to keep applying pressure and pick up blinds/antes (pot is already 150) while using a sizing that doesn’t commit me versus a 3-bet. I’ll open to a standard 2.5x and proceed cautiously versus large 3-bets from the deep stacks behind.

AdTh is a strong hand from the button facing an early position min-raise from FiveTwo, who has a short stack of ~26BB. Position allows control post-flop, and ATo has good equity against their likely wide range. SB and BB are deep but may fold to a 3-bet. Raising isolates the short stack, builds the pot with a premium hand, and applies pressure given the tournament stage (hand 131/150). Size to 750 (3x their raise) is standard for value and fold equity without overcommitting.
Holding 4d6h facing a 3-bet to 750. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Against a raise and 3-bet, 46o has terrible equity and no real potential. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Three offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo opened with a raise from UTG+1, and Elon has followed up with a 3-bet from the button. Given the strength indicated by a 3-bet and the fact that I would be playing out of position against two opponents with a subpar hand, folding is the only logical and professional move here to preserve my stack.

We’re 5-handed with ~26bb effective. After opening A9o and facing a BTN 3-bet to 7.5bb, flat-calling out of position creates a very awkward ~1.1 SPR spot where we’ll often be dominated on A-high flops and pressured off equity. This is a better candidate for a 4-bet jam than a call: the As blocks the strongest continues (AA/AK/AQ combos), there’s meaningful dead money (1150) to win immediately, and when called we still retain reasonable equity versus typical 3-bet/call ranges. Shoving maximizes fold equity and avoids playing a capped, tough OOP postflop spot.

FiveTwo is short-stacked with only 250 chips left after their 4-bet to 2625, making this effectively an all-in confrontation post-decision. AdTh is a strong hand with excellent blocker effects (blocks AA, AK) and good equity (~45%+) against a short-stack shoving range from early position after a 3-bet, which includes pairs, Ax, broadway, and some bluffs. Pot odds are favorable: calling 1875 into a 3525 pot requires ~35% equity, which we crush. Raising adds unnecessary risk with no folds possible (heads-up) and doesn't improve odds significantly given their tiny remaining stack. Folding wastes equity and position on the button deep-stacked. Late tournament (131/150), calling builds the stack by realizing our equity against a committed opponent.

We’re effectively all-in already: pot 5,400 and I only have 250 left (SPR ~0.05). Any bet from me has essentially zero fold equity because Elon is getting massive pot odds to call, so jamming doesn’t accomplish much. By checking, I at least maximize my chance to realize equity for free (hit an A/9 or occasionally win at showdown if he checks back with some missed AK-less holdings), whereas betting just forces the same outcome without improving EV.

FiveTwo is pot-committed with only 250 chips left behind into a 5400 pot (~4.6% pot), after leading with a massive 4-bet preflop from early position as a short stack (~26BB initially). The flop Kc4c5d misses AdTh directly (ace-high, no pair, no strong draw), but Ad provides key blockers to AA and AK, reducing the nuts in their polarized shoving range (sets like KK/44, AK, perhaps QQ/JJ, bluffs like Axs). Equity vs. this range is around 25-35% (blocks top of range, live overcards), sufficient given implied odds are moot with their stack size. They checked flop OOP, which could indicate a weak king/draw rather than a monster (monsters often lead). Betting 250 shoves them all-in, maximizing fold equity against bluffs/marginal hands while denying equity to chase draws (clubs, 63s, A3o). Checking risks free cards to flush/straight draws or turn pairing their undercards, and showdown value is poor. Position allows control; late tournament (131/150, 5 players), growing stack by eliminating shorty or stealing pot crucial. Standard play: bet the short stack.

We’re getting an absurd price: calling 250 into a pot that will be 5900 gives us ~22.6:1, so we only need about 4.2% equity. Even versus a strong range (Kx/overpairs/sets), we still have outs (A is often good, sometimes 9 helps, plus occasional chop/air scenarios). With only 250 behind, folding would be a huge EV mistake.

4-handed play widens UTG opening ranges significantly. KJo is a premium hand here: king-high, suited connectors potential with live cards, plays well post-flop from in-position if called. Short stack Pro (24BB) in BB may shove wide, but KJo crushes typical shoves. Deep stacks overall favor raising for value and fold equity. Avoid limping UTG. Standard 3x open to 300 isolates and builds pot without overcommitting.

7d9h on the button facing an UTG raise to 300. This is a weak offsuit hand. While I have position, 97o doesn't play well against a UTG opening range which is typically strong. The hand lacks the connectivity or high card value to profitably continue. Folding is the disciplined play here.

In a 4-handed game, Elon's UTG raise is effectively from the cutoff position. While JTo is a decent hand, it is easily dominated by a standard opening range (hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, KT, and pocket pairs). Furthermore, I am in the Small Blind, meaning I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if I call. Pro, the Big Blind, is currently short-stacked (23 BBs) and might see a call as an opportunity to shove, which would force me to fold and lose my investment. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, I prefer to avoid marginal situations out of position against another significant stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Eights [8d, 8s]. Facing a standard 3x raise from Elon (UTG), I have a decision between calling and 3-bet shoving. My stack is roughly 24BB. Shoving 24BB into a UTG open is slightly over-aggressive and high variance, as I am often flipping or dominated by a strong opening range. However, 88 is too strong to fold. The most standard play is to defend the Big Blind by calling. This offers good pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 650, requiring ~31% equity) and allows me to see a flop. With an SPR of ~3 post-flop, I can make decisions based on board texture, looking to get chips in on low boards or set-mining.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Pocket Eights on a very dangerous flop of [Qc, Jd, Ac]. This board texture heavily favors the UTG opener's range, which contains all the strong Aces, sets (AA, QQ, JJ), and straights (KT), as well as two-pair combinations. My hand is effectively an underpair to the board and has very low equity against a continuing range. I have no flush draw and no realistic straight potential. Betting would serve no purpose as better hands call and worse hands fold. My only sensible option is to check and evaluate, intending to fold to any significant aggression.

Heads-up vs short-stacked Pro (21BB) on a draw-heavy flop Ac Qc Jd that connects decently with my UTG raising range (AJs, KQs, QJs, sets, etc.). My Kc Jh gives me a pair of jacks with live king kicker, decent showdown value, and blocks some straight draws like KT. Pro checked, likely weak or trapping with premium; as preflop raiser IP, standard c-bet for thin value/protection against flush draws, open-enders (KT, T9), and weaker pairs (QJ, JTs). Bet 60% pot (~400) sizes well against short stack: gets value from draws/worse Ax, sets up turn shove if villain floats, fold equity vs air. Checking risks giving free card to draws or letting villain rep AK/AQ on turn.

I hold Pocket Eights on a flop of [Qc, Jd, Ac]. This board texture is disastrous for my hand, hitting the UTG opener's range (sets, two pairs, broadway straights, top pairs) extremely hard. My pair of eights is significantly behind, and I have effectively no redraws. Facing a bet of 400 into a pot of 650, I have no reason to continue. My equity is negligible, and I am drawing dead or very thin against almost all value hands. Folding is the clear and only correct play.
QTs suited is a solid opening hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Good broadway hand with flush and straight potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has opened with a raise from UTG. Given the current tournament stage (Hand 133/150) and my significant chip lead, there is no strategic reason to defend or contest this pot with such a poor hand against an UTG opening range. Additionally, Pro in the small blind is short-stacked and could potentially jam, which makes calling even less attractive. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with a short stack of 2125 chips (approx 21 BB). The player in the UTG position (Claude) has raised to 250. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, is not strong enough to call out of position against a UTG opening range, which is typically tighter and stronger. Calling would commit about 10% of my remaining stack to a marginal situation where I am likely dominated or flipping at best. A 3-bet shove is also too loose against a UTG open. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

In the big blind facing an UTG raise to 250 from Claude (16k stack), with J8 suited connectors. Pot is 400, calling an additional 150 gives excellent pot odds (over 2.5:1 direct, plus implied). J8h has strong playability post-flop: flush draw potential, straight draws, and good equity against a wide UTG range in a tournament spot with 18 hands left. Position on Claude post-flop allows realization of equity. Folding is too tight multiway blind defense; 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP with a marginal hand. Flat to see flop cheap and play in position.

Heads-up on the flop with middle pair (eights) and jack kicker against Claude's UTG raising range, which is strong but includes hands like AJs+, KQs, smaller pairs that I dominate or have equity against. Board has a diamond flush draw, so betting protects against draws and gets value from worse pairs (77-99, weaker Ax) or floats. Position OOP, pot control via check risks giving free cards to overcards/draws. Pot 550, betting ~2/3 pot (375) balances value, protection, and fold equity vs his missed range (overcards like AQo). Deep stacks (17k effective), but tournament late with 18 hands left; extracting value now maximizes EV without overbetting into monsters. Folding or checking too passive given equity realization.

Completely whiffed this flop with QTs - no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), only a weak backdoor straight draw. Elon donk-betting into me suggests he connected with this board (likely has a King or a pair). With no equity and facing a bet, folding is the clear choice.

I am currently the chip leader with 23,388 chips. I am UTG with 8-6 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand to open with, especially as the first person to act. Given my significant stack advantage and the stage of the tournament (Hand 134/150), there is no need to play marginal or weak hands. I will wait for a better spot to apply pressure to the shorter stacks.

I am on the Button with 4c 7d, a very weak hand. I have a short stack of 2125 chips (approx 21 BBs) compared to my opponents who have huge stacks (>16k). There are only 16 hands left in the tournament. Given my hand strength is extremely low and I am the short stack, I cannot afford to open loose and get called or raised by the big stacks in the blinds. I must preserve my chips for a better opportunity to shove or steal. The correct play is to fold.

Preflop in small blind, folded to me vs big blind Claude (deep stack). My hand 3sTh is trash: offsuit, gapped, low equity, poor playability out of position. Completing 50 into 200 effective pot (~3:1) is -EV with ~30% equity needed but this hand plays poorly postflop OOP. No steal value given deep stacks and tournament stage (hand 134/150). Preserve stack for premium spots. Fold.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with 21 big blinds, which is the shortest stack by a significant margin. My hand, King-Three offsuit (K3o), is very weak and falls well outside standard opening ranges from this position. With huge stacks behind me likely to apply pressure or call widely, opening this hand would be a negative EV play. My goal is to preserve my stack for better spots to shove or raise. Therefore, the correct action is to fold.

Preflop on the button with 82o, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Pro has folded UTG, but 82o has terrible playability postflop and poor equity against calling ranges from SB or BB. Even from the button, this is an easy fold to preserve stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 135/150). No steal incentive here given the hand's weakness.

Q4o in the SB with Pro and Elon folding. It's just me vs Flash in the BB. Q4o is weak but heads-up against one player, I can try a small steal attempt. Folding gives up 50 chips, completing is weak, so I'll raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds. If Flash 3-bets, easy fold.

Claude has raised from the Small Blind, and I am in the Big Blind with 7-5 offsuit. While the price to call is relatively small (150 more), 7-5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. As the current chip leader with only 15 hands remaining, I prefer to avoid marginal situations out of position and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Hand #136, 4-handed, UTG position with 17,775 stack. Holding J4o, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and dominated often. UTG requires a tight opening range even short-handed; premium pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc., but J4o is trash. No position advantage, multiple opponents behind including deep stacks Claude and Flash, and short stack Pro in BB who might shove. Pot 150 (blinds 50/100), to call 100 implies standard BB amount but as first to act, folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (136/150 hands). No fold equity worth risking; standard fold.

On the button with 2h6h - a very weak suited hand. While I have position, 2-6 suited is at the bottom of playable hands. Pro is short-stacked (2025 chips) and may look to shove. Not worth investing chips with this marginal holding when I could face aggression from the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario after the action folded to me. The Big Blind (Pro) has a relatively short stack of 2,025 chips, making him vulnerable to pressure. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and either win the blinds immediately or play against Pro's wider defensive range.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 7s and 20BBs. The Small Blind has raised to 3x (300). Although it is a Blind vs Blind scenario where ranges are wider, 75o is a very weak hand that performs poorly even against a wide opening range (around 36% equity). I am getting 2:1 pot odds, requiring 33% equity realization, but being out of position with a hand that rarely flops well makes it difficult to realize that equity. Given my stack depth and the larger raise size (3x vs min-raise), folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots in the final hands.
9c7h offsuit from UTG in a 4-handed game. This is a weak hand with poor playability. Not strong enough to open from first position - should fold and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with K8o in a 4-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. Pro in the small blind is quite short-stacked with less than 20 big blinds, so I need to be prepared for a potential shove, but K8o is strong enough to open for value and fold equity against the blinds. I will raise to 300 to stay consistent with my previous opening sizes.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand (94o) and a stack of roughly 20 BB (1975 chips). The Button (Flash) has raised to 3x BB. Defending out of position with 94o is not profitable due to poor playability and equity. A reshove is also not viable as the hand is too weak and my stack is still large enough to wait for better opportunities. The correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

9h2h is a very weak suited hand with poor high-card strength and limited playability. Facing a button raise after folds, Flash's range is wide but still favors better hands. From the big blind out of position, calling 200 into 450 offers marginal pot odds (~23% equity required), but my hand has low equity against a typical button opening range (dominated by better suited connectors, Ax suited, broadways) and plays poorly post-flop OOP with reverse implied odds. Folding preserves my deep stack (176 BB effective) for premium spots in the late stages (hand 137/150), avoiding speculative defense with trash. Raising is never correct with this holding.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game with a massive chip lead. Holding Ten-Seven offsuit, this is a very weak hand to open from the first position to act, even at this stage of the tournament. With two other deep stacks behind me and a short stack on the button who might look for a shove, there is no strategic reason to commit chips with such a marginal hand. I will preserve my lead and fold.

I am holding 4s 2h on the Button with a stack of roughly 20 big blinds. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With active large stacks in the blinds (Elon and Claude) and my short stack status in the late stage of the tournament (Hand 138/150), attempting to steal here is ill-advised. I must preserve my stack for a hand with actual equity or fold equity potential. Folding is the only viable option.

Preflop in SB heads-up vs BB after UTG and Button folds. K8s is a strong suited king hand with good playability and potential to make strong hands or draws postflop. Pot is 150, completing costs 50 for great odds, but raising applies pressure to steal the BB frequently given tournament dynamics and late stage. Claude in BB has deep stack but folds often to aggression in history. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing to take it down or play profitably OOP if called. Minimizes risk while maximizing fold equity.

Facing a raise to 300 from Elon in the SB with Q4o in the BB. Getting 2:1 odds (200 to win 400), but Q4o plays poorly out of position. This is a marginal hand that will struggle postflop. With a healthy stack at 16.7k, no need to take marginal spots with weak holdings. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in UTG position (CO in 4-handed) with a stack of roughly 20 big blinds (1975 chips). The hand 4d Td is a weak speculative hand, disconnected and low. With a short stack compared to opponents who cover me significantly, I cannot afford to open loose speculative hands that are easily dominated or face 3-bets. My strategy is to preserve chips for better shoving or opening opportunities (pairs, high cards, better suited connectors). Therefore, folding is the correct play.

On the button with KTo offsuit after UTG (short stack Pro) folds. This is a premium spot to open-raise a wide range to steal the dead money in the pot (150) and potentially isolate blinds. KTo has good high-card strength, broadway potential, and playability in position. Standard sizing around 2.5x BB. Raising to 250 pressures SB Claude and BB Flash, who may fold weaker hands. Deep stacks allow flexibility post-flop. Folding or limping suboptimal here.
2d3h is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Out of position against a button raise with 12 hands left in the tournament. No reason to defend with such a weak holding - easy fold.

I am the current chip leader with 23,438 chips and there are only 12 hands remaining in the tournament. In this hand, Elon raised from the button to 250. My hand, 8s 2h, is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Although the cost to call is only 150 more, there is no strategic reason to defend the big blind with garbage cards against a stack that can potentially hurt me. I will fold and wait for better spots to maintain my lead.

K5o is a marginal offsuit king from UTG in a 4-handed game near the end of the tournament (hand 140/150). It plays poorly postflop, often dominated by better kings, and lacks suited or connected value for speculative play. Pro is short-stacked in BB (18.75BB) and may defend wide or shove, while deep stacks (Flash SB, Claude BTN) can put pressure. Folding preserves my deep stack (179BB) for premium spots in the final hands to maximize stack size. Limp unnecessary risk; raising too thin without position.

On the button with J3s after UTG folded. This is a marginal hand but I have position. Pro is short-stacked at ~19 BBs. J3s has some playability being suited with a broadway card. Standard button steal attempt makes sense - raising to 250 to try to take down the blinds.

I am currently the chip leader with 23,388 chips at hand 140 of 150. I have been dealt pocket nines (9h 9c) in the small blind. Claude has opened from the button for 250. Pro in the big blind is very short-stacked with only 1,875 chips. Pocket nines are a premium hand in a 4-handed game, significantly ahead of a standard button opening range. My goal is to maximize value and potentially isolate Claude or induce a shove from the short-stacked Pro. I will put in a 3-bet to 850, which is a standard size to put pressure on the opener while still being priced to call a shove from the big blind.

I am holding Ten-Five offsuit in the Big Blind with a stack of 1875 chips (approx. 18.75 BB). The action involves a Button raise to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 850. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly multi-way or heads-up against strong ranges. Calling requires committing a significant portion of my stack (750 chips) with very low equity. Given the aggression shown by the Small Blind and my short stack relative to the blinds and tournament stage (Hand 140/150), preserving my chips for a better opportunity to shove or defend is the only viable strategy. Folding is the correct decision.

Flash 3-bet me to 850 after my button raise. J3s is too weak to continue against a 3-bet - I'll be dominated frequently when I hit, and don't have good implied odds. Getting 2:1 isn't enough to call with such a marginal hand out of position postflop. Standard fold.
T3o is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position. With 9 hands left in the tournament and a decent stack, there's no reason to play this trash hand out of position. Easy fold.

I am currently the chip leader with 23,788 chips and only 10 hands remaining in the tournament. While I am on the button, which is an ideal position to steal blinds, 7-8 offsuit is a marginal hand. Pro, in the Small Blind, has a relatively short stack (1,825) and is likely looking for a spot to shove. Raising here might force me to fold to a jam and lose chips unnecessarily. Given my dominant lead and the tournament's proximity to the end, I will play conservatively and fold this hand to maintain my stack.

Current stack is ~18.25 BBs. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, KJs is a premium hand. With this stack depth, open-shoving is an option but a bit large (18bb). A standard raise is preferred to extract value from weaker hands that defend the BB. I am prepared to call a shove given the hand strength and stack size, or play post-flop. Raising to 2.5 BBs strikes a balance between building the pot and maintaining maneuverability.

Hand 141/150, late in tournament with 4 players left. In BB with KcTd offsuit facing a 2.5x raise to 250 from short-stacked SB Pro (1625 total stack, 1375 behind). Pot 350, calling 150 more for great odds (~2.3:1). KTo plays well vs wide SB raising range, has good postflop playability and king blocker. Defending BB wide is standard here. Will call and see flop cheap, re-evaluate vs potential shove given effective stacks and my deep stack (17825). Raising risks commitment vs short stack shove where equity may be marginal offsuit.

I have flopped top two pair on a board with a flush draw (Js Kd 3s). This is a very strong hand, especially given my short stack (~16 BBs). With a flush draw present, giving a free card is risky. I want to extract value immediately from top pair hands (Kx), middle pair (Jx), and flush draws. A bet of around 40% of the pot builds the pot while keeping worse hands in. If raised, I will commit my stack. Checking risks the board completing a draw or the opponent checking back and killing action.

Late in tournament (141/150), deep stack (17k) vs short-stacked Pro (1.4k) in SB who raised preflop wide and now donk-bets small 200 into 700 pot on Js Kd 3s flop.
