Hold Kc Td for top pair top kicker (TPTK), which is very strong vs his likely wide SB range including Ax, QJ, suited connectors/draws. Board drawy with spade flush draw and gutshots (e.g. QTs, ATs). Raising extracts value from worse (KQ, AJ, pairs, draws), protects vs equity denial, and applies pressure to short stack who may fold, call light, or shove (where I'm ahead). Calling too passive, risks multiway or free cards. Raising to ~4.5x bet size for pot control and max value given effective stacks.

I hold Top Two Pair (Kings and Jacks) on a wet board (Js Kd 3s) containing a flush draw. I bet 200 and my opponent check-raised to 900. Given the board texture, my hand is extremely strong but vulnerable to turns that complete flushes or straights. My stack is 1425 chips, and the pot is 1600. Calling would leave me with only ~725 chips in a ~2300 pot (0.3 SPR), which is an awkward stack size. The opponent's range includes flush draws, top pairs (Kx), and possibly sets (33). I am ahead of most of this range. Shoving all-in maximizes value against draws and top pairs while protecting my equity. I will move all-in for my remaining chips.

Facing a short-stacked Pro's all-in reraise to 1625 total (725 more) into a 3025 pot (~4:1 odds) on a drawy Js Kd 3s flop with TPTK (Kc Td). Pro's SB raise pre and donk-shove range includes strong hands like sets (33/JJ rare, KK blocked), KJ, QJs/ATs draws, Ax spades, but I'm ahead of most (TPTK crushes draws, worse Ax/pairs). No fold equity needed vs all-in; pot odds demand a call. Deep stack (16k+) can afford; maximizes EV late in tournament (141/150, 4 players). Flash out, heads-up spot.

I am dealt pocket Jacks (Jc, Js) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a premium starting hand. My goal is to maximize my stack as we approach the end of the tournament (Hand 142 of 150). I have the largest stack at the table. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to build a pot and narrow the field. Pro is relatively short-stacked, so I should be prepared for a potential shove from him, which I would likely call.

I am holding 2d 6d on the Button, facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the UTG player (Flash). My stack is 3750 (37.5 BBs), making me the short stack at the table. 2d 6d is a very weak hand, especially against a UTG opening range which is typically stronger. While I have position, the hand has poor playability and equity. Calling would commit nearly 8% of my stack with a marginal holding, and 3-betting is too risky given my stack depth and hand strength. Conserving chips for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

Hand Ts3h is complete garbage: offsuit, gapped, low cards, no straight or flush potential worth playing OOP. Flash raised from UTG (early position in 4-handed), indicating strength. Pot odds are marginal (calling 250 into 450, ~2:1), but postflop I'll be OOP with no equity against his range. Stacks are deep (160BB+), no fold equity for a 3bet bluff. Late in tournament (hand 142/150), preserve stack for better spots. Clear fold.

Holding 3s8s in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. This is a very weak hand - 83 suited has minimal playability even with the suited aspect. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds isn't enough to justify calling with such a weak holding against an UTG open. With only 8 hands left and a healthy stack of 16k, I should preserve chips and wait for better spots.

I am in UTG (first to act) in a 4-handed game with a stack of 37.5 BBs. My hand, King-Deuce suited (K2s), is a very marginal holding. While suited Kings have some playability, opening from UTG with a weak kicker is risky, especially against three opponents who all cover me significantly and can apply pressure. K2s is generally below the opening threshold for this position. Preserving my stack for better opportunities in these final hands is the optimal strategy.

Pocket Tens is a premium hand on the button with position on both blinds. Pro has folded UTG, leaving dead money in the pot (150). Raising standard 3x the BB builds the pot for value while denying equity to weaker hands and potentially isolating if they call or fold. Effective stacks are deep (160BB for me), so TT plays well postflop. Raise to 300 total.

A5 suited is a decent hand to defend from the SB against a button open. I have the nut flush draw potential and blocker to aces. Calling 250 to see a flop is reasonable given our stack depth and the hand's playability. 3-betting is also viable but calling keeps the pot smaller while out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6h. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. While I am getting good pot odds to call (200 to win 700), my hand is very weak and I will be out of position against both opponents for the rest of the hand. Given that we are at hand 143 of 150 and I currently hold the chip lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks or enter marginal pots with poor starting hands. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities in the final few hands.

I flopped QUAD ACES! This is the absolute nuts. With Ac5c on a Jd As Ad board, I have four aces. The best way to maximize value here is to check and let Elon continue his aggression as the preflop raiser. If he has any piece of this board (Jx, Ax) or a pocket pair, he'll likely c-bet. Betting might scare him off. Slow-playing is optimal with this monster.

Flop of As Ad Jd is disastrous for pocket Tens, as it crushes my hand with paired top Aces on board. Claude's SB OOP call to my button raise includes a wide range heavy on Ax (dominating me), pairs below T, suited connectors, or broadway. Many Ax improve to top pair/top kicker or better. Diamond flush draw possible. He checks first, suggesting possible weakness or trap, but betting TT here is pure bluff with low equity vs his calling range (Ax calls, folds worse). With deep stacks (157BB), position, and pot 700, check preserves stack. If he bets turn/river, easy fold; if checks back, turn may improve or allow cheap showdown. Maximize EV by realizing equity selectively late in tournament (hand 143/150). Check to pot control and induce bluffs later if board stays clean.

I have QUAD ACES - the absolute nuts. Elon checked back the flop, so he likely has a marginal hand at best. The Qs turn adds potential holdings like Qx or straight draws (KT). If I check again, he might check back and I extract zero value. A small bet looks like I'm trying to steal or have a marginal hand, which could induce a call from Qx/Jx/pocket pairs or even a bluff raise. Betting around 1/3 pot seems optimal to get value while appearing weak.

Pocket Tens is completely crushed on this As Ad Jd Qs board after checking through flop. Claude's check on the paired Aces flop suggests possible Ax slowplay, medium pair, or draw, but his small 250 bet into 700 pot on turn (now 950 pot) after bringing Qs (spades flush draw, broadway possibilities) smells like value from Ax, AJ/AQ, or even better. TT has minimal equity (~10-15% at best vs his OOP calling range: heavy Ax, KQ/KJ suited, suited connectors/draws) and poor showdown value. Pot odds ~4:1 require ~17% equity, but villain likely continues on river with better. Deep stacks (157BB), late tournament (143/150), position irrelevant with no fold equity needed; folding preserves stack for better spots with 8 hands left. No bluff raise viable OOP trap potential or history doesn't support.

UTG with 4 active players in a deep-stack tournament spot late in the event (hand 144/150). As2s is a speculative suited ace but marginal for an open-raise from early position due to poor playability OOP with a weak kicker and limited straight potential. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots, especially with Pro short-stacked in BB who may shove wide. Standard discipline maximizes long-term EV.

On the button with T7o after Elon folded. This is a marginal hand but decent position for a steal attempt against the blinds. Pro is short-stacked at 3650, which could mean they either fold or shove. T7o is on the weaker side of button opens, but with only 7 hands left in the tournament, I should be selectively aggressive. I'll make a standard raise to try to take down the blinds.

I am holding Q3o in the Small Blind. Claude, the button, has raised to 250. This is a standard steal position, but Q3o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. Pro in the Big Blind is relatively short-stacked (3,650) and might decide to shove over a call. Since I am the chip leader with only 7 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips or play a marginal hand out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to protect my lead.

Current blinds are 50/100. I am in the Big Blind with Qc7h. The Button (Claude, Chip Leader) raises to 250. The Small Blind folds. The pot is 400 (including my 100 blind), and I need to call 150 to see a flop. This offers pot odds of approximately 27% (150/550). While Q7o has roughly 41-44% raw equity against a standard button opening range, realizing that equity out of position is difficult, especially against a deep-stacked opponent who can apply significant pressure. With only 6 hands remaining in the tournament and being the shortest stack (36.5 BBs), preserving chips is prioritized over taking marginal/neutral EV spots that carry high reverse implied odds (domination issues with Qx). Folding is the disciplined play to avoid bleeding chips.
T7o from UTG is a weak hand. With 6 hands remaining and a comfortable stack of 16,862, there's no need to open with such a marginal hand from early position. Easy fold.

I am the chip leader with approximately 238 BBs. Hand 145 of 150 is approaching the end of the tournament, and I am on the Button. Jh 9d is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Pro (SB) is relatively short with 36 BBs, while Elon (BB) has about 156 BBs. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with a pair of Fours (4h 4c). The Button (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB). My stack is 3,600 (36BB), making me the short stack at the table. While a low pair has some potential for set mining, calling 3BB out of position against the chip leader is risky. I would need to hit a set on the flop to continue confidently, which happens only about 12% of the time. The Big Blind is also yet to act and could squeeze, forcing me to fold. Given the stack depth and the disadvantage of playing a small pair out of position without initiative, folding is the most prudent long-term play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB) with pot already at 450, offering great implied odds (pot odds ~2.25:1, needing ~31% equity). A3o blocks AA and AK+, has ~38-42% equity against a typical button opening range (40-50% hands). Others folded preflop (UTG and SB), so likely HU vs Flash. Deep effective stacks (my 156BB), so can realize equity postflop with positionally tricky but playable hand. Ace blocker good for bluffs if needed. Standard defend; folding too tight late in tournament with dead money.

HU vs button on a KK2 rainbow flop, dry board that smashes villain's wide opening range (many Kx, broadway, pairs). Ad3h has ace-high with Ad blocker to top of range but no pair, no draw, and very low equity (~20-25% vs typical button range postflop). Act first out of position; betting polar and offers no value/fold equity with this weak ace. Check-calling commits too much equity on missed flop without position. Check-fold standard: realize preflop defense was for position/multi-street play, but this texture too bad. Deep stacks but near end of tournament (hand 145/150), preserve stack for better spots rather than spew.

On a very dry, paired board like 2c-Ks-Kd, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage. Since I raised from the Button and Elon checked the flop, a standard continuation bet is highly effective here. Elon likely missed this board with most of his defending range, and a small bet puts him in a difficult position to continue with air or marginal hands. I will bet 300 into the 650 pot to take it down immediately.

Facing a bet of 300 into 950 on a KK2 rainbow flop OOP with Ad3h (ace-high, no pair, no draw). This board heavily favors villain's button opening range, which includes KK+, AK, KQ-KT, pairs, broadway suited connectors. Ad blocks some AK/AA but Kd on board; equity ~20-25% vs polarized betting range (value: sets, two-pair, trips with Kx; bluffs: Ax suited, gutshots like QJ). Pot odds ~3:1 (need 25%) but realized equity poor OOP on dry paired board--villain rarely folds better, and turning the corner unlikely (few live cards). Calling bloats pot (to ~1550 OOP), sets up difficult turns/rivers without initiative. Raising unprofitable without strong fold equity blockers or combo draws. Preflop defense was multiway/positional, but flop texture demands check-fold to preserve deep stack late in tournament (hand 145/150) for premium spots. Standard exploitative fold vs aggressive button.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is essentially the Cutoff. Ace-Five offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position. Given that I am the chip leader and we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 146 of 150), I want to maintain my aggressive image and apply pressure to the shorter stacks. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my recent play and serves to either take down the blinds or define the hand against the remaining opponents.

Current tournament situation: 4 players left, hand 146 of 150. I am on the Button with 3600 chips (36 BB). The opponent 'Flash' (UTG/CO) is the chip leader with over 23k and opens to 300. Flash has been opening frequently recently. Although K8s is a playable hand, especially 4-handed, facing an open from the chip leader puts me in a spot where I am often dominated (by better Kings or Aces). Additionally, calling invites the two massive stacks in the blinds (Elon and Claude) to squeeze or call, putting me in a difficult multi-way pot. With only a few hands remaining, preserving my stack of 36 BB is preferable to speculating with a marginal hand in a spot where I lack initiative. Folding is the prudent low-variance play to maintain my stack size.

Hand #146, preflop in SB with K7o (King-seven offsuit). Flash raised to 300 from UTG in a 4-handed game, which suggests a strong range (premium hands like pairs, big aces, broadway). Pro folded on button. Pot is 450, to call 250 more for ~1.8:1 odds, but K7o has poor equity (~30-35% at best vs tight UTG range) and plays terribly OOP postflop. Deep stacks (153 BB), near end of tournament (146/150 hands), no need to speculate with marginal hand. Raising is bluff-only, suicidal OOP vs strong range. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

Q8o in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. Getting pot odds of about 2.25:1 (200 to win 450). Q8o is marginal but playable as a BB defense against a standard raise. With 5 hands left and a healthy stack, I can take a flop here and play cautiously postflop. Folding is also reasonable but the price is good enough to see a flop in position relative to the raiser.

Missed flop completely with Q8o. Board is Ad 9c 2d - ace high board favors Flash's UTG opening range. I have no pair, no draw. Standard check here and likely fold to any bet. No reason to bluff into this board against the preflop aggressor.

On a board of Ad-9c-2d, I have flopped top pair with an Ace. Although my kicker (5) is weak, I am ahead of most of Claude's big blind calling range, which likely includes middle pairs, suited connectors, and diamond draws. Claude has checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. I will place a standard continuation bet of approximately half the pot (300 chips) to extract value from weaker hands like a 9 or a diamond draw, and to protect my hand while maintaining control of the pot in position.
Completely missed flop with Q8o on Ad 9c 2d. No pair, no draws. Ace-high board heavily favors Flash's UTG range. Easy fold - no reason to continue with zero equity.

Current Hand 147/150. I am UTG in a 4-handed game (effectively Cutoff). My stack is 3600 (36 BB). A5s is a strong suited connector that falls well within the standard opening range for the Cutoff. Given the late stage of the tournament and my position as the short stack, I need to accumulate chips where possible. A min-raise or slightly larger (2.5x) is standard here to steal blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. I will raise to 250.

9s5d is a complete trash hand with no straight or flush potential against an UTG raise from Pro, who likely has a strong range (pairs, broadway, suited connectors). Position on button is nice, but equity is too low (~20-30% vs tight range), pot odds not sufficient (calling 250 into 400 for ~2.6:1), and playability postflop is poor with dominated hands and reverse implied odds. Late in tournament (hand 147/150), stacks deep except Pro shortish at 3350, better to preserve 15350 stack for premium spots rather than spew chips on speculative hand.

JTs is a solid suited connector with good playability. Pro raised from UTG with a short stack (~33bb), suggesting decent strength. Calling here gives me position against Pro postflop and keeps the pot small while allowing Flash to come along. 3-betting would commit Pro with his short stack. Calling 200 more is the best line to see a flop with this drawing hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ac 4d. Pro (UTG) raised to 250, and Claude (SB) called. I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 600, giving me 4:1 odds. Pro is playing a relatively short stack (3,350), so his opening range might be wider or more desperate as we near the end of the tournament (Hand 147 of 150). While my kicker is weak, the price to see a flop is excellent. Raising here would likely commit me against Pro if he jams, which I'd rather avoid with A4o. Calling allows me to see the flop and play post-flop with position on Claude and relative position on the aggressor Pro.

I flopped top pair with Jacks and a Ten kicker on 4d-Jd-8h. This is a great spot to bet for value and protection. The board has flush draw potential (two diamonds) and some straight draw possibilities. Pro is short-stacked (~13bb remaining) and raised preflop from UTG. I should lead out with a ~55% pot bet to extract value from worse hands and charge draws.

On this flop of Jd-8h-4d, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Claude, the Small Blind, has led out with a 'donk bet' of 400 into a pot of 750. Leading into two players, including the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), usually indicates a hand like a Jack, a strong draw, or a mid-pair. My bottom pair is very vulnerable, especially with Pro still to act behind me. Pro is short-stacked (approx. 31bb) and could easily shove over the top if he has an overpair or a Jack. Given that we are at hand 147 of 150 and I am the current chip leader, it is more prudent to fold this weak hand and avoid potentially being squeezed out of a larger pot, preserving my stack for the final few hands.

I am in the UTG position (4-handed) with As5s. I raised pre-flop and got called by both blinds. The flop comes 4d Jd 8h, giving me only Ace-high with backdoor flush and straight possibilities. The Small Blind leads out (donk bets) for 400 into a pot of 750. The Big Blind folds. This lead often indicates a piece of the board (like an 8, 4, or weak J) or a flush draw wanting to set the price. With only Ace-high, no immediate draw, and being the short stack deep in the tournament (Hand 147/150), I cannot justify floating here. The pot odds are insufficient for my equity against a standard donk-betting range. Preserving my 3350 stack is more important than chasing a marginal spot.

UTG position with 2h6s, a complete trash hand lacking high card strength, suitedness, or straight potential. Standard play is to fold preflop from early position to avoid playing out of position with marginal equity. Pot odds to limp are poor long-term in a tournament setting, especially with only 3 hands left after this one. Preserve stack for premium hands.

A8o on the button is a clear raise after UTG folds. This is a standard open spot with a decent ace. Pro in the BB is short-stacked at 32.5bb, so I should be prepared to face a shove. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x).

We are at hand 148 of 150, and I currently hold a significant chip lead with approximately 24,000 chips. The next closest player is Claude with roughly 16,800. My primary objective in these final hands is to protect my stack and avoid unnecessary high-variance situations against the other big stacks. Claude has raised from the button, and I am in the small blind with Jack-Six of hearts. While suited, this is a marginal hand to play out of position, especially since a call might invite the short-stacked Pro (BB) to shove. Given the stage of the tournament and my position, folding is the most prudent play to maintain my lead for the final two hands.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo, facing a standard 2.5x open from the Button (Claude). Given the wide range of a Button opener, QJo is well ahead of their bottom range and has good playability post-flop. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 400, requiring ~27% equity), and my hand has significantly more equity than that against a typical stealing range. With a stack of ~32bb, 3-betting is a viable option but risks bloating the pot out of position with a hand that is often dominated by a 4-bet or calling range (like AQ/KQ/pairs). Calling is the most standard and lower-variance +EV line to realize my equity.

I have Top Pair with a Queen kicker on a dry, rainbow board (3h 8s Jd). This is a strong hand, especially in a Blind vs. Button scenario. The standard play out of position is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Checking allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs and worse value hands (like lower pairs or straight draws). Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his air and only get action from hands that beat me or have strong equity. I plan to check and call if he bets.

I flopped middle pair with top kicker (8h) on a 3h-8s-Jd board. Pro checked from BB. This is a good spot to c-bet for value - I'm ahead of most of Pro's checking range (low pairs, ace-highs, random hands). The board is fairly dry with only the J as an overcard. A ~50% pot bet is appropriate for value and protection against overcards.

I hold Top Pair with a strong Queen kicker (QJo) on a dry J-8-3 rainbow board. This is a very strong hand in a Button vs. Big Blind scenario. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (275 into 550), I am well ahead of the opponent's wide button opening range, which includes many worse pairs (8x, 3x, pocket pairs like 99, TT), draws (T9, etc.), and pure bluffs. Raising would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot and isolate me against hands that beat me (AJ, sets, overpairs). Calling is the standard play to control the pot size out of position, keep the opponent's range wide, and allow them to potentially fire another barrel on the turn. Given I am the short stack with only 3 hands remaining, I must take this +EV spot to accumulate chips without taking unnecessary risks by bloating the pot immediately.

The turn card is the Ten of Spades (Ts), making the board [3h 8s Jd Ts]. This card connects somewhat with the board, creating straight possibilities (9-7 makes a straight, Q-9 makes a straight) and bringing a backdoor flush draw. However, I still hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a strong kicker (Queen). Additionally, I have picked up a gutshot straight draw (a 9 would make the 8-9-T-J-Q straight). Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who c-betted the flop, the standard play is to check again. Leading out (donking) usually allows the opponent to play perfectly—folding their air and continuing with better hands or strong draws. Checking gives the opponent a chance to bluff with missed overcards or semi-bluff with spade draws, or to check back with a weaker made hand for pot control. I intend to check and evaluate his action.

Turn brought Ts which is a dangerous card - makes straights possible (Q9, KQ), adds flush draws, and gives Pro trips if they had T. My middle pair 8s with ace kicker is now more vulnerable. Pro has checked again with only 2825 behind (~2.5x pot). Betting risks getting check-raised by better hands (Jx, Tx, straights). Checking back gives pot control with a marginal made hand and lets me evaluate the river. If Pro was floating with draws, I can call a reasonable river bet.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker on a board of [3h 8s Jd Ts 8c]. The opponent opened on the Button, c-betted the flop, but checked back the turn. This turn check usually indicates a capped range—likely a weak Jack, a Ten, a pocket pair, or a draw that decided to take a free card. The river pairs the 8, which is unlikely to have improved his range significantly given the turn check (though A8/98 are remote possibilities). Betting here is thin; worse hands like Tx or 99 might fold, and better hands (trips, straights, better Jacks) will call or raise. Checking is the superior play as it allows me to safely get to showdown against his marginal made hands (which he will likely check back) and potentially induces bluffs from his missed spade or straight draws. With only 3 hands left in the tournament and being the short stack, avoiding a spot where I get raised off my equity is prudent.

The river 8c gave me trip 8s with ace kicker - a very strong hand! Pro has checked again, showing weakness throughout this hand. Their passive line (call pre, check-call flop, check turn, check river) suggests a capped range like Jx, Tx, or weak pairs trying to get to showdown. I should value bet to extract from their pairs. With Pro having 2825 behind and pot at 1100, a medium bet around 550 gets value from Jx/Tx while not scaring them off. Only J8/T8 full houses beat me, which are unlikely given the action.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker. The hand started with a standard defense in the Big Blind against a Button open. I called a c-bet on the flop. Crucially, the opponent checked back the turn on a Ts card, which offered straight and flush draws. This check often indicates a capped range—either a marginal made hand (like a weak Jack, Ten, or pocket pair) or a draw taking a free card. A strong hand like AJ, two pair, or a set would likely bet the turn to charge draws. The river pairs the 8. While this could improve an 8x hand to trips, an 8 would rarely check the turn. The opponent bets 550 into 1100 (half pot). This sizing is often used for thin value or as a cheap bluff. I am getting 3:1 odds (25% equity required). With Top Pair, I beat all missed draws (spades, straight draws like KQ/Q9) and random bluffs. While I lose to AJ/KJ or the occasional slow-played monster, the turn check makes those less likely. In a Button vs Blind scenario, folding Top Pair to a single bet after a turn check is too exploitable. Given the tournament situation (short stack, very late stage), I must call to capture the pot against his bluffs.
J3o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak offsuit hand that plays poorly from early position. No reason to open raise with this - I'll wait for a better spot.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin as we approach the final hand of the tournament. Holding 7-5 offsuit on the button is a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, Pro (the small blind) is short-stacked with only 2,225 chips and is likely looking for a spot to shove. Elon (the big blind) also has a healthy stack. Given my priority is to protect my lead and minimize unnecessary risk in the penultimate hand, I will choose the safest path and fold this trash hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2d, a very weak suited hand. The action has folded to me. My stack is 2225 (22bb). While the pot odds to complete are tempting (calling 50 to play a pot of 200), playing a weak hand out of position against the Big Blind is generally negative EV, especially with this stack depth where I cannot easily navigate post-flop without hitting hard. With only 1 hand remaining after this, preserving chips is slightly prioritized over taking marginal -EV spots. 92s is near the bottom of the range and is a standard fold.

This is the final hand of the tournament (Hand 150). I currently hold a significant lead with 24,188 chips, approximately 6,000 more than the second-highest stack (Claude). Queen-Eight offsuit (Qh 8s) is a marginal hand to open from Under The Gun (UTG) even in a 4-handed game. My primary goal is to maximize my stack size, and given the weakness of the hand and the risk of being re-raised by the blinds or the button, folding is the most prudent play to secure my current lead and avoid unnecessary losses on the last hand.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am on the Button with a very weak hand, 5d Th (T5 offsuit). My stack is 2225 chips (22 BBs), which puts me firmly in 4th place with no mathematical possibility of improving my rank in a single hand (3rd place has over 15,000 chips). My objective is to maximize my final stack size. Shoving 22 BBs with T5o is negative chip EV due to the high likelihood of being dominated if called and the low equity of the hand. Trying to steal the blinds is risky against massive stacks who can call widely. Preserving the 2225 chips by folding is the highest EV play compared to risking the stack with a garbage hand.

Last hand of the tournament. In SB with KdJs, a strong hand for stealing blinds after UTG and Button folds. Only BB behind with deep stack. Raising to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure, builds pot with good equity, and takes initiative postflop. Folding or limping suboptimal with position and hand strength.

Final hand of the tournament. I have 5h3c - a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Elon raised to 300 from SB. With 18087 chips, I'm in a solid position - no need to defend my big blind with such a weak holding. Easy fold.
